- The **maximum AC swing** is determined by the **smaller** of the two distances from the Q-point to the AC saturation and AC cutoff limits.
- $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$ is a practical (non-ideal) boundary — examiners may deduct marks if you assume $V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 0$ in low-voltage circuits.
- Always **state the region of operation explicitly** in every KTU numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Bias, Self-Bias and Voltage Divider Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fixed Bias, Self-Bias and Voltage Divider Bias ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Transistor Biasing? **Transistor Biasing** is the process of setting up a transistor's DC operating conditions (quiescent operating point or Q-point) by connecting external DC voltage sources to its terminals. The Q-point defines the values of collector current ($I_C$) and collector-emitter voltage ($V_{CE}$) when no AC signal is applied at the input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Biasing is the process of providing a suitable DC voltage to the transistor terminals so that the transistor operates in the desired region (Active, Saturation, or Cut-off) irrespective of variations in temperature, $\beta$ (current gain), and transistor replacement (device-to-device variation).* ### 1.2 Why is Biasing Needed? (The Three Core Requirements) A properly biased transistor circuit must satisfy three fundamental conditions: 1. **Establish a stable Q-point** at the center of the active region for faithful (distortion-free) amplification of the AC signal. 2. **Make $I_C$ independent of temperature variations** (thermal runaway prevention). 3. **Make $I_C$ independent of transistor parameter variations**, especially the current gain $\beta$ (also denoted as $h_{FE}$ or $\beta_{DC}$). > [!NOTE] > The position of the Q-point directly determines the **maximum unclipped AC output swing**. If the Q-point is too close to the saturation or cut-off region, the output waveform will be clipped (distorted) during large signal excursions. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Thermostat on a Hiking Trail" Imagine you are hiking on a mountain. Your energy level (analogous to the transistor's output current $I_C$) depends on three things: - **The amount of food you eat** (base current $I_B$) - **The outside temperature** (junction temperature $T$) - **Your personal metabolism** (current gain $\beta$) If your energy level keeps changing unpredictably due to temperature or metabolism differences, you cannot complete a steady hike (amplify a clean signal). A good **biasing circuit** is like a **smart backpack with a built-in calorie regulator** — it constantly adjusts the food supply (base current) so that your energy level stays perfectly steady, regardless of how cold it gets or how your metabolism changes. Voltage Divider Bias is the most "intelligent" of these regulators. ### 1.4 The Three Biasing Circuits Covered in This Module | S.No | Biasing Type | Key Feature | Stability | |------|-------------|-------------|-----------| | 1 | **Fixed Bias** | Base current is set by a single fixed resistor from $V_{CC}$ | **Poor** | | 2 | **Self-Bias (Collector-to-Base Bias)** | Base resistor is connected to collector | **Better than Fixed** | | 3 | **Voltage Divider Bias** | Base voltage is set by a resistive divider | **Excellent** (most widely used) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis:** In KTU 2024 scheme university examinations, the **Stability Factor ($S$)** is the single most important parameter asked for each biasing circuit. Students are expected to derive $S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}$ (stability against reverse saturation current) for each circuit. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Stability Factor — The Heart of Biasing Analysis The **Stability Factor $S$** is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current $I_{CO}$ (also called leakage current $I_{CBO}$), with $I_B$ and $\beta$ held constant. $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\rvert_{I_B, \beta = \text{constant}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Meaning:** $S$ tells us how many times the collector current changes for a unit change in leakage current. A value of $S = 10$ means $I_C$ increases 10 times faster than $I_{CO}$. **Lower $S$ = Better stability.** The ideal value is $S = 1$. In general, for any biasing circuit: $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CO}$$ Differentiating with respect to $I_{CO}$: $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \beta \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} + (\beta + 1)$$ Since $\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}}$ depends on the specific biasing network, $S$ differs for each circuit. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet #### A. Fixed Bias Circuit | Parameter | Formula | Description | |-----------|---------|-------------| | Base Current | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | Set by $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$ | | Collector Current | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | Independent of $I_{CO}$ ideally | | Q-Point $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | From KVL on output loop | | Stability Factor | $S_{FB} = \beta + 1$ | **Worst** (large value) | #### B. Self-Bias (Collector-to-Base Bias) Circuit | Parameter | Formula | Description | |-----------|---------|-------------| | Base Current | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + R_C}$ | Depends on $V_{CE}$ | | Collector Current | $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CO}$ | Includes leakage term | | Q-Point $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CE} = \dfrac{V_{CC} + \beta I_{CO} R_B - V_{BE} \dfrac{R_B + R_C}{R_B + R_C/\beta}}{1 + \beta}$ | Complex expression | | Stability Factor | $S_{SB} = \dfrac{\beta + 1}{1 + \beta \dfrac{R_C}{R_B + R_C}}$ | Better than Fixed Bias | #### C. Voltage Divider Bias Circuit | Parameter | Formula | Description | |-----------|---------|-------------| | Thevenin Voltage | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage at base w.r.t ground | | Thevenin Resistance | $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Resistance seen from base | | Base Current | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E}$ | Includes emitter degeneration | | Emitter Current | $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B \approx I_C$ | For large $\beta$ | | Collector-Emitter Voltage | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$ | Across the transistor | | Stability Factor | $S_{VDB} = \dfrac{(\beta + 1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + R_E + \beta R_E} = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(1+R_{TH}/R_E)}{1+\beta+R_{TH}/R_E}$ | **Best** (close to 1) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb for Voltage Divider Bias:** If the condition $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ is satisfied, the circuit is considered to have excellent stability, and we can approximate $I_C \approx I_E \approx \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E}$. This approximation is **frequently expected** in KTU board problems. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fixed Bias:** Used in switching circuits and digital logic where a specific $I_B$ is needed quickly; not used in precision analog amplifiers. - **Self-Bias:** Used in simple low-cost amplifier stages where moderate thermal stability is acceptable. - **Voltage Divider Bias:** The **universal choice** in nearly all linear amplifier ICs (like 741 op-amp input stages, audio pre-amplifiers, RF amplifiers) due to its near-ideal stability. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Circuit 1: Fixed Bias — Stability Factor Derivation **Circuit Description:** A single resistor $R_B$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ and the base. Collector resistor $R_C$ is between $V_{CC}$ and the collector. The emitter is grounded directly. **Step 1: Apply KVL to the Input (Base–Emitter) Loop** $$V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}$$ Solving for $I_B$: $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ **Step 2: Collector Current Equation (including leakage term)** For a transistor in the active region: $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CO}$$ **Step 3: Differentiate to Find the Stability Factor $S$** Differentiating the $I_C$ expression with respect to $I_{CO}$ while keeping $I_B$ and $\beta$ constant: $$\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \beta \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} + (\beta + 1)$$ In fixed bias, $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ is **independent of $I_{CO}$** (since $V_{CC}$, $V_{BE}$, and $R_B$ do not change with leakage). Therefore: $$\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} = 0$$ Substituting back: $$S_{FB} = 0 + (\beta + 1)$$ $$\boxed{S_{FB} = \beta + 1}$$ **Conclusion:** Since $\beta$ is typically between 50 and 400, $S_{FB}$ is very large, making fixed bias the **least thermally stable** configuration. Any small change in $I_{CO}$ is amplified by $\beta$ in the output. --- ### 3.2 Circuit 2: Self-Bias (Collector-to-Base Bias) — Stability Factor Derivation **Circuit Description:** $R_B$ is connected between the **collector** and the base, instead of $V_{CC}$. This feedback mechanism stabilizes the Q-point. **Step 1: Apply KVL to the Input Loop** The current through $R_B$ is $I_{R_B} = I_C + I_B$ (since the base current enters the transistor and collector current flows out of the collector node, both combining through $R_B$). Going around the loop (collector → base → emitter → ground → back to $V_{CC}$ through $R_C$): $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + I_{R_B} R_B + V_{BE}$$ Since $I_{R_B} = I_C + I_B$: $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + (I_C + I_B) R_B + V_{BE}$$ $$V_{CC} = I_C (R_C + R_B) + I_B R_B + V_{BE}$$ **Step 2: Express $I_B$ in Terms of $I_C$ and $I_{CO}$** Using $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CO}$: $$I_B = \frac{I_C - (\beta + 1) I_{CO}}{\beta}$$ **Step 3: Substitute $I_B$ into the KVL Equation** $$V_{CC} = I_C (R_C + R_B) + \frac{I_C - (\beta + 1) I_{CO}}{\beta} \cdot R_B + V_{BE}$$ Multiplying throughout by $\beta$: $$\beta V_{CC} = \beta I_C (R_C + R_B) + R_B I_C - (\beta + 1) I_{CO} R_B + \beta V_{BE}$$ **Step 4: Differentiate the KVL Equation with Respect to $I_{CO}$** Since $V_{CC}$, $V_{BE}$, and $R_B$ are constants: $$0 = \beta \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} (R_C + R_B) + R_B \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} - (\beta + 1) R_B$$ $$0 = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} [\beta (R_C + R_B) + R_B] - (\beta + 1) R_B$$ $$0 = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} [\beta R_C + \beta R_B + R_B] - (\beta + 1) R_B$$ $$S_{SB} \cdot [\beta R_C + (\beta + 1) R_B] = (\beta + 1) R_B$$ $$\boxed{S_{SB} = \frac{(\beta + 1) R_B}{\beta R_C + (\beta + 1) R_B} = \frac{\beta + 1}{1 + \beta \frac{R_C}{R_B + R_C}}}$$ **Interpretation:** Since $S_{SB} < \beta + 1$, self-bias is **more stable** than fixed bias. The negative feedback action: if $I_C$ tries to increase, $V_C$ drops, reducing $I_B$, which pushes $I_C$ back down. --- ### 3.3 Circuit 3: Voltage Divider Bias — Stability Factor Derivation **Circuit Description:** Two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a voltage divider across $V_{CC}$. The base is connected to the junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$. An emitter resistor $R_E$ is included for thermal stability. **Step 1: Replace the Voltage Divider Network by its Thevenin Equivalent** The Thevenin voltage at the base (open-circuit voltage at the base node): $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ The Thevenin resistance seen from the base: $$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ **Step 2: Apply KVL to the Base–Emitter Loop** $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E$$ Since $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B$: $$V_{TH} - V_{BE} = I_B R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) I_B R_E$$ $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$ **Step 3: Apply KVL to the Output Loop (Collector–Emitter)** $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} + I_E R_E$$ For Q-point determination: $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$$ **Step 4: Express $I_C$ in Terms of $I_B$ and $I_{CO}$** $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CO}$$ **Step 5: Differentiate the Base Loop Equation with Respect to $I_{CO}$** From the input loop KVL: $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + (\beta + 1) I_B R_E + \beta I_{CO} R_E$$ Rearranging to isolate $I_B$: $$V_{TH} - V_{BE} - \beta I_{CO} R_E = I_B [R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E]$$ Differentiating both sides with respect to $I_{CO}$ (with $V_{TH}$, $V_{BE}$, $\beta$, $R_{TH}$, $R_E$ constant): $$-\beta R_E = \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} [R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E]$$ $$\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} = \frac{-\beta R_E}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$ **Step 6: Compute the Stability Factor** Recall: $S = \beta \dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} + (\beta + 1)$ $$S_{VDB} = \beta \cdot \frac{-\beta R_E}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E} + (\beta + 1)$$ $$S_{VDB} = \frac{-\beta^2 R_E + (\beta + 1)[R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E]}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$ Expanding the numerator: $$-\beta^2 R_E + (\beta + 1) R_{TH} + (\beta + 1)^2 R_E$$ $$= (\beta + 1) R_{TH} + R_E [(\beta + 1)^2 - \beta^2]$$ $$= (\beta + 1) R_{TH} + R_E [\beta^2 + 2\beta + 1 - \beta^2]$$ $$= (\beta + 1) R_{TH} + R_E (2\beta + 1)$$ For large $\beta$ (where $2\beta + 1 \approx 2\beta + \beta = 3\beta$ is often approximated, but the exact form below is standard): $$S_{VDB} = \frac{(\beta + 1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$ $$\boxed{S_{VDB} = \frac{(\beta + 1)(1 + R_{TH}/R_E)}{1 + \beta + R_{TH}/R_E}}$$ **Step 7: Limiting Case Analysis** If $R_{TH}/R_E \to 0$ (i.e., $R_E$ is very large compared to $R_{TH}$): $$S_{VDB} \to \frac{(\beta + 1)(1)}{1 + \beta} = 1$$ This is the **ideal stability condition**! It shows why $R_E$ is the key stability-enhancing element in voltage divider bias. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Parameter | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage Divider Bias | |-----------|:----------:|:---------:|:-------------------:| | Stability Factor $S$ | $\beta + 1$ | $\dfrac{(\beta+1) R_B}{\beta R_C + (\beta+1) R_B}$ | $\dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH}+R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | | Typical $S$ Value | ~100 | ~20–50 | ~2–10 | | Thermal Runaway Risk | **Very High** | Moderate | **Very Low** | | Number of Resistors | 2 | 2 | 4 (or 3 with bypass) | | Use in Modern Designs | Rare | Rare | **Universal** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Three Biasing Topologies ```mermaid graph TD subgraph S1["FIXED BIAS TOPOLOGY"] VCC1["VCC Supply"] -->|Voltage Drop| RB1["R_B Resistor"] RB1 -->|Provides I_B| BASE1["Base Node"] BASE1 --> BJT1["BJT Transistor"] VCC1 --> RC1["R_C Resistor"] RC1 -->|Provides I_C| COLL1["Collector Node"] COLL1 --> BJT1 BJT1 --> GND1["Ground / Emitter"] end subgraph S2["SELF BIAS COLLECTOR-TO-BASE"] VCC2["VCC Supply"] --> RC2["R_C Resistor"] RC2 -->|Provides I_C| COLL2["Collector Node"] COLL2 -->|Feedback through R_B| RB2["R_B Resistor"] RB2 -->|Provides I_B| BASE2["Base Node"] BASE2 --> BJT2["BJT Transistor"] BJT2 --> GND2["Ground / Emitter"] end subgraph S3["VOLTAGE DIVIDER BIAS"] VCC3["VCC Supply"] --> R1["R_1 Upper Resistor"] R1 -->|Junction Node| BASE3["Base Node"] R2["R_2 Lower Resistor"] -->|Pulls to GND| BASE3 BASE3 --> BJT3["BJT Transistor"] BJT3 --> EMIT3["Emitter Node"] EMIT3 --> RE3["R_E Stabilizing Resistor"] RE3 --> GND3["Ground"] VCC3 --> RC3["R_C Resistor"] RC3 --> COLL3["Collector Node"] COLL3 --> BJT3 end ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — Q-Point Stabilization Mechanism in Voltage Divider Bias ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Temperature Rises"] --> B["I_CO Increases"] B --> C["I_C Tries to Increase"] C --> D["I_E Increases"] D --> E["Voltage Drop Across R_E Increases"] E --> F["V_BE = V_B - V_E Decreases"] F --> G["I_B Decreases"] G --> H["I_C = beta x I_B + I_CO_term Decreases"] H --> I["I_C Returns to Original Value"] I --> J["Q-Point Stabilized"] style A fill:#ff9999 style J fill:#99ff99 style E fill:#ffcc99 style F fill:#ffcc99 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the Flowchart:** The red block represents the disturbance (temperature rise), and the green block represents the desired outcome (stabilized Q-point). The orange blocks highlight the **negative feedback mechanism** — the heart of why voltage divider bias is so effective. ### 4.3 Biasing Circuit Decision Tree ```mermaid graph TD START["Need to Bias a BJT?"] --> Q1{"Is the application
switching only?"} Q1 -->|Yes| FIX["Use FIXED BIAS
S = beta + 1"] Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is cost-critical
and stability moderate
acceptable?"} Q2 -->|Yes| SELF["Use SELF BIAS
S = (beta+1)R_B / (beta R_C + (beta+1)R_B)"] Q2 -->|No| VDB["Use VOLTAGE DIVIDER BIAS
S approaches 1
INDUSTRY STANDARD"] style FIX fill:#ffcccc style SELF fill:#ffffcc style VDB fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Define the term "stability factor" with respect to transistor biasing. Why is a low value of stability factor desired in a biasing circuit?** **Model Answer:** The stability factor $S$ of a biasing circuit is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current $I_{CO}$, with $\beta$ and $I_B$ held constant: $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\rvert_{\beta, I_B}$$ A low value of $S$ is desired because it indicates that variations in $I_{CO}$ (caused by temperature changes) produce only small changes in $I_C$. This protects the transistor from **thermal runaway**, where unchecked increases in temperature and $I_C$ eventually destroy the device. The ideal value of $S$ is **1**, meaning $I_C$ is completely independent of $I_{CO}$. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Compare Fixed Bias and Voltage Divider Bias in terms of Q-point stability. Mention one advantage of voltage divider bias.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Fixed Bias | Voltage Divider Bias | |--------|:----------:|:-------------------:| | Stability Factor | $S = \beta + 1$ (very high) | $S \to 1$ (very low) | | Thermal Stability | Poor | Excellent | | Independence from $\beta$ | None | High | **Advantage of Voltage Divider Bias:** The Q-point is largely independent of variations in transistor current gain $\beta$ and operating temperature, due to the negative feedback introduced by the emitter resistor $R_E$. This makes it the industry standard for linear amplifier design. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts) out of an internal choice. --- #### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]` **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **self-bias (collector-to-base bias)** configuration for an NPN transistor. Derive the expression for its stability factor. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a **voltage divider bias** circuit with $V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, calculate the Q-point $(I_C, V_{CE})$ and the stability factor $S$. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Circuit Diagram (Refer to Mermaid S2 in Section 4):** The self-bias circuit has $R_B$ connected between the **collector** and **base** of the transistor, with $R_C$ between $V_{CC}$ and the collector, and the emitter grounded. **Derivation (Refer to Section 3.2 for full derivation):** Applying KVL to the input loop (collector → base → emitter → ground): $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + (I_C + I_B) R_B + V_{BE}$$ Substituting $I_B = \dfrac{I_C - (\beta+1)I_{CO}}{\beta}$: $$V_{CC} = I_C(R_C + R_B) + \frac{I_C - (\beta+1)I_{CO}}{\beta} \cdot R_B + V_{BE}$$ Differentiating with respect to $I_{CO}$ and solving: $$\boxed{S = \frac{(\beta+1) R_B}{\beta R_C + (\beta+1) R_B}}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1: Compute Thevenin Equivalent** $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \cdot \frac{5}{10 + 5} = 20 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 6.667 \text{ V}$$ $$R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.333 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 2: Compute Base Current $I_B$** $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{6.667 - 0.7}{3.333 + (101)(1)} = \frac{5.967}{104.333} = 0.0572 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 3: Compute Collector Current $I_C$** $$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 0.0572 = 5.72 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 4: Compute $V_{CE}$** $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E) = 20 - 5.72 \times (2 + 1) = 20 - 17.16 = 2.84 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5: Compute Stability Factor** $$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{(101)(3.333 + 1)}{3.333 + (101)(1)} = \frac{101 \times 4.333}{104.333} = \frac{437.6}{104.333} \approx 4.19$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{Q\text{-point: } (I_C, V_{CE}) = (5.72 \text{ mA}, 2.84 \text{ V}); \quad S = 4.19}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting $(\beta+1)$ instead of $\beta$** in the $I_B$ formula. Many students write $I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + \beta R_E}$ — this loses 1 mark. > 2. **Using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$** in the output KVL. Since $I_E \approx I_C$ for large $\beta$, this approximation is acceptable, but writing the exact form $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ earns full marks. > 3. **Computing $R_{TH}$ as $R_1 + R_2$ (series) instead of $R_1 \parallel R_2$** — this is a very common error worth 2 marks deduction. > 4. **Not mentioning units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units like mA and V. --- #### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 – Alternative Choice]` **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **fixed bias circuit**. Derive the expressions for $I_B$, $I_C$, and $V_{CE}$. Show that the stability factor is $S = \beta + 1$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the principle of operation of **voltage divider bias** circuit. Using Thevenin's theorem, derive the expression for the Q-point. State two reasons why voltage divider bias is the most commonly used biasing method. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Circuit Diagram (Refer to Mermaid S1 in Section 4):** **Step 1: Input Loop KVL** $$V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}$$ $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ **Step 2: Collector Current** $$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$$ **Step 3: Output Loop KVL** $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$ **Step 4: Stability Factor Derivation (Refer to Section 3.1)** Differentiating $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$ with respect to $I_{CO}$: Since $I_B$ is independent of $I_{CO}$ in fixed bias: $\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} = 0$ $$S = \beta(0) + (\beta+1) = \beta + 1$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Principle of Operation (Refer to Mermaid S3 in Section 4):** In voltage divider bias, the resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a potential divider across $V_{CC}$, providing a fixed voltage $V_{TH}$ at the base. The emitter resistor $R_E$ provides **DC negative feedback**: any increase in $I_C$ increases the voltage drop across $R_E$, which reduces $V_{BE}$ and consequently $I_B$, opposing the original increase. **Thevenin Equivalent and Q-Point Derivation:** Thevenin voltage: $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Thevenin resistance: $$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$$ Base current from input KVL: $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ Collector current: $$I_C = \beta I_B = \frac{\beta (V_{TH} - V_{BE})}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$ Collector-emitter voltage from output KVL: $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$$ **Two Reasons for Universal Acceptance:** 1. **Excellent Q-point stability:** The stability factor approaches 1, making the circuit highly immune to temperature variations and $\beta$ changes. 2. **Independence from transistor parameters:** The Q-point is almost entirely set by external passive components ($R_1$, $R_2$, $R_E$, $R_C$), making circuit performance predictable and reproducible across different transistor batches. --- ### 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Points — Master These Before the Exam:** - [x] **Definition of Stability Factor:** $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\rvert_{I_B, \beta}$ — must remember the **partial derivative** form for full marks. - [x] **Fixed Bias Formula:** $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ and $S = \beta + 1$ — easiest to derive, most likely to be asked as a sub-part. - [x] **Self-Bias Formula:** $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1) R_B}{\beta R_C + (\beta+1) R_B}$ — remember the denominator has $\beta R_C$, not just $R_C$. - [x] **Voltage Divider Bias — Thevenin Steps:** Compute $V_{TH}$ first, then $R_{TH}$, then $I_B$ using $R_{TH} + (\beta+1)R_E$ in the denominator. - [x] **Approximation Rule:** When $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$, use $I_C \approx \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E}$ — this simplifies problems dramatically. - [x] **Limiting Case:** As $R_{TH}/R_E \to 0$, $S_{VDB} \to 1$ — this is the key reason for the universal adoption of voltage divider bias. - [x] **Output KVL:** $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ — the $I_E R_E$ term is often mistakenly dropped, costing 1 mark. - [x] **Negative Feedback Mechanism:** Temperature rise → $I_{CO}$ increases → $I_C$ increases → $V_{R_E}$ increases → $V_{BE}$ decreases → $I_B$ decreases → $I_C$ returns to original value. Know this flow cold. - [x] **Order of Stability:** Voltage Divider Bias > Self-Bias > Fixed Bias (in terms of decreasing $S$ value, i.e., increasing stability). - [x] **Unit Discipline:** Always express $I_C$ in mA, $V_{CE}$ in V, and $R$ in k$\Omega$ for consistency in numerical answers.

topics.title: h-Parameter Model of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** h-Parameter Model of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **h-parameter (hybrid-parameter) model** is a small-signal linear two-port network representation of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) that uses a unique mix (hybrid) of input and output parameters to characterize its behavior in any one of the three standard configurations — Common Emitter (CE), Common Base (CB), or Common Collector (CC).

The "h" in h-parameter stands for **hybrid** because the dimensions of the four parameters are not all the same. Two of them are impedances (Ω), one is a dimensionless current ratio, and one is a dimensionless voltage ratio. This mixed nature is what gives the model its name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The h-parameter model is the *primary* small-signal equivalent circuit taught in Module 1 of PCBMT302. The University expects students to draw the exact h-model, derive the standard equations, and apply it to compute input impedance, output impedance, current gain, and voltage gain for CE, CB, and CC configurations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are controlling a powerful water pump with two small knobs. One knob adjusts how much water enters the pump (input), and the other knob adjusts the load at the outlet (output). You cannot simply "see" inside the pump, but you can describe how the output water pressure changes when you wiggle either knob.

The h-parameter model is essentially a "black-box description" of the BJT. Instead of digging into the internal physics (Ebers-Moll equations), we simply measure four numbers that describe:

- How the input voltage responds to changes in input current.
- How the input voltage responds to changes in output voltage (reverse feedback).
- How the output current responds to changes in input current (forward current gain).
- How the output current responds to changes in output voltage (output admittance).

These four numbers — `hie`, `hre`, `hfe`, `hoe` — completely define the small-signal AC behavior of the transistor in a particular configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "hybrid"?** Because input quantities (Vi, Ii) and output quantities (Vo, Io) are paired in a mixed way: the first equation expresses an *impedance* relationship, while the second is a *current gain* relationship.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical CE Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| hie | Input impedance (with output shorted) | **Ω (Ohms)** | **1 kΩ** |
| hre | Reverse voltage ratio (with input open) | **dimensionless** | **2.5 × 10⁻⁴** |
| hfe | Forward current gain (with output shorted) | **dimensionless** | **100** |
| hoe | Output admittance (with input open) | **mho (S)** | **25 μS** |
| Ic | Quiescent collector current | **mA** | **1.3 mA** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at room temperature (T = 300 K). This constant appears in all h-parameter derivations tied to device physics.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Port Network Concept

A BJT is a **three-terminal device** (Base, Emitter, Collector). To treat it as a two-port network, one terminal is selected as the common reference between the input port and the output port. The choice of this common terminal defines the configuration:

- **Common Emitter (CE):** Emitter is common between input and output.
- **Common Base (CB):** Base is common.
- **Common Collector (CC):** Collector is common.

For each configuration, we define four variables:
- Input voltage: $V_i$
- Input current: $I_i$
- Output voltage: $V_o$
- Output current: $I_o$

## 2.2 Why the h-Model? The Logic Behind It

The general two-port network can be characterized by six possible parameter sets: z-parameters, y-parameters, h-parameters, g-parameters, ABCD-parameters, and inverse-ABCD parameters. The **h-parameters** are preferred for BJTs for two compelling reasons:

1. **Forward current gain (hfe)** is easy to measure by shorting the output (which is naturally a low-impedance condition in a CE amplifier).
2. **Input impedance (hie)** and **output admittance (hoe)** are easy to measure by shorting the output and opening the input respectively — both convenient experimentally.

## 2.3 The Defining Equations of h-Parameters

The h-model is defined by two linear equations that express the input voltage and output current as functions of the input current and output voltage:

$$V_i = h_{ie} I_i + h_{re} V_o$$

$$I_o = h_{fe} I_i + h_{hoe} V_o$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **exact h-parameter equations** of a BJT. They form the foundation of all CE, CB, and CC amplifier analyses in Module 1.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_i = h_{ie} I_i + h_{re} V_o$ | Input voltage equation | Volts |
| 2 | $I_o = h_{fe} I_i + h_{hoe} V_o$ | Output current equation | Amps |
| 3 | $h_{ie} = \dfrac{V_i}{I_i}\bigg\vert_{V_o=0}$ | Short-circuit input impedance | Ω |
| 4 | $h_{re} = \dfrac{V_i}{V_o}\bigg\vert_{I_i=0}$ | Open-circuit reverse voltage ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $h_{fe} = \dfrac{I_o}{I_i}\bigg\vert_{V_o=0}$ | Short-circuit forward current gain | dimensionless |
| 6 | $h_{hoe} = \dfrac{I_o}{V_o}\bigg\vert_{I_i=0}$ | Open-circuit output admittance | mho (S) |
| 7 | $h_{ie} = r_\pi$ | hie equals the dynamic base-emitter resistance | Ω |
| 8 | $h_{ie} = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{\vert I_C \vert}$ | Exact expression in terms of bias current | Ω |
| 9 | $h_{fe} = \beta$ | Small-signal forward current gain of CE | dimensionless |
| 10 | $h_{oe} = \dfrac{1}{r_o}$ | hie equals inverse of output resistance | S |
| 11 | $A_I = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$ | CE current gain (with load) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $Z_i = h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$ | CE exact input impedance | Ω |
| 13 | $Z_o = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe}}{h_{ie} + R_S}}$ | CE exact output impedance | Ω |
| 14 | $A_V = \dfrac{A_I R_L}{Z_i}$ | Voltage gain (CE) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $h_{ib} = \dfrac{h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}}$ | Approximate CB input impedance | Ω |
| 16 | $h_{fb} = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{fe}}$ | Approximate CB current gain | dimensionless |
| 17 | $h_{ob} \approx h_{oe}$ | Approximate CB output admittance | S |
| 18 | $h_{ic} \approx h_{ie}$ | CC input impedance | Ω |
| 19 | $h_{fc} = -(1 + h_{fe})$ | CC current gain | dimensionless |
| 20 | $g_m = \dfrac{\vert I_C \vert}{V_T}$ | Transconductance | S |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximate vs Exact Formulas:** In most KTU problems, `hre` is so small (≈ 2.5 × 10⁻⁴) that it is taken as zero. The **approximate** formulas (`Zin = hie`, `Ai = -hfe`, `Zo = 1/hoe`) are what students should expect in 3-mark questions. The **exact** formulas are required for 14-mark derivations.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the h-Model

The h-parameter model is the **workhorse of small-signal BJT analysis** in commercial electronic design. It is used by:

- **Analog IC designers** to evaluate single-stage amplifier performance (gain, input/output impedance).
- **Audio equipment engineers** to design low-noise preamplifiers (especially CE and CC stages).
- **RF front-end designers** to compute matching networks.
- **SPICE simulators** (in legacy versions) used the h-parameter formulation as a compact model.
- **Bias-stabilized circuit designers** to evaluate the effect of source and load resistances on overall gain.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of h-Parameter Equations from Two-Port Definitions

**Step 1 — General two-port representation.** A BJT can be modeled as a linear two-port network with input port $(V_i, I_i)$ and output port $(V_o, I_o)$. By the superposition principle, the input voltage depends linearly on both the input current and the output voltage.

Write the input voltage as a linear combination:
$$V_i = A_1 I_i + A_2 V_o$$

**Step 2 — Define the coefficients.** The coefficients are determined by boundary conditions. With the output port short-circuited $(V_o = 0)$, the first coefficient gives the short-circuit input impedance. With the input port open-circuited $(I_i = 0)$, the second coefficient gives the open-circuit reverse voltage ratio.

Substituting the standard names:
$$A_1 = h_{ie}, \quad A_2 = h_{re}$$

Therefore, the first h-equation is:
$$V_i = h_{ie} I_i + h_{re} V_o$$

**Step 3 — Output current equation.** By similar logic, the output current depends linearly on both the input current and the output voltage:
$$I_o = B_1 I_i + B_2 V_o$$

With $V_o = 0$, $B_1$ becomes the short-circuit forward current gain ($h_{fe}$). With $I_i = 0$, $B_2$ becomes the open-circuit output admittance ($h_{hoe}$). Therefore:
$$I_o = h_{fe} I_i + h_{hoe} V_o$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the CE Current Gain (Ai) with Load RL

**Step 1 — Apply load condition.** With load resistance $R_L$ connected at the output, the output voltage is $V_o = -I_o R_L$ (the negative sign arises because the current $I_o$ flows *out* of the device into $R_L$).

**Step 2 — Substitute into output current equation.**
$$I_o = h_{fe} I_i + h_{hoe}(-I_o R_L)$$

**Step 3 — Collect Io terms.**
$$I_o + h_{hoe} R_L I_o = h_{fe} I_i$$
$$I_o (1 + h_{oe} R_L) = h_{fe} I_i$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Ai.**
$$A_I = \dfrac{I_o}{I_i} = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$$

**Step 5 — Sign convention in CE configuration.** In a CE amplifier, the output current convention is reversed (the output current is taken as flowing *into* the collector, which is opposite to the two-port convention). The actual current through the load is the negative of $I_o$. Therefore:
$$A_I = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of CE Input Impedance (Zin)

**Step 1 — Substitute $V_o = -I_o R_L$ into the input voltage equation.**
$$V_i = h_{ie} I_i + h_{re} (-I_o R_L)$$

**Step 2 — Replace Io with the expression from the current equation.**
$$I_o = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} I_i$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$V_i = h_{ie} I_i - h_{re} R_L \cdot \dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} I_i$$

**Step 4 — Factor out Ii.**
$$V_i = \left[ h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} \right] I_i$$

**Step 5 — Therefore Zin is the bracketed quantity.**
$$Z_{in} = h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of CE Output Impedance (Zout)

**Step 1 — Definition.** $Z_{out}$ is the impedance seen looking into the output port with the input connected to a source resistance $R_S$ and the load removed.

**Step 2 — Set RL = ∞ and apply V_o, find resulting I_o.** With the input port terminated by $R_S$, the input current is $I_i = -V_i / R_S$ (because the input voltage drives current back through the source).

**Step 3 — Express the input voltage in terms of source and Vi.**
$$V_i = \dfrac{V_{source} R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$$

For the output impedance calculation, we set $V_{source} = 0$, so $I_i = -V_i / R_S$. Substituting $I_i = -V_i/R_S$ into the input voltage equation:
$$V_i = h_{ie} \left(-\dfrac{V_i}{R_S}\right) + h_{re} V_o$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Vi in terms of Vo.**
$$V_i \left(1 + \dfrac{h_{ie}}{R_S}\right) = h_{re} V_o$$
$$V_i = \dfrac{h_{re} R_S}{R_S + h_{ie}} V_o$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the output current equation.**
$$I_o = h_{fe} I_i + h_{oe} V_o = h_{fe} \left(-\dfrac{V_i}{R_S}\right) + h_{oe} V_o$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Vi.**
$$I_o = -\dfrac{h_{fe} h_{re} V_o}{R_S + h_{ie}} + h_{oe} V_o$$
$$I_o = \left[ h_{oe} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe}}{R_S + h_{ie}} \right] V_o$$

**Step 7 — Output impedance.**
$$Z_{out} = \dfrac{V_o}{I_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe}}{R_S + h_{ie}}}$$

## 3.5 Conversion Between CE and CB h-Parameters

The CB h-parameters are obtained by **swapping the roles of input and output ports** in the CE h-equations. Applying the standard two-port transformation rules:

$$h_{ib} = \dfrac{h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}}$$

$$h_{rb} = \dfrac{h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} (1 + h_{fe})}{1 + h_{fe}}$$

$$h_{fb} = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{fe}}$$

$$h_{ob} = \dfrac{h_{oe}}{1 + h_{fe}}$$

**Derivation logic for hfb:** In CB configuration, the input is the emitter current and the output is the collector current. By KCL at the transistor, $I_E = I_B + I_C$. With $I_C = -I_o$ (sign change), and $I_B = I_i / h_{fe}$ (small-signal relation from CE), we have:
$$I_E = \dfrac{I_i}{h_{fe}} - I_o$$

Substituting into the relation $I_E = -(I_i - I_o)$ and solving for $I_o / I_i$ yields $h_{fb} = -h_{fe} / (1 + h_{fe})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Approximate Conversion:** Because $h_{fe} \gg 1$, the CB h-parameters are approximately: $h_{ib} \approx 1/g_m$, $h_{fb} \approx -1$, $h_{ob} \approx h_{oe}$, $h_{rb} \approx 1$. These approximations are what students use in 90% of KTU exam problems.

## 3.6 Conversion to CC h-Parameters

$$h_{ic} \approx h_{ie}$$
$$h_{rc} \approx 1 - h_{re} \approx 1$$
$$h_{fc} = -(1 + h_{fe})$$
$$h_{oc} = h_{oe}$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the h-Parameter Two-Port

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of the h-parameter model: how input and output quantities interact through the four hybrid parameters.

```mermaid
graph LR
    ViIn[Input Voltage Vi] --> SumNode1{Summing Node}
    IiIn[Input Current Ii] --> Mult1[hie]
    Mult1 --> SumNode1
    SumNode1 --> ViOut[Input Port Voltage]
    IiIn --> Mult2[hfe]
    Mult2 --> SumNode2{Summing Node}
    VoIn[Output Voltage Vo] --> Mult3[hoe]
    Mult3 --> SumNode2
    SumNode2 --> IoOut[Output Port Current]
    VoIn --> Mult4[hre]
    Mult4 --> SumNode1
    style Mult1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Mult2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Mult3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Mult4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SumNode1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SumNode2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The `hie` block multiplies $I_i$ and feeds it into the input summing node.
- The `hre` block multiplies $V_o$ and feeds it back into the input summing node (reverse path).
- The `hfe` block multiplies $I_i$ and feeds it into the output summing node.
- The `hoe` block multiplies $V_o$ and feeds it into the output summing node.

## 4.2 Signal Flow: CE Amplifier Using h-Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    Source[Source Vs with Rs] --> Node1[Input Node]
    Node1 --> Hie[hie - Input Impedance]
    Hie --> Node2[Internal Node]
    Node2 --> Hre[hre Vo - Reverse Feedback]
    Hre --> Node1
    Node2 --> Hfe[hfe Ii - Current Source]
    Hfe --> Node3[Output Node]
    Node3 --> Hoe[hoe Vo - Output Admittance]
    Hoe --> Node3
    Node3 --> Load[Load RL]

```
