# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the four h-parameters of a BJT in the CE configuration. State their units.**

**Model Answer:**

The four h-parameters of a BJT in CE configuration are:

1. **hie** — Short-circuit input impedance. Defined as $h_{ie} = \dfrac{V_{be}}{I_b}\bigg\vert_{V_{ce}=0}$. Unit: **Ω (Ohm)**.

2. **hre** — Open-circuit reverse voltage ratio. Defined as $h_{re} = \dfrac{V_{be}}{V_{ce}}\bigg\vert_{I_b=0}$. Unit: **dimensionless**.

3. **hfe** — Short-circuit forward current gain. Defined as $h_{fe} = \dfrac{I_c}{I_b}\bigg\vert_{V_{ce}=0}$. Unit: **dimensionless**.

4. **hoe** — Open-circuit output admittance. Defined as $h_{oe} = \dfrac{I_c}{V_{ce}}\bigg\vert_{I_b=0}$. Unit: **mho (Siemens)**.

The model is called "hybrid" because the four parameters have mixed dimensions.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Why are h-parameters preferred over z-parameters or y-parameters for the small-signal analysis of a BJT?**

**Model Answer:**

h-parameters are preferred because:

1. They are **easier to measure** experimentally: hie and hfe are measured with the output shorted (a convenient low-impedance condition), while hre and hoe are measured with the input open (a convenient high-impedance condition). In contrast, z-parameters need both ports open and y-parameters need both ports shorted, which are difficult to achieve at high frequencies for a BJT.

2. Two of the four parameters (hie and hoe) are **real, easily measurable quantities** that correspond to physically meaningful transistor resistances.

3. The forward current gain hfe is the most useful transistor parameter in amplifier design, and it appears directly as one of the h-parameters.

4. **Numerical values** of h-parameters for a BJT are of convenient magnitude: hie is in kΩ, hfe in hundreds, and hre and hoe are very small, making approximations valid for hand calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) Derive the exact expressions for the input impedance and current gain of a CE amplifier using h-parameters. State the approximate expressions by neglecting hre.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Input Impedance Derivation:**

The h-parameter equations for the CE configuration with load $R_L$ connected at the output are:

$$V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b + h_{re} V_{ce} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$I_c = h_{fe} I_b + h_{oe} V_{ce} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

With $R_L$ connected: $V_{ce} = -I_c R_L$ (negative sign because output current flows out of the device).

Substituting $V_{ce} = -I_c R_L$ into equation (1):

$$V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b - h_{re} I_c R_L \quad \text{...(3)}$$

From equation (2), solving for $I_c$:

$$I_c (1 + h_{oe} R_L) = h_{fe} I_b$$
$$I_c = \dfrac{h_{fe} I_b}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Substituting (4) into (3):

$$V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b - h_{re} R_L \cdot \dfrac{h_{fe} I_b}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$$

$$V_{be} = I_b \left[ h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} \right]$$

Therefore, the exact input impedance is:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{V_{be}}{I_b} = h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Approximate expression:** Neglecting hre (since hre ≈ 0):

$$Z_{in} \approx h_{ie} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Current Gain Derivation:**

The current gain is defined as $A_I = I_{load} / I_b$. In the two-port convention, $I_{load} = -I_c$:

$$A_I = \dfrac{-I_c}{I_b}$$

From equation (4):

$$\boxed{A_I = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}}$$

**Approximate expression:** Neglecting hoe (small value):

$$A_I \approx -h_{fe} \quad \text{...(7)}$$

**(b) For a CE amplifier with hie = 1100 Ω, hre = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴, hfe = 50, hoe = 25 μS, RL = 10 kΩ, and source resistance Rs = 1 kΩ, calculate the exact and approximate values of input impedance, current gain, and voltage gain.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $h_{ie} = 1100 \,\Omega$, $h_{re} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{fe} = 50$, $h_{oe} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{S}$, $R_L = 10{,}000 \,\Omega$, $R_S = 1000 \,\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute current gain Ai (exact):**

$$1 + h_{oe} R_L = 1 + (25 \times 10^{-6})(10^4) = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25$$

$$A_I = \dfrac{-50}{1.25} = -40$$

**Step 2 — Compute input impedance Zin (exact):**

$$Z_{in} = h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$$

$$Z_{in} = 1100 - \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50)(10{,}000)}{1.25}$$

$$Z_{in} = 1100 - \dfrac{125}{1.25} = 1100 - 100 = 1000 \,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute voltage gain Av:**

$$A_V = \dfrac{A_I \cdot R_L}{Z_{in}} = \dfrac{(-40)(10{,}000)}{1000} = -400$$

**Step 4 — Approximate values:**

- $A_I \approx -h_{fe} = -50$
- $Z_{in} \approx h_{ie} = 1100 \,\Omega$
- $A_V \approx \dfrac{(-50)(10{,}000)}{1100} \approx -454.5$

**Comparison Table:**

| Quantity | Exact | Approximate | % Error |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ai | -40 | -50 | 25% |
| Zin | 1000 Ω | 1100 Ω | 10% |
| Av | -400 | -454.5 | 13.6% |

**Conclusion:** The approximate formulas are reasonably accurate for a quick hand calculation but overestimate the gain.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) Draw the exact h-parameter equivalent circuit of a CE amplifier. Using this model, derive the expressions for voltage gain and output impedance.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Equivalent Circuit:**

The exact h-parameter equivalent circuit of a CE amplifier consists of:
- Input port: $h_{ie}$ in series with the input, $h_{re} V_{ce}$ as a controlled voltage source in the input loop.
- Output port: $h_{fe} I_b$ as a controlled current source, with $1/h_{oe}$ in parallel with it.

**Voltage Gain Derivation:**

The voltage gain is $A_V = V_{out} / V_{in}$, where $V_{out} = -I_c R_L$.

From equation (4) of the previous derivation:
$$I_c = \dfrac{h_{fe} I_b}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$$

So: $V_{out} = -I_c R_L = \dfrac{-h_{fe} I_b R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$

From equation (1): $V_{in} = V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b + h_{re} V_{ce}$

Using $V_{ce} = -I_c R_L = \dfrac{-h_{fe} I_b R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$:

$$V_{in} = h_{ie} I_b - h_{re} \cdot \dfrac{h_{fe} I_b R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} = I_b \left[ h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L} \right] = I_b \cdot Z_{in}$$

Therefore:
$$A_V = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie}(1 + h_{oe} R_L) - h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $(1 + h_{oe} R_L)$:

$$\boxed{A_V = \dfrac{-h_{fe} R_L}{Z_{in} (1 + h_{oe} R_L)} = \dfrac{A_I R_L}{Z_{in}}}$$

**Approximate expression:** Neglecting hre and hoe:
$$A_V \approx \dfrac{-h_{fe} R_L}{h_{ie}}$$

**Output Impedance Derivation:**

Output impedance is found by setting $V_S = 0$ (no source signal), keeping $R_S$ in place, and removing $R_L$. Apply a test voltage $V_T$ at the output and find the resulting current $I_T$.

With $V_S = 0$: $I_b = -V_{be} / R_S$

From the input equation:
$$V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b + h_{re} V_T$$
$$V_{be} = -h_{ie} \dfrac{V_{be}}{R_S} + h_{re} V_T$$
$$V_{be} \left(1 + \dfrac{h_{ie}}{R_S}\right) = h_{re} V_T$$
$$V_{be} = \dfrac{h_{re} R_S V_T}{R_S + h_{ie}}$$

The output current is:
$$I_T = -I_c = -[h_{fe} I_b + h_{oe} V_T]$$

With $I_b = -V_{be} / R_S$ and using $V_{be}$ from above:
$$I_T = -h_{fe} \left(-\dfrac{V_{be}}{R_S}\right) - h_{oe} V_T = \dfrac{h_{fe} V_{be}}{R_S} - h_{oe} V_T$$

Substituting $V_{be}$:
$$I_T = \dfrac{h_{fe} h_{re} R_S V_T}{R_S(R_S + h_{ie})} - h_{oe} V_T = \left[ \dfrac{h_{fe} h_{re}}{R_S + h_{ie}} - h_{oe} \right] V_T$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{Z_{out} = \dfrac{V_T}{I_T} = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe}}{h_{ie} + R_S}}}$$

**Approximate expression:** Neglecting hre:
$$Z_{out} \approx \dfrac{1}{h_{oe}}$$

**(b) A CE amplifier has the following h-parameters: hie = 1.1 kΩ, hre = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴, hfe = 50, hoe = 25 μS. The source resistance Rs = 1 kΩ and load resistance RL = 4 kΩ. Calculate the exact and approximate values of Zin, Zout, Ai, and Av.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Exact Input Impedance:**

$$1 + h_{oe} R_L = 1 + (25 \times 10^{-6})(4000) = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1$$

$$Z_{in} = 1100 - \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50)(4000)}{1.1} = 1100 - \dfrac{50}{1.1} = 1100 - 45.45 \approx 1054.5 \,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Exact Current Gain:**

$$A_I = \dfrac{-50}{1.1} = -45.45$$

**Step 3 — Exact Voltage Gain:**

$$A_V = \dfrac{A_I \cdot R_L}{Z_{in}} = \dfrac{(-45.45)(4000)}{1054.5} \approx -172.4$$

**Step 4 — Exact Output Impedance:**

$$Z_{out} = \dfrac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6} - \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50)}{1100 + 1000}}$$

$$Z_{out} = \dfrac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6} - \dfrac{0.0125}{2100}}$$

$$Z_{out} = \dfrac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6} - 5.95 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1}{19.05 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 52{,}500 \,\Omega \approx 52.5 \,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Approximate values:**

| Quantity | Exact | Approximate |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Zin | 1054.5 Ω | 1100 Ω |
| Ai | -45.45 | -50 |
| Av | -172.4 | -181.8 |
| Zout | 52.5 kΩ | 40 kΩ |

**Conclusion:** The approximations are within 10-25% of the exact values and are useful for quick design calculations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in Ai:** Many students forget the negative sign in $A_I = -h_{fe} / (1 + h_{oe} R_L)$ for a CE amplifier. The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion between input and output — a defining property of the CE configuration. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Unit errors:** Always quote `Zin` in Ω, `Zout` in Ω, `hie` in Ω, `hfe` as dimensionless, and `hoe` in Siemens (or mho). Mixing units is a common error. **Loss: 0.5 mark per wrong unit.**
> 3. **Forgetting the approximation assumption:** When using $A_I = -h_{fe}$, the student must explicitly state that `hre` is being neglected because it is very small. Otherwise, the answer is "incomplete." **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Swapping hie and hoe:** `hie` is an impedance (Ω) and `hoe` is an admittance (S). Students often confuse them. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Numerical computation errors:** Always cross-check $1 + h_{oe} R_L$ first. With $h_{oe} = 25 \,\mu\text{S}$ and $R_L = 10 \,\text{k}\Omega$, the product is 0.25, so the denominator is 1.25. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 6. **Forgetting to use the right h-parameter set for the configuration:** A CE problem must use hie/hre/hfe/hoe. A CB problem requires conversion to hib/hrb/hfb/hob first. **Loss: 2 marks.**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **h-parameter model** = hybrid (mixed-dimension) two-port small-signal model of a BJT.
- **Four parameters:** hie (Ω), hre (dimensionless), hfe (dimensionless), hoe (S).
- **Two governing equations:** $V_i = h_{ie} I_i + h_{re} V_o$ and $I_o = h_{fe} I_i + h_{hoe} V_o$.
- **CE configuration** is the most common; the h-parameters are subscripted as hie, hre, hfe, hoe.
- **Exact CE input impedance:** $Z_{in} = h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$.
- **Approximate CE Zin:** $\approx h_{ie}$ (neglect hre).
- **Exact CE current gain:** $A_I = \dfrac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L}$ (note the **negative sign**).
- **Approximate CE Ai:** $\approx -h_{fe}$.
- **Exact CE output impedance:** $Z_{out} = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe} - \dfrac{h_{re} h_{fe}}{h_{ie} + R_S}}$.
- **Approximate CE Zout:** $\approx 1 / h_{oe}$.
- **Voltage gain:** $A_V = A_I R_L / Z_{in}$.
- **CB h-parameters (approximate):** $h_{ib} \approx h_{ie}/(1+h_{fe})$, $h_{fb} \approx -1$, $h_{ob} \approx h_{oe}$, $h_{rb} \approx 1$.
- **CC h-parameters (approximate):** $h_{ic} \approx h_{ie}$, $h_{fc} \approx -(1+h_{fe})$, $h_{oc} \approx h_{oe}$, $h_{rc} \approx 1$.
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = 26 \,\text{mV}$ at room temperature.
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T = I_C / 26\,\text{mV}$.
- **Input impedance formula:** $h_{ie} = \beta V_T / I_C = \beta / g_m$.
- **Typical magnitudes (CE):** hie = 1 kΩ, hfe = 100, hre = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴, hoe = 25 μS.
- **Sign convention:** In CE, the output voltage is 180° out of phase with the input — captured by the negative sign in Ai and Av.
- **Approximation validity:** Neglecting hre gives < 10% error in most practical cases. Neglecting hoe is valid when $h_{oe} R_L \ll 1$.
- **Two-port boundary conditions:** hie and hfe are short-circuit (output) parameters; hre and hoe are open-circuit (input) parameters.
- **Why h-model is preferred over z/y:** Easier measurement, hfe is the most useful transistor parameter, and the parameters are of convenient magnitude.
- **Always re-verify** the configuration (CE/CB/CC) before applying formulas. The h-parameter values change with configuration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Amplifiers, RC Coupled Amplifier - Frequency Response, Analysis for Mid Frequency Gain, Typical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Amplifiers, RC Coupled Amplifier - Frequency Response, Analysis for Mid Frequency Gain, Typical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Amplifiers

An **amplifier** is an electronic circuit that uses a transistor, op-amp, or similar active device to increase the amplitude of an input signal. The power for amplification is drawn from an external DC supply (Vcc); the small input AC signal merely *controls* the transfer of this DC power to the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Amplifiers are classified based on **(a)** the type of coupling between stages, **(b)** the class of operation (A, B, AB, C, D), **(c)** the transistor configuration (CE, CB, CC), and **(d)** the function performed (voltage, current, power).

### Comprehensive Classification Table

| Classification Basis | Categories | Key Distinguishing Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Magnitude** | Small-Signal Amplifier | Operates in linear region; signal $\ll V_T$ |
| | Large-Signal Amplifier | Signal comparable to DC bias; handles power |
| **Configuration** | Common Emitter (CE) | Current + Voltage gain; phase inversion |
| | Common Base (CB) | Voltage gain only; low input impedance |
| | Common Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower | Current gain only; unity voltage gain |
| **Coupling Type** | RC Coupled | Uses capacitors; blocks DC between stages |
| | Transformer Coupled | Impedance matching; bulky, expensive |
| | Direct Coupled (DC Amp) | No coupling cap; amplifies DC; suffers drift |
| **Output Quantity** | Voltage Amplifier | High $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ |
| | Current Amplifier | Low $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$ |
| | Power Amplifier | Delivers maximum power to low $Z_{load}$ |
| **Class of Operation** | Class A | Conduction angle = 360°; Q-point centered |
| | Class B | Conduction angle = 180°; push-pull operation |
| | Class AB | Conduction angle between 180° and 360° |
| | Class C | Conduction angle $\ll$ 180°; high efficiency |

### Real-World Analogy: The Water-Tap Amplifier

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a large water reservoir (the **Vcc supply**) connected to a pipe fitted with a control valve (the **transistor**). A gentle finger-flick on the valve handle (the **small input signal**) releases a powerful jet of water (the **amplified output signal**). The faucet does not "create" water — it merely *controls* the flow from the reservoir. This is exactly what an amplifier does with electrical energy.

---

## 1.2 The RC Coupled Amplifier — Overview

The **RC Coupled Amplifier** is the most popular small-signal audio-frequency amplifier used in practice. It is built by cascading two or more **Common Emitter (CE) stages** using a **resistor–capacitor (RC) network** as the inter-stage coupling element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "RC Coupled"?**
> The coupling network between two consecutive stages consists of a **coupling capacitor $C_c$** in series with the **collector resistor $R_C$** of the previous stage. This RC combination passes AC signals but blocks DC, ensuring each transistor has its **own independent DC bias** while the AC signal flows freely from one stage to the next.

### Block-Level Picture

> A typical two-stage RC coupled amplifier contains:
> - **Stage 1 (CE)** → $C_{c1}$ → **Stage 2 (CE)** → $C_{c2}$ → **Load $R_L$**
> - Each stage has its own bias network ($R_1, R_2, R_E, C_E$).
> - The coupling capacitor $C_c$ isolates the DC bias of one stage from the next.

### Why is RC Coupling So Popular?

> [!TIP]
> 1. **Excellent frequency response** in the audio band (20 Hz – 20 kHz).
> 2. **Low cost** and **compact size** (no bulky transformers).
> 3. **No core-saturation** or hum problems (unlike transformer coupling).
> 4. Each stage can be analyzed and designed **independently** for DC bias.

### Typical Applications

> Real-world uses: **Public-address (PA) systems, radio receivers, tape recorders, pre-amplifiers in TV/audio equipment, and the front-end of any audio amplifier chain.**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier — Circuit Description

A standard two-stage RC coupled amplifier has the following components per stage:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| $R_1, R_2$ | Voltage-divider bias (set Q-point) |
| $R_C$ | Collector load resistor; converts $i_C$ to $v_{out}$ |
| $R_E$ | Emitter resistor; provides thermal stability |
| $C_E$ | Emitter bypass capacitor; shorts $R_E$ at signal frequency |
| $C_c$ | Coupling capacitor; couples AC, blocks DC |
| $V_{CC}$ | DC supply (typically **+12 V** in KTU textbook examples) |

> [!NOTE]
> The output of Stage 1 (taken at the collector of $Q_1$) is fed through $C_{c2}$ to the base of $Q_2$ in Stage 2, which is also a CE amplifier.

---

## 2.2 Frequency Response — The Big Picture

The **frequency response** of an RC coupled amplifier is the plot of **voltage gain magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$** (in dB) versus **signal frequency $f$** (in Hz, on a log scale). It has **three distinct regions**:

| Region | Frequency Range | Governing Elements | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Frequency Region** | $f < f_1$ | Coupling cap $C_c$ and bypass cap $C_E$ | Gain rises at +20 dB/decade |
| **Mid-Frequency Region** | $f_1 < f < f_2$ | All caps ideal ($C_c$ → short, $C_E$ → short, junction caps → open) | Gain is **flat & maximum** |
| **High-Frequency Region** | $f > f_2$ | Junction / Miller capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{ce}$) | Gain falls at −20 dB/decade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept (must memorize):**
> - **Lower cutoff frequency** $f_L$ = frequency at which gain drops to **0.707** of mid-band value (i.e., −3 dB point).
> - **Upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$ = frequency at which gain drops to **0.707** of mid-band value on the high side.
> - **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$ in audio amplifiers).

### Intuitive Explanation of the Three Regions

> [!TIP]
> - **At low $f$:** The reactance $X_{C} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ is **very large**, so coupling/bypass caps behave like **open circuits** → signal cannot pass efficiently → gain falls.
> - **At mid $f$:** $X_C$ is **negligibly small** (caps = short), AND junction capacitances are still **negligibly large** (open). The amplifier is "ideal" → gain is flat and maximum.
> - **At high $f$:** $X_C$ of junction capacitances becomes **small** → they **shunt** the signal to ground → gain falls.

---

## 2.3 Mid-Frequency Equivalent Circuit (Small-Signal h-Model)

At mid-band, replace:
- $C_c$ → **short circuit** (negligible $X_C$)
- $C_E$ → **short circuit** (fully bypasses $R_E$)
- Junction capacitances → **open circuit**

The mid-frequency small-signal equivalent circuit of a single CE stage becomes:

> **Input loop:** $V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie}$
> **Output loop:** $I_c = h_{fe} \cdot I_b$ and $V_{out} = -I_c \cdot R_{C,ac}$
> where $R_{C,ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$

### What if $R_E$ is *not* fully bypassed?

When only a part $R_{E1}$ is bypassed and $R_{E2}$ remains in circuit:

> $A_v = \dfrac{-h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot R_{E2}}$

When $R_E$ is **fully bypassed** ($R_{E2} = 0$):

> $A_v = \dfrac{-h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas — these appear in nearly every KTU 2024 Module-2 question.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE, full bypass) | $A_{v(mid)} = -\dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}$ | Negative sign = 180° phase inversion |
| 2 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE, partial bypass) | $A_v = -\dfrac{h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_{E2}}$ | $R_{E2}$ = unbypassed part |
| 3 | AC Load Resistance | $R_{C,ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L}$ | Used for AC analysis |
| 4 | Input Impedance (one stage) | $Z_{in} = h_{ie} \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$ | Always $\approx h_{ie}$ for typical values |
| 5 | Output Impedance | $Z_{out} = R_C$ | Independent of $h_{fe}$ |
| 6 | Current Gain | $A_i = -A_v \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{R_L}$ | Useful for multi-stage calculations |
| 7 | Power Gain | $A_P = A_v \cdot A_i$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | Overall Gain (two stages, cascaded) | $A_{v(total)} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ | Valid because stages are isolated by $C_c$ |
| 9 | Gain in dB | $A_v(dB) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Standard dB definition |
| 10 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Audio amp: typically 20 Hz – 20 kHz |
| 11 | Lower Cutoff (approx.) | $f_L \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{eq}) C_{eq}}$ | Depends on $C_c$ and $C_E$ |
| 12 | Upper Cutoff (approx.) | $f_H \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} \parallel R_{in(next)}) C_{Miller}}$ | Depends on Miller capacitance |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> The mid-band gain formula is the **workhorse** of audio amplifier design. Once a designer chooses a transistor (giving $h_{ie}$ and $h_{fe}$) and sets the Q-point, the collector resistor $R_C$ is selected to achieve the desired $A_v$. This is the **first calculation** in any audio pre-amplifier design.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Frequency Voltage Gain (CE, Fully Bypassed $R_E$)

We begin with the **exact small-signal h-parameter equivalent circuit** of a single CE stage with $R_E$ fully bypassed by $C_E$.

**Given:**
- Input: $V_i$ (small-signal source)
- $R_S$ = source resistance
- $R_1, R_2$ = bias resistors
- $h_{ie}$ = input resistance of transistor
- $h_{fe}$ = forward current gain
- $R_C$ = collector resistor
- $R_L$ = load resistor (coupled via $C_{c2}$)
- $C_c$ and $C_E$ are shorts at mid-band.

**Step 1 — Identify the AC load seen by the collector.**

At the output node, both $R_C$ and $R_L$ are effectively in parallel (since $C_{c2}$ is a short at mid-band and the load is connected to AC ground via the next stage).

$$
R_{C,ac} = R_C \parallel R_L
$$

$$
R_{C,ac} = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L}
$$

**Why this is so:** The coupling capacitor $C_{c2}$ passes the AC signal current $i_c$ into both $R_C$ (to Vcc, which is AC ground) and $R_L$ (to ground), placing them in parallel from the AC viewpoint.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL on the input (base–emitter) loop.**

Since $C_E$ is a short at mid-band, the emitter is at AC ground. The full input voltage $V_i$ appears across $h_{ie}$ (assuming the source has zero internal resistance; if $R_S$ is present, $V_i$ is the Thévenin voltage at the base).

$$
V_i = I_b \cdot h_{ie}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the current-control relationship of the h-model.**

The collector current (controlled source) is:

$$
I_c = h_{fe} \cdot I_b
$$

**Step 4 — Apply KVL on the output (collector) loop.**

The controlled current $I_c$ flows through $R_{C,ac}$, producing an output voltage. The negative sign arises because increasing $I_c$ causes the collector voltage to *decrease* (180° phase inversion).

$$
V_o = -I_c \cdot R_{C,ac}
$$

$$
V_o = -h_{fe} \cdot I_b \cdot R_{C,ac}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio $V_o / V_i$ to get the voltage gain.**

$$
A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{-h_{fe} \cdot I_b \cdot R_{C,ac}}{I_b \cdot h_{ie}}
$$

Cancel $I_b$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
A_v = \frac{-h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_{v(mid)} = -\frac{h_{fe} \, R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative sign** indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output — a defining property of the Common Emitter amplifier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mid-Frequency Voltage Gain (CE, Partially Bypassed $R_E$)

When the bypass capacitor $C_E$ shorts only a portion $R_{E1}$ and leaves $R_{E2}$ in the emitter leg:

**Step 1 — Modified input equation with emitter degeneration.**

The voltage across the input loop now includes the drop across $R_{E2}$:

$$
V_i = I_b \cdot h_{ie} + I_e \cdot R_{E2}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation $I_e = (1 + h_{fe}) I_b$** (since $I_e = I_b + I_c = I_b + h_{fe} I_b$).

$$
V_i = I_b \cdot h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) I_b \cdot R_{E2}
$$

$$
V_i = I_b \left[ h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_{E2} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Output remains $V_o = -h_{fe} I_b R_{C,ac}$.**

**Step 4 — Form the gain ratio.**

$$
A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{-h_{fe} I_b R_{C,ac}}{I_b \left[ h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_{E2} \right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_v = -\frac{h_{fe} \, R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_{E2}}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** The unbypassed resistor $R_{E2}$ reduces the gain by the factor $1 + (1+h_{fe})R_{E2}/h_{ie}$, but improves linearity and stability. Designers use this **gain-vs-stability trade-off** constantly.

---

## 3.3 Overall Gain of a Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier

Since the coupling capacitor isolates the DC bias but transmits the AC signal without attenuation at mid-band, the **overall voltage gain is simply the product** of the individual stage gains:

$$
A_{v(total)} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2}
$$

For two identical stages:

$$
A_{v(total)} = \left( -\frac{h_{fe} R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}} \right)^2
$$

In decibels:

$$
A_{v(total)}(\text{dB}) = A_{v1}(\text{dB}) + A_{v2}(\text{dB}) = 2 \times 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{h_{fe} R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}} \right)
$$

---

## 3.4 Input and Output Impedance of a CE Stage (Mid-Band)

### Input Impedance

The impedance looking into the **base** of the transistor is $h_{ie}$. The bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ appear in parallel with this from the signal source's perspective.

$$
Z_{in} = h_{ie} \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2
$$

$$
Z_{in} = \left[ \frac{1}{h_{ie}} + \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right]^{-1}
$$

> In practice, $h_{ie} \ll R_1 \parallel R_2$ for typical bias values, so $Z_{in} \approx h_{ie}$.

### Output Impedance

The dependent current source in the h-model has **infinite output impedance** (ideal), so the output impedance is set entirely by the collector resistor:

$$
\boxed{\,Z_{out} = R_C\,}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Example (Typical KTU 2024 Style Problem)

> **[KTU 2024 Style — Solved Example 1]**
> A two-stage RC coupled amplifier uses identical transistors with $h_{fe} = 100$, $h_{ie} = 1.1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$. The emitter resistor is fully bypassed. Calculate:
> (a) The mid-band voltage gain of each stage.
> (b) The overall voltage gain.
> (c) The input impedance of each stage.
> (d) The output impedance.
> (e) Express the overall gain in dB.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — AC load resistance per stage:**

$$
R_{C,ac} = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L} = \frac{4.7 \text{k} \times 4.7 \text{k}}{4.7 \text{k} + 4.7 \text{k}} = \frac{22.09}{9.4} = 2.35 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step (b) — Voltage gain of one stage:**

$$
A_{v1} = -\frac{h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}} = -\frac{100 \times 2.35 \text{ k}\Omega}{1.1 \text{ k}\Omega} = -\frac{235}{1.1} = -213.64
$$

**Step (c) — Overall gain (two identical stages):**

$$
A_{v(total)} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} = (-213.64) \times (-213.64) = 45{,}642
$$

**Step (d) — Input impedance per stage:**

$$
Z_{in} = h_{ie} \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 = 1100 \parallel 10000 \parallel 5000
$$

$$
R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{10000 \times 5000}{10000 + 5000} = \frac{50{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000} = 3333.33 \text{ }\Omega
$$

$$
Z_{in} = \frac{1100 \times 3333.33}{1100 + 3333.33} = \frac{3{,}666{,}667}{4433.33} \approx 827 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step (e) — Output impedance:**

$$
Z_{out} = R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step (f) — Gain in dB:**

$$
A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_{v(total)} \vert = 20 \log_{10}(45642) = 20 \times 4.6593 = 93.19 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Final Answers:** $A_{v1} \approx -213.6$, $A_{v(total)} \approx 45{,}642$, $Z_{in} \approx 827 \text{ }\Omega$, $Z_{out} = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $A_v(\text{dB}) \approx 93.2 \text{ dB}$.

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Example — Partially Bypassed Emitter

> **[KTU 2024 Style — Solved Example 2]**
> A CE stage has $h_{fe} = 120$, $h_{ie} = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and the emitter circuit has $R_{E1} = 100 \text{ }\Omega$ (bypassed) in series with $R_{E2} = 200 \text{ }\Omega$ (unbypassed). Find the mid-band voltage gain.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — AC load:**

$$
R_{C,ac} = \frac{5000 \times 5000}{5000 + 5000} = 2500 \text{ }\Omega = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the partial-bypass gain formula:**

$$
A_v = -\frac{h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_{E2}}
$$

$$
A_v = -\frac{120 \times 2500}{1500 + (1 + 120) \times 200} = -\frac{300{,}000}{1500 + 121 \times 200}
$$

$$
A_v = -\frac{300{,}000}{1500 + 24{,}200} = -\frac{300{,}000}{25{,}700} \approx -11.67
$$

> **Gain comparison:** With $R_E$ fully bypassed, the gain would have been $-\frac{120 \times 2500}{1500} = -200$. The 200 Ω unbypassed part thus reduces the gain by a factor of $\approx 17$, demonstrating the strong gain-stabilizing effect of emitter degeneration.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram of a Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAGE1["STAGE 1 - COMMON EMITTER"]
        IN1["V_in (AC Source)"] -->|coupled| N1A["Base of Q1"]
        N1A --> N1B["Emitter of Q1"]
        Q1A["Q1 Transistor"] --- N1A
        Q1A --- N1C["Collector of Q1"]
        N1C -->|R_C1| VCC1["V_CC (DC Supply)"]
        N1B -->|R_E1| GND1A["GND"]
        C_E1["C_E1 bypasses R_E1"] --- N1B
        C_E1 --- GND1A
        R1A["R1 bias"] --- N1A
        R2A["R2 bias"] --- N1A
        R2A --- GND1B["GND"]
        R1A --- VCC1
    end

    COUPLING["C_c2 (Coupling Capacitor)"]

    subgraph STAGE2["STAGE 2 - COMMON EMITTER"]
        N2A["Base of Q2"]
        N2B["Emitter of Q2"]
        Q2A["Q2 Transistor"] --- N2A
        Q2A --- N2C["Collector of Q2"]
        N2C -->|R_C2| VCC2["V_CC"]
        N2B -->|R_E2| GND2A["GND"]
        C_E2["C_E2 bypasses R_E2"] --- N2B
        C_E2 --- GND2A
        R1B["R3 bias"] --- N2A
        R2B["R4 bias"] --- N2A
        R2B --- GND2B["GND"]
        R1B --- VCC2
    end

    LOAD["R_L (Load)"]
    OUT["V_out"]

    N1C --> COUPLING
    COUPLING --> N2A
    N2C --> LOAD
    LOAD --> OUT
    OUT --> GND2C["GND"]
```

## 4.2 Mid-Frequency Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["V_s, R_s"] -->|input loop| BNODE["Base Node"]
    BNODE -->|h_ie| ENODE["Emitter (AC GND)"]
    BNODE -->|h_fe I_b| DEP["Dependent Current Source h_fe I_b"]
    DEP --> CNODE["Collector Node"]
    CNODE -->|R_C parallel R_L| GNDLOAD["GND"]
    CNODE -->|V_out| OUTPUT["V_out Terminal"]
    BNODE ---|R1 parallel R2| GNDB["GND"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow on the dependent current source flows from collector to emitter (internally), producing a voltage drop across $R_C \parallel R_L$. The negative sign in $A_v$ reflects that when $I_b$ increases, $V_{out}$ decreases.

## 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristic (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AXIS["Frequency Axis (log scale): f_L → f_H"]
    AXIS --> LOW["LOW-FREQUENCY REGION: Gain rises at +20 dB/decade. Dominated by C_c and C_E"]
    AXIS --> MID["MID-FREQUENCY REGION: Gain is FLAT and MAXIMUM. C_c and C_E are short circuits; junction caps are open"]
    AXIS --> HIGH["HIGH-FREQUENCY REGION: Gain falls at -20 dB/decade. Dominated by junction / Miller capacitances C_bc and C_ce"]

    LOW -.->|at f_L| MID
    MID -.->|at f_H| HIGH
```

> **Key takeaway for KTU 2024:** The mid-band region is the *useful* operating range of an RC coupled amplifier. The bandwidth is bounded by $f_L$ and $f_H$ at the **−3 dB** points.

## 4.4 Classification Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AMP["AMPLIFIERS"] --> SIG["By Signal Size"]
    AMP --> CONF["By Configuration"]
    AMP --> CPL["By Coupling"]
    AMP --> OUT["By Output Type"]
    AMP --> CLS["By Class of Operation"]

    SIG --> SIG1["Small-Signal"]
    SIG --> SIG2["Large-Signal (Power)"]

    CONF --> CONF1["CE - Voltage + Current Gain"]
    CONF --> CONF2["CB - Voltage Gain only"]
    CONF --> CONF3["CC - Current Gain only"]

    CPL --> CPL1["RC Coupled"]
    CPL --> CPL2["Transformer Coupled"]
    CPL --> CPL3["Direct Coupled"]

    OUT --> OUT1["Voltage Amplifier"]
    OUT --> OUT2["Current Amplifier"]
    OUT --> OUT3["Power Amplifier"]

    CLS --> CLS1["Class A - 360 degree"]
    CLS --> CLS2["Class B - 180 degree"]
    CLS --> CLS3["Class AB - 180 to 360"]
    CLS --> CLS4["Class C - less than 180"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Model Paper Style]**
> **Classify amplifiers based on (i) coupling, (ii) configuration, and (iii) class of operation. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Coupling:** RC coupled, transformer coupled, direct coupled. *Application:* RC coupled — audio pre-amplifiers; Transformer coupled — RF power amplifiers; Direct coupled — operational amplifiers / DC amplifiers.
>
> **(ii) Configuration:** Common Emitter (CE), Common Base (CB), Common Collector (CC). *Application:* CE — general-purpose voltage amplifier; CB — high-frequency RF amplifier; CC — buffer / impedance-matching stage.
>
> **(iii) Class of Operation:** Class A, Class B, Class AB, Class C. *Application:* Class A — high-fidelity audio; Class B — push-pull power output; Class AB — audio power output; Class C — tuned RF transmitters.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Model Paper Style]**
> **What is meant by mid-frequency region of an RC coupled amplifier? Why is the gain maximum and flat in this region?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **mid-frequency region** is the band of frequencies lying between the lower cutoff $f_L$ and the upper cutoff $f_H$ where the voltage gain of the amplifier is essentially constant and equal to its maximum value.
>
> In this region:
> - The **reactances of coupling capacitor $C_c$ and bypass capacitor $C_E$ are negligibly small** (they act as short circuits).
> - The **reactances of junction capacitances** ($C_{bc}$, $C_{ce}$) are very large (they act as open circuits).
> - Hence no capacitive element causes signal attenuation, and the gain depends only on the resistive h-parameters and resistors, yielding a **flat, maximum gain**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **two-stage RC coupled amplifier**. Draw its **frequency response** and identify the three regions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **mid-band voltage gain** of a single CE stage with the emitter resistor **fully bypassed**. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Operation:**

A two-stage RC coupled amplifier consists of two cascaded CE stages coupled by a capacitor $C_{c2}$. Each stage has independent biasing ($R_1, R_2, R_E, C_E$) and its own collector load $R_C$.

**Working Principle:**

1. The **small AC input signal** $V_{in}$ is applied to the base of $Q_1$ through coupling capacitor $C_{c1}$.
2. Amplified and inverted output appears at the **collector of $Q_1$**.
3. This is passed through $C_{c2}$ (blocks DC) to the **base of $Q_2$**.
4. $Q_2$ amplifies it further (and inverts again — overall two inversions = in-phase with input).
5. Final output $V_{out}$ is taken at the collector of $Q_2$ across $R_L$ via $C_{c3}$.

**Frequency Response:** Three regions as described earlier:

> - **Low-frequency region ($f < f_L$):** Gain rises at **+20 dB/decade** because $C_c$ and $C_E$ introduce attenuation that decreases as $f$ increases.
> - **Mid-frequency region ($f_L \le f \le f_H$):** Gain is **flat and maximum** ($A_{v(\text{mid})}$); all coupling/bypass caps are effective shorts, junction caps are open.
> - **High-frequency region ($f > f_H$):** Gain falls at **−20 dB/decade** because junction (Miller) capacitances shunt the signal to ground.

**Bandwidth** = $f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$).

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions for mid-band analysis:**

> 1. All coupling and bypass capacitors → **short circuits** (negligible $X_C$).
> 2. All internal junction capacitances → **open circuits** (very high $X_C$).
> 3. Transistor is replaced by its **simplified small-signal h-parameter model**.
> 4. Source resistance $R_S = 0$ (ideal source).
> 5. The emitter resistor is **fully bypassed** by $C_E$.

**Derivation** — Already covered in Section 3.1. Final expression:

$$
A_{v(\text{mid})} = -\frac{h_{fe} \, R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}, \quad \text{where} \quad R_{C,ac} = R_C \parallel R_L
$$

> **Physical meaning of the negative sign:** The CE stage introduces a **180° phase reversal** between input and output — a fundamental property of the common-emitter topology.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **mid-band voltage gain of a CE stage with the emitter resistor partially bypassed** (only $R_{E2}$ unbypassed, $R_{E1}$ bypassed). Compare it with the fully bypassed case. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a two-stage RC coupled amplifier with identical stages ($h_{fe} = 100$, $h_{ie} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, fully bypassed $R_E$), calculate: (i) per-stage gain, (ii) overall gain, (iii) gain in dB, (iv) input and output impedances per stage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation** — Already covered in Section 3.2:

$$
A_v = -\frac{h_{fe} \, R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1 + h_{fe}) R_{E2}}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Fully Bypassed | Partially Bypassed |
|---|---|---|
| Gain Magnitude | $\dfrac{h_{fe} R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}$ | $\dfrac{h_{fe} R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})R_{E2}}$ |
| Input Impedance | $h_{ie}$ | $h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})R_{E2}$ |
| Bandwidth | Smaller | Larger (better high-frequency response) |
| Linearity | Lower | Higher (negative feedback via $R_{E2}$) |
| Q-point Stability | Lower | Higher |

**Key Insight:** Unbypassed $R_{E2}$ introduces **series–series negative feedback** (current sampling, voltage mixing), reducing gain but improving linearity, stability, and bandwidth.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $h_{fe} = 100$, $h_{ie} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**(i) Per-stage AC load resistance:**

$$
R_{C,ac} = \frac{2000 \times 2000}{2000 + 2000} = 1000 \text{ }\Omega = 1 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**(ii) Per-stage voltage gain:**

$$
A_{v1} = -\frac{h_{fe} \cdot R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}} = -\frac{100 \times 1000}{1000} = -100
$$

**(iii) Overall gain (two stages):**

$$
A_{v(total)} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} = (-100) \times (-100) = +10{,}000
$$

> **Note:** The overall gain is **positive** because two inversions cancel out (two CE stages).

**(iv) Gain in dB:**

$$
A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert 10{,}000 \vert = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

**(v) Input Impedance per stage:**

$$
Z_{in} = h_{ie} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega \quad (\text{assuming } R_1, R_2 \gg h_{ie})
$$

**(vi) Output Impedance per stage:**

$$
Z_{out} = R_C = 2 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

> **Final Answer Summary:** $A_{v1} = -100$, $A_{v(total)} = +10{,}000$, $A_v(\text{dB}) = 80 \text{ dB}$, $Z_{in} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $Z_{out} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $A_v$ — The 180° phase shift is a defining property of CE; you WILL lose a mark for omitting it.
> 2. **Using $R_C$ instead of $R_C \parallel R_L$** in the gain formula — Always compute the **AC load** $R_{C,ac}$; using DC load $R_C$ alone gives a higher but **incorrect** gain.
> 3. **Forgetting that the overall gain of two CE stages is positive** (the two inversions cancel). Writing a negative overall gain is a common slip.
> 4. **Mixing up the cutoff frequencies** — $f_L$ is set by coupling/bypass caps (low side), $f_H$ by junction/Miller caps (high side). Writing them backwards costs marks.
> 5. **Unit errors** — $h_{ie}$ is in ohms (often kΩ), $R_C$ in ohms. Keep units consistent or convert to a single unit before substituting.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert gain to dB** when the question specifies it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **RC Coupled Amplifier** = cascaded CE stages linked by coupling capacitor $C_c$. It is the **most popular** audio-frequency amplifier.
- The coupling capacitor **blocks DC** between stages, allowing each transistor to have **independent bias**, while passing AC signals freely.
- **Three regions of frequency response:**
  - **Low-frequency region:** $C_c$ and $C_E$ dominate; gain falls at +20 dB/decade as $f$ decreases.
  - **Mid-frequency region:** All capacitors behave ideally ($C_c$ and $C_E$ short; junction caps open); gain is **flat and maximum**.
  - **High-frequency region:** Junction / Miller capacitance $C_{bc}$ dominates; gain falls at −20 dB/decade as $f$ increases.
- **Lower cutoff $f_L$** is determined by $C_c$ and $C_E$; **upper cutoff $f_H$** is determined by $C_{bc}$ (Miller effect).
- **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for audio amplifiers.
- **Mid-band voltage gain (CE, fully bypassed):**
  $$A_{v(\text{mid})} = -\frac{h_{fe} R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie}}, \quad R_{C,ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$$
- **Mid-band voltage gain (CE, partially bypassed):**
  $$A_v = -\frac{h_{fe} R_{C,ac}}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_{E2}}$$
- **Negative sign** = 180° phase inversion in CE.
- **Input impedance** $Z_{in} = h_{ie} \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \approx h_{ie}$.
- **Output impedance** $Z_{out} = R_C$.
- **Overall gain of cascaded stages** is the **product** of individual gains: $A_{v(total)} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot \ldots$
- For **two CE stages**, overall gain is **positive** (inversions cancel).
- **dB conversion:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
- **Classification memory trick:** "SCCD-COP" → **S**ignal size, **C**onfiguration, **C**oupling, **D**irect-coupled variant, **C**lass of operation, **O**utput type, **P**ower.
- **Class of operation summary:**
  - Class A: 360° conduction, Q at center, high linearity, low efficiency (~25%).
  - Class B: 180° conduction, push-pull, efficiency ~78.5%.
  - Class AB: 180°–360°, eliminates crossover distortion.
  - Class C: <180°, high efficiency, used in tuned RF.
- **RC coupling vs transformer coupling:** RC is cheaper, smaller, and gives a flatter frequency response — hence preferred for audio.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifiers: Definitions and Classification, Typical Circuits of Class A, Class B, Class AB and Class C Amplifiers, Analysis for Conversion Efficiency of Class B and AB, Simple Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Amplifiers: Definitions and Classification, Typical Circuits of Class A, Class B, Class AB and Class C Amplifiers, Analysis for Conversion Efficiency of Class B and AB, Simple Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Power Amplifier?

A **Power Amplifier (PA)** is a large-signal electronic amplifier designed to deliver substantial amounts of electrical power to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, or motor) with high efficiency. Unlike voltage amplifiers whose primary goal is to boost signal magnitude (voltage gain), a power amplifier's primary objective is to deliver maximum **AC power** to the load while converting the **DC power** drawn from the supply into useful signal power with minimum waste (heat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> A power amplifier is an amplifier that operates in the large-signal regime, where the output voltage and current swings are a significant fraction of the DC bias values. The figure of merit is **Conversion Efficiency (η)**, defined as:
> $$\eta = \frac{P_{AC\,(load)}}{P_{DC\,(supply)}} \times 100\%$$

### 1.2 Classification of Power Amplifiers

Power amplifiers are classified based on the **conduction angle (θ)** of the output transistor — that is, the portion of the input signal cycle (out of 360°) during which the transistor conducts current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — Conduction Angle (θ):**
> The angular measure (in degrees) of the input cycle for which the transistor is forward-biased and conducting. A Class A amplifier conducts for the **full 360°**, while a Class B amplifier conducts for only **180°**.

| Class | Conduction Angle (θ) | Operating Point Location | Typical Max. Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class A** | 360° | Center of load line (mid-point bias) | 25% (series-fed), 50% (transformer) |
| **Class B** | 180° | Exactly at cutoff (cutoff bias) | 78.5% (π/4) |
| **Class AB** | 180° < θ < 360° | Slightly above cutoff | 50% – 78.5% |
| **Class C** | θ < 180° | Well below cutoff | > 78.5% (theoretical 100%) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Hose & Pump:**
> Think of a **voltage amplifier** as a *small faucet* that finely controls the *pressure* (voltage) of a tiny stream of water — efficiency does not matter much because the volume is tiny. A **power amplifier** is a *high-pressure water pump*: its job is to deliver a *massive flow* (current) to a heavy load (the sprinkler). Here, *efficiency* matters enormously because a wasteful pump will burn out its motor. The **classification** (A, B, AB, C) is analogous to how the pump's valve is operated:
> - **Class A:** The valve is held *half-open continuously* — smooth but wastes a lot of water (DC power).
> - **Class B:** The valve is opened *only when pushing* (one half-cycle) — efficient but choppy.
> - **Class AB:** The valve is held *slightly open* between pushes — a smart balance of smoothness and efficiency.
> - **Class C:** The valve snaps open for *less than half* the cycle — highly efficient but produces pulses, not a smooth stream (requires a tank/resonator to smooth it out — hence used in RF/tuned circuits).

### 1.4 Why a Special Class of Amplifier?

In a small-signal (voltage) amplifier, the transistor operates in a tiny region around its Q-point, and the **AC power** handled is negligible compared to the **DC bias power**. The conversion efficiency can therefore be ignored. In a power amplifier, however, the output signal swing is large, and the AC power delivered to the load is comparable to the DC power drawn from the supply $V_{CC}$. Therefore, **efficiency becomes the central design parameter**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse *Power Amplifier* with *high-gain amplifier*. The two are fundamentally different. A power amplifier may have a *voltage gain of only 5–10*, but it can deliver *amperes* of current. Conversely, an op-amp may have a gain of 100,000 but cannot drive a loudspeaker directly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Class A Power Amplifier

**Operating Principle:** The transistor is biased at the **midpoint of the load line**, so it conducts for the *entire 360°* of the input cycle. The output is a faithful, undistorted reproduction of the input.

#### 2.1.1 Series-Fed Class A Amplifier (Direct-Coupled Load)

In this configuration, the load $R_L$ is connected directly in the collector circuit (no transformer). The operating point is set so that $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ and $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/(2R_L)$.

**DC Power Input (from supply):**

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

Because the DC collector current is *constant* (independent of signal), the DC power is also *constant* — the supply delivers full power even when there is **no input signal**.

**AC Power Delivered to Load:**

If the input signal drives the transistor between cutoff and saturation symmetrically, the peak AC output voltage is $V_m = V_{CC}/2$ and peak current is $I_m = V_{CC}/(2R_L)$.

$$P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L}$$

**Power Dissipated by the Transistor:**

$$P_{D\,(max)} = P_{DC} - P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{4 R_L} - \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L}$$

**Conversion Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 / 8 R_L}{V_{CC}^2 / 4 R_L} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

But wait — this is for the *AC* $P_{DC}$ which assumes $I_{CQ}$ has been halved. Actually, the correct KTU textbook derivation gives:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 25\% \text{ (Series-Fed Class A)}}$$

The discrepancy arises because the *true* DC supply current is $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/(2R_L)$, making $P_{DC} = V_{CC}^2/(2R_L)$ and the ratio becomes exactly **1/4**.

#### 2.1.2 Transformer-Coupled Class A Amplifier

A **step-down transformer** is used to couple the load $R_L$ to the collector. The transformer's primary DC resistance is nearly zero, so **no DC power is wasted in the load**. The effective AC load reflected to the primary is $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, where $n$ is the turns ratio.

**Key advantage:** The DC drop across the load is zero, so the transistor can swing from near 0 V to nearly $2V_{CC}$ across its collector-emitter terminals.

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\% \text{ (Transformer-Coupled Class A)}}$$

### 2.2 Class B Power Amplifier

**Operating Principle:** The transistor is biased exactly at the **cutoff point** ($V_{BE} = 0$ V, $I_{CQ} = 0$). Each transistor conducts for **exactly 180°** (half a cycle). Therefore, a **push-pull configuration** using *two complementary transistors* (NPN + PNP) is required to reproduce the full waveform.

- During the **positive half-cycle**, the NPN transistor conducts.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, the PNP transistor conducts.
- The two halves are combined at the load to reconstruct the full sine wave.

**Distortion Artifact — Crossover Distortion:** Because each transistor needs $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ to start conducting, the output is zero for a small region near the zero-crossing. This produces **crossover distortion**, which is the central flaw of Class B and the primary reason for the invention of Class AB.

### 2.3 Class AB Power Amplifier

**Operating Principle:** The transistor is biased at a *small quiescent current* $I_{CQ}$ — just enough to keep it in the active region when the input crosses zero. This eliminates crossover distortion while retaining most of the efficiency of Class B.

- **Conduction angle:** $180° < \theta < 360°$
- **Quiescent current:** Small but nonzero.
- **Efficiency:** Lies between Class A (50%) and Class B (78.5%).

### 2.4 Class C Power Amplifier

**Operating Principle:** The transistor is biased **well below cutoff**, so it conducts for **less than 180°** of the input cycle — typically only 60° to 90°. The output is a series of narrow current pulses, which is highly distorted.

**Application:** Because the output is pulsed (not sinusoidal), Class C amplifiers are used only with a **tuned (resonant) load** (LC tank circuit) that filters the pulses into a sinusoidal waveform. They are extensively used in **RF transmitters, radio-frequency power amplifiers, and IF stages**.

- **Efficiency:** Can theoretically approach **100%**, with practical values of **80% – 90%**.
- **Distortion:** Very high (not usable for audio without filtering).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU problem on this topic uses one of these formulas.**

| Parameter | Class A (Series-Fed) | Class A (Transformer) | Class B (Push-Pull) | Class AB | Class C (Tuned) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conduction Angle** | 360° | 360° | 180° | 180°–360° | < 180° |
| **$P_{DC}$** | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$ | $V_{CC} I_{DC}$ |
| **$P_{AC\,(load)}$** | $\dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L'}$ | $\dfrac{V_m I_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m I_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m I_m}{2}$ |
| **$P_{D\,(transistor)}$** | $P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ | $P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ | $P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ | $P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ | $P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ |
| **$\eta_{max}$** | **25%** | **50%** | **78.5% ($\pi/4$)** | **50% – 78.5%** | **≈ 100%** |
| **Distortion** | Low | Low | Crossover distortion | Very Low | Very High |
| **Application** | Hi-Fi audio pre-driver | Audio output | Audio output | Audio power amp | RF transmitters |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

Power amplifiers are at the heart of **every audio system, wireless transmitter, sonar, motor controller, and RF communication system**. The push-pull Class B and Class AB topologies (e.g., the LM386, TDA2050, TPA3116) dominate consumer audio. Class C is the workhorse of AM/FM broadcast transmitters. Modern Class D (switching) amplifiers achieve > 90% efficiency and are used in mobile phones and electric vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency of Class A (Series-Fed) Amplifier

**Given Circuit:** A common-emitter amplifier with load $R_L$ directly in the collector, biased at the midpoint of the load line so that $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the DC operating point.**
The collector current at quiescence (no signal) is set by the bias network:

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{R_L} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CC}/2}{R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the DC power drawn from the supply.**
The supply voltage is $V_{CC}$ and the current drawn is $I_{CQ}$ (constant, independent of signal):

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the maximum AC output voltage and current.**
The transistor can swing symmetrically about the Q-point. The peak output voltage (at maximum signal) is:

$$V_m = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

Similarly, the peak AC current is:

$$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum AC power delivered to the load.**
For a sinusoidal signal, the average power delivered to a resistive load is:

$$P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(V_{CC}/2)^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 \,/\, 8 R_L}{V_{CC}^2 \,/\, 2 R_L} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 6 — Express as a percentage.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 25\% \text{ for Series-Fed Class A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Notice that even at *zero input signal*, the supply delivers $P_{DC} = V_{CC}^2 / (2R_L)$, but the load receives *no* AC power. All of this DC power is dissipated as heat in the transistor. This is why a Class A amplifier is said to be "always on" thermally.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier

**Given Circuit:** A complementary-symmetry push-pull amplifier with an NPN transistor $Q_1$ and a PNP transistor $Q_2$. Each transistor conducts for exactly 180°.

**Step 1 — Set up the output waveforms.**
Assume the load current is sinusoidal:

$$i_L(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

During the positive half-cycle, $Q_1$ (NPN) supplies current to the load, and $Q_2$ (PNP) is off. During the negative half-cycle, $Q_2$ supplies current and $Q_1$ is off.

**Step 2 — Compute the average DC current drawn from the supply.**
Each transistor draws current for *only half* the cycle. The average current from the $V_{CC}$ supply is the average of the half-sine waveform:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \big[-\cos(\theta)\big]_{0}^{\pi}$$

$$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \cdot (1 - (-1)) = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{I_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total DC power from the supply.**

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In Class B, the DC power is *not* constant — it varies with the signal amplitude $I_m$. When there is no input, $I_m = 0$ and $P_{DC} = 0$. This is a huge advantage over Class A.

**Step 4 — Compute the AC power delivered to the load.**
The load sees the full sinusoidal current $i_L(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ (because the two halves are recombined by the push-pull action). The RMS current is $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$. With load resistance $R_L$:

$$P_{AC} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{I_m^2}{2} R_L$$

Alternatively, if $V_m = I_m R_L$ is the peak output voltage:

$$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_m I_m \,/\, 2}{V_{CC} I_m \,/\, \pi} = \frac{\pi V_m}{2 V_{CC}}$$

**Step 6 — Maximize by setting $V_m = V_{CC}$.**
In the ideal case (no saturation loss, no $V_{CE(sat)}$ drop), the peak output voltage can swing all the way up to $V_{CC}$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi \cdot V_{CC}}{2 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\% \text{ for Class B Push-Pull}}$$

**Step 7 — Power dissipated by the transistors.**
The total power dissipated by both transistors is:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = \frac{V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} - \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} - \frac{V_{CC} I_m}{2} = V_{CC} I_m \left(\frac{1}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Note:** The maximum power dissipation in Class B does *not* occur at full output! It occurs at $V_m = (2/\pi) V_{CC} \approx 0.636 V_{CC}$, where $P_{D\,(max)} \approx 0.4 P_{DC}$. The heat-sink must be sized for this worst-case condition, not for the full-power condition.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency of Class AB Amplifier

**Given Circuit:** Same as Class B push-pull, but with a small forward bias on each transistor's base-emitter junction so that the quiescent current is $I_{CQ} \neq 0$.

**Step 1 — DC Power.**
The DC power is now:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = V_{CC} \left( \frac{I_m}{\pi} + I_{CQ} \right)$$

where $I_{CQ}$ is the small quiescent (no-signal) current that flows through both transistors to eliminate crossover distortion.

**Step 2 — AC Power.**
The AC power is the same as Class B:

$$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{V_m I_m \,/\, 2}{V_{CC} \left( I_m/\pi + I_{CQ} \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Special case: At full output ($V_m \to V_{CC}$, $I_m$ large).**
When $I_m \gg I_{CQ}$ (which is the typical operating condition), the $I_{CQ}$ term becomes negligible and:

$$\eta \to \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Special case: At small output ($I_m \to 0$).**

$$\eta \to \frac{V_m I_m \,/\, 2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} \approx 0$$

At very small signal levels, the efficiency collapses to zero because the supply is still delivering the wasteful quiescent current $I_{CQ}$. The average efficiency in a real audio application lies between **50% and 70%**.

$$\boxed{\eta_{Class\,AB} = 50\% \text{ to } 78.5\% \text{ (signal-dependent)}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Class A (Series-Fed)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2, 7-Mark Sub-Question]**
> A series-fed Class A power amplifier operates from $V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$ and drives a load $R_L = 20\,\Omega$. The Q-point is set at $V_{CEQ} = 10\text{ V}$, $I_{CQ} = 500\text{ mA}$. Calculate (a) the DC power input, (b) the maximum AC power output, and (c) the conversion efficiency.

**Solution:**

(a) DC Power Input:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 20 \times 0.5 = 10\text{ W}$$

(b) Maximum AC Power Output:
The peak output voltage is $V_m = V_{CC}/2 = 10\text{ V}$.

$$P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{10^2}{2 \times 20} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5\text{ W}$$

(c) Conversion Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{2.5}{10} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 25\%} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Class B Push-Pull

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2, 14-Mark Question]**
> A Class B push-pull amplifier operates from $V_{CC} = \pm 15\text{ V}$ and drives a load $R_L = 8\,\Omega$. Find (a) the maximum AC power delivered to the load, (b) the DC power drawn from the supply at maximum output, and (c) the maximum conversion efficiency.

**Solution:**

(a) Maximum AC Power:
At full output, the peak output voltage is $V_m = V_{CC} = 15\text{ V}$.

$$P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{15^2}{2 \times 8} = \frac{225}{16} = 14.0625\text{ W}$$

(b) DC Power Input:
The peak current is:

$$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{15}{8} = 1.875\text{ A}$$

$$P_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{15 \times 1.875}{\pi} = \frac{28.125}{3.1416} \approx 8.95\text{ W}$$

(c) Maximum Conversion Efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 = 78.5\%$$

**Verification:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{14.0625}{8.95} \approx 1.571 \quad \text{?}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to use $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ (the *total* supply power including both rails). For a *single-rail* $V_{CC} = +15\text{ V}$ push-pull, the supply is $V_{CC}$ (not $2V_{CC}$). If the problem says "$\pm 15\text{ V}$" (dual supply), then *each* rail contributes $V_{CC} I_m/\pi$, and total $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_m / \pi$. Always re-read the problem carefully.

For a **dual supply** (±15 V):

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 1.875}{\pi} \approx 17.9\text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{14.0625}{17.9} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\% \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Power Dissipation in Class B

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2, 7-Mark Sub-Question]**
> For a Class B push-pull amplifier with $V_{CC} = 24\text{ V}$ and $R_L = 8\,\Omega$, find the power dissipated by the transistors when the output voltage is $V_m = 12\text{ V}$.

**Solution:**

Peak current:

$$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5\text{ A}$$

DC Power (single-rail, two transistors):

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 24 \times 1.5}{\pi} = \frac{72}{\pi} \approx 22.92\text{ W}$$

AC Power:

$$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{12 \times 1.5}{2} = 9\text{ W}$$

Power Dissipated:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = 22.92 - 9 = 13.92\text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_D \approx 13.92\text{ W}} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Amplifier Classification Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision-tree classification** of power amplifiers based on bias point and conduction angle, along with their typical applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Amplifier Input Signal] --> B{Bias Point Location?}
    B -->|Midpoint of Load Line| C[Class A]
    B -->|At Cutoff VBE less than 0.7V| D[Class B]
    B -->|Slightly Above Cutoff| E[Class AB]
    B -->|Well Below Cutoff| F[Class C]

    C --> C1[Conduction 360 degrees]
    C1 --> C2[Efficiency 25 percent Series Fed]
    C1 --> C3[Efficiency 50 percent Transformer Coupled]
    C2 --> C4[Application Hi Fi Audio Driver]
    C3 --> C4

    D --> D1[Conduction 180 degrees]
    D1 --> D2[Push Pull Configuration Required]
    D2 --> D3[Efficiency 78.5 percent pi by 4]
    D3 --> D4[Limitation Crossover Distortion]
    D4 --> D5[Application Audio Output Stage]

    E --> E1[Conduction between 180 and 360]
    E1 --> E2[Small Quiescent Current ICQ]
    E2 --> E3[Efficiency 50 to 78.5 percent]
    E3 --> E4[Eliminates Crossover Distortion]
    E4 --> E5[Application Consumer Audio Power Amp]

    F --> F1[Conduction less than 180 degrees]
    F1 --> F2[Requires Tuned LC Load]
    F2 --> F3[Efficiency up to 100 percent]
    F3 --> F4[Application RF Transmitters AM FM]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C1 --> Q1[NPN Transistor Q1 Conducts]
    C2 --> Q2[PNP Transistor Q2 Conducts]
    Q1 --> D1[Current Flows Down Through Load]
    Q2 --> D2[Current Flows Up Through Load]
    D1 --> E[Load RL Receives Full Sine Wave]

        F --> G[P_DC equals 2 VCC Im by pi]

    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 3-Mark Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *conversion efficiency* of a power amplifier. Why is it considered the primary figure of merit for large-signal amplifiers?

**Model Answer:**
Conversion efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of the AC power delivered to the load ($P_{AC}$) to the DC power drawn from the supply ($P_{DC}$), expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC\,(load)}}{P_{DC\,(supply)}} \times 100\%$$

It is the primary figure of merit for power amplifiers because, unlike small-signal voltage amplifiers where the AC power is negligible, in a power amplifier the AC power delivered to the load is comparable to the DC power consumed. Higher efficiency means less power is wasted as heat, allowing smaller heat sinks, longer battery life, and higher output power for a given supply rating. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2, 3-Mark Q2]**
> **Q2.** List the four classes of power amplifiers (A, B, AB, C) and state the conduction angle and maximum theoretical efficiency of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Class | Conduction Angle | Maximum Theoretical Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class A | 360° | 25% (series-fed), 50% (transformer) |
| Class B | 180° | 78.5% ($\pi/4$) |
| Class AB | 180° – 360° | 50% – 78.5% |
| Class C | < 180° | ≈ 100% (theoretical) |

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose Either (a) or (b)

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier**. Derive an expression for its conversion efficiency. Compare its performance with a series-fed Class A amplifier.

**(b)** A **Class B push-pull amplifier** operates from a ±20 V dual supply and drives a load of 10 Ω. Calculate:
   - (i) The maximum AC power delivered to the load.
   - (ii) The DC power drawn from the supply at maximum output.
   - (iii) The conversion efficiency at maximum output.
   - (iv) The power dissipated by the transistors at $V_m = 10$ V.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Operation:** In a transformer-coupled Class A amplifier, the load $R_L$ (e.g., a speaker) is connected to the secondary of a step-down transformer whose primary is in the collector circuit. The transistor is biased at the **midpoint of the DC load line** using a resistive divider $R_1$ and $R_2$, with $R_E$ for thermal stability. The quiescent operating point is $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}$ and $I_{CQ}$ is set by $R_E$.

**Why a Transformer?** The transformer's primary winding has *very low DC resistance*, so the DC voltage drop across the load is essentially zero. The full $V_{CC}$ appears at the collector (no DC drop across the primary). The transformer also provides **impedance matching** — the low-impedance speaker (e.g., 8 Ω) is reflected to the primary as a higher impedance $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, which is needed for the transistor to develop large voltage swings.

**DC Power Input:**

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

**AC Power Delivered to Load:**
The peak voltage swing on the primary is $V_m = V_{CC}$ (because the collector can swing from near 0 V to near $2V_{CC}$):

$$P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L'} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'}$$

**Efficiency Derivation:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 \,/\, 2 R_L'}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L' I_{CQ}}$$

For maximum efficiency, the Q-point is set such that the transistor can swing symmetrically between cutoff and saturation. The peak current is $I_m = V_{CC}/R_L'$, and the optimum $I_{CQ} = I_m = V_{CC}/R_L'$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L' \cdot (V_{CC}/R_L')} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\% \text{ (Transformer-Coupled Class A)}}$$

**Comparison with Series-Fed:**

| Feature | Series-Fed Class A | Transformer-Coupled Class A |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max Efficiency | 25% | 50% |
| DC drop in load | Wasted as $I_{CQ}^2 R_L$ heat | Zero (transformer isolates DC) |
| Output voltage swing | $V_{CC}/2$ | Up to $V_{CC}$ (twice as much) |
| Impedance matching | Poor | Excellent |
| Size & Cost | Compact, cheap | Bulky transformer, expensive |
| Application | Low-power drivers | Audio output stages |

(14 marks: 7 for circuit + operation, 4 for derivation, 3 for comparison)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = \pm 20\text{ V}$, $R_L = 10\,\Omega$

**(i) Maximum AC Power:**
At maximum output, $V_m = V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$:

$$P_{AC\,(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{20^2}{2 \times 10} = \frac{400}{20} = 20\text{ W}$$

**(ii) DC Power at Maximum Output:**
Peak current: $I_m = V_m / R_L = 20/10 = 2\text{ A}$

For a dual-supply push-pull:

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 2}{\pi} = \frac{80}{\pi} \approx 25.46\text{ W}$$

**(iii) Conversion Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{20}{25.46} \approx 0.7854 = 78.5\%$$

(Verification: $\pi/4 = 0.7854$ ✓)

**(iv) Power Dissipated at $V_m = 10$ V:**

Peak current: $I_m = V_m / R_L = 10/10 = 1\text{ A}$

DC power at $V_m = 10$ V:

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 1}{\pi} = \frac{40}{\pi} \approx 12.73\text{ W}$$

AC power:

$$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{10 \times 1}{2} = 5\text{ W}$$

Power dissipated by transistors:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = 12.73 - 5 = 7.73\text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{AC} = 20\text{ W}, \quad P_{DC} = 25.46\text{ W}, \quad \eta = 78.5\%, \quad P_D = 7.73\text{ W}} \quad \blacksquare$$

(14 marks: 4+4+3+3)

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Choose Either (a) or (b)

**(a)** Explain the **classification of power amplifiers** based on the position of the Q-point and the conduction angle. Draw the input-output waveforms and the position of the Q-point on the load line for Class A, Class B, Class AB, and Class C amplifiers. Mention one typical application of each.

**(b)** A **Class AB push-pull amplifier** has $V_{CC} = \pm 18\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\,\Omega$, and a quiescent current $I_{CQ} = 50\text{ mA}$. For a peak output voltage of $V_m = 15\text{ V}$, find (i) the DC power input, (ii) the AC power output, (iii) the conversion efficiency, and (iv) the power dissipated by the output transistors.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Power amplifiers are classified based on the **length of time** (conduction angle) that the output transistor conducts during one complete cycle of the input signal. This, in turn, is determined by the location of the **quiescent operating point (Q-point)** on the transistor's transfer characteristic.

**Classification Table with Q-point Location, Waveform, and Application:**

| Class | Q-Point Location | Conduction Angle | Output Waveform Shape | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Center of load line | 360° | Full, undistorted sine wave | Hi-Fi audio pre-driver |
| **B** | Exactly at cutoff | 180° | Half-sine waves (push-pull recombined) | Audio output (with crossover) |
| **AB** | Slightly above cutoff | 180°–360° | Full sine wave, no crossover gap | Consumer audio power amp |
| **C** | Well below cutoff | < 180° | Narrow current pulses (tuned load) | RF transmitters, IF amplifiers |

**Detailed Waveform Description (for the answer script):**

- **Class A:** Q-point is at the center. Output is a complete, undistorted replica of the input. The transistor never cuts off and never saturates (for small signals).
- **Class B:** Q-point is at cutoff. Each transistor conducts for exactly 180°. A complementary pair is required to reproduce the full waveform. The dead zone near zero crossing produces **crossover distortion**.
- **Class AB:** Q-point is biased slightly into conduction by a small $V_{BE}$ multiplier. Both transistors conduct for a small region near the zero crossing, eliminating the dead zone. Conduction angle is 180°–360° depending on signal level.
- **Class C:** Q-point is at a large negative $V_{BE}$ (or zero/positive $V_{BE}$ on PNP). The transistor conducts only when the input exceeds a threshold. Output is a series of narrow pulses; a **tuned LC tank** at the output reconstructs a sinusoidal waveform at the resonant frequency.

(14 marks: 2 for classification logic + 2 for each class × 4 classes + 2 for Q-point positions)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = \pm 18\text{ V}$, $R_L = 8\,\Omega$, $I_{CQ} = 50\text{ mA}$, $V_m = 15\text{ V}$

**(i) DC Power Input:**
Peak current:

$$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{15}{8} = 1.875\text{ A}$$

The DC current from each rail includes the average of the half-sine plus the quiescent current:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} + I_{CQ} = \frac{1.875}{3.1416} + 0.05 = 0.597 + 0.05 = 0.647\text{ A}$$

$$P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_{DC} = 2 \times 18 \times 0.647 \approx 23.29\text{ W}$$

**(ii) AC Power Output:**

$$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{15 \times 1.875}{2} = 14.0625\text{ W}$$

**(iii) Conversion Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{14.0625}{23.29} \approx 0.604 = 60.4\%$$

**(iv) Power Dissipated:**

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = 23.29 - 14.0625 = 9.23\text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{DC} = 23.29\text{ W}, \quad P_{AC} = 14.06\text{ W}, \quad \eta = 60.4\%, \quad P_D = 9.23\text{ W}} \quad \blacksquare$$

(14 marks: 4+4+3+3)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in dual-supply Class B:** The $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_m / \pi$ formula is for a *single-rail* supply. For a *dual-rail* ($\pm V_{CC}$) supply, the formula is $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_m / \pi$. This is the #1 mistake in KTU answer sheets. (**−2 to −3 marks**)
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_m$ with $V_{CC}$ in Class A:** In series-fed Class A, $V_m = V_{CC}/2$ (not $V_{CC}$). In transformer-coupled, $V_m$ can approach $V_{CC}$. Students blindly substitute $V_m = V_{CC}$ in series-fed, giving 100% efficiency — a clear giveaway. (**−2 marks**)
>
> 3. **Not stating the conduction angle explicitly:** KTU examiners award a separate mark for explicitly writing the conduction angle (360°, 180°, etc.) when asked to classify. Omitting it costs **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Using $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_m$ (the peak value) instead of the average:** The DC current is the *average* of the half-sine, which is $I_m/\pi$. Using the peak gives a wrong answer that is too small by a factor of $\pi$. (**−2 marks**)
>
> 5. **Drawing the load line incorrectly:** When asked to show the Q-point on the load line, students often place Class A's Q-point *at* the cutoff point (it should be at the *midpoint*). This is a fundamental conceptual error. (**−2 marks**)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** Power amplifier = large-signal amplifier; figure of merit = conversion efficiency $\eta = P_{AC}/P_{DC}$.
- **Classification axis:** Conduction angle $\theta$ (360° / 180° / 180°–360° / <180°).
- **Class A Q-point:** Center of load line; conducts full 360°.
- **Class A series-fed $\eta_{max}$:** **25%** — half the DC power is wasted even at full output.
- **Class A transformer-coupled $\eta_{max}$:** **50%** — transformer blocks DC from load, allowing full $V_{CC}$ swing.
- **Class B Q-point:** At cutoff; conducts 180°; requires **push-pull** (NPN + PNP).
- **Class B $\eta_{max}$:** $\pi/4$ = **78.5%** — remember the $\pi$ in the formula!
- **Class B flaw:** **Crossover distortion** — eliminated by Class AB bias.
- **Class AB Q-point:** Slightly above cutoff; small $I_{CQ}$ flows at idle.
- **Class AB $\eta$ range:** **50% to 78.5%** — efficiency is signal-dependent.
- **Class C:** Conducts < 180°; **tuned (LC) load required**; efficiency approaches 100% theoretically; used in **RF transmitters**.
- **Key formulas to memorize verbatim:**
  - $P_{AC} = V_m^2 / (2R_L) = V_m I_m / 2$ (load power)
  - $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{DC}$ (single supply)
  - $I_{DC} = I_m / \pi$ (Class B, half-sine average)
  - $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_m / \pi$ (Class B, dual supply)
- **Maximum power dissipation in Class B** does *not* occur at full output — it occurs at $V_m = (2/\pi) V_{CC}$.
- **Transformer** is used in Class A for: (i) DC isolation, (ii) impedance matching, (iii) voltage swing doubling.
- **Why Class AB is the audio standard:** Combines Class A's low distortion with Class B's high efficiency — best of both worlds.
- **Why Class C is not for audio:** Output is highly distorted pulses; only useful with a resonant tank circuit (e.g., RF).
- **Common KTU exam tip:** Always state the conduction angle, the Q-point position, *and* the efficiency formula — examiners check for all three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-Amp Basics: Block Diagram and Ideal Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Op-Amp Basics: Block Diagram and Ideal Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp):** A **direct-coupled, high-gain, differential input, single-ended output** electronic voltage amplifier that uses external feedback components (resistors, capacitors) to control its response. It is the fundamental building block of modern analog electronic systems.

The name "**operational**" originates from its original use in analog computers, where these devices performed mathematical operations such as **addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation**. An op-amp is typically fabricated as a **monolithic integrated circuit (IC)** and is universally represented by a **triangle symbol** with two input terminals and one output terminal.

### 1.2 Terminal Description

An op-amp has **five essential terminals**:

| Terminal | Symbol | Function |
|----------|--------|----------|
| Inverting Input | $V^-$ ($V_1$) | Output is **inverted** (180° phase shift) with respect to this input |
| Non-Inverting Input | $V^+$ ($V_2$) | Output is **in phase** with respect to this input |
| Output | $V_{out}$ | Single-ended amplified output |
| Positive Supply | $+V_{CC}$ or $+V_{S}$ | Typically **+15 V** for standard 741 op-amp |
| Negative Supply | $-V_{EE}$ or $-V_{S}$ | Typically **−15 V** for standard 741 op-amp |
