### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Precision Lever" Analogy:**
> Think of an op-amp as a **frictionless, infinitely long lever** that compares the voltages applied to its two input terminals. Just as a mechanical lever amplifies a small movement at one end into a large movement at the other, an op-amp amplifies the **tiny voltage difference** between $V^+$ and $V^-$ into a large output swing at $V_{out}$. The "frictionless" property corresponds to its **infinite input impedance** (no current drawn from the source), while the "rigid mount" corresponds to its **zero output impedance** (delivers voltage regardless of load).

### 1.4 The Standard Output Equation

The fundamental governing equation of any op-amp (ideal or practical) is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-)$$

where the differential input voltage is defined as:

$$V_{id} = V^+ - V^-$$

So we may equivalently write:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{id}$$

Here, $A_{OL}$ is the **open-loop differential voltage gain** — the intrinsic amplification factor of the device when no external feedback is applied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Block Diagram of an Op-Amp

A general-purpose op-amp IC is internally constructed from **three cascaded functional stages**. Each stage has a clearly defined role, and the entire amplifier behaves as the cascade of these three blocks.

**Stage 1 — Input Stage (Differential Amplifier):**
- Provides **high input impedance** (so the source is not loaded).
- Provides **differential gain** ($A_d$) for the difference signal.
- Provides **common-mode rejection** (CMRR) to reject noise common to both inputs.
- Typically a **Darlington emitter-coupled pair** or **FET long-tailed pair**.

**Stage 2 — Gain Stage (Intermediate High-Gain Amplifier):**
- Provides the **bulk of the voltage gain** (often 80 dB to 100 dB).
- Uses **active loads** (current sources) to maximize gain without high power.
- Performs **level shifting** (DC offset compensation) so output can swing symmetrically around 0 V.
- Typically a **common-emitter amplifier** with current-mirror load.

**Stage 3 — Output Stage (Push-Pull Buffer):**
- Provides **low output impedance** for driving low-resistance loads.
- Provides **current boosting** to deliver sufficient load current.
- Typically a **Class-AB complementary push-pull** (emitter follower) stage.
- Often includes **short-circuit protection** circuitry.

### 2.2 Ideal Characteristics of an Op-Amp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — High-Yield Note:**
> The "ideal op-amp" is a **theoretical abstraction** used to simplify hand-analysis. The standard **μA741** (a popular real-world IC) is the closest practical approximation. In KTU problems, you will be expected to **assume ideal op-amp behavior** unless told otherwise.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Ideal Value | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Open-Loop Voltage Gain | $\infty$ | $A_{OL} \to \infty$ | Infinite amplification of differential input |
| 2 | Input Impedance | $\infty$ | $R_{in} \to \infty$ | No current flows into input terminals |
| 3 | Output Impedance | $0$ | $R_{out} \to 0$ | Output voltage independent of load |
| 4 | Bandwidth | $\infty$ | $BW \to \infty$ | Amplifies DC and all frequencies equally |
| 5 | CMRR | $\infty$ | $\text{CMRR} \to \infty$ | Perfect rejection of common-mode signals |
| 6 | Slew Rate | $\infty$ | $SR \to \infty$ | Output changes instantaneously with input |
| 7 | Input Offset Voltage | $0$ | $V_{io} = 0$ | Output is zero when both inputs are zero |
| 8 | Input Bias Current | $0$ | $I_B = 0$ | No DC current required at inputs |
| 9 | Input Offset Current | $0$ | $I_{io} = 0$ | Difference between bias currents is zero |
| 10 | Power Supply Rejection Ratio | $\infty$ | $\text{PSRR} \to \infty$ | Output unaffected by supply variations |
| 11 | Noise | $0$ | $N = 0$ | No internal noise generation |
| 12 | Output Voltage Swing | $\pm V_{S}$ | $V_{out} \in [-V_S, +V_S]$ | Rail-to-rail in ideal case |

### 2.4 Derived Output Voltage Expression

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ideal" Makes Analysis Trivial:**
> With $A_{OL} \to \infty$ and $V_{out}$ bounded by finite supply rails, the only way the equation $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{id}$ can hold is if $V_{id} \to 0$. This gives the famous **"Virtual Short"** principle.

The differential input voltage is:

$$V_{id} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}}$$

Taking the limit as $A_{OL} \to \infty$ (with $V_{out}$ finite):

$$\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} V_{id} = \lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$V_{id} = V^+ - V^- = 0 \quad \implies \quad V^+ = V^- \quad \text{(Virtual Short)}$$

Similarly, since $R_{in} \to \infty$ and the input voltage is finite:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{id}}{R_{in}} = \lim_{R_{in} \to \infty} \frac{V_{id}}{R_{in}} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$I^+ = I^- = 0 \quad \text{(Virtual Open)}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ideal op-amp model is the **cornerstone of all analog design** because it allows engineers to design **feedback amplifiers** (inverting, non-inverting, integrators, differentiators, summers, active filters) using simple algebraic analysis rather than solving complex transistor-level circuit equations. Every major analog IC family — from **μA741**, **LM358**, **TL081** to modern **Op-Amps from Texas Instruments, Analog Devices, and STMicroelectronics** — is analyzed this way at the system level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Open-Loop Output Voltage Equation

**Step 1 — Define the differential input voltage.**
The op-amp responds to the **algebraic difference** between its two inputs. Define:

$$V_{id} = V^+ - V^-$$

This is the actual signal that gets amplified. The op-amp ignores any signal that is common to both inputs (the "common-mode" signal).

**Step 2 — Apply the open-loop gain to the differential input.**
The output is the differential input scaled by the open-loop gain factor:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{id}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the definition of $V_{id}$ from Step 1.**

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-)$$

**Step 4 — Acknowledge saturation limits.**
In a **real** op-amp, the output cannot exceed the supply rails. So the complete expression is:

$$V_{out} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} \approx +V_S & \text{if } A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-) > +V_S \\ A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-) & \text{if } -V_{sat} \le A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-) \le +V_{sat} \\ -V_{sat} \approx -V_S & \text{if } A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-) < -V_{sat} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Take the ideal limit.**
For an **ideal** op-amp, $A_{OL} \to \infty$. Since the output is bounded by finite supply rails, the input difference $V_{id}$ must approach **zero** for the output to remain finite:

$$\boxed{\; V_{id} = V^+ - V^- = 0 \quad \text{(Ideal Op-Amp Condition)}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Input Current at Op-Amp Terminals

**Step 1 — Express the input current using Ohm's law applied to input impedance.**

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{id}}{R_{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ideal limit $R_{in} \to \infty$.**

$$\lim_{R_{in} \to \infty} I_{in} = \lim_{R_{in} \to \infty} \frac{V_{id}}{R_{in}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the limit.**
Since $V_{id}$ remains finite (it is a physical voltage applied to the terminals):

$$I_{in} \to 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the virtual open principle.**

$$\boxed{\; I_{in} = I^+ = I^- = 0 \quad \text{(Ideal Op-Amp Condition)}\;}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Significance of Each Ideal Characteristic

| Ideal Property | Mathematical Implication | Consequence in Circuit Analysis | KTU Problem Type |
|----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| $A_{OL} \to \infty$ | $V_{id} = V_{out}/A_{OL} = 0$ | **Virtual Short** between inputs | Solving closed-loop gain |
| $R_{in} \to \infty$ | $I_{in} = V_{id}/R_{in} = 0$ | **Virtual Open** at input terminals | KCL analysis at inverting node |
| $R_{out} = 0$ | $V_{out}$ independent of $R_L$ | Output is an **ideal voltage source** | Loading effect calculations |
| $BW \to \infty$ | Gain constant for all frequencies | No frequency compensation needed | Time-domain response analysis |
| $\text{CMRR} \to \infty$ | Common-mode gain $A_c = 0$ | Noise on both inputs is fully rejected | Instrumentation amplifier design |
| $SR \to \infty$ | Output tracks input instantly | No high-frequency distortion | Square wave / pulse response |

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Ideal vs Practical (μA741) Op-Amp

| Characteristic | Ideal Op-Amp | Practical μA741 Op-Amp |
|----------------|--------------|------------------------|
| Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^{5}$ (106 dB) |
| Input impedance $R_{in}$ | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \, \text{M}\Omega$ (BJT) / $10^{12} \, \Omega$ (FET) |
| Output impedance $R_{out}$ | $0$ | $\approx 75 \, \Omega$ |
| CMRR | $\infty$ | $\approx 90 \, \text{dB}$ |
| Slew Rate | $\infty$ | $\approx 0.5 \, \text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ |
| Bandwidth | $\infty$ | $\approx 1 \, \text{MHz}$ (unity-gain) |
| Input offset voltage | $0$ | $\approx 1 \, \text{mV}$ (typical), $6 \, \text{mV}$ (max) |
| Supply voltage | Not defined | $\pm 15 \, \text{V}$ (nominal) |
| Output swing | $\pm V_S$ (rail-to-rail) | $\pm 13 \, \text{V}$ (typical with $\pm 15 \, \text{V}$ supply) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Symbol of an Op-Amp

The op-amp is universally represented by a **triangle** with three signal terminals and two power supply terminals:

```
                +V_CC (+V_S)
                  |
                  |
                 ___                   
                /   \                  
   V^+   >-----|     |  
   (V2)        |  A  |-----> V_out
   V^-   >-----|  OL |     (Single-Ended)
   (V1)        \___ /                  
                  |                    
                  |                    
                -V_EE (-V_S)
```

- The **"−"** (inverting) input is on the top, the **"+"** (non-inverting) input is on the bottom by convention.
- The triangle apex points to the output side.
- $A_{OL}$ inside the triangle represents the open-loop gain (a multiplier).

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Internal Three-Stage Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the internal architecture of a typical op-amp and the signal flow from input to output.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DIFFERENTIAL INPUT SIGNALS Vplus AND Vminus"] --> B["STAGE 1: INPUT STAGE - DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER"]
    B --> B1["High Input Impedance Rin INFINITY"]
    B --> B2["Differential Gain Ad AND Common Mode Rejection CMRR"]
    B --> C["DIFFERENTIAL TO SINGLE ENDED CONVERSION"]
    C --> D["STAGE 2: GAIN STAGE - HIGH GAIN VOLTAGE AMPLIFIER"]
    D --> D1["Provides Bulk Voltage Gain AOL APPROX 10 POWER 5"]
    D --> D2["Level Shifting for DC Bias"]
    D --> D3["Active Load Current Mirror"]
    D --> E["STAGE 3: OUTPUT STAGE - PUSH PULL BUFFER"]
    E --> E1["Low Output Impedance Rout NEAR ZERO"]
    E --> E2["Current Boosting and Load Drive"]
    E --> F["FINAL SINGLE ENDED OUTPUT Vout"]
    G["SUPPLY RAILS plus Vcc AND minus Vee TYPICALLY plus minus 15 V"] --> B
    G --> D
    G --> E
    style B fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#B4D5FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Functional Description of Each Block

| Block | Function | Typical Realization | Why It Matters |
|-------|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Stage 1: Input Stage** | Differential amplification, high $Z_{in}$, CMRR | BJT differential pair with current-mirror load | Sets noise performance and input bias requirements |
| **Stage 2: Gain Stage** | Provides bulk voltage gain (often > 80 dB) | Common-emitter amp with active current-source load | Determines DC gain and dominant pole compensation |
| **Stage 3: Output Stage** | Current boost, low $Z_{out}$, short-circuit protection | Complementary Class-AB push-pull emitter follower | Sets load-driving capability and thermal behavior |

### 4.4 Op-Amp as a Controlled Voltage Source (Thevenin View)

From the **external terminals**, an op-amp can be modeled as a **Thevenin equivalent** as shown in the flowchart below.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input Voltage Vid = Vplus minus Vminus"] --> B["Multiply by AOL Open Loop Gain"]
    B --> C["Internal Thevenin Source Vout ideal"]
    C --> D["Series Output Resistance Rout ideal equals ZERO"]
    D --> E["External Load RL - any value"]
    E --> F["Voltage across RL equals Vout EXACTLY"]
    style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

The key insight from this diagram is that because $R_{out} = 0$ in the ideal case, **$V_{out}$ is completely independent of the load resistance $R_L$**. This is what makes the op-amp behave as an "ideal" voltage source from the perspective of the external circuit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

Define an **operational amplifier**. List any **six ideal characteristics** of an op-amp.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **operational amplifier (op-amp)** is a **direct-coupled, high-gain, DC electronic voltage amplifier** with two inputs (inverting $V^-$ and non-inverting $V^+$) and a single-ended output, designed to be used with external feedback to perform a variety of linear and non-linear operations.

The **six ideal characteristics** of an op-amp are:
1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain** ($A_{OL} = \infty$)
2. **Infinite input impedance** ($R_{in} = \infty$)
3. **Zero output impedance** ($R_{out} = 0$)
4. **Infinite bandwidth** ($BW = \infty$)
5. **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio** (CMRR $= \infty$)
6. **Infinite slew rate** ($SR = \infty$)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

Write the **general output voltage equation** of an op-amp and explain the meaning of each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The general output voltage equation of an op-amp is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-)$$

Where:
- $V_{out}$ = output voltage of the op-amp
- $V^+$ = voltage at the non-inverting input terminal
- $V^-$ = voltage at the inverting input terminal
- $(V^+ - V^-)$ = **differential input voltage** $V_{id}$
- $A_{OL}$ = **open-loop differential voltage gain** of the op-amp

The equation states that the output voltage is the **algebraic difference** between the two inputs, multiplied by the open-loop gain. When $V^+ > V^-$, the output is **positive**; when $V^+ < V^-$, the output is **negative**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Draw the **block diagram of a general-purpose op-amp** showing all three internal stages. Explain the function of each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A general-purpose op-amp is internally constructed from **three cascaded stages**, as shown in the block diagram below:

$$\text{Input Stage} \rightarrow \text{Gain Stage} \rightarrow \text{Output Stage} \rightarrow V_{out}$$

**Stage 1 — Input Stage (Differential Amplifier):**
- Implemented using a **Darlington differential pair** or **long-tailed pair** with a current-mirror active load.
- Provides **high input impedance** ($R_{in} \approx 2 \, \text{M}\Omega$ to $10^{12} \, \Omega$).
- Provides **differential gain** $A_d$ for the difference signal $V_{id} = V^+ - V^-$.
- Provides **common-mode rejection** to suppress noise appearing equally on both inputs (characterized by CMRR).
- Performs **differential-to-single-ended conversion** to feed the next stage.

**Stage 2 — Gain Stage (Intermediate High-Gain Amplifier):**
- Implemented using a **common-emitter amplifier** with **active current-source load** (current mirror).
- Provides the **bulk of the voltage gain** — typically **100 dB to 106 dB** (i.e., $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to $2 \times 10^5$).
- Performs **level shifting** to ensure the output DC operating point is at 0 V when both inputs are grounded.
- Often includes **Miller compensation capacitor** (typically $\approx 30 \, \text{pF}$ in μA741) to set the dominant pole and ensure closed-loop stability.

**Stage 3 — Output Stage (Push-Pull Buffer):**
- Implemented using a **Class-AB complementary push-pull emitter follower** (NPN + PNP).
- Provides **low output impedance** ($R_{out} \approx 75 \, \Omega$ for μA741).
- Provides **current boosting** to drive low-impedance loads (typically up to $\pm 20 \, \text{mA}$).
- Includes **short-circuit protection** circuitry to prevent damage from output overcurrent.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **ideal characteristics** of an op-amp. Derive the **virtual short** and **virtual open** conditions from the ideal assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **ideal op-amp** is a theoretical device with the following characteristics:
1. Infinite open-loop gain ($A_{OL} = \infty$)
2. Infinite input impedance ($R_{in} = \infty$)
3. Zero output impedance ($R_{out} = 0$)
4. Infinite bandwidth
5. Infinite CMRR
6. Infinite slew rate
7. Zero input offset voltage
8. Zero noise

**Derivation of Virtual Short:**

The output voltage equation is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{id}$$

Rearranging for $V_{id}$:

$$V_{id} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}}$$

For an **ideal** op-amp, $A_{OL} \to \infty$. Since $V_{out}$ is finite (bounded by supply rails):

$$\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} V_{id} = \lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}} = 0$$

Therefore, $V_{id} = V^+ - V^- = 0$, which gives the **Virtual Short** condition:

$$\boxed{\; V^+ = V^- \;}$$

**Derivation of Virtual Open:**

The input current is given by Ohm's law at the input terminals:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{id}}{R_{in}}$$

For an **ideal** op-amp, $R_{in} \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{R_{in} \to \infty} I_{in} = \lim_{R_{in} \to \infty} \frac{V_{id}}{R_{in}} = 0$$

Therefore, the **Virtual Open** condition:

$$\boxed{\; I^+ = I^- = 0 \;}$$

These two conditions are the **foundation of all op-amp circuit analysis** in KTU exams.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** List the **ideal characteristics** of an op-amp and **compare** them with the practical **μA741** op-amp characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Table: Ideal vs Practical (μA741) Op-Amp**

| # | Characteristic | Ideal Value | μA741 Practical Value |
|---|----------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^{5}$ (106 dB) |
| 2 | Input impedance $R_{in}$ | $\infty$ | $\approx 2 \, \text{M}\Omega$ |
| 3 | Output impedance $R_{out}$ | $0$ | $\approx 75 \, \Omega$ |
| 4 | CMRR | $\infty$ | $\approx 90 \, \text{dB}$ |
| 5 | Slew rate | $\infty$ | $\approx 0.5 \, \text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ |
| 6 | Bandwidth | $\infty$ | $\approx 1 \, \text{MHz}$ (at unity gain) |
| 7 | Input offset voltage | $0$ | $\approx 1 \, \text{mV}$ (typ), $6 \, \text{mV}$ (max) |
| 8 | Supply voltage | — | $\pm 15 \, \text{V}$ (nominal) |

**Conclusion:** The μA741 closely approximates the ideal op-amp in many respects, but is not perfect. Modern op-amps (e.g., **OP-27**, **OP-07**, **LT1012**) offer much better characteristics — lower offset, higher CMRR, and lower noise — but the μA741 remains the standard teaching example in KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

---

**Q. (b)** Define **differential gain**, **common-mode gain**, and **CMRR**. Why is **high CMRR** essential in instrumentation applications? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Differential Gain ($A_d$):**
It is the gain of the op-amp for signals applied **differentially** (i.e., $V_{id} = V^+ - V^-$), with both inputs having opposite polarities. For an ideal op-amp:

$$A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{id}} = A_{OL}$$

**Common-Mode Gain ($A_c$):**
It is the gain of the op-amp for signals applied **equally** to both inputs (i.e., $V_{id} = 0$ but $V^+ = V^- = V_{cm}$). The output ideally should be zero. The common-mode gain is defined as:

$$A_c = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{cm}}$$

**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):**
It is the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain, expressed in **decibels**:

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert \quad \text{(in absolute ratio)}$$

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

**Why High CMRR is Essential in Instrumentation:**
In real-world applications (e.g., biomedical signal acquisition, strain gauge bridges, thermocouple amplifiers), the **useful signal** is a small differential voltage riding on top of a **large common-mode voltage** (such as 50 Hz mains hum pickup or ground-potential differences). A high CMRR ensures that the **interfering common-mode signal is rejected**, while the **desired differential signal is amplified**. For an ideal op-amp, $A_c = 0$, giving $\text{CMRR} = \infty$. For the μA741, CMRR $\approx 90$ dB means common-mode signals are rejected by a factor of about **31,623** relative to the differential signal.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting to write units:** $A_{OL} = 10^5$ must be stated as a dimensionless ratio; $R_{in}$ must carry $\Omega$ units; Slew Rate must carry V/μs units. **−0.5 mark per missing unit.**
> 2. **Conflating "open-loop" and "closed-loop" gain:** Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ is the intrinsic gain **without feedback**. Closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ is set by the **external feedback network**. Mixing them up is a frequent cause of full-mark loss.
> 3. **Wrong CMRR formula:** Some students write $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log(A_c/A_d)$ instead of $20 \log(A_d/A_c)$. **Always ratio is $A_d$ over $A_c$.**
> 4. **Saturation not mentioned:** When computing $V_{out}$ from $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{id}$, students often forget that real op-amps **saturate** at $\pm V_{sat} \approx \pm V_S$. Always check the saturation condition.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** For a 7-mark "Draw and Explain" question, the block diagram carries **at least 2 to 3 marks**. Never answer without a properly labeled diagram.
> 6. **Order of magnitude errors:** μA741 has $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ (not $10^3$ and not $10^7$). Slew rate is $\approx 0.5 \, \text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ (not 0.5 V/ms).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Op-Amp Basics**

- **Op-Amp Definition:** Direct-coupled, high-gain, differential-input, single-ended-output voltage amplifier with external feedback.
- **Output Equation:** $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-) = A_{OL} \cdot V_{id}$
- **Three Internal Stages:** Input (Differential Amp) $\rightarrow$ Gain (High-Gain Voltage Amp) $\rightarrow$ Output (Push-Pull Buffer)
- **12 Ideal Characteristics to Memorize:** $A_{OL}=\infty$, $R_{in}=\infty$, $R_{out}=0$, $BW=\infty$, $\text{CMRR}=\infty$, $SR=\infty$, $V_{io}=0$, $I_B=0$, $I_{io}=0$, $\text{PSRR}=\infty$, Noise $= 0$, Output swing $=\pm V_S$
- **Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis:**
  1. **Virtual Short:** $V^+ = V^-$ (because $A_{OL} = \infty$)
  2. **Virtual Open:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (because $R_{in} = \infty$)
- **μA741 Practical Reference Numbers:** $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$, $R_{in} \approx 2 \, \text{M}\Omega$, $R_{out} \approx 75 \, \Omega$, CMRR $\approx 90$ dB, $SR \approx 0.5 \, \text{V/}\mu\text{s}$, $BW \approx 1 \, \text{MHz}$, supply $= \pm 15 \, \text{V}$
- **CMRR Formula:** $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_c \vert$
- **Saturation Condition:** $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$ whenever $A_{OL} \cdot V_{id} > \pm V_S$
- **KTU 2024 High-Yield Exam Tip:** Master the derivation of Virtual Short and Virtual Open — these are guaranteed sub-questions in every ESE Part B.
- **Engineering Applications of Op-Amps:** Inverting/non-inverting amplifiers, summing/difference amplifiers, integrators, differentiators, active filters, comparators, oscillators, voltage regulators, instrumentation amplifiers, and ADC/DAC front-ends.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-Amp Parameters: Gain, Bandwidth, Slew Rate, CMRR, Offset Voltage and Offset Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Op-Amp Parameters: Gain, Bandwidth, Slew Rate, CMRR, Offset Voltage and Offset Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Welcome to one of the **highest-weightage and most frequently tested** topics in KTU's Analog Electronics paper. The **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is *never* ideal in practice. Every real IC op-amp (like the famous µA741) deviates from the ideal model due to manufacturing imperfections, internal transistor mismatches, and thermal effects. The parameters below *quantify* these imperfections.

## 1.1 Differential Mode Gain ($A_d$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The differential mode gain is the amplification provided by the op-amp to the **difference** between the two input voltages. Mathematically,
>
> $$A_d = \frac{V_o}{V_{id}} = \frac{V_o}{V_1 - V_2}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a balance scale. The differential gain is the sensitivity of the scale to *which side is heavier by how much*. An ideal scale would be infinitely sensitive to even a grain of rice — that's why ideal $A_d = \infty$.

## 1.2 Common-Mode Gain ($A_{cm}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The common-mode gain is the amplification given to signals that appear **identically (in phase and amplitude)** on both input terminals.
>
> $$A_{cm} = \frac{V_o}{V_{cm}}, \quad \text{where} \quad V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Suppose you and your friend stand on the same balance scale together. The scale should **ignore** your combined weight (it is common to both sides). Common-mode gain is the *unwanted* "sensitivity" the scale has to this common weight. For a perfect scale, $A_{cm} = 0$.

## 1.3 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** CMRR is the ability of the op-amp to **reject** common-mode signals while amplifying differential signals. It is the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain.
>
> $$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \rho = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}}$$
>
> In decibels:
>
> $$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right) \text{ dB}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a fancy restaurant waiter listening to *your* table's order (differential signal) while ignoring the loud chatter from *all the other tables* (common-mode noise, like 50 Hz power line hum picked up on long cables). A great waiter has **high CMRR**.

## 1.4 Bandwidth & Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Bandwidth (BW) is the range of frequencies over which the op-amp's gain remains within **3 dB** of its DC value. Beyond the dominant pole frequency $f_1$, the gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**.
>
> $$\text{GBW} = A_d \times f = \text{constant}$$
>
> For unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$:
>
> $$f_T = A_d \times f_1 \approx \text{GBW}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** A speaker has a "sweet spot" frequency range. Outside it, the bass gets muddy and the treble gets tinny. An op-amp's bandwidth is exactly that sweet spot — a measure of its **frequency fidelity**.

## 1.5 Slew Rate (SR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Slew rate is the **maximum rate of change of output voltage** with respect to time, under large-signal conditions. It defines the op-amp's high-speed response limit.
>
> $$\boxed{\text{SR} = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right|_{\max}}$$
>
> Standard unit: **V/µs**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a horse-drawn carriage trying to turn sharply. The carriage *cannot* turn faster than the horse can run. SR is the "horse's top speed" — how quickly the output slews (sweeps) through voltage.

## 1.6 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Input offset voltage is the small DC voltage that must be applied between the input terminals to **make the output exactly zero**.
>
> $$\boxed{V_{io} = \frac{V_o \big\vert_{V_1=V_2=0}}{A_d}}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** A weighing scale that reads "1 kg" when nothing is on it. You must manually place a 1 kg weight to *zero* it. $V_{io}$ is that "phantom weight" the op-amp thinks it sees at its input.

## 1.7 Input Bias Current ($I_B$) and Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitions:**
> - **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** The average of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals.
>
> $$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$
>
> - **Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$):** The difference between the two input bias currents.
>
> $$\boxed{I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Two water taps are supposed to deliver the *same* trickle of water. $I_B$ is the average trickle. $I_{io}$ is the *unwanted* mismatch between the two — it causes the bucket (output) to slowly drift off balance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of a Non-Ideal Op-Amp

A real op-amp's output is governed by the superposition of both differential and common-mode behavior:

$$V_o = A_d V_{id} + A_{cm} V_{cm}$$

where
- $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$ (differential input)
- $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ (common-mode input)

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Examiner Insight:** The KTU paper setter *loves* the rearrangement:
> $$V_o = A_d \left( V_{id} + \frac{V_{cm}}{\text{CMRR}} \right)$$
> This form is used to calculate **output error** due to finite CMRR.

## 2.2 Why CMRR Matters in Engineering

In real-world instrumentation (e.g., ECG machines, strain gauges, thermocouple amplifiers), the signal of interest is a **small differential voltage** riding on top of a **large common-mode voltage** (often from 50 Hz mains pickup or ground loops). Without high CMRR, the op-amp would amplify the noise along with the signal, ruining the measurement. Medical-grade instrumentation demands CMRR > **100 dB**.

## 2.3 Derivation Philosophy of Slew Rate

Consider the simplified internal model: the input differential stage drives a high-gain stage that has a **Miller compensating capacitor $C_c$**. The maximum current available to charge/discharge this capacitor is the bias current $I$.

* The capacitor charging law: $i = C_c \dfrac{dV_c}{dt}$
* The maximum current is $I$, so $I = C_c \cdot \text{SR}$
* Therefore, $\text{SR} = \dfrac{I}{C_c}$ — a property **set by internal design**, not by the external circuit.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below. Every cell is a KTU 2024 ESE potential question.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value (µA741) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential Gain | $A_d = \dfrac{V_o}{V_1 - V_2}$ | $2 \times 10^5$ | V/V |
| 2 | Common-Mode Gain | $A_{cm} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{cm}}$ | $\approx 0.2$ | V/V |
| 3 | CMRR (numeric) | $\rho = \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$ | $10^6$ | — |
| 4 | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$ | $120$ | dB |
| 5 | Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = f_1 = \dfrac{f_T}{A_d}$ | $5 \text{ Hz}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = A_d \cdot f_1 = f_T$ | $1 \text{ MHz}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Slew Rate | $\text{SR} = \left. \dfrac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max} = \dfrac{I}{C_c}$ | $0.5$ | V/µs |
| 8 | Full-Power Bandwidth | $f_p = \dfrac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_{om}}$ | — | Hz |
| 9 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{io} = \dfrac{V_o \big\vert_{V_1=V_2=0}}{A_d}$ | $1 \text{ mV}$ | V |
| 10 | Input Bias Current | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$ | $80 \text{ nA}$ | A |
| 11 | Input Offset Current | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ | $5 \text{ nA}$ | A |
| 12 | Output Offset (inverting amp) | $V_{oo} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} - I_{io} R_f$ | — | V |
| 13 | Output Offset (non-inverting) | $V_{oo} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} - I_{io} R_f$ | — | V |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **modulus** and is written using LaTeX `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table grid. Do **not** use the bare pipe character in your exam scripts — it can confuse the KTU online template system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CMRR in Terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$

**Given:** $V_o = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm} \left(\dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$

**Step 1.** Factor out the input voltages:

$$V_o = A_d V_1 - A_d V_2 + \frac{A_{cm}}{2} V_1 + \frac{A_{cm}}{2} V_2$$

**Step 2.** Group $V_1$ and $V_2$ terms:

$$V_o = \left( A_d + \frac{A_{cm}}{2} \right) V_1 - \left( A_d - \frac{A_{cm}}{2} \right) V_2$$

**Step 3.** Define a "modified differential gain" — for $A_d \gg A_{cm}$:

$$A_{d}' \approx A_d, \quad A_{cm}' \approx \frac{A_{cm}}{2}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute back into the standard formula:

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = \rho}$$

This derivation shows that CMRR is a **dimensionless ratio** independent of input magnitude — it is a *property of the device's symmetry*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Output Offset Voltage of an Inverting Amplifier

**Circuit:** Inverting amplifier with $R_1$ (input), $R_f$ (feedback), and a bias-compensating resistor $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f$ at the non-inverting input.

**Step 1.** Model the non-ideal op-amp with $V_{io}$ and bias currents $I_{B1}, I_{B2}$.

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) V_{io} - I_{B2} R_f + I_{B1} R_1'$$

**Step 2.** Set $V_1 = 0$ to find the output *offset* (no input signal):

$$V_{oo} = \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) V_{io} - I_{B2} R_f + I_{B1} (R_1 \parallel R_f)$$

**Step 3.** Express in terms of bias and offset currents. Recall:
- $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$
- $I_{io} = I_{B1} - I_{B2}$

**Step 4.** Solve $I_{B1} = I_B + \dfrac{I_{io}}{2}$ and $I_{B2} = I_B - \dfrac{I_{io}}{2}$:

$$I_{B1} R_1' - I_{B2} R_f = I_B R_1' + \frac{I_{io}}{2} R_1' - I_B R_f + \frac{I_{io}}{2} R_f$$

**Step 5.** Group $I_B$ and $I_{io}$ terms:

$$= I_B (R_1' - R_f) + \frac{I_{io}}{2} (R_1' + R_f)$$

**Step 6.** Recognize that with the compensating resistor $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f$, the $I_B$ term is engineered to be near zero because $R_1' \ll R_f$. The **dominant residual offset** is:

$$\boxed{V_{oo} \approx \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) V_{io} \pm I_{io} R_f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Trick:** If the examiner asks "what is the *purpose* of $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f$?", the answer is to **cancel the input bias current $I_B$** from producing an output offset, leaving only the smaller $I_{io} \cdot R_f$ term.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Slew Rate Limiting on a Sine Wave

**Given:** A sine wave applied to the op-amp output: $V_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$.

**Step 1.** Differentiate to find the maximum rate of change:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2.** The maximum occurs when $\cos(\cdot) = 1$:

$$\left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right|_{\max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 3.** Slew rate limiting begins when this max rate *exceeds* the op-amp's SR:

$$2\pi f V_m \le \text{SR}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the maximum allowable frequency for a given output amplitude $V_m$ (called the **Full-Power Bandwidth** $f_p$):

$$\boxed{f_p = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_m}}$$

**Step 5.** Example: For an op-amp with $\text{SR} = 0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ producing $V_m = 5 \text{ V}$ peak:

$$f_p = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{500{,}000}{31.416} \approx 15{,}915 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}$$

Beyond $f_p$, the sine wave becomes a **distorted triangle wave** — a classic KTU question on identifying slew-rate distortion.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off

**Given:** Open-loop response $A_d(f) = \dfrac{A_{d0}}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$

**Step 1.** Magnitude: $\vert A_d(f) \vert = \dfrac{A_{d0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}}$

**Step 2.** Define unity-gain frequency $f_T$ where $\vert A_d \vert = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{A_{d0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_1)^2}} \Rightarrow 1 + (f_T/f_1)^2 = A_{d0}^2$$

**Step 3.** For $f_T \gg f_1$, drop the 1:

$$(f_T/f_1)^2 \approx A_{d0}^2 \Rightarrow f_T = A_{d0} \cdot f_1$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, the **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is a constant:

$$\boxed{\text{GBW} = A_{d0} \cdot f_1 = f_T = \text{constant}}$$

This means: if you set up a non-inverting amplifier with closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$, the **new −3 dB bandwidth** becomes:

$$f_{1,CL} = \frac{f_T}{A_{CL}}$$

This is a **trade-off** — more gain, less bandwidth, and vice versa.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Non-Ideal Op-Amp Internal Model

This diagram captures **all the non-idealities** discussed above as functional blocks interacting with the input stage. It replaces a literal transistor-level schematic with a clear architectural view of how the imperfections propagate to the output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["Input V1"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    V2["Input V2"] --> SUM2(("−"))
    Vio["Input Offset Voltage Vio"] --> SUM2
    Ib1["Bias Current IB1"] --> SUM2
    Ib2["Bias Current IB2"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> DM["Differential Gain Stage Ad"]
    SUM2 --> DM
    Vcm["Common Mode Signal Vcm"] --> CM["Common Mode Gain Stage Acm"]
    DM --> ADDOUT(("Σ"))
    CM --> ADDOUT
    ADDOUT --> CC["Miller Cap Cc Limits SR"]
    CC --> OUT["Output Vo"]
    BW["Dominant Pole f1 Controls BW"] --> DM
    BW --> CM
    CMRR["CMRR rho equals Ad by Acm"] -.-> ADDOUT
    OUT --> OFF["Total Output Offset Voo"]
    OFF --> STAGE["Final Vo with all error terms"]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Relationship Between All Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Non-Ideal Op-Amp"] --> B["Differential Gain Ad"]
    A --> C["Common Mode Gain Acm"]
    A --> D["Bandwidth BW and GBW"]
    A --> E["Slew Rate SR"]
    A --> F["Offset Voltage Vio"]
    A --> G["Bias and Offset Currents IB and Iio"]
    B --> H["CMRR equals Ad over Acm"]
    C --> H
    D --> I["Full Power Bandwidth fp equals SR over two pi Vm"]
    E --> I
    F --> J["Output Offset Voo"]
    G --> J
    H --> K["Total Output Error"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Bode Plot: Gain Roll-off and Slew-Rate Limit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FREQ["Frequency Response"]
        DC["DC Gain: AdO in dB flat region"] --> F1["f1: Dominant Pole −3 dB point"]
        F1 --> F2["Roll off at −20 dB per decade"]
        F2 --> FT["fT: Unity Gain Crossover"]
    end
    subgraph SR["Slew Rate Domain"]
        FPB["fp equals SR over two pi Vm"]
        LOWF["Below fp: Sine wave undistorted"]
        HIGHF["Above fp: Triangle wave distortion"]
    end
    FT -.-> FPB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above intentionally use **uppercase alphanumeric labels only** (e.g., `V1`, `Ad`, `CMRR`) to comply with Mermaid rendering safeguards. No bold tags, italics, or HTML tables are embedded inside the node strings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Slew rate is in **V/µs** in datasheets, but the formula $2\pi f V_m$ gives V/s. *Convert* before comparing.
> 2. **CMRR units** — Always state the *numeric ratio* AND the *dB value*. Marks are split.
> 3. **Sign error in $V_{oo}$** — The output offset due to $I_{io}$ carries a $\pm$ sign depending on the direction of mismatch. Examiners award **partial credit** if you write the magnitude but lose 1 mark for the sign.
> 4. **Forgetting $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f$** — If a problem says "compensating resistor is used", you *must* include it in $V_{oo}$ or the answer is marked wrong.

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define CMRR of an op-amp. For an op-amp, $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and $A_{cm} = 0.5$. Calculate the CMRR in dB.**

**Model Answer:**

CMRR is the ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode-mode gain. It measures the op-amp's ability to reject signals common to both inputs.

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{0.5} = 4 \times 10^5$$

In decibels:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(4 \times 10^5) = 20 \times 5.602 = 112.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Answer: CMRR ≈ 112 dB**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is slew rate? An op-amp has a slew rate of 0.5 V/µs. What is the maximum frequency at which it can deliver an undistorted sine wave of 5 V peak amplitude?**

**Model Answer:**

Slew rate is the maximum rate of change of the output voltage of an op-amp under large-signal conditions.

For an undistorted sine output, the maximum rate of change must not exceed the slew rate:

$$2\pi f_m V_m \le \text{SR}$$

Solving for the maximum frequency $f_m$ (the full-power bandwidth):

$$f_m = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_m}$$

Substituting values:

$$f_m = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{500{,}000}{31.416}$$

$$\boxed{f_m \approx 15{,}915 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}}$$

---

## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

#### **(a)** Explain the following op-amp parameters with typical values for µA741: (i) Input offset voltage, (ii) Input bias current, (iii) Input offset current. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$):**
In an ideal op-amp, when both inputs are grounded, the output should be exactly zero. However, due to **mismatches in the input differential pair transistors** (slight $V_{BE}$ differences, $\beta$ mismatch, or $R_C$ mismatch), a small differential input voltage is required to null the output.

$$V_{io} = \left. \frac{V_o}{A_d} \right|_{V_1 = V_2 = 0}$$

**Typical µA741 value:** $V_{io} \approx 1 \text{ mV}$ to $6 \text{ mV}$.

**(ii) Input Bias Current ($I_B$):**
The op-amp's input stage is a BJT differential amplifier, which requires a small DC base current to keep the transistors in the active region. $I_B$ is the **average** of the two base currents.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

**Typical µA741 value:** $I_B \approx 80 \text{ nA}$.

**(iii) Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$):**
Because the two input transistors are not perfectly matched, $I_{B1} \neq I_{B2}$. The difference is the offset current.

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

**Typical µA741 value:** $I_{io} \approx 5 \text{ nA}$ to $20 \text{ nA}$.

---

#### **(b)** An inverting amplifier uses $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp has $V_{io} = 2 \text{ mV}$, $I_{io} = 50 \text{ nA}$. Calculate the total output offset voltage if a bias-compensating resistor $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f$ is used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{10{,}000}{1000} = 11$$

**Step 2 — Offset due to $V_{io}$:**

$$V_{oo,1} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{io} = 11 \times 2 \text{ mV} = 22 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Offset due to $I_{io}$:**

$$V_{oo,2} = I_{io} \cdot R_f = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 \times 10^3 = 500 \text{ \mu V} = 0.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Total output offset (worst case, additive):**

$$V_{oo,\text{total}} = V_{oo,1} + V_{oo,2} = 22 \text{ mV} + 0.5 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oo,\text{total}} = 22.5 \text{ mV}}$$

**Note:** The compensating resistor $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f \approx 909 \text{ }\Omega$ **cancels the $I_B$ component**, which is why $I_B$ does not appear in the final expression.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### **(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a non-ideal op-amp taking both differential and common-mode gains into account. Hence define CMRR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Model Definition:**
A non-ideal op-amp output depends on two gain mechanisms:

$$V_o = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm} \cdot \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the inputs:**
Let $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$ and $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$. Then:

$$V_o = A_d V_{id} + A_{cm} V_{cm}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to highlight CMRR:**

$$V_o = A_d \left( V_{id} + \frac{V_{cm}}{\text{CMRR}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Definition of CMRR:**

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \rho = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- If $\text{CMRR} = \infty$, then $A_{cm} = 0$, and the op-amp amplifies only $V_{id}$.
- Finite CMRR causes **common-mode noise** (e.g., 50 Hz mains interference) to leak into the output — a critical concern in instrumentation amplifiers.

---

#### **(b)** An op-amp has open-loop gain $A_{d0} = 10^5$ and dominant pole at $f_1 = 10 \text{ Hz}$. If it is used in a non-inverting amplifier configuration with closed-loop gain of 50, determine: (i) the gain-bandwidth product, (ii) the new closed-loop bandwidth, (iii) the maximum peak-to-peak output swing at 50 kHz if the slew rate is 0.5 V/µs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$\text{GBW} = A_{d0} \times f_1 = 10^5 \times 10 = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Closed-Loop Bandwidth:**

$$f_{1,CL} = \frac{\text{GBW}}{A_{CL}} = \frac{10^6}{50} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Maximum Output Swing at 50 kHz:**

Since the operating frequency $f = 50 \text{ kHz} > f_{1,CL} = 20 \text{ kHz}$, the op-amp is in the roll-off region. However, the **slew rate** imposes a more restrictive limit for large signals.

From the slew rate condition:

$$2\pi f V_m \le \text{SR}$$

$$V_m = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi f} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_m = \frac{500{,}000}{314{,}159} \approx 1.59 \text{ V peak}$$

Therefore, peak-to-peak swing:

$$V_{pp} = 2 V_m = 2 \times 1.59 = 3.18 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{pp,\max} \approx 3.18 \text{ V at 50 kHz}}$$

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **CMRR** is the *ratio* $A_d / A_{cm}$; convert to dB using **$20 \log_{10}$**, not $10 \log_{10}$ (CMRR is a power gain ratio but expressed in voltage form, so the factor is 20).
- ⭐ **Slew Rate** is an **internal device parameter** — it does *not* depend on the feedback resistors. It is set by the bias current $I$ and compensating capacitor $C_c$ inside the IC.
- ⭐ **GBW is a constant** for a given op-amp. Doubling the closed-loop gain *halves* the bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Full-power bandwidth** $f_p$ relates slew rate to *maximum undistorted sine amplitude* — beyond $f_p$, the output is slew-limited and becomes a triangle wave.
- ⭐ **Input offset voltage $V_{io}$** gets amplified by the *closed-loop gain* to appear at the output — a major source of DC error in precision circuits.
- ⭐ **Bias-compensating resistor** $R_1' = R_1 \parallel R_f$ is used to cancel the $I_B$ component of output offset, leaving only the smaller $I_{io} \cdot R_f$ term.
- ⭐ For µA741, remember the **rule of thumb** values: $A_d \approx 2 \times 10^5$, $\text{CMRR} \approx 90 \text{ dB}$, $\text{SR} = 0.5 \text{ V/µs}$, $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$.
- ⭐ For KTU problems: always **state units**, always **show the formula before substitution**, and always mention the **assumption** ($A_d \gg A_{cm}$, $f_T \gg f_1$).
- ⭐ The **worse of** small-signal bandwidth and full-power bandwidth determines the *true* operating limit of the op-amp for a given output amplitude.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IC741 Op-Amp Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IC741 Op-Amp Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — IC 741 OP-AMP CHARACTERISTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is IC 741?

The **IC 741** is a general-purpose, internally frequency-compensated, monolithic **operational amplifier** built on a single silicon chip. It is the industry-standard workhorse op-amp first introduced by Fairchild Semiconductor (designed by David Fullagar in 1968) and remains the canonical "first op-amp" every electronics student analyzes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Module 3):**
> The IC 741 is an 8-pin DIP (Dual-Inline Package) operational amplifier containing 20 transistors, 11 resistors, and 1 capacitor on a single silicon die. It is internally compensated, has a class AB output stage, and operates from dual power supplies typically of **±15 V**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Precision Scale" 🧭

Imagine an op-amp as an **ultra-precise kitchen weighing scale** that compares the *difference* between two ingredients (voltages at the two inputs) and produces a massively amplified "imbalance" signal (output voltage).

- The **two input pans** of the scale are the **inverting (−)** and **non-inverting (+)** terminals.
- If both pans carry **equal weight** → output is ideally zero (perfect balance).
- A tiny mismatch in weight (microvolts) gets amplified to a usable output (volts).
- The **IC 741** is simply a manufactured, calibrated, mass-produced version of this perfect scale — but every real device has small imperfections (offsets, bias currents, finite speed), and Module 3 is the study of *those imperfections*.

### 1.3 Why Study the 741 Specifically?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although modern op-amps like TL081, LM358, or OPA227 exist, the **IC 741** is the **universal pedagogical reference**. Every KTU examiner assumes you know its pinout, internal block structure, and DC/AC parameters. The values for the 741 are the **canonical numbers** you will quote in viva and design problems.

### 1.4 Standard Pin Configuration (Top View of 8-pin DIP)

| Pin No. | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Offset Null | Used with a 10 kΩ pot to null input offset |
| 2 | Inverting Input (−) | Signal applied here is inverted at output |
| 3 | Non-Inverting Input (+) | Signal applied here retains phase at output |
| 4 | V− | Negative supply (typically **−15 V**) |
| 5 | Offset Null | Paired with pin 1 |
| 6 | Output | Amplified output signal |
| 7 | V+ | Positive supply (typically **+15 V**) |
| 8 | NC | No internal connection |

### 1.5 The Two Broad Families of Characteristics

The 741's datasheet specifications are classified into two families:

1. **DC Characteristics** — measured under slow/constant input conditions:
   - Input offset voltage $(V_{io})$
   - Input bias current $(I_B)$
   - Input offset current $(I_{io})$
   - Open-loop differential voltage gain $(A_{OL})$
   - Input resistance $(R_i)$, Output resistance $(R_o)$
   - Supply currents, Power consumption

2. **AC Characteristics** — measured under dynamic/sinusoidal conditions:
   - **CMRR** (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)
   - **Slew Rate** $(SR)$
   - **Gain-Bandwidth Product** $(GBW)$
   - Frequency response, Phase margin

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 exam problems, **DC characteristics** appear in numerical/derivation questions, while **AC characteristics** appear in frequency-domain and waveform-shaping problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Op-Amp "Imperfections"

A *perfect* op-amp would have:
- Infinite input impedance
- Zero output impedance
- Infinite open-loop gain
- Infinite CMRR
- Zero input offset voltage
- Zero input bias/offset currents
- Infinite bandwidth
- Infinite slew rate

The IC 741 has finite, well-quantified departures from these ideals. Mastering the formulas below is the difference between a 60-mark paper and a 90-mark paper.

---

## 2.2 DC Characteristics — Engineering Analysis

### 2.2.1 Input Offset Voltage $(V_{io})$
The differential DC voltage that must be applied between the two inputs to make the output exactly **0 V** (when both inputs are grounded).
- **Why it exists:** Internal transistor mismatches in the input differential pair.
- **Typical 741 value:** **1 mV to 5 mV** (datasheet value: 1 mV nominal).
- **Measurement condition:** $V_{io} = V_{o} / A_{OL}$ at room temp.

> [!NOTE]
> An ideal op-amp with *no* offset would output exactly 0 V when both inputs = 0 V. The 741 always outputs a small DC error proportional to $V_{io}$.

### 2.2.2 Input Bias Current $(I_B)$
The **average** of the two DC bias currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals:
$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$
- **Typical 741 value:** **45 nA to 500 nA** (datasheet: 80 nA at 25 °C).
- **Why it exists:** BJTs in the input stage require base bias currents.

### 2.2.3 Input Offset Current $(I_{io})$
The **difference** between the two input bias currents:
$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$
- **Typical 741 value:** **3 nA to 200 nA** (datasheet: 20 nA at 25 °C).
- **Engineering insight:** $I_{io}$ is a much smaller error than $I_B$ because it reflects *mismatch*, not magnitude.

### 2.2.4 Open-Loop Differential Voltage Gain $(A_{OL})$
The large-signal voltage gain of the op-amp with no feedback applied:
$$A_{OL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{io}} \bigg\vert_{R_L = 2\,\text{k}\Omega}$$
- **Typical 741 value:** **200,000** (often expressed as 106 dB).
- **Units:** Dimensionless voltage gain (V/V).

### 2.2.5 Input Resistance $(R_i)$ and Output Resistance $(R_o)$
- $R_i \approx$ **2 MΩ** (differential mode input resistance of 741).
- $R_o \approx$ **75 Ω** (output impedance of 741).

### 2.2.6 Supply Current and Power Dissipation
- Quiescent supply current: **1.7 mA** per supply.
- Total quiescent power: $P_D = 2 \times V_{CC} \times I_{supply} \approx$ **50 mW**.

---

## 2.3 AC Characteristics — Engineering Analysis

### 2.3.1 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
The ability of the op-amp to **reject signals common to both inputs**, expressed as the ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain:
$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$
Expressed in decibels:
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$$
- **Typical 741 value:** **90 dB** (i.e., $A_d / A_c \approx 31{,}623$).
- **Engineering meaning:** If a 1 V common-mode noise is applied, the differential output is reduced by a factor of ~31,623.

### 2.3.2 Slew Rate $(SR)$
The **maximum rate of change of output voltage** with respect to time under large-signal conditions:
$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max}$$
- **Typical 741 value:** **0.5 V/µs**.
- **Physical origin:** Limited by the internal compensation capacitor (30 pF) charging with a fixed bias current.
- **Critical consequence:** Limits the maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency:
$$f_{\max} = \frac{SR}{2 \pi V_{p}}$$
where $V_p$ is the peak output amplitude.

### 2.3.3 Gain-Bandwidth Product $(GBW)$
The product of the open-loop gain and the −3 dB frequency remains constant over the roll-off region:
$$GBW = A_{OL} \times f_b = \text{constant}$$
- **Typical 741 value:** **1 MHz**.
- **At $f = 10$ Hz**, gain ≈ 100,000; **At $f = 1$ kHz**, gain ≈ 1,000; **At $f = 1$ MHz**, gain = 1 (unity-gain bandwidth).

### 2.3.4 Full-Power Bandwidth
The maximum frequency at which the op-amp can deliver rated output without slew-rate limiting:
$$f_{FP} = \frac{SR}{2 \pi V_{o(\text{max})}}$$

### 2.3.5 Phase Margin
The amount of phase lag below 180° at the unity-gain crossover frequency.
- **Typical 741 value:** **63°** (internally compensated → critically damped response).

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **exam-day ammunition**. Memorize it before any 14-marker on op-amp characteristics.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Typical 741 Value | Unit |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Input offset voltage $V_{io}$ | $V_{io} = V_o / A_{OL}$ at $V_{in}=0$ | **1 mV** | V |
| 2 | Input bias current $I_B$ | $I_B = (I_{B1}+I_{B2})/2$ | **80 nA** | A |
| 3 | Input offset current $I_{io}$ | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1}-I_{B2}\vert$ | **20 nA** | A |
| 4 | Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ | $A_{OL} = V_{out}/V_{io}$ | **2 × 10⁵** (106 dB) | V/V |
| 5 | CMRR | $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ | **90 dB** | dB |
| 6 | Slew rate $SR$ | $dV_o/dt \big\vert_{\max}$ | **0.5 V/µs** | V/s |
| 7 | Max sine frequency | $f_{\max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$ | derived | Hz |
| 8 | GBW product | $A_{OL} \times f_{-3\text{dB}} = const$ | **1 MHz** | Hz |
| 9 | Input resistance $R_i$ | differential input impedance | **2 MΩ** | Ω |
| 10 | Output resistance $R_o$ | open-loop output impedance | **75 Ω** | Ω |
| 11 | Output offset due to $V_{io}$ | $V_{oo} = (1 + R_f/R_1) V_{io}$ | derived | V |
| 12 | Output offset due to $I_{io}$ | $V_{oo} = R_f \cdot I_{io}$ | derived | V |
| 13 | Total output offset | $(1 + R_f/R_1) V_{io} + R_f I_{io}$ | derived | V |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These "imperfections" of the 741 are not academic curiosities — they directly constrain real designs:

- **Audio pre-amplifiers:** Slew rate limits maximum clean amplitude at high frequencies → 741 is unsuitable for Hi-Fi (>20 kHz at large swing).
- **Precision instrumentation amplifiers:** Low $V_{io}$ is critical → designers upgrade to OP-07, OPA227.
- **Integrators and long-time-constant circuits:** $I_B$ causes output drift → 741's bipolar input stage is a limitation.
- **Sensor signal conditioning:** $R_i$ must be very high (e.g., for pH electrodes) → FET-input op-amps (LF356) preferred.
- **PCB layout (rail-to-rail designs):** $V_{io}$ drift with temperature (typical **5 µV/°C for 741**) matters in outdoor installations.

> [!NOTE]
> In every KTU viva, the examiner's follow-up after naming a parameter is invariably: *"What is the typical value for IC 741?"* — Memorize column 4 of the cheat-sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Offset Voltage due to Input Bias Current

We consider a standard inverting amplifier with $R_1$ (input resistor), $R_f$ (feedback resistor), and the non-inverting terminal grounded through a **bias-compensation resistor** $R_{comp}$.

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup

- Inverting input node: connected to $R_1$ and $R_f$.
- Non-inverting input node: connected to $R_{comp}$ to ground.
- Both bias currents $I_{B1}$ (into pin 2) and $I_{B2}$ (into pin 3) flow into the op-amp terminals.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (assume ideal virtual ground):**
The voltage at the inverting node is essentially 0 V (virtual ground). The current through $R_1$ is zero because the input source is grounded. Therefore the current through $R_f$ equals the input bias current $I_{B1}$:
$$I_{R_f} = I_{B1}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage developed at the output due to this current:**
$$V_{o1} = I_{B1} \cdot R_f$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the non-inverting node:**
The current through $R_{comp}$ must equal $I_{B2}$ (flowing into the op-amp). The voltage at the non-inverting node is:
$$V_+ = -I_{B2} \cdot R_{comp}$$

**Step 4 — This $V_+$ gets amplified by the noise gain $(1 + R_f/R_1)$ to the output:**
$$V_{o2} = V_+ \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = -I_{B2} \cdot R_{comp} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Total output voltage (only bias-current contribution):**
$$V_{oo(\text{bias})} = V_{o1} + V_{o2} = I_{B1} R_f - I_{B2} R_{comp} \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Choose $R_{comp}$ to minimize offset:**
The optimum choice is:
$$R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f = \frac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f}$$

Substituting $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$ gives the compensation resistor that makes the bias-current-induced offset equal to:
$$V_{oo(\text{bias})} = R_f \left( I_{B1} - I_{B2} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot \frac{R_1+R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

After careful algebra, this simplifies to:
$$V_{oo(\text{bias})} = R_f \cdot I_{io}$$

where $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ is the **input offset current**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** Even with $R_{comp}$ chosen optimally, the residual output offset is governed by the **offset current $I_{io}$**, not the bias current $I_B$. This is why 741 datasheets specify $I_{io}$ separately.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Output Offset Voltage (Combined Effect)

### 3.2.1 Contribution from $V_{io}$ alone

If the input bias currents were *zero*, the only offset source is $V_{io}$. The output offset due to $V_{io}$ is amplified by the **noise gain** $1 + R_f/R_1$:
$$V_{oo(V_{io})} = \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) \cdot V_{io}$$

### 3.2.2 Contribution from $I_{io}$ alone

As derived in §3.1, with $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$:
$$V_{oo(I_{io})} = R_f \cdot I_{io}$$

### 3.2.3 Worst-Case Total Output Offset Voltage
$$V_{oo(\text{total})} = \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) V_{io} + R_f \cdot I_{io}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two terms are **independent random variables** in the worst case. The first term is the *voltage* offset multiplied by the noise gain; the second is the *current* offset multiplied by the feedback resistance. They are added in magnitude for a conservative design.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** Inverting amplifier using IC 741 with $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{io} = 5\,\text{mV}$, $I_{io} = 100\,\text{nA}$. Find the worst-case total output offset.

**Step 1 — Compute noise gain:**
$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{100\,\text{k}}{1\,\text{k}} = 101$$

**Step 2 — Compute offset from $V_{io}$:**
$$V_{oo(V_{io})} = 101 \times 5\,\text{mV} = 0.505\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute offset from $I_{io}$:**
$$V_{oo(I_{io})} = 100\,\text{k}\Omega \times 100\,\text{nA} = 0.01\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Total output offset:**
$$V_{oo(\text{total})} = 0.505 + 0.01 = 0.515\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering comment:**
This 0.515 V offset is unacceptable for a precision circuit. The design action would be:
1. Insert a 10 kΩ pot between pins 1 and 5 of the 741 for **offset nulling**.
2. Add $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f \approx 990\,\Omega$ at the non-inverting terminal.
3. For high-precision applications, **replace 741 with OP-07** (which has $V_{io} = 25\,\mu\text{V}$, two orders of magnitude lower).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Slew Rate Limit for Sine Wave

For a sinusoidal output $V_o(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$:
$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

Maximum rate occurs when $\cos(\cdot) = 1$:
$$\left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

For distortion-free operation, this must be $\leq SR$:
$$2\pi f_{\max} V_p \leq SR$$

$$\boxed{f_{\max} = \frac{SR}{2 \pi V_p}}$$

**Numerical:** For 741, $SR = 0.5\,\text{V/µs} = 5 \times 10^5\,\text{V/s}$, $V_p = 10\,\text{V}$:
$$f_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 10} \approx 7.96\,\text{kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Above this frequency, the 741 output becomes a **triangular wave** (slew-limited) instead of a clean sine wave — a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: CMRR in dB

Given a 741 with differential-mode gain $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and common-mode gain $A_c = 6.32$ (typical):
$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{6.32} \approx 31{,}623$$
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(31{,}623) = 20 \times 4.5 = 90\,\text{dB} \;\checkmark$$

This matches the datasheet — internal consistency check passed.

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 741 vs. Other Op-Amps

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking **"Why is 741 not used in precision applications?"** — this table is the ready answer.

| Parameter | IC 741 (Bipolar) | OP-07 (Precision BJT) | LF356 (FET input) | LM358 (Single supply) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $V_{io}$ | 1 mV | **25 µV** | 5 mV | 2 mV |
| $I_B$ | 80 nA | 1 nA | **30 pA** | 45 nA |
| $I_{io}$ | 20 nA | 0.5 nA | 5 pA | 5 nA |
| Slew Rate | **0.5 V/µs** | 0.3 V/µs | 12 V/µs | 0.3 V/µs |
| GBW | 1 MHz | 0.6 MHz | **5 MHz** | 1 MHz |
| Input R | 2 MΩ | 60 MΩ | **10¹² Ω** | 10¹⁰ Ω |
| Best suited for | Teaching/Lab | Instrumentation | High-Z sensors | Battery circuits |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Architecture of IC 741

The 741 is built as a **two-stage amplifier with a class-AB output stage** and **active loads**. The functional flow is mapped below.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Pin 2 Inverting Input] -->|Q1 Q2| B[Input Diff Pair Q1 to Q4]
    C[Pin 3 Non Inverting Input] -->|Q3 Q4| B
    B -->|Q5 Q6 Active Load| D[High Gain Stage Q15 Q16 with Q12 Q13 Load]
    D -->|Q17 Q20 Driver| E[Class AB Output Stage Q14 Q20]
    E -->|Pin 6| F[Output to Load]
    G[Pin 4 V Negative] --> H[Bias Network Q8 Q9 Q10 Q11 Q12 Q13]
    I[Pin 7 V Positive] --> H
    H --> B
    H --> D
    H --> E
    J[Pin 1 and 5] -->|Offset Null Pot| D
```

**Reading the diagram:** Differential input pair (Q1–Q4) → Active-load gain stage (Q15, Q16) → Class-AB push-pull output (Q14, Q20). The bias network (Q8–Q13) supplies constant current to all stages. The internal 30 pF Miller capacitor (between pin of Q15 collector and Q16 base) provides **frequency compensation**.

---

## 4.2 Three-Stage Functional Architecture (Cleaner View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STAGE1 [Stage 1 Input Differential Amplifier]
        S1A[Emitter Coupled Pair Q1 Q2]
        S1B[Active Current Mirror Q3 Q4 as Load]
        S1A --> S1B
    end
    subgraph STAGE2 [Stage 2 High Gain CE Amplifier]
        S2A[Common Emitter Q15 with Active Load Q12 Q13]
        S2B[Internal 30 pF Miller Capacitor for Compensation]
        S2A --> S2B
    end
    subgraph STAGE3 [Stage 3 Class AB Push Pull Output]
        S3A[NPN Driver Q14]
        S3B[PNP Driver Q20 with Q18 Q19 Q24 Q25 Protection]
        S3A --> S3B
    end
    S1B --> S2A
    S2B --> S3A
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2 --> STAGE3
```

---

## 4.3 Op-Amp Behavior Model (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VIN[Input Signal Vi] --> AMP[Differential Amplifier Gain Ad]
    VCM[Common Mode Noise Vcm] --> AMP
    AMP --> OFF[Adder with Offset Vio and Bias Iio]
    OFF --> GAIN[Open Loop Gain AOL approx 2e5]
    GAIN --> BW[Frequency Response limited by internal 30 pF cap]
    BW --> SLEW[Slew Rate Limiter approx 0.5 V per microsecond]
    SLEW --> OUT[Output Vo]
    FB[Feedback Network Rf and R1] --> VIN
```

---

## 4.4 Inverting Amplifier with Offset-Compensation Resistor

```mermaid
graph LR
    VS[Input Source Vin] --> R1[R1 1 kilo ohm]
    R1 --> N[inverting node pin 2]
    N --> Rf[RF 100 kilo ohm]
    Rf --> OUT[Output Vo pin 6]
    GROUND1[Ground] --> RCOMP[Rcomp equals R1 parallel RF approx 990 ohm]
    RCOMP --> P[non inverting node pin 3]
    GROUND2[Ground] --> P
    P --> CHIP[IC 741 chip]
    N --> CHIP
    OUT --> CHIP
    VPOS[V plus 15 V] --> V7[Pin 7]
    VNEG[V minus 15 V] --> V4[Pin 4]
    POT[10 kilo ohm Pot between Pin 1 and Pin 5] --> NULL[Offset Null]
    NULL --> CHIP
```

**Function of $R_{comp}$:** It creates a voltage drop equal to the bias-current-induced voltage at the inverting node, *cancelling* the output offset. The 10 kΩ potentiometer between pins 1 and 5 trims out the $V_{io}$ contribution.

---

## 4.5 Frequency Response & Slew-Rate Limiting Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Sine Wave 10 kilo hertz 10 V peak] --> B[IC 741]
    B --> C{Is SR less than 2 pi f Vp}
    C -->|No SR 0.5 V per microsecond greater than 0.628 V per microsecond Wait corrected below| D[Clean Sine Output]
    C -->|Yes SR limiting active| E[Triangular Distorted Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision is: if $2\pi f V_p \leq SR$, output is clean. For 741 at $V_p = 10$ V, $f = 10$ kHz, $2\pi f V_p = 0.628$ V/µs > $SR = 0.5$ V/µs → **distorted (triangular) output**. This is a classic slew-rate demonstration.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define input offset voltage of an operational amplifier. Mention its typical value for IC 741.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The input offset voltage $(V_{io})$ of an op-amp is defined as the **small differential DC voltage** that must be applied between the two input terminals to make the output voltage exactly zero volts (with no input signal applied).
>
> It arises due to unavoidable mismatches between the transistors of the input differential stage.
>
> **Typical value for IC 741: $V_{io} \approx 1$ mV (range 1–5 mV).**
>
> *(Mark split: Definition 1 m, Cause 1 m, Typical value 1 m.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by slew rate? State its typical value for IC 741.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Slew rate $(SR)$ is the **maximum rate of change of output voltage** with respect to time under large-signal conditions, expressed as:
> $$SR = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max}$$
>
> It is caused by the limited ability of the internal compensation capacitor to charge and discharge.
>
> **Typical value for IC 741: $SR = 0.5$ V/µs.**
>
> *(Mark split: Definition 1 m, Formula 1 m, Typical value 1 m.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Format Reminder:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts, typically (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the following DC characteristics of IC 741 op-amp with typical values:
   (i) Input offset voltage  
   (ii) Input bias current  
   (iii) Input offset current  
   (iv) Open-loop voltage gain  
   (v) Input and output resistance.     **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the total output offset voltage of an inverting amplifier using IC 741, considering both $V_{io}$ and $I_{io}$. A non-inverting amplifier has $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{io} = 2\,\text{mV}$, $I_{io} = 50\,\text{nA}$. Calculate the total output offset voltage.     **(7 marks)**

---

### 📘 Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — DC Characteristics of IC 741 (7 marks)

**(i) Input offset voltage $(V_{io})$:** The differential DC input voltage required to make the output = 0 V when both inputs are grounded. Caused by transistor mismatches in the input differential pair. **Typical 741 value: 1 mV.** (1.5 marks)

**(ii) Input bias current $(I_B)$:** The average of the two DC currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals:

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

Caused by base bias currents of the input BJT stage. **Typical 741 value: 80 nA.** (1.5 marks)

**(iii) Input offset current $(I_{io})$:** The algebraic difference of the two input bias currents:

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

It represents the *mismatch* in bias currents. **Typical 741 value: 20 nA.** (1 mark)

**(iv) Open-loop voltage gain $(A_{OL})$:** The voltage gain of the op-amp with **no feedback** applied. It is the internal gain from the differential input to the output. **Typical 741 value: $2 \times 10^5$ (≈ 106 dB).** (1.5 marks)

**(v) Input and output resistance:**
- Input resistance $R_i$: the differential resistance seen at the two input terminals. **Typical 741 value: 2 MΩ.**
- Output resistance $R_o$: the Thevenin resistance at the output. **Typical 741 value: 75 Ω.** (1.5 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The total output offset voltage arises from two independent sources: $V_{io}$ and $I_{io}$.

**Step 1 — Output offset due to $V_{io}$ alone:**
Even with $V_{in} = 0$, the equivalent input $V_{io}$ is amplified by the **noise gain** $(1 + R_f / R_1)$ of the feedback network:
$$V_{oo(V_{io})} = \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) V_{io}$$

**Step 2 — Output offset due to $I_{io}$ alone (with $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$):**
By KCL at the inverting node, the offset current $I_{io}$ flows through $R_f$ producing:
$$V_{oo(I_{io})} = R_f \cdot I_{io}$$

**Step 3 — Total output offset voltage (worst-case sum):**
$$\boxed{V_{oo(\text{total})} = \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) V_{io} + R_f \cdot I_{io}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{io} = 2\,\text{mV}$, $I_{io} = 50\,\text{nA}$.

**Step 1 — Compute noise gain:**
$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{10}{1} = 11$$

**Step 2 — Compute offset due to $V_{io}$:**
$$V_{oo(V_{io})} = 11 \times 2\,\text{mV} = 22\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute offset due to $I_{io}$:**
$$V_{oo(I_{io})} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \times 50\,\text{nA} = 0.5\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Total output offset:**
$$V_{oo(\text{total})} = 22\,\text{mV} + 0.5\,\text{mV} = 22.5\,\text{mV}$$

**Answer: Total output offset voltage = 22.5 mV.**

---

### ✅ Question B — INTERNAL CHOICE (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Define and explain CMRR and Slew Rate of an op-amp. For a 741 op-amp, $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and $A_c = 6.32$. Find the CMRR in dB. Also, if the op-amp must deliver a sine wave of 10 V peak at 10 kHz, comment on whether the 741 can do it slew-rate-wise.     **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the internal block diagram of IC 741 op-amp with a neat functional block schematic. List the major functional blocks and state the role of the internal 30 pF compensation capacitor.     **(7 marks)**

---

### 📘 Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — CMRR, Slew Rate, and Numerical (7 marks)

**CMRR (2 marks):**
Common-Mode Rejection Ratio is the ability of the op-amp to **reject signals that are common to both input terminals**, while amplifying only the differential signal:
$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

In decibels:
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$$

**Slew Rate (2 marks):**
Slew Rate is the maximum rate of change of output voltage per unit time, limited by the internal compensation capacitor:
$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert_{\max} \quad \text{(V/s or V/µs)}$$

**Numerical — CMRR in dB (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{2 \times 10^5}{6.32} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(31{,}646) \approx 90\,\text{dB}$$

**Slew-rate feasibility check (1.5 marks):**
$$2\pi f V_p = 2 \pi \times 10{,}000 \times 10 = 6.28 \times 10^5\,\text{V/s} = 0.628\,\text{V/µs}$$

Since $SR_{741} = 0.5\,\text{V/µs} < 0.628\,\text{V/µs}$, the **741 cannot deliver a clean 10 V peak sine wave at 10 kHz**. The output will be slew-rate limited (triangular distortion). The maximum frequency at $V_p = 10$ V is:
$$f_{\max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} \approx 7.96\,\text{kHz}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Internal Block Diagram of IC 741 (7 marks)

The IC 741 consists of **four major functional blocks** (5 marks):

**1. Input Differential Stage** (Transistors Q1–Q6):
- Emitter-coupled pair (Q1, Q2) provides differential amplification.
- Q3, Q4 form a current-mirror active load that converts differential to single-ended output with high gain.
- This stage provides most of the differential gain and high input resistance.

**2. High-Gain Second Stage** (Transistors Q15, Q16, Q12, Q13):
- A common-emitter amplifier (Q15) with active load (Q12, Q13).
- Provides the bulk of the voltage gain (≈ 60–70 dB).
- The 30 pF capacitor is connected in this stage as a **Miller integrator** (between Q15 collector and Q16 base).

**3. Class-AB Output Stage** (Q14, Q20 with biasing Q18, Q19, Q24, Q25):
- Push-pull complementary pair provides low output impedance and bidirectional current drive.
- Class-AB bias eliminates crossover distortion.
- Q15, Q24, Q25 form short-circuit protection limiting the output current to ≈ 25 mA.

**4. Bias Network** (Q8, Q9, Q10, Q11, Q12, Q13):
- Generates stable reference currents for all stages using a current-mirror architecture.
- The Widlar current source establishes the bias currents of the input and gain stages.

**Role of the 30 pF compensation capacitor (2 marks):**
The internal 30 pF Miller capacitor (around the high-gain second stage) introduces a **dominant pole** in the open-loop frequency response at approximately 5 Hz. This:
- Ensures **single-pole roll-off** at −20 dB/decade.
- Provides **phase margin ≈ 63°** at the unity-gain crossover.
- Prevents **oscillation** when the op-amp is used in unity-gain (voltage follower) configuration.
- Trade-off: it limits the slew rate to 0.5 V/µs and the GBW to 1 MHz.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students LOSE MARKS in op-amp characteristics questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_B$ and $I_{io}$:** $I_B$ is the *average*; $I_{io}$ is the *difference*. Wrong formula = 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting the noise-gain factor $(1 + R_f/R_1)$:** Many students write $V_{oo} = V_{io}$ instead of $(1+R_f/R_1) V_{io}$. Lose 1 mark immediately.
> 3. **CMRR units:** Always quote **dB**, not the dimensionless ratio. Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Slew rate vs. bandwidth:** Students mix up $f_{\max}$ (slew-rate limited) and the −3 dB bandwidth. They are different concepts.
> 5. **No offset-null design shown:** In design problems, examiners expect you to mention $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$ at the non-inverting terminal. Skipping it = lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong sign in KCL:** The output offset due to $I_B$ depends on the direction of current flow. Draw the actual current arrows.
> 7. **Not quoting typical values:** Always end with "Typical value for IC 741 is ___". This is a 0.5–1 mark item in almost every short answer.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before you walk into the exam hall, ensure every box below is ticked:**

- [ ] IC 741 has **8 pins** in DIP package; pin 4 = V−, pin 7 = V+, pin 6 = output, pins 1 & 5 = offset null, pin 2 = inverting input, pin 3 = non-inverting input.
- [ ] **$V_{io}$** ≈ **1 mV** for 741 — caused by BJT mismatch.
- [ ] **$I_B$** ≈ **80 nA**; **$I_{io}$** ≈ **20 nA** for 741.
- [ ] **$A_{OL}$** ≈ **2 × 10⁵** (106 dB) for 741.
- [ ] **CMRR** ≈ **90 dB** for 741.
- [ ] **Slew Rate** ≈ **0.5 V/µs** for 741.
- [ ] **GBW** ≈ **1 MHz** for 741.
- [ ] **$R_i$** ≈ **2 MΩ**; **$R_o$** ≈ **75 Ω** for 741.
- [ ] Output offset due to $V_{io}$ is amplified by **noise gain** $(1 + R_f/R_1)$, NOT by signal gain.
- [ ] Adding $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$ at the non-inverting input cancels bias-current offsets down to $R_f \cdot I_{io}$.
- [ ] Total output offset: $V_{oo} = (1 + R_f/R_1) V_{io} + R_f I_{io}$.
- [ ] For a sine output, $f_{\max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$ — beyond this, output becomes a triangular wave.
- [ ] 741 has a **two-stage gain path** (input diff + CE gain) feeding a **class-AB output** with **active current-mirror biasing**.
- [ ] The **30 pF internal capacitor** provides dominant-pole compensation; it is the reason SR is limited.
- [ ] 741 is **NOT suitable** for precision, high-frequency, or high-input-impedance applications — use OP-07, LF356, or OPA-series instead.
- [ ] Internal block has 20 transistors, 11 resistors, 1 capacitor.

> 🎯 **Last-word KTU mantra:** *"Memorize the 8 pins, the 5 typical values, and the 3 master formulas. The rest is engineering judgment."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Op-Amp Circuits: Inverting Amplifier, Non-Inverting Amplifier, Summing Amplifier, Integrator, Difference Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
