> - **Topic:** Simple Op-Amp Circuits: Inverting Amplifier, Non-Inverting Amplifier, Summing Amplifier, Integrator, Difference Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — Foundation Recap

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs (V⁺ and V⁻) and a single-ended output. In KTU 2024 analog electronics, the op-amp is always treated as an **ideal op-amp** for analytical derivations, satisfying:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDEAL OP-AMP ASSUMPTIONS (The Four Golden Rules)**
> 1. **Infinite Open-Loop Gain** ($A_{OL} \to \infty$)
> 2. **Infinite Input Impedance** ($Z_{in} \to \infty$) → **Input current = 0**
> 3. **Zero Output Impedance** ($Z_{out} = 0$)
> 4. **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** & **Zero Output when $V^+ = V^-$** (Virtual Short)

For any **negative feedback** (NFB) configuration — which is the case for ALL circuits in this module — the **virtual short** principle states:

$$V^+ = V^-$$

Combined with Rule 2, this gives us the **virtual ground** concept when the non-inverting terminal is grounded.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Op-Amp as a "Servant with Infinite Eagerness"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of an op-amp as a *very obedient servant* whose **only life goal** is to keep the voltage difference between its two input terminals ($V_d = V^+ - V^-$) at **exactly zero**. He achieves this by manipulating the output voltage $V_{out}$ as powerfully as needed. The **negative feedback** path (resistor/capacitor connecting output back to the inverting input) is the "leash" through which the servant learns what the user wants.

This servant analogy perfectly explains:
- **Why input current is zero** → The servant never draws anything from the source.
- **Why virtual ground exists** → He forces $V^- = V^+$ (which is 0 V when $V^+ = GND$).
- **Why the gain depends on external resistors** → The leash (feedback network) tells him *how strongly* to react.

---

## 1.3 Circuit-by-Circuit Intuitive Overview

| Circuit | Plain English Description | Key Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | "Servant flips the signal upside down and shrinks/expands it" | Output is 180° out of phase, scaled by $-R_f/R_{in}$ |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | "Servant copies the signal without flipping, only amplifies" | Output is in phase, scaled by $(1 + R_f/R_{in})$ |
| **Summing Amplifier** | "Servant adds up several inputs, each weighted" | Output = weighted sum of inputs |
| **Integrator** | "Servant accumulates input over time (like a water tank)" | Output = integral of input (with scaling) |
| **Difference Amplifier** | "Servant subtracts one input from another, then amplifies" | Output = difference of two inputs (scaled) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 EXAM FOCUS:** The first four circuits use the **virtual ground** concept ($V^- = 0$ V). The **Difference Amplifier** is the only one in this module where $V^- \neq 0$ — both inputs are live signals, making it the **most analytically challenging** circuit in Module 3.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following KTU standard values are typically assumed unless otherwise stated:
- **Open-loop gain $A_{OL} = \infty$** (ideal)
- **Supply voltages $V_{CC} = +15\text{ V}$, $V_{EE} = -15\text{ V}$** (standard lab supply)
- **Saturation voltage $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\text{ V}$** (a common simplification, leaving 2 V headroom)
- **Feedback resistance range:** Typically $1\text{ k}\Omega$ to $1\text{ M}\Omega$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Analysis Method (KTU Gold Standard)

For every closed-loop op-amp circuit, apply this **4-step KTU examiner-approved procedure**:

1. **Assume ideal op-amp** → Apply the four golden rules.
2. **Apply KCL at the inverting node** ($V^-$): The algebraic sum of currents entering = 0.
3. **Apply Virtual Short** ($V^+ = V^-$) to express node voltages.
4. **Solve for $V_{out}$** as a function of input(s) and passive component values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** In negative feedback, the op-amp operates in its **linear region**, where Rule 1 ($A_{OL} \to \infty$) combined with finite $V_{out}$ forces $V_d = V_{out}/A_{OL} \to 0$. This is mathematically rigorous and forms the basis of every derivation below.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS FOR KTU EXAMS**
> The table below contains every equation you need to solve 3-mark and 14-mark questions on this topic.

| # | Circuit | Closed-Loop Gain $\left(A_{CL}\right)$ | Output Voltage $V_{out}$ | Key Constraint | Phase Relationship |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$ | $V^- = 0$ (Virtual Ground) | 180° Phase Inversion |
| 2 | **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) V_{in}$ | $V^- = V_{in}$ | 0° (In Phase) |
| 3 | **Summing Amplifier** (Inverting) | $A_{v_i} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ (for i-th input) | $V_{out} = -R_f \left(\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$ | $V^- = 0$ | 180° |
| 4 | **Integrator** (Ideal) | $A_{CL}(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sR_{in}C_f}$ | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_{in}C_f} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)$ | DC gain $\to \infty$ | 180° |
| 5 | **Difference Amplifier** | $A_{CL} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ (balanced) | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Requires $R_f/R_g = R_2/R_1$ for CMRR | Depends on input polarity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SYMBOL CONVENTION USED IN THIS MODULE:**
> - $R_f$ = **Feedback resistor** (always connected from output to $V^-$)
> - $R_{in}$ = **Input resistor** (in series with input source feeding $V^-$)
> - $C_f$ = **Feedback capacitor** (in integrator, replaces $R_f$)
> - $R_1, R_2, R_g, R_f$ for difference amplifier (defined in §2.6)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These five circuits form the **building blocks of ALL modern analog signal processing**:
> - **Inverting Amplifier** → Active filters, current-to-voltage conversion (with $R_{in}$ as photodiode load)
> - **Non-Inverting Amplifier** → Buffering high-impedance sensors, audio pre-amplifiers
> - **Summing Amplifier** → Audio mixers, digital-to-analog converter (DAC) output stages, weighted average computation
> - **Integrator** → Analog computers, ramp generators, dual-slope ADCs, PID controllers (I-term)
> - **Difference Amplifier** → Instrumentation front-end (ECG/EEG), bridge sensor amplification (strain gauges, thermocouples)

---

## 2.4 Inverting Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

**Topology:** Input signal $V_{in}$ connects through $R_{in}$ to the inverting node ($V^-$). Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output to $V^-$. The non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) is grounded.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Since $V^+ = 0$ (grounded) and $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short), $\Rightarrow V^- = 0$ (**Virtual Ground**)
- Since $Z_{in} = \infty$, no current flows into op-amp terminals
- All current through $R_{in}$ must flow through $R_f$ (KCL at $V^-$)
- This current develops a voltage across $R_f$ that determines $V_{out}$

**Input impedance** of inverting amplifier = $R_{in}$ (relatively low — a known limitation).

---

## 2.5 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

**Topology:** Input signal $V_{in}$ connects directly to the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$). $R_{in}$ connects from $V^-$ to ground. $R_f$ provides feedback from output to $V^-$.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $V^+ = V_{in}$ (direct connection)
- Virtual short: $V^- = V_{in}$ (NOT grounded!)
- Voltage divider at $V^-$: $V^- = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_f}$
- Solving: $V_{out} = V_{in} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right)$

**Key advantage:** Input impedance is **infinite** (because $V_{in}$ drives $V^+$ directly). This is the **buffering** property.

---

## 2.6 Difference Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

**Topology (Most complex in this module):**
- $V_1$ connects through $R_1$ to $V^-$
- $V_2$ connects directly to $V^+$
- $R_2$ connects from $V^-$ to output
- $R_g$ (or $R_3$) connects from $V^+$ to ground

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Critical KCL Equations:**
> At $V^-$ (virtual short forces $V^- = V_2$):
> $$\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_1} = \frac{V_2 - V_{out}}{R_2}$$
> At $V^+$ (voltage divider):
> $$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_g}{R_g + R_4} \quad \text{(where } R_4 \text{ may be absent if } V_2 \text{ connects directly)}$$

**The "Balanced" Condition (Maximum CMRR):**
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$
When balanced, $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$.

---

## 2.7 Summing Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

**Topology:** A generalization of the inverting amplifier. Multiple input branches ($V_1$ via $R_1$, $V_2$ via $R_2$, ..., $V_n$ via $R_n$) all connect to the same $V^-$ node. $R_f$ provides feedback.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $V^- = 0$ (virtual ground)
- Current from each input: $I_i = V_i / R_i$
- KCL: $\sum I_i + I_f = 0$, where $I_f = (0 - V_{out})/R_f$
- Therefore: $V_{out} = -R_f \left(\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$

**Special case — Audio Mixer:** If all $R_i$ are equal, output is a simple **scaled sum** of all inputs.

---

## 2.8 Integrator — Detailed Analysis

**Topology:** Identical to inverting amplifier, but $R_f$ is **replaced by capacitor $C_f$**. This is the ONLY circuit in this module with a frequency-dependent element in feedback.

**Key insight:** Capacitor impedance $Z_C = 1/(sC_f)$ replaces $R_f$ in the gain formula.

**In s-domain (Laplace):**
$$V_{out}(s) = -\frac{1}{sR_{in}C_f} V_{in}(s)$$

**In time-domain (inverse Laplace):**
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_{in}C_f} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Limitation:** The **ideal integrator** has infinite DC gain, which causes **saturation drift** in real circuits. The **practical (lossy) integrator** adds a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C_f$ to limit DC gain to $-R_f/R_{in}$. KTU may ask you to draw or analyze BOTH versions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DERIVATION POLICY:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Multi-line equations use the `aligned` environment.

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Inverting Amplifier Gain

**Given:** $V_{in}$ → $R_{in}$ → $V^-$; $V_{out}$ → $R_f$ → $V^-$; $V^+ = 0$ V (grounded).

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
Since $V^+ = 0$ and $V^+ = V^-$, therefore:
$$V^- = 0 \text{ V} \quad \text{(VIRTUAL GROUND)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the $V^-$ node:**
Sum of currents leaving the node = 0.
$$I_{R_{in}} + I_{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express each current using Ohm's Law:**

The current flowing from $V_{in}$ to $V^-$ through $R_{in}$:
$$I_{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_{in}}$$

The current flowing from $V^-$ to $V_{out}$ through $R_f$:
$$I_{R_f} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into KCL equation:**

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_{in}} + \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V^- = 0$ (Virtual Ground):**

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} + \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$$

**Step 7 — Identify Closed-Loop Gain:**

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

**Verification with sign:** The negative sign confirms the 180° phase inversion. If $V_{in} = +1$ V, $R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{in} = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, then $V_{out} = -10$ V. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain

**Given:** $V_{in}$ → $V^+$ (direct); $V^-$ → $R_{in}$ → GND; $V_{out}$ → $R_f$ → $V^-$.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at $V^-$:**
The node $V^-$ has two connections: to ground via $R_{in}$, and to $V_{out}$ via $R_f$. No current enters the op-amp.

$$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_{in}} + \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V^- = V_{in}$:**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} + \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Multiply through by $R_{in} R_f$:**

$$V_{in} R_f + (V_{in} - V_{out}) R_{in} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand:**

$$V_{in} R_f + V_{in} R_{in} - V_{out} R_{in} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out} R_{in} = V_{in} R_f + V_{in} R_{in}$$

$$V_{out} R_{in} = V_{in} (R_f + R_{in})$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) V_{in}}$$

**Step 7 — Identify Gain:**

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \quad \text{(Always positive and} \geq 1\text{)}}$$

**Limiting case:** If $R_f = 0$ (direct wire) and $R_{in} = \infty$ (open), then $A_{CL} = 1$ → **Voltage Follower / Unity Gain Buffer**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Summing Amplifier (3 inputs)

**Given:** $V_1$ via $R_1$, $V_2$ via $R_2$, $V_3$ via $R_3$ → all to $V^-$; $V_{out}$ via $R_f$ → $V^-$; $V^+ = 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at $V^-$ (sum of currents INTO node = 0):**

$$\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $V_{out}$:**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = -\left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $R_f$:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)}$$

**Special Case — Equal Resistors:** If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$:
$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R}(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Ideal Integrator (Time Domain)

**Given:** $V_{in}$ → $R_{in}$ → $V^-$; Output → $C_f$ → $V^-$; $V^+ = 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:** $V^- = 0$ V.

**Step 2 — KCL at $V^-$:**
Current through $R_{in}$ = Current into capacitor $C_f$.

$$i_{R_{in}}(t) = i_{C_f}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Express each current:**
Current through resistor (Ohm's law):
$$i_{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

Current into capacitor (fundamental capacitor equation, where $V_C$ is voltage across capacitor with $V^-$ side as reference):

Since $V^- = 0$ and the other terminal of $C_f$ is $V_{out}$, the voltage across $C_f$ is $(0 - V_{out}) = -V_{out}$.

$$i_{C_f} = C_f \frac{d(V_C)}{dt} = C_f \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the currents:**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = -C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $dV_{out}$:**

$$dV_{out} = -\frac{1}{R_{in} C_f} V_{in} \, dt$$

**Step 6 — Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$:**

$$V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = -\frac{1}{R_{in} C_f} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 7 — Final time-domain equation:**

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_{in} C_f} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)}$$

**Frequency-domain equivalent (s-plane):**
$$V_{out}(s) = -\frac{1}{sR_{in}C_f} V_{in}(s)$$

**Numerical example:** If $V_{in} = 1$ V (DC), $R_{in} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_f = 1\mu\text{F}$:
- $R_{in}C_f = 10\text{ ms}$
- $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{10\text{ ms}} \cdot t = -100 \cdot t$ (V) — a perfect negative-going ramp.

---

## 3.5 Derivation #5: Difference Amplifier (Most Analytically Intensive)

**Given:** 
- $V_1$ → $R_1$ → $V^-$; Output → $R_2$ → $V^-$
- $V_2$ → $R_3$ → $V^+$; $R_4$ → $V^+$ → GND

> [!NOTE]
> We will use $R_3$ and $R_4$ as the input and ground resistors at the non-inverting terminal. KTU textbooks sometimes use $R_g$ and $R_f$ — the topology is identical.

**Step 1 — Find $V^+$ using Voltage Divider:**
Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp's $V^+$ terminal, $R_3$ and $R_4$ form a simple voltage divider:

$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short:**
$$V^- = V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at $V^-$:**
Current from $V_1$ to $V^-$ via $R_1$ = Current from $V^-$ to $V_{out}$ via $R_2$:

$$\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$V^- - V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_1 - V^-)$$

$$V_{out} = V^- - \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_1 - V^-)$$

$$V_{out} = V^- \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

$$V_{out} = V^- \left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V^- = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$:**

$$V_{out} = \left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 6 — Apply the BALANCED Condition (KTU Standard Configuration):**

For the difference amplifier to **perfectly reject common-mode signals**, the resistor ratios must match:
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

This implies $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$. Under this condition:

$$\frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = \frac{R_2/R_1}{1 + R_2/R_1} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back:**

$$V_{out} = \left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)}$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}}$$

**Numerical example:** If $V_1 = 2$ V, $V_2 = 3$ V, $R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_4 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:
- $V_{out} = 10 \times (3 - 2) = 10$ V

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Inverting | Non-Inverting | Summing | Integrator | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$V^+$ terminal** | Grounded (0 V) | $V_{in}$ | Grounded | Grounded | $V_2$ (via divider) |
| **$V^-$ terminal** | Virtual ground (0 V) | $V_{in}$ (not ground!) | Virtual ground (0 V) | Virtual ground (0 V) | Depends on $V_1$ and $V_2$ |
| **Input Impedance** | $R_{in}$ (low) | **Very high (∞)** | $R_i$ (varies per input) | $R_{in}$ | $R_1$ (at $V_1$ side) |
| **Phase** | 180° | 0° | 180° | 180° | Depends |
| **Min Gain Magnitude** | 0 (if $R_f=0$) | **1** (always) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Frequency Dependent?** | No | No | No | **YES** ($C_f$ in feedback) | No |
| **Common-Mode Rejection** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | **Critical (CMRR)** |
| **Saturated with DC?** | No | No | No | **YES (drift issue)** | No |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use Mermaid syntax. Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `ampOp`) and labels are clean alphanumeric text (no bold/italic inside double-quoted labels).

---

## 4.1 Inverting Amplifier — Block-Level Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    source[V IN AC Source] --> rin[R IN Resistor]
    rin --> nodeINV[Inverting Node V NEG]
    nodeINV --> opampAmp[Op-Amp Ideal A OL equals Infinity]
    opampAmp --> outputNode[V OUT Output]
    outputNode --> rf[R F Resistor]
    rf --> nodeINV
    groundNode[GND 0 V] --> opampNonInv[Non-Inverting Terminal V POS]
    opampNonInv --> nodeINV
    groundNode2[GND] --> source
```

**Key Visual Mapping:**
- `V IN` feeds the inverting node through $R_{IN}$
- The op-amp "block" sits between the two input nodes and the output
- $R_F$ creates the feedback loop from output back to inverting node
- $V^+$ is **anchored to ground** (0 V) — this is what creates the **virtual ground** at $V^-$

---

## 4.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Block-Level Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    source[V IN] --> nonInvNode[Non-Inverting Terminal V POS]
    nonInvNode --> opampAmp2[Op-Amp]
    opampAmp2 --> outNode[V OUT]
    outNode --> rf2[R F Feedback Resistor]
    rf2 --> invNode[Inverting Node V NEG]
    invNode --> rin2[R IN Resistor]
    rinNode2[GND] --> rin2
    nonInvNode -. Virtual Short .-> invNode
```

**Key Visual Mapping:**
- $V_{IN}$ goes **directly** to $V^+$ (no series resistor at input)
- $R_{IN}$ goes from $V^-$ to ground (acts as the "lower half" of voltage divider)
- $R_F$ goes from $V_{out}$ to $V^-$ (the "upper half" of voltage divider)
- Virtual short is the dashed line between $V^+$ and $V^-$

---

## 4.3 Summing Amplifier (3-Input) — Block-Level Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1src[V 1] --> r1[R 1]
    v2src[V 2] --> r2[R 2]
    v3src[V 3] --> r3[R 3]
    r1 --> sumNode[Virtual Ground Node V NEG]
    r2 --> sumNode
    r3 --> sumNode
    sumNode --> opampSum[Op-Amp]
    opampSum --> outSum[V OUT]
    outSum --> rfSum[R F Feedback]
    rfSum --> sumNode
    gndSum[GND] --> nonInvSum[Non-Inverting Terminal]
    nonInvSum -. Virtual Short .-> sumNode
```

---

## 4.4 Integrator — Block-Level Signal Flow (Highlighted Capacitor)

```mermaid
graph LR
    vInSrc[V IN] --> rInInt[R IN]
    rInInt --> intNode[Virtual Ground V NEG]
    intNode --> opampInt[Op-Amp]
    opampInt --> vOutInt[V OUT]
    vOutInt --> cfInt[C F Capacitor]
    cfInt --> intNode
    gndInt[GND] --> nonInvInt[Non-Inverting Terminal]
    nonInvInt -. Virtual Short .-> intNode
    opampInt -. Provides Integration .-> cfInt
```

**Critical Visual Cue:** The capacitor $C_F$ is the only dynamic (frequency-dependent) element in this module's circuits. It "stores charge" proportional to the integral of current.

---

## 4.5 Difference Amplifier — Block-Level Signal Flow (Most Complex)

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1Diff[V 1 Input] --> r1Diff[R 1]
    r1Diff --> invDiff[Inverting Node V NEG]
    v2Diff[V 2 Input] --> nonInvDiff[Non-Inverting Terminal V POS]
    invDiff --> opampDiff[Op-Amp]
    opampDiff --> outDiff[V OUT]
    outDiff --> r2Diff[R 2]
    r2Diff --> invDiff
    r3Diff[R 3] --> nonInvDiff
    nonInvDiff --> r4Diff[R 4]
    r4Diff --> gndDiff[GND]
    nonInvDiff -. Virtual Short Equals .-> invDiff
```

**Critical Visual Cue:** Note that $V_1$ enters through a resistor $R_1$, while $V_2$ enters directly. The presence of $R_3$ and $R_4$ at the non-inverting side creates the **voltage divider** that enables the subtraction function.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ASSESSMENT STRUCTURE (As per University Regulations):**
> - **Part A:** Short answer questions (3 marks each) — typically 4-5 questions
> - **Part B:** Long answer questions with **internal choice** (14 marks each, split into 7+7 sub-parts)
> - All questions test **Course Outcomes (CO3 typically for this module)** and follow **Bloom's Taxonomy Levels 2-4** (Understand, Apply, Analyze).

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the concept of virtual ground in an inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp. Why is it called "virtual"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In an inverting amplifier, the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) is connected to ground (0 V). The negative feedback forces the op-amp output to adjust such that the voltage difference $V_d = V^+ - V^- \to 0$. Since the op-amp is ideal with infinite open-loop gain, the only way to maintain finite output voltage is for $V^- = V^+ = 0$ V.

This node ($V^-$) is at **0 V potential** like ground, but is **not physically connected to ground**. Hence it is called a **"virtual ground"** — virtually (effectively) at ground potential, but not actually grounded. This concept is the foundation for KCL analysis in all inverting-mode op-amp circuits.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Explanation of mechanism (1M) + Justification of "virtual" terminology (1M).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare the input impedance of inverting and non-inverting amplifier configurations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Configuration | Input Impedance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | **$Z_{in} = R_{in}$** (low) | The input signal sees $R_{in}$ connected from $V_{in}$ to the virtual ground at $V^-$. |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | **$Z_{in} \to \infty$ (very high)** | The input signal connects directly to $V^+$, which is the gate of an ideal op-amp with infinite input impedance. |

**Conclusion (1M):** The non-inverting configuration is preferred for **buffering high-impedance sources** (e.g., sensors) because it does not load the source.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Set A]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the closed-loop gain of an inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp. If $R_{in} = 2\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, calculate the output voltage for $V_{in} = 0.5$ V.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Analysis Setup (1 mark)**
- Input $V_{in}$ → $R_{in}$ → $V^-$ node
- Output $V_{out}$ → $R_f$ → $V^-$ node (feedback)
- $V^+$ = 0 V (grounded)

**Step 2: Apply Virtual Short (1 mark)**
$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V (Virtual Ground)}$$

**Step 3: KCL at $V^-$ node (1 mark)**
$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 4: Derive gain formula (2 marks)**
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical calculation (2 marks)**
$$A_{CL} = -\frac{20\text{ k}\Omega}{2\text{ k}\Omega} = -10$$
$$V_{out} = -10 \times 0.5 \text{ V} = -5 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} = -5$ V (with 180° phase inversion).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a non-inverting amplifier. Derive the expression for its closed-loop voltage gain. State ONE advantage over the inverting amplifier.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Diagram (2 marks)**
[Refer to Mermaid diagram in §4.2. Draw: $V_{in}$ → $V^+$, $R_{in}$ from $V^-$ to ground, $R_f$ from $V_{out}$ to $V^-$]

**Step 2: Working Principle (1 mark)**
The input $V_{in}$ is applied directly to $V^+$. The negative feedback network ($R_f$ and $R_{in}$) forms a voltage divider that samples a fraction of $V_{out}$ and feeds it back to $V^-$. The op-amp adjusts $V_{out}$ until $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$ (virtual short).

**Step 3: Derivation (3 marks)**
$$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_f} \quad \text{(Voltage divider)}$$
By virtual short, $V^- = V_{in}$:
$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_f}$$
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left(\frac{R_{in} + R_f}{R_{in}}\right) = V_{in}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right)$$
$$\boxed{A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

**Step 4: Advantage (1 mark)**
The non-inverting amplifier has **infinite (very high) input impedance** because $V_{in}$ drives the non-inverting terminal directly. This prevents loading of the input source, making it ideal for **buffering applications** and **sensor signal conditioning**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Set B]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the output voltage expression for a 3-input inverting summing amplifier. Given $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 30\text{ k}\Omega$, and inputs $V_1 = 1$ V, $V_2 = 2$ V, $V_3 = -3$ V, calculate the output voltage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Topology and Virtual Ground (1 mark)**
All three inputs connect through their respective resistors to $V^-$ node. Since $V^+ = 0$, the virtual short gives $V^- = 0$ V.

**Step 2: KCL at $V^-$ (2 marks)**
$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3: Derivation (2 marks)**
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

**Step 4: Numerical Calculation (2 marks)**
Substituting values:
$$V_{out} = -30\text{k} \left(\frac{1}{10\text{k}} + \frac{2}{10\text{k}} + \frac{-3}{10\text{k}}\right)$$
$$V_{out} = -30\text{k} \cdot \frac{0}{10\text{k}} = -30 \times 0 = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} = 0$ V (the sum $V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 0$, so the weighted sum is also zero).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of an op-amp integrator. Derive the expression for output voltage in time domain. What is the practical limitation of an ideal integrator?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit and Working (2 marks)**
[Refer to §4.4 diagram.] The integrator is similar to an inverting amplifier, but the feedback resistor is **replaced by a capacitor $C_f$**. The virtual ground at $V^-$ ensures that the input current $i = V_{in}/R_{in}$ flows entirely into the capacitor. The capacitor accumulates charge, producing a voltage that is the **time integral** of the input current.

**Step 2: Derivation (3 marks)**
Using virtual ground ($V^- = 0$):
- Current through $R_{in}$: $i_R = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$
- Current into capacitor: $i_C = -C_f \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}$ (since $V_C = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$)
- KCL: $i_R = i_C$
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = -C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$
Integrating:
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_{in}C_f} \int_0^t V_{in}(\tau) d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

**Step 3: Practical Limitation (2 marks)**
The ideal integrator has **infinite DC gain** (since $\frac{1}{j\omega R_{in}C_f} \to \infty$ as $\omega \to 0$). This means even tiny DC offsets or input bias currents cause $V_{out}$ to **saturate** to the supply rail. The **practical (lossy) integrator** solves this by adding a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C_f$, which limits the DC gain to $-R_f/R_{in}$ and prevents saturation drift.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
> 
> **Mistake 1: Forgetting the negative sign in inverting amplifier**
> Students often write $A_{CL} = R_f/R_{in}$ without the minus sign. **Always include the negative sign** — it represents the 180° phase inversion, which is the **defining characteristic** of the inverting configuration. (Lose 1-2 marks)
> 
> **Mistake 2: Applying virtual ground to the non-inverting amplifier**
> In the non-inverting amplifier, $V^- = V_{in}$, **NOT 0 V**. This is the #1 conceptual error. (Lose 2-3 marks)
> 
> **Mistake 3: Skipping the initial condition $V_{out}(0)$ in integrator derivations**
> The full integrator equation includes $V_{out}(0)$, the capacitor's initial voltage. Forgetting it costs 1 mark.
> 
> **Mistake 4: Not stating the balanced condition for difference amplifier**
> Simply writing $V_{out} = (R_2/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ without mentioning that this requires $R_2/R_1 = R_4/R_3$ is incomplete. Examiners expect you to state the **condition for maximum CMRR**. (Lose 2 marks)
> 
> **Mistake 5: Drawing the integrator with $R_f$ instead of $C_f$**
> Mixing up the symbol — drawing a resistor in the feedback path of an integrator — is a **fatal diagram error**. Use the standard capacitor symbol (two parallel lines). (Lose 1 mark)
> 
> **Mistake 6: Not mentioning phase relationship**
> Every circuit in this module has a defined phase relationship. Forgetting to state "180° out of phase" or "in phase" loses you the conceptual clarity mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Use this the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Virtual Ground:** $V^- = 0$ V when $V^+ = 0$ V (in inverting configurations only).
- [x] **Inverting Amplifier:** $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_{in}$; Input $Z = R_{in}$ (low); **180° phase shift**.
- [x] **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$; Input $Z = \infty$ (high); **0° phase shift**; $V^- = V_{in}$ (not ground!).
- [x] **Voltage Follower:** Special case when $R_f = 0$, $R_{in} = \infty$ → $A_{CL} = 1$ (unity gain buffer).
- [x] **Summing Amplifier:** $V_{out} = -R_f(V_1/R_1 + V_2/R_2 + V_3/R_3)$; extends to n inputs.
- [x] **Audio Mixer Application:** Equal resistors at all inputs create a simple scaled sum.
- [x] **Integrator:** $V_{out}(t) = -(1/R_{in}C_f)\int V_{in}dt + V_{out}(0)$; DC gain is infinite.
- [x] **Practical Integrator Fix:** Add large $R_f \parallel C_f$ to limit DC gain to $-R_f/R_{in}$.
- [x] **Difference Amplifier (Balanced):** $V_{out} = (R_2/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ with $R_2/R_1 = R_4/R_3$.
- [x] **CMRR Condition:** The balanced resistor ratio is **essential** for common-mode rejection in difference amplifier.
- [x] **KCL is the workhorse:** Every derivation begins with sum of currents = 0 at the $V^-$ node.
- [x] **Virtual Short is universal:** $V^+ = V^-$ holds in **all** negative feedback configurations, not just inverting.
- [x] **The only frequency-dependent circuit:** Integrator (contains $C_f$). All others are resistive.
- [x] **Sign convention:** Negative output means 180° phase inversion — always mention in your answer.
- [x] **Input impedance ranking:** Non-Inverting ($\infty$) > Difference (moderate) > Inverting/Summing/Integrator ($R_{in}$).
- [x] **Phase inversion ranking:** Inverting, Summing, Integrator → 180°; Non-Inverting → 0°; Difference → depends on input polarity.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — SIMPLE OP-AMP CIRCUITS**

topics.title: Op-Amp Applications: Instrumentation Amplifier, Schmitt Trigger, Precision Rectifiers, Comparators, Zero Crossing Detector, Level Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Op-Amp Applications: Instrumentation Amplifier, Schmitt Trigger, Precision Rectifiers, Comparators, Zero Crossing Detector, Level Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Comparators

### Formal Definition
An **operational amplifier comparator** is a non-linear application of an op-amp that compares two input voltages (one applied to the inverting terminal, the other to the non-inverting terminal) and produces a digital-like output indicating which input is larger. The op-amp is operated in **open-loop** configuration (no feedback), driving it into saturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** In comparator mode, the op-amp operates **open-loop** with gain approaching infinity. Output swings between $+\,V_{sat}$ and $-\,V_{sat}$ (typically $\pm 13\,\text{V}$ for $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply with 741 op-amp).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a comparator as a **balance scale judge**. Two voltages are placed on either side; whichever is heavier (greater) tips the scale. The judge then shouts either "HIGH" ($+\,V_{sat}$) or "LOW" ($-\,V_{sat}$). There is no in-between — only two extreme verdicts.

The transfer equation is:

$$V_{out} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{if } V_+ > V_- \\ -V_{sat} & \text{if } V_+ < V_- \end{cases}$$

where $V_{sat} \approx 0.9 \times V_{CC}$.

---

## 1.2 Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)

### Formal Definition
A **Zero Crossing Detector** is a special comparator configuration where the reference voltage is **ground (0 V)**, and the input signal is applied to the inverting terminal. The output changes state every time the input signal crosses zero volts.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **swinging pendulum** observed by a person who shouts "FORWARD" when the pendulum passes center going right and "BACKWARD" when it passes center going left. The crossing of the **center (zero)** is what triggers the action. In signal processing, ZCDs are the heart of **sine-to-square wave converters**.

---

## 1.3 Level Detector

### Formal Definition
A **Level Detector** is a comparator with a **non-zero reference voltage** $V_{ref}$. The op-amp output indicates when the input signal exceeds (or falls below) this preset threshold level. Used in **battery monitors, over-temperature alarms, and threshold switching circuits**.

### Conceptual Analogy
A **fire alarm** triggers when temperature (input) exceeds a set threshold (reference). The alarm either sounds ($+\,V_{sat}$) or stays silent ($-\,V_{sat}$) — there is no proportional output.

---

## 1.4 Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator)

### Formal Definition
A **Schmitt Trigger** is a comparator with **positive feedback** (regenerative feedback) that introduces **hysteresis** into the switching threshold. It has two distinct threshold voltages: the **Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP)** and the **Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP)**. The output switches state only when the input crosses these thresholds, providing **noise immunity**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Consider a **thermostat with a 2°C dead band**. The AC turns ON only when temperature rises to 28°C (UTP) and turns OFF only when it falls to 24°C (LTP). It does not rapidly switch ON/OFF for small fluctuations near 26°C — this is **hysteresis**, and it prevents "chattering" of the comparator output due to noise.

> [!NOTE]
> The difference $\Delta V_T = \text{UTP} - \text{LTP}$ is called the **Hysteresis Width** and is critical for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 1.5 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)

### Formal Definition
An **Instrumentation Amplifier** is a precision differential amplifier with **high input impedance**, **high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, **low noise**, and **variable gain** controlled by a single external resistor. It is the workhorse of **biomedical (ECG/EEG)** and **sensor signal conditioning**.

### Conceptual Analogy
A **stethoscope** for electrical signals. It amplifies only the tiny, weak difference between two signals (e.g., heart signals at two body points) while **rejecting common electrical noise** (like 50 Hz mains interference picked up equally at both points). The single gain-resistor makes it easy to "tune the volume" without disturbing the balanced input.

---

## 1.6 Precision Rectifiers

### Formal Definition
A **Precision Rectifier** (also called a **Super Diode** or **Active Rectifier**) uses an op-amp combined with diodes to overcome the forward voltage drop ($V_F \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$) of ordinary silicon diodes. The op-amp forces the diode to conduct as if it were an **ideal diode** ($V_F = 0$), enabling accurate rectification of signals **below 0.7 V** — impossible with passive diode rectifiers.

### Conceptual Analogy
An ordinary **door with a 0.7 kg weight** refuses to open for small pushes. The op-amp acts as a **giant mechanical advantage booster**: even a tiny push becomes a powerful shove, easily overcoming the door's weight. So signals as small as 10 mV can be rectified accurately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The **Half-Wave Precision Rectifier** and the **Full-Wave Precision Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)** are the two sub-topics examined. Pay close attention to the **peak detector** application.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger (The KTU Favorite)

**Circuit Topology:** Input $V_{in}$ applied to the inverting terminal. A fraction of the output (via $R_1$ and $R_2$ voltage divider) is fed back to the **non-inverting** terminal — this is the **positive feedback**.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- The voltage at the non-inverting terminal $V_+$ is given by superposition (with $V_{out}$ saturated at $\pm V_{sat}$):

$$V_+ = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{out}$$

- Since $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$, $V_+$ can take only two possible values:

$$\text{UTP} = +\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$$

$$\text{LTP} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$$

- Switching action:
  - If $V_{in} > V_+$: output saturates to $-\,V_{sat}$ → feedback makes $V_+ = \text{LTP}$.
  - If $V_{in} < V_+$: output saturates to $+\,V_{sat}$ → feedback makes $V_+ = \text{UTP}$.

### KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS**

| Circuit | Key Formula | Symbol/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Non-Inv. Schmitt UTP | $\text{UTP} = +\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| Non-Inv. Schmitt LTP | $\text{LTP} = -\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| Hysteresis Width | $V_H = \text{UTP} - \text{LTP} = \dfrac{2R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| Inverting Schmitt UTP | $\text{UTP} = -\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{ref}$ (with $V_{ref}$) | Volts (V) |
| Inverting Schmitt LTP | $\text{LTP} = +\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{ref}$ | Volts (V) |
| Inverting Schmitt Hysteresis | $V_H = \dfrac{2R_1}{R_2}\,V_{ref}$ | Volts (V) |
| Instrumentation Amplifier Gain | $A_v = \dfrac{R_3}{R_g}\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| Instrumentation Amp Output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_g}\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$ | Volts (V) |
| Half-Wave Precision Rectifier | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ for $V_{in} > 0$, else $0$ | Volts (V) |
| Full-Wave Precision (Absolute) | $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$ | Volts (V) |
| Comparator Output | $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| $V_{sat}$ (741 op-amp) | $V_{sat} \approx 0.9 \times V_{CC}$ | Volts (V) |

> **Tip for Tables:** When using mathematical absolute values in tables, always write $\vert V_{in} \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown pipe delimiter.

---

## 2.2 Why Schmitt Trigger? — Engineering Utility

| Problem with Plain Comparator | Schmitt Trigger Solution |
|---|---|
| Rapid output chattering when input hovers near threshold | Hysteresis creates a "dead zone" |
| Sensitive to noise spikes on slow-moving signals | Noise smaller than $V_H$ is rejected |
| Unstable output for noisy sensor signals | Clean, jitter-free square wave output |

**Real-world applications:** Waveform generators (function generators), 555 timer input stages, debouncing mechanical switch signals, A/D converter front-ends, relaxation oscillators.

---

## 2.3 Instrumentation Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

The INA has **three op-amps**: two input buffers (A1, A2) and one difference amplifier (A3).

### Why three op-amps? (The "Why")
- Op-amps A1 and A2 provide **high input impedance** so the source is not loaded.
- A1 and A2 amplify the **differential signal** $V_1 - V_2$ across resistor $R_g$ (the gain-setting resistor).
- A3 is a standard **difference amplifier** that subtracts the two buffered signals and rejects the common-mode voltage.

### Why CMRR is so high
- Both input signals see **identical** input stages → any common-mode noise is amplified **equally** and then **cancelled** by the difference amplifier.
- Resistors in A3 must be **precision-matched** ($R_3 = R_4$, $R_5 = R_6$) to preserve CMRR.

---

## 2.4 Precision Rectifiers — The "Super Diode" Concept

### The Problem with Passive Diodes
A silicon diode needs $\sim 0.7\,\text{V}$ to conduct. So:
- Signals below 0.7 V are **invisible** to passive rectifiers.
- In signal-metering and instrumentation, this is unacceptable.

### The Op-Amp Solution
The op-amp has **huge open-loop gain** ($A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ for 741). When configured as a precision half-wave rectifier:
- Even a tiny input voltage produces a large op-amp output.
- This large output easily overcomes the diode's 0.7 V drop.
- The diode then conducts, and feedback forces the **cathode voltage to equal $V_{in}$**.
- Effective threshold becomes $V_{in} = \dfrac{0.7}{A_{OL}} \approx 3.5\,\mu\text{V}$ — practically **zero**.

### Engineering Utility
- **Signal-metering circuits** (measuring AC voltages accurately).
- **Peak detectors** in audio and communication equipment.
- **AC voltmeters** of high precision.
- **Absolute-value circuits** in phase-sensitive detectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger Thresholds

### Step 1: Identify the feedback network
The output $V_{out}$ is fed back to the non-inverting terminal $V_+$ through the voltage divider $R_1$ and $R_2$, where $R_1$ is the resistor from $V_+$ to ground, and $R_2$ is from $V_+$ to $V_{out}$.

### Step 2: Apply the voltage divider rule
Using the standard voltage divider (with the inverting input drawing no current):

$$V_+ = V_{out} \times \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the saturated output
In the open-loop/positive feedback configuration, the op-amp output can only be at saturation:

$$V_{out} = +V_{sat} \quad \text{or} \quad V_{out} = -V_{sat}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP)
When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$:

$$\text{UTP} = +V_{sat} \times \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP)
When $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$:

$$\text{LTP} = -V_{sat} \times \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 6: Compute Hysteresis Width
Subtract LTP from UTP:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_H &= \text{UTP} - \text{LTP} \\
&= \left(+\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\right)V_{sat} - \left(-\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\right)V_{sat} \\
&= \frac{2R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverting Schmitt Trigger Thresholds (with $V_{ref}$)

### Step 1: Identify the input side
Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal via $R_1$. Reference $V_{ref}$ is applied to the non-inverting side via $R_2$. The output is fed back to the non-inverting terminal as well — this is positive feedback.

### Step 2: Use superposition at the non-inverting node $V_+$
The non-inverting input voltage is the sum of two contributions:

$$V_+ = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{in} + \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{out}$$

### Step 3: At the switching instant
Switching occurs when $V_{in}$ (at the inverting input) equals $V_+$:

$$V_{in} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{in} + \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{out}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $V_{in}$
Rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{in}\left(1 - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right) &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{out} \\
V_{in}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\right) &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{out}
\end{aligned}$$

This simplifies cleanly to give us the threshold for $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ and $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$, yielding:

$$\text{UTP} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{ref} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{LTP} = +\frac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{ref}$$

### Step 5: Hysteresis Width

$$\begin{aligned}
V_H &= \text{UTP} - \text{LTP} \\
&= \frac{2R_1}{R_2}\,V_{ref}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Output

### Step 1: Analyze the input stage (A1 and A2)
Both inputs of A1 and A2 are at the same potential (virtual short) — let this common potential be $V_x$. Current through $R_g$:

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

This same current flows through both feedback resistors (let's call them $R_1$):

$$V_{O1} - V_{O2} = 2R_1 \cdot I + (V_1 - V_2) = 2R_1 \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g} + (V_1 - V_2)$$

### Step 2: Factor out $(V_1 - V_2)$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{O1} - V_{O2} &= (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Apply the difference amplifier (A3) equation
The standard difference amplifier (with $R_3 = R_5$ and $R_4 = R_6$) produces:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}(V_{O2} - V_{O1})$$

### Step 4: Substitute and simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{R_3}{R_4}\left[(V_2 - V_1)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\right] \\
V_{out} &= \frac{R_3}{R_4}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_2 - V_1)
\end{aligned}$$

Taking magnitude (gain is positive magnitude of amplification):

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_g}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)}$$

(Note: Some textbooks use $R_g$ as the gain resistor symbol while others use $R_G$; both refer to the same external resistor between the two input op-amp inverting terminals.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Half-Wave Precision Rectifier (Super Diode)

### Step 1: Analyze the negative half-cycle ($V_{in} < 0$)
- $V_{in}$ at the inverting input is **negative**.
- The op-amp output $V_{OA}$ swings to a large **positive** value (since negative input → tries to make output positive for negative feedback).
- Diode D1 is **reverse-biased** (off).
- Diode D2 is **forward-biased** (on).
- No current flows through the feedback resistor $R$, so $V_{out} = 0$.

### Step 2: Analyze the positive half-cycle ($V_{in} > 0$)
- $V_{in}$ is **positive**.
- The op-amp output $V_{OA}$ swings to a large **negative** value.
- D1 is **forward-biased** (on), D2 is **reverse-biased** (off).
- Feedback loop closes through D1 and $R$.
- The op-amp's huge open-loop gain forces the inverting input to equal $V_{in}$ (virtual short).
- Therefore, $V_{out} = V_{in}$ for the positive half-cycle.

### Step 3: Consolidated Transfer Function

$$V_{out} = \begin{cases} V_{in} & \text{if } V_{in} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } V_{in} < 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the **improved precision half-wave rectifier** (with diode D2 in feedback), the transfer is **inverted**: $V_{out} = 0$ for $V_{in} > 0$ and $V_{out} = -V_{in}$ for $V_{in} < 0$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Plain Comparator | Schmitt Trigger | INA | Precision Rectifier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback Type | Open loop (none) | Positive (regenerative) | Negative (3 stages) | Negative (diode-coupled) |
| Threshold Levels | Single | Two (UTP, LTP) | N/A (linear amp) | Zero (offset compensated) |
| Noise Immunity | Poor | Excellent (hysteresis) | Excellent (CMRR) | N/A |
| Output | $\pm V_{sat}$ | $\pm V_{sat}$ | Linear scaled | Rectified version of input |
| Primary Use | Threshold detection | Waveform squaring | Sensor signal conditioning | Small-signal rectification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1Node[V1 Input Signal] --> A1Buffer[Op-Amp A1 Buffer]
    V2Node[V2 Input Signal] --> A2Buffer[Op-Amp A2 Buffer]
    RgResistor[Gain Resistor Rg] --> A1Buffer
    RgResistor --> A2Buffer
    R1aFeedback[R1 Feedback A1] --> A1Buffer
    R1bFeedback[R1 Feedback A2] --> A2Buffer
    A1Buffer --> A3Diff[Op-Amp A3 Difference Stage]
    A2Buffer --> A3Diff
    R3Resistor[R3 Resistor] --> A3Diff
    R4Resistor[R4 Resistor] --> A3Diff
    R5Resistor[R5 Resistor] --> A3Diff
    R6Resistor[R6 Resistor] --> A3Diff
    A3Diff --> VoutNode[Final Vout Linear Differential Output]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The input stage (A1, A2) buffers the high-impedance differential signal $V_1 - V_2$. The external resistor $R_g$ between their inverting inputs sets the gain. The second stage (A3) is a unity-style difference amplifier that subtracts the two buffered signals, rejecting common-mode noise. $R_g$ is the **only** resistor the user changes to vary gain.

---

## 4.2 Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic

```mermaid
graph LR
    StartX[Vin Axis Horizontal] --> B1[Lower Saturation Region Vout equals minus Vsat]
    StartX --> B2[High Output Region Vout equals plus Vsat]
    B1 --> TransitionUp[Switching Up at UTP Vin crosses plus R1 over R1 plus R2 times Vsat]
    B2 --> TransitionDown[Switching Down at LTP Vin crosses minus R1 over R1 plus R2 times Vsat]
    TransitionUp --> HysteresisWidth[Hysteresis Loop Width equals UTP minus LTP]
    TransitionDown --> HysteresisWidth
```

**Key visual takeaway:** A Schmitt trigger's transfer characteristic is a **rectangular hysteresis loop**. As $V_{in}$ increases from below LTP, $V_{out}$ stays at $-V_{sat}$ until $V_{in}$ crosses UTP, where it snaps to $+V_{sat}$. As $V_{in}$ decreases, $V_{out}$ stays at $+V_{sat}$ until $V_{in}$ crosses LTP. The horizontal gap between UTP and LTP is the **hysteresis band $V_H$**.

---

## 4.3 Half-Wave Precision Rectifier — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    InNode[Vin Small AC Signal] --> OpAmpStage[Op-Amp Open-Loop Gain Stage]
    OpAmpStage --> D1Path[Diode D1 Conducts for Vin greater than 0]
    OpAmpStage --> D2Path[Diode D2 Conducts for Vin less than 0]
    D1Path --> FeedbackLoop[Negative Feedback Through R Forces Vout equals Vin]
    D2Path --> GroundSink[Output Pulled to Zero Volts via D2]
    FeedbackLoop --> OutputNode[Final Vout Equals Vin for Positive Half Cycle Only]
    GroundSink --> OutputNode
```

**Key visual takeaway:** The op-amp compensates for diode drop dynamically. When $V_{in} > 0$, the op-amp output rises just enough to forward-bias D1, and the feedback forces the output to track $V_{in}$ accurately. When $V_{in} < 0$, the op-amp output inverts polarity, D2 conducts, and the output is grounded. The 0.7 V diode drop is effectively divided by the op-amp's open-loop gain, becoming negligible.

---

## 4.4 Zero Crossing Detector — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SineInput[AC Sine Wave Input Vin] --> InvertInput[Applied to Inverting Terminal]
    GroundRef[Ground Reference 0 Volts] --> NonInvertInput[Applied to Non-Inverting Terminal]
    InvertInput --> OpAmpOpen[Op-Amp in Open Loop Comparator Mode]
    NonInvertInput --> OpAmpOpen
    OpAmpOpen --> Decision[Output Decision plus Vsat or minus Vsat]
    Decision --> SquareOutput[Square Wave Output Switching at Every Zero Crossing]
```

**Key visual takeaway:** Every time the sine wave crosses zero, the op-amp output flips polarity. A 1 kHz sine input becomes a 1 kHz square wave. This is the basis of **sine-to-square converters** and **phase-locked loop** front-ends.

---

## 4.5 Level Detector — Reference-Set Threshold Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    SensorInput[Sensor Signal Vin E.g. Temperature or Voltage] --> InvertInput[Inverting Input of Op-Amp]
    VrefSet[Adjustable Vref Reference Voltage] --> NonInvertInput[Non-Inverting Input of Op-Amp]
    InvertInput --> ComparatorCore[Op-Amp Open Loop Comparator]
    NonInvertInput --> ComparatorCore
    ComparatorCore --> AlarmTrigger[Output plus Vsat Activates Alarm or Relay]
    ComparatorCore --> SafeState[Output minus Vsat Idle or Safe State]
```

**Key visual takeaway:** When the monitored input $V_{in}$ exceeds the preset reference $V_{ref}$, the comparator output saturates to $+V_{sat}$, triggering a downstream relay, LED, or buzzer. The reference $V_{ref}$ acts as the **trip threshold**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define a comparator and explain the operation of a zero crossing detector with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **comparator** is an open-loop op-amp circuit that compares two input voltages and produces a digital output indicating which is greater.

$$\text{Output: } V_{out} = +V_{sat} \text{ if } V_+ > V_-; \quad -V_{sat} \text{ if } V_+ < V_-$$

In a **zero crossing detector**, the inverting input receives the input signal $V_{in}$ and the non-inverting input is grounded ($V_{ref} = 0$). Every time $V_{in}$ crosses 0 V, the output flips between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$, producing a square wave whose frequency equals the input sine wave's frequency. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for circuit, 1 mark for output waveform explanation.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is hysteresis in a Schmitt trigger? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hysteresis** is the phenomenon in a Schmitt trigger where the switching threshold for increasing input (UTP) is different from the switching threshold for decreasing input (LTP).

$$\text{Hysteresis Width: } V_H = \text{UTP} - \text{LTP}$$

It is necessary because a **plain comparator** suffers from output chattering when the input signal hovers near the threshold or contains noise. The hysteresis band creates a **dead zone** in which the output does not change, providing **noise immunity** and a clean, stable output. (1 mark for definition with formula, 1 mark for chatter problem, 1 mark for noise immunity solution.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers **internal choice** between two questions, typically with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Both sub-answers must be attempted at a conceptual + numerical depth.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger with UTP = +4 V and LTP = -4 V using op-amp 741 with $\pm 15$ V supply. Assume $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the value of $R_2$ and the hysteresis width.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**Step 1:** For a 741 op-amp with $\pm 15$ V supply, the saturation voltage is:

$$V_{sat} \approx 0.9 \times 15 = 13.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Use the UTP formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UTP} &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} \\
4 &= \frac{10\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + R_2} \times 13.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $R_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{4}{13.5} &= \frac{10\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + R_2} \\
0.2963\,(10\,\text{k} + R_2) &= 10\,\text{k} \\
2.963\,\text{k} + 0.2963\,R_2 &= 10\,\text{k} \\
0.2963\,R_2 &= 7.037\,\text{k} \\
R_2 &= 23.75\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute hysteresis width:

$$V_H = \text{UTP} - \text{LTP} = 4 - (-4) = 8\,\text{V}$$

(2 marks for $V_{sat}$ calculation, 2 marks for $R_2$ derivation, 2 marks for hysteresis width, 1 mark for unit & conclusion.)

**(b)** With reference to a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier, derive the expression for output voltage and explain why it is preferred for biomedical signal acquisition. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:** The INA consists of two input buffer op-amps (A1, A2) and one difference amplifier (A3). The input signal $V_1$ goes to A1's non-inverting input; $V_2$ goes to A2's non-inverting input. A gain-setting resistor $R_g$ connects the inverting inputs of A1 and A2.

**Step 2 — Input Stage Analysis:** By virtual short, both op-amp inputs are at the same potential. The voltage across $R_g$ is $(V_1 - V_2)$, so the current through $R_g$ is:

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

The same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$, producing:

$$V_{O1} - V_{O2} = (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Difference Stage Output:** The standard difference amplifier equation (with matched $R_3, R_4, R_5, R_6$):

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_g}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

The overall differential gain is:

$$A_v = \frac{R_3}{R_g}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Why Preferred for Biomedical Signals:**
- **High input impedance:** Does not load sensitive biological electrodes (skin-contact sensors have source impedance up to several kΩ).
- **High CMRR:** Rejects 50 Hz mains interference that couples equally into both electrode leads.
- **Variable gain via single resistor $R_g$:** Allows easy gain tuning without disturbing the balanced bridge.
- **Low noise and low offset:** Critical for amplifying micro-volt-level ECG/EEG signals.

(1 mark for circuit description, 2 marks for input stage derivation, 2 marks for output equation, 2 marks for biomedical application explanation.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a precision half-wave rectifier with a circuit diagram. Show that it can rectify signals of magnitude less than 0.7 V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Circuit Topology:** The precision half-wave rectifier (super diode) consists of an op-amp, two diodes (D1 in the feedback path, D2 from the inverting input to ground), and an output resistor $R$. The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting input.

**Step 2 — Positive Half-Cycle ($V_{in} > 0$):**
- The op-amp output $V_{OA}$ swings negative.
- D1 becomes forward-biased and D2 becomes reverse-biased.
- Negative feedback closes through D1 and $R$.
- Virtual short principle forces the inverting input to equal $V_{in}$, so $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

**Step 3 — Negative Half-Cycle ($V_{in} < 0$):**
- The op-amp output swings positive.
- D1 is reverse-biased; D2 is forward-biased.
- The feedback loop is broken; no current flows through $R$.
- $V_{out} = 0$ V.

**Step 4 — Why It Rectifies Below 0.7 V:**

Suppose a passive rectifier would need 0.7 V to turn on. In the precision version, the op-amp's open-loop gain $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ multiplies the input. The voltage required at the op-amp output to overcome the diode drop is:

$$V_{in,\text{min}} = \frac{V_F}{A_{OL}} = \frac{0.7}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.5\,\mu\text{V}$$

Thus, the precision rectifier can rectify signals as small as a few microvolts — impossible for a passive diode rectifier.

(2 marks for circuit and operation in each half-cycle, 2 marks for transfer characteristics, 1 mark for the 0.7 V problem statement, 2 marks for the $V_{in,\text{min}}$ derivation.)

**(b)** Design a Schmitt trigger using op-amp 741 ($\pm 15$ V supply) for a hysteresis width of 6 V with UTP = +3 V. Draw the transfer characteristic and label all critical points. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Determine $V_{sat}$:**

$$V_{sat} = 0.9 \times 15 = 13.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Use the Hysteresis Width Formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_H &= \frac{2R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} \\
6 &= \frac{2R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \times 13.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Use the UTP Formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UTP} &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} \\
3 &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \times 13.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the System:**

From Step 3: $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{3}{13.5} = 0.2222$

Substituting into Step 2:
$6 = 2 \times 0.2222 \times 13.5 = 6$ ✓ (Consistent)

Let $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (standard choice):

$$0.2222 = \frac{10\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + R_2}$$

$$0.2222\,(10\,\text{k} + R_2) = 10\,\text{k}$$

$$2.222\,\text{k} + 0.2222\,R_2 = 10\,\text{k}$$

$$0.2222\,R_2 = 7.778\,\text{k}$$

$$R_2 = 35\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Verify LTP:**

$$\text{LTP} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} = -0.2222 \times 13.5 = -3\,\text{V}$$

(1 mark for $V_{sat}$ calculation, 2 marks for the system of equations, 2 marks for resistor derivation, 1 mark for LTP verification, 1 mark for transfer characteristic drawing.)

**Transfer Characteristic (textual description for the answer sheet):**

- $V_{in} < -3$ V → $V_{out} = +13.5$ V (Upper saturation)
- $-3$ V $< V_{in} < +3$ V → $V_{out}$ depends on history (hysteresis region)
- $V_{in} > +3$ V → $V_{out} = -13.5$ V (Lower saturation)
- Hysteresis width $V_H = 6$ V centered at 0 V.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.9 multiplier for $V_{sat}$.** Students often write $V_{sat} = V_{CC} = 15$ V. The actual saturation for a 741 op-amp is closer to **13.5 V**. Using 15 V will yield a wrong $R_2$ value and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Swapping UTP and LTP.** UTP is the threshold for **increasing** $V_{in}$ (output switches to $-V_{sat}$). LTP is the threshold for **decreasing** $V_{in}$ (output switches to $+V_{sat}$). Confusing these reverses the hysteresis loop orientation.
>
> 3. **Inverting vs. Non-inverting Schmitt Trigger confusion.** The two circuits have **different formulas** for thresholds. The non-inverting version has thresholds $\pm \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}V_{sat}$, while the inverting version has thresholds $\pm \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}V_{ref}$. Memorize both.
>
> 4. **INA gain formula symbol error.** Some textbooks write $R_G$ (capital), some write $R_g$ (lowercase). Both refer to the **same gain-setting resistor** between the two input op-amp inverting terminals. Do not confuse it with $R_1$ (the feedback resistor of the input buffers).
>
> 5. **Precision rectifier direction error.** Students often draw the diodes backward, making the rectifier pass the wrong half-cycle. Always check: the diode in the feedback path must conduct when you want the output to follow the input.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to draw the transfer characteristic.** For Schmitt trigger and comparator questions, a 1-mark bonus is reserved for a **correctly labeled** hysteresis loop / square wave output. Skipping the diagram can lose easy marks.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning CMRR for INA.** Many students derive the gain correctly but forget to mention **why** the INA is preferred — high CMRR, high input impedance, and adjustable gain are the three pillars. Examiners specifically look for these in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Comparator:** Open-loop op-amp, output $\pm V_{sat}$, no feedback. Used as a **threshold detector**.
- **Zero Crossing Detector:** Comparator with $V_{ref} = 0$. Output flips at every zero crossing of $V_{in}$ — converts sine to square wave.
- **Level Detector:** Comparator with non-zero $V_{ref}$. Used for **battery alarms, temperature alerts**.
- **Schmitt Trigger:** Comparator with **positive (regenerative) feedback**. Introduces **hysteresis** with two thresholds: **UTP** and **LTP**.
  - Non-inverting type: $\text{UTP} = +\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}V_{sat}$, $\text{LTP} = -\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}V_{sat}$
  - Inverting type: $\text{UTP} = -\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}V_{ref}$, $\text{LTP} = +\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}V_{ref}$
  - Hysteresis width: $V_H = \text{UTP} - \text{LTP}$
- **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Three op-amps, high input impedance, high CMRR, gain set by single resistor $R_g$.
  - $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_g}\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$
  - Applications: ECG/EEG amplifiers, transducer signal conditioning, weigh scales, strain gauges.
- **Precision Rectifier:** Op-amp + diode combination that overcomes the 0.7 V silicon diode drop.
  - Half-wave: $V_{out} = V_{in}$ for $V_{in} > 0$, else 0.
  - Full-wave (absolute value): $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$.
  - Can rectify signals as small as **3.5 μV** (limited only by op-amp open-loop gain).
- **$V_{sat}$ for 741:** Always use $V_{sat} \approx 0.9 \times V_{CC}$, **not** $V_{CC}$ itself.
- **Hysteresis is the key advantage** of Schmitt trigger over plain comparator — it rejects noise and prevents chatter.
- **INA vs. Difference Amplifier:** INA has high input impedance because of the **buffer stage** at the input; plain difference amplifier loads the source.
- **Precision Rectifier vs. Passive Rectifier:** Precision version uses op-amp feedback to dynamically cancel the diode's $V_F$; passive version always loses 0.7 V per diode.
- **KTU Hot Topics for Module 3:** Schmitt trigger threshold numerical problems, INA gain derivation, precision rectifier operation, and comparator waveform sketches. Master these four for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Filters: Design of LPF, BPF and Notch Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Filters: Design of LPF, BPF and Notch Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Active Filter?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An **active filter** is a frequency-selective electronic circuit that uses active components (typically **operational amplifiers**), resistors, and capacitors to pass or reject specific frequency bands of an input signal while providing **gain** (signal amplification) and eliminating the need for bulky inductors.

In contrast, **passive filters** (built only with R, L, C) suffer from:
- **Insertion loss** (output amplitude < input amplitude)
- **Loading effect** (output depends on load impedance)
- **Bulky inductors** at low frequencies (a 1 Hz inductor could weigh kilograms)
- **No isolation** between source and load

Active filters solve all of this by inserting an op-amp as a **buffer/amplifier stage** between the RC network and the load.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Bouncer at a Disco

Imagine a nightclub entrance. The **bouncer (op-amp)** is very selective:

- **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** = A bouncer who lets in only people below a certain *height* (frequency). Tall guys (high frequency) are rejected.
- **High Pass Filter (HPF)** = The opposite. Only tall people (high frequency) are allowed in.
- **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** = Only people within a specific *height range* (e.g., 5'6" to 6'0") are allowed. Both very short and very tall are rejected.
- **Notch Filter (Band Reject)** = The bouncer rejects only one specific height — say, exactly 5'10". Everyone else gets in.

The **op-amp acts as the bouncer** who enforces the rule firmly (with gain and isolation), while the **RC network acts as the height-measuring tool**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology You MUST Know

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff Frequency** | The −3 dB point where signal power drops to half | $f_c$ |
| **Passband** | Frequencies that pass through with minimal attenuation | — |
| **Stopband** | Frequencies that are heavily attenuated | — |
| **Quality Factor** | Sharpness/selectivity of the filter | $Q$ |
| **Roll-off Rate** | How steeply attenuation increases per decade (in dB/decade) | — |
| **Order of Filter** | Number of reactive components (R/C pairs) determining roll-off | $n$ |
| **Bandwidth** | Difference between upper and lower cutoff frequencies | $BW = f_H - f_L$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll-off Rates to Memorize:**
> - **1st-order filter:** −20 dB/decade
> - **2nd-order Butterworth:** −40 dB/decade
> - **2nd-order Bessel/Chebyshev:** also −40 dB/decade but different phase response
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (LPF)

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology
A **non-inverting first-order active LPF** is built by cascading a passive RC low-pass section with a voltage-follower (or non-inverting amplifier).

**Transfer Function Derivation Logic:**
1. The RC network forms a voltage divider in the frequency domain.
2. The op-amp provides gain $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$ (for non-inverting) or $A = 1$ (voltage follower).
3. The op-amp isolates the RC section from the load → **no loading effect**.

### 2.1.2 Transfer Function
For the passive RC network alone:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

The −3 dB cutoff frequency:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

With op-amp gain $A_v$ included:

$$H(s) = \frac{A_v}{1 + sRC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of 1st-order LPF:** Roll-off is only **−20 dB/decade**, which is a *gentle slope*. In real-world applications (e.g., audio crossover networks, anti-aliasing), this is **insufficient**. Hence we need **second-order filters**.

---

## 2.2 Second-Order Active LPF — Sallen-Key Topology (Butterworth)

The **Sallen-Key** is the most popular active filter topology because it uses **equal component values**, making design and tuning very simple.

### 2.2.1 Sallen-Key LPF Circuit Equations

For a **unity-gain Sallen-Key LPF** with $C_1 = C_2 = C$ and $R_1 = R_2 = R$:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{s^2(R_1R_2C_1C_2) + s(R_1C_1 + R_2C_1) + (1 - K)R_2C_2 + 1}$$

The **standard second-order transfer function** is:

$$H(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + s\frac{\omega_0}{Q} + \omega_0^2}$$

By **coefficient matching** for Butterworth response ($Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$):

$$K = 3 - \frac{1}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$$

**Therefore:** For a 2nd-order Butterworth LPF, the **non-inverting op-amp gain must be 1.586** (≈ 4 dB).

> [!NOTE]
> This is achieved using: $\frac{R_f}{R_i} = K - 1 = 0.586$, so if $R_i = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 5.86\text{ k}\Omega$.

### 2.2.2 Pole Frequency

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

---

## 2.3 Active Band Pass Filter (BPF) — Multiple Feedback (MFB) Topology

A BPF passes a **narrow band** of frequencies and rejects both low and high extremes.

### 2.3.1 Wideband BPF (Cascaded HPF + LPF)
The simplest design: **cascade** a 2nd-order HPF with a 2nd-order LPF, both tuned to:
- LPF cutoff = $f_H$ (upper cutoff)
- HPF cutoff = $f_L$ (lower cutoff)

$$f_{center} = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$$

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

$$Q = \frac{f_{center}}{BW} = \frac{\sqrt{f_L f_H}}{f_H - f_L}$$

### 2.3.2 Narrowband BPF (MFB / Deliyannis-Friend)
For a **single op-amp narrowband BPF** with high Q:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C}\sqrt{\frac{R_1 + R_3}{R_1 R_2 R_3}}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi f_0 C \sqrt{R_1 R_2 R_3 (R_1 + R_3)}}{R_1 + R_3}$$

$$H_0 = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1} + \frac{R_2}{R_3}}$$

---

## 2.4 Active Notch Filter (Band Reject Filter)

A **notch filter** rejects a **single sharp frequency** (e.g., 50 Hz power-line hum) while passing everything else.

### 2.4.1 Twin-T Notch Topology
The **Twin-T notch filter** is the classical design, using:
- One **T-network** with two series resistors and one shunt capacitor
- One **T-network** with two series capacitors and one shunt resistor

**Center (notch) frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Notch depth** is theoretically **infinite**, but practically limited by component tolerances (Q = 10 to 50 typical).

### 2.4.2 Active Twin-T with Op-Amp (Improved Q)
By adding a **voltage follower** to the passive Twin-T, the notch depth becomes virtually infinite, and Q improves dramatically. The op-amp also:
1. Eliminates loading on the Twin-T network.
2. Provides low output impedance.
3. Allows cascading for sharper notch.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Filter Type | Key Formula | Roll-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1st-Order LPF | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | −20 dB/decade |
| 2 | 2nd-Order Butterworth LPF | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, $K = 1.586$ | −40 dB/decade |
| 3 | 1st-Order HPF | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | +20 dB/decade |
| 4 | BPF (wideband) | $f_{center} = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ | Flat in passband |
| 5 | BPF (narrowband MFB) | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C}\sqrt{\frac{R_1 + R_3}{R_1 R_2 R_3}}$ | Steep on both sides |
| 6 | Q factor | $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$ | — |
| 7 | Notch (Twin-T) | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Very sharp dip |
| 8 | Butterworth $Q$ | $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | Maximally flat |
| 9 | Chebyshev $Q$ | $Q \geq 0.707$ | Rippled passband |
| 10 | Bessel $Q$ | $Q \leq 0.707$ | Linear phase |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **LPF:** Anti-aliasing in ADC front-ends, audio sub-woofers, switching power supply noise filtering.
- **HPF:** DC blocking, rumble filters in audio, AC coupling.
- **BPF:** Radio/TV tuners (selecting a station), EEG/ECG signal extraction, RFID readers.
- **Notch:** **50/60 Hz power-line hum removal** in biomedical instrumentation, removing carrier frequencies in communication receivers, audio feedback suppression in PA systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 1: First-Order Active LPF Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Define the passive RC voltage divider:**
The input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the series combination of $R$ and $C$. The output is taken across $C$.

**Step 2 — Express impedances in s-domain:**

$$Z_R = R \quad \text{and} \quad Z_C = \frac{1}{sC}$$

**Step 3 — Apply voltage divider rule:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute impedances:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{sRC + 1}$$

**Step 6 — Write transfer function:**

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 7 — Identify pole location:**
The pole is at $s = -\frac{1}{RC}$. The magnitude response is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Find −3 dB cutoff:**
At $\omega = \omega_c$, $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2$$

$$\omega_c RC = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 9 — Include op-amp gain $A_v$:**
The non-inverting amplifier gain is $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$. Since the op-amp acts as a buffer, the transfer function becomes:

$$H(s) = \frac{A_v}{1 + sRC}$$

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 2: Second-Order Butterworth Sallen-Key LPF

**Step 1 — Standard second-order low-pass transfer function:**

$$H(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \left(\frac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}$$

**Step 2 — Sallen-Key with $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$:**
For this equal-component case, the Sallen-Key transfer function reduces to:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + s(2 - K)RC + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Compare coefficients with the standard form:**

The standard form is:

$$H(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \left(\frac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $R^2C^2$:

$$H(s) = \frac{K / (R^2 C^2)}{s^2 + s\frac{(2 - K)}{RC} + \frac{1}{R^2C^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate coefficients:**

$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R^2C^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{2 - K}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{QRC} = \frac{2 - K}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 2 - \frac{1}{Q}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Butterworth condition:**
For a Butterworth (maximally flat) response:

$$Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{Q} = \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for K:**

$$K = 2 - \sqrt{2} = 2 - 1.4142 = 0.5858$$

**WAIT!** This $K$ value is for an **inverting** topology. For a **non-inverting** Sallen-Key with $K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$:

$$K = 3 - \sqrt{2} = 3 - 1.4142 = 1.5858 \approx 1.586$$

**Step 7 — Calculate feedback resistors:**

$$K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 1.586 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 0.586$$

Choose $R_i = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_f = 0.586 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 5.86\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 8 — Calculate R and C for a target $f_c$:**
If $f_c = 1\text{ kHz}$, choose $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = 15.92\text{ k}\Omega$$

---

## 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 3: BPF — Center Frequency, BW, and Q

**Step 1 — Define the cascade of HPF and LPF:**
- HPF cutoff = $f_L$ (lower cutoff, −3 dB point on the high-pass response)
- LPF cutoff = $f_H$ (upper cutoff, −3 dB point on the low-pass response)

**Step 2 — Center frequency (geometric mean):**
The −3 dB points of the cascaded filter define the bandwidth. The geometric center is:

$$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

**Step 4 — Quality factor:**

$$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{\sqrt{f_L f_H}}{f_H - f_L}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
Design a BPF with $f_L = 990\text{ Hz}$ and $f_H = 1010\text{ Hz}$:

$$f_0 = \sqrt{990 \times 1010} = \sqrt{999{,}900} = 999.95 \approx 1000\text{ Hz}$$

$$BW = 1010 - 990 = 20\text{ Hz}$$

$$Q = \frac{1000}{20} = 50$$

This is a **narrowband BPF** with high selectivity — only signals near 1 kHz pass.

---

## 3.4 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 4: Twin-T Notch Filter

**Step 1 — Two T-networks in parallel:**
- **T-Network 1 (RC high-pass):** $R - R$ in series arms, $C$ in shunt
- **T-Network 2 (RC low-pass):** $C - C$ in series arms, $R$ in shunt

**Step 2 — At very low frequency ($f \to 0$):**
- The two series capacitors in T-Network 2 are open circuits → no signal passes.
- The shunt resistor in T-Network 2 short-circuits the output to ground.
- **Result:** $V_{out} \to 0$. Wait, that's not right for notch.

**Correction:** The two T-networks are **in parallel between input and ground**, and the output is taken from the **common node**. At low frequency, T1 (with shunt C) blocks signal, but T2 (with shunt R) passes signal. The transfer function balances at $f_0$.

**Step 3 — Write transfer function (simplified):**

$$H(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + 4s\omega_0 + \omega_0^2}$$

where $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$.

**Step 4 — Notch frequency:**
At $s = j\omega_0$, the numerator $s^2 + \omega_0^2 = -\omega_0^2 + \omega_0^2 = 0$. **So the output is zero.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 5 — Q factor of passive Twin-T:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

This is a **low Q**, meaning the notch is **wide and shallow**. To sharpen it, add a feedback op-amp to boost Q to 30–50.

**Step 6 — Numerical Example:**
Design a 50 Hz notch to remove power-line hum:
- Choose $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$
- Calculate $R$:
