$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 31.83\text{ k}\Omega$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Filter | Order | Roll-off | Component Count | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st-Order LPF | 1 | −20 dB/dec | 1 R, 1 C, 1 op-amp | Audio DC blocker |
| 2nd-Order Butterworth LPF | 2 | −40 dB/dec | 2 R, 2 C, 1 op-amp | Anti-aliasing, crossovers |
| Wideband BPF | 4 | ±40 dB/dec | 4 R, 4 C, 2 op-amps | Radio IF stages |
| Narrowband MFB BPF | 2 | ±40 dB/dec | 4 R, 2 C, 1 op-amp | Tone decoder (LM567) |
| Twin-T Notch | 2 | Steep at $f_0$ | 6 R, 3 C, 1 op-amp | 50/60 Hz hum rejection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Active Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Source] --> B[RC Frequency Selective Network]
    B --> C[Op-Amp Active Stage]
    C --> D[Feedback Network Rf and Ri]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Low Output Impedance Buffer]
    E --> F[Load]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFA07A
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
    style F fill:#F0E68C
```

> This shows the **functional pipeline**: Source → Frequency-selective RC → Amplification with feedback → Buffer → Load. The op-amp's feedback network (D) sets the gain and stabilizes the response.

---

## 4.2 First-Order Active LPF — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Vin] --> RC1[R1]
    RC1 --> N1[Node X]
    N1 --> C1[C1 to GND]
    N1 --> OPA1[Op-Amp Non-Inverting Input]
    OPA1 --> OPBUF[Op-Amp Voltage Follower or Non-Inverting]
    OPBUF --> OUT1[Vout]
    OPAFB[Rf] --> OPBUF
    OPAFI[Ri] --> OPAFB
    GND1[Ground] --> OPAFB
    style IN fill:#FFD700
    style OUT1 fill:#90EE90
    style N1 fill:#FFB6C1
```

> The RC low-pass section drives the op-amp's non-inverting input. The op-amp provides gain $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_i$ and isolates the RC network from the load.

---

## 4.3 Second-Order Sallen-Key LPF — Detailed Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN2[Vin] --> R1A[R1]
    R1A --> NX[Node A]
    NX --> R2A[R2]
    R2A --> NA[Node B Vout of Filter]
    NX --> C1A[C1 to Vout of Op-Amp]
    NA --> C2A[C2 to GND]
    NA --> OP2[Op-Amp Non-Inverting Input]
    OP2 --> OPAMP[Op-Amp Output]
    OPAMP --> VFINAL[Vout Filtered]
    OPAMP --> RFA[Rf]
    RFA --> NF[Node C]
    NF --> RIA[Ri]
    RIA --> GND2[Ground]
    NF --> OP2
    style IN2 fill:#FFD700
    style VFINAL fill:#90EE90
    style OPAMP fill:#FFA07A
    style NX fill:#FFB6C1
    style NA fill:#FFB6C1
    style NF fill:#FFB6C1
```

> The two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ form the reactive part. $C_1$ creates a **positive feedback path** from the op-amp output to Node A, which is what gives the Sallen-Key its **voltage gain sensitivity** for tuning the Q.

---

## 4.4 Active Band Pass Filter — Cascade Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Vin Source] --> STG1[2nd Order High Pass Section]
    STG1 --> STG2[2nd Order Low Pass Section]
    STG2 --> AMP[Op-Amp Buffer or Amplifier]
    AMP --> LOAD[Load]
    STG1 -.Cuts frequencies below fL.-> STG1
    STG2 -.Passes frequencies below fH.-> STG2
    style SRC fill:#FFD700
    style LOAD fill:#90EE90
    style STG1 fill:#87CEEB
    style STG2 fill:#DDA0DD
    style AMP fill:#FFA07A
```

> A wideband BPF is **cascaded HPF + LPF**. The HPF rejects everything below $f_L$, the LPF rejects everything above $f_H$, and only the band $[f_L, f_H]$ passes through.

---

## 4.5 Twin-T Notch Filter — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN3[Vin] --> T1[T-Network 1: R R Series, C Shunt]
    T1 --> NOUT[Output Node]
    IN3 --> T2[T-Network 2: C C Series, R Shunt]
    T2 --> GND3[Ground]
    NOUT --> OP3[Op-Amp Voltage Follower]
    OP3 --> VON[Vout Buffered]
    T1 -.Parallel.-> T2
    style IN3 fill:#FFD700
    style VON fill:#90EE90
    style T1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style T2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style OP3 fill:#FFA07A
```

> The two T-networks work in **complementary fashion** at the notch frequency. At $f_0$, the two paths produce **equal-amplitude, opposite-phase** signals that cancel, creating a deep null in the output. The op-amp buffers the high-impedance output node.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define an active filter. List any two advantages of active filters over passive filters.**

**Model Answer:**

An **active filter** is a frequency-selective circuit that employs active devices like **operational amplifiers** along with passive components (R and C) to achieve the desired filtering action.

**Two advantages over passive filters:**
1. **Gain is available** — the op-amp can amplify the signal in the passband (gain > 1), whereas passive filters always have insertion loss (gain < 1).
2. **No loading effect** — the high input impedance and low output impedance of the op-amp isolate the filter from the source and load, so the response is independent of source/load impedance.
3. *(Bonus point)*: No bulky inductors required — entire filter can be fabricated on a single IC.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a notch filter? Mention one practical application.**

**Model Answer:**

A **notch filter** (also called a **band-reject filter**) is a filter that rejects a very narrow band of frequencies while passing all other frequencies with little attenuation. It produces a sharp dip (the "notch") in its frequency response at the **notch frequency** $f_0$.

**Practical Application:**
- **50/60 Hz power-line hum removal** in biomedical instrumentation (ECG, EEG machines) to eliminate interference from mains electricity.
- *(Other valid applications)*: Audio feedback suppression in PA systems, removing carrier frequencies in communication receivers.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the gain $K = 1.586$** for Butterworth LPF is the most common error — students often design with $K = 1$ and end up with a Chebyshev-like underdamped response.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_L$, $f_H$, and $f_{center}$** in BPF problems — remember: $f_{center} = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$, **NOT** $\frac{f_L + f_H}{2}$.
> 3. **Not stating the roll-off rate** explicitly loses easy 1–2 marks in design problems.
> 4. **Forgetting the voltage follower / buffer** in Twin-T notch — examiners specifically look for the op-amp integration.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

**(a)** Design a second-order Butterworth low pass filter (Sallen-Key topology) with cutoff frequency $f_c = 2\text{ kHz}$. Assume equal component values. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Draw the circuit and explain the working of a Twin-T notch filter used to remove 50 Hz hum. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Recall the design equations for equal-component Sallen-Key Butterworth LPF:**

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Gain required:** $K = 1.586$ (Butterworth), so $\frac{R_f}{R_i} = 0.586$.

**Step 2 — Choose a convenient value for C:**
Let $C_1 = C_2 = C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\text{ F}$.

**Step 3 — Calculate R:**

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7958\text{ }\Omega \approx 7.96\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Choose feedback resistors:**
Let $R_i = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_f = 0.586 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 5.86\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Summarize the design:**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| $R_1 = R_2$ | **7.96 kΩ** |
| $C_1 = C_2$ | **0.01 μF** |
| $R_i$ | **10 kΩ** |
| $R_f$ | **5.86 kΩ** |
| Op-amp | **741 or LM358** |
| $f_c$ achieved | **2 kHz** |
| Roll-off | **−40 dB/decade** |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Working of Twin-T Notch Filter:**

1. The Twin-T network consists of **two T-sections in parallel**:
   - **T1 (high-pass T):** Two series resistors $R, R$ with a shunt capacitor $C$.
   - **T2 (low-pass T):** Two series capacitors $C, C$ with a shunt resistor $R$.

2. **At the notch frequency $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$:** The two T-networks produce signals at the output node that are **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase**. They **cancel out**, producing a deep null (the "notch") in the output.

3. **Below $f_0$:** T2 (low-pass T) dominates and passes the signal.
4. **Above $f_0$:** T1 (high-pass T) dominates and passes the signal.

5. The **op-amp voltage follower** at the output:
   - Prevents the load from loading the Twin-T network.
   - Provides a low-impedance output drive.
   - Improves the **Q factor** of the notch from 0.25 to 30–50.

6. **Application:** In a noisy ECG recording with 50 Hz mains interference, this filter is placed in the signal path to remove only the 50 Hz hum, while preserving the ECG waveform (0.5 Hz to 100 Hz).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the design of a first-order active low pass filter. Derive the expression for cutoff frequency. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design a wideband band pass filter with $f_L = 1\text{ kHz}$ and $f_H = 5\text{ kHz}$. Calculate the center frequency, bandwidth, and quality factor. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**First-Order Active LPF — Design and Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A first-order active LPF consists of a **passive RC low-pass network** followed by an **op-amp in non-inverting configuration**. The RC section sets the cutoff, and the op-amp provides gain and isolation.

**Step 2 — Derivation:**
The RC network forms a voltage divider. The transfer function is:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

With op-amp gain $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$:

$$H(s) = \frac{A_v}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 3 — Cutoff frequency:**
At $\omega = \omega_c$, the magnitude is $\frac{A_v}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., −3 dB point):

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 4 — Roll-off:**
Above $f_c$, the magnitude falls at **−20 dB/decade**.

**Step 5 — Numerical design example for $f_c = 1\text{ kHz}$:**
Choose $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1591.5\text{ }\Omega \approx 1.59\text{ k}\Omega$$

For unity gain: $R_f = 0$, $R_i = $ open (voltage follower) or $R_f = R_i$ for gain = 2.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Wideband BPF Design:**

**Given:** $f_L = 1\text{ kHz}$, $f_H = 5\text{ kHz}$

**Step 1 — Center frequency (geometric mean):**

$$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \sqrt{1000 \times 5000} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$f_0 = 2236.07\text{ Hz} \approx 2.236\text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L = 5000 - 1000 = 4000\text{ Hz} = 4\text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Quality factor:**

$$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{2236.07}{4000} = 0.559$$

**Step 4 — Implementation:**
- **HPF section:** 1st-order or 2nd-order with $f_c = f_L = 1\text{ kHz}$
- **LPF section:** 1st-order or 2nd-order with $f_c = f_H = 5\text{ kHz}$
- Cascade them with a unity-gain buffer between them.

**For 1st-order LPF section (cutoff = 5 kHz):**
Choose $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 10^{-8}} = 3183\text{ }\Omega \approx 3.18\text{ k}\Omega$$

**For 1st-order HPF section (cutoff = 1 kHz):**
Choose $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10^{-8}} = 15.92\text{ k}\Omega$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Active Filters**

- [x] An **active filter** uses an op-amp + RC network — provides gain and isolation.
- [x] **First-order LPF**: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, roll-off = **−20 dB/decade**.
- [x] **Second-order Butterworth Sallen-Key LPF** (equal R, C): $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, **Gain $K = 1.586$**, roll-off = **−40 dB/decade**.
- [x] Butterworth = **Maximally flat** passband; $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$.
- [x] **BPF center frequency** (wideband): $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ — **not** arithmetic mean.
- [x] **Q factor of BPF**: $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$.
- [x] **Twin-T Notch Filter** notch frequency: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
- [x] Notch filter $Q$ (passive) = 0.25; with op-amp follower, $Q$ rises to 30–50.
- [x] **Roll-off rates**: 1st-order → ±20 dB/dec; 2nd-order → ±40 dB/dec.
- [x] **Passband gain** of an active filter depends on the op-amp feedback network: $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$ (non-inverting).
- [x] The op-amp in an active filter serves **3 purposes**: gain, isolation, and impedance buffering.
- [x] **Common exam traps**: (i) using arithmetic mean instead of geometric mean for BPF center, (ii) forgetting $K = 1.586$ for Butterworth, (iii) not specifying roll-off in design answers.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always end your design answer with a **component-value table** and explicitly state the **roll-off rate**. This alone can earn 1–2 extra marks in KTU valuation, as examiners love structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feedback Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedback** in an electronic amplifier is the process of coupling a portion of the **output signal** back to the **input terminal** through an external network (called the **feedback network**), so that it alters the net input applied to the active amplifying device.

Mathematically, a feedback amplifier is a **closed-loop system** in which a sampling network senses the output, scales it by a **feedback factor** $\beta$, and a mixing network combines it with the source signal. The closed-loop transfer function is given by the classical **Black's Feedback Equation**:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where:
- $A$ = Open-loop gain of the basic amplifier
- $\beta$ = Reverse transmission (feedback factor) of the feedback network
- $A\beta$ = **Loop gain** (return ratio)
- $(1 + A\beta)$ = **Return difference** (desensitivity factor)

The **sign** of the feedback determines its nature:
- **Negative Feedback (NFB)**: The feedback signal *opposes* the input. For NFB, $\beta$ is such that $1 + A\beta > 1$, and $A_f < A$. The output is stabilized.
- **Positive Feedback (Regenerative)**: The feedback signal *aids* the input. Here $1 - A\beta$ is the denominator and the gain *increases*. Used in oscillators.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect you to clearly distinguish between **"Sampling"** (how output is sensed — series or shunt) and **"Mixing"** (how feedback is combined with input — series or shunt). This 2×2 matrix yields the **four canonical feedback topologies**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Steering Wheel of an Automobile

Imagine you are driving a car down a winding road.

- The **forward path** is the road itself — full of bumps, slopes, and turns.
- The car's **steering wheel** is the feedback element. Every tiny deviation you make with the wheel (output) is sensed, and a small corrective *counter-action* is applied by your hands (feedback) to the steering mechanism.
- If you turn the wheel too much, the feedback from the road (resistance from the tires) **reduces** the effective input to the wheel.
- This is exactly what negative feedback does in an amplifier: it **reduces gain** in exchange for **stability, linearity, bandwidth, and predictability**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook**: *"Feedback trades gain for goodness."* — The amplifier loses magnitude of gain but gains immunity to noise, distortion, parameter variations, and frequency limitations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Open-loop gain (A):** Typically $10^3$ to $10^5$ in discrete BJT/FET amplifiers.
- **Feedback factor ($\beta$):** Dimensionless for voltage-voltage feedback; has units otherwise.
- **Loop gain ($A\beta$):** Should be $> 10$ for effective feedback — a KTU rule of thumb.
- **Phase Margin ($\phi_m$):** A measure of stability; KTU expects $\phi_m \geq 45°$ for a stable closed-loop system.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** Approximately **constant** for a given amplifier when NFB is applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Feedback Block Diagram

The universal structure of a feedback amplifier consists of **two** fundamental blocks:

1. **The Basic Amplifier (A-block):** Provides the forward path gain $A$, with input impedance $Z_i$ and output impedance $Z_o$.
2. **The Feedback Network ($\beta$-block):** A passive (usually resistive) network that senses the output and produces a feedback voltage/current proportional to it.

A **mixing/subtraction node** (the comparator or $\Sigma$-node) algebraically subtracts the feedback signal from the source signal to produce the **error signal** $x_d = x_s - x_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions for Ideal Block-Diagram Analysis (Two-Port Network Theorem):**
> 1. The input port of the feedback network does **not load** the output port of the basic amplifier (unilateral forward path).
> 2. The output port of the feedback network does **not load** the input port of the basic amplifier (unilateral feedback path).
> 3. The forward transmission through the $\beta$-network is negligible.
> 4. The reverse transmission through the A-network is negligible.

These assumptions allow the clean two-port decomposition that KTU problems expect.

## 2.2 Classification of Feedback Topologies — The Four Canonical Forms

The combination of **(a) output sampling** and **(b) input mixing** yields the four feedback topologies. The KTU syllabus places heavy emphasis on identifying the topology and writing the correct $A$, $\beta$, $Z_i$, and $Z_o$ for each.

| Topology Name | Output Sampling | Input Mixing | Input Signal Type | Output Signal Type | Ideal Amplifier Type | $Z_{if}$ Behavior | $Z_{of}$ Behavior |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Voltage-Series** (Series-Shunt) | Voltage (Shunt at output) | Series at input | Voltage $v_s$ | Voltage $v_o$ | Voltage Amplifier ($A_v$) | $Z_i$ Increases | $Z_o$ Decreases |
| **Voltage-Shunt** (Shunt-Shunt) | Voltage (Shunt at output) | Shunt at input | Current $i_s$ | Voltage $v_o$ | Transresistance ($A_r = v_o/i_s$) | $Z_i$ Decreases | $Z_o$ Decreases |
| **Current-Series** (Series-Series) | Current (Series at output) | Series at input | Voltage $v_s$ | Current $i_o$ | Transconductance ($A_g = i_o/v_s$) | $Z_i$ Increases | $Z_o$ Increases |
| **Current-Shunt** (Shunt-Series) | Current (Series at output) | Shunt at input | Current $i_s$ | Current $i_o$ | Current Amplifier ($A_i$) | $Z_i$ Decreases | $Z_o$ Increases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid:** *"Mixing is at the input; Sampling is at the output."* Series mixing means the feedback voltage is subtracted from the source voltage. Shunt mixing means the feedback current is subtracted from the source current.

## 2.3 Effects of Negative Feedback (NFB) — The "Four Improvements and Two Penalties"

| Parameter | With NFB | Formula | Interpretation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Gain** | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | Predictable, stabilized value |
| **Sensitivity to parameter variations** | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $S_A^{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$ | Robust against temperature, device aging |
| **Bandwidth** | Increases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$ | Higher usable frequency range |
| **Gain-Bandwidth Product** | **Constant** (for dominant-pole case) | $A_f \cdot f_{Hf} = A \cdot f_H$ | Fundamental invariant |
| **Input Impedance (Series mixing)** | Increases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $Z_{if} = Z_i(1+A\beta)$ | Better voltage amplifier loading |
| **Input Impedance (Shunt mixing)** | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $Z_{if} = \dfrac{Z_i}{1+A\beta}$ | Better current amplifier loading |
| **Output Impedance (Voltage sampling)** | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1+A\beta}$ | Better voltage source behavior |
| **Output Impedance (Current sampling)** | Increases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $Z_{of} = Z_o(1+A\beta)$ | Better current source behavior |
| **Nonlinear Distortion** | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1+A\beta}$ | Improved linearity |
| **Internal Noise** | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | $N_f = \dfrac{N}{1+A\beta}$ | Cleaner signal |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Two Penalties of NFB:**
> 1. **Loss of Gain** — must be compensated by cascading additional stages.
> 2. **Potential Instability / Oscillation** — if extra phase shifts in the loop cause $1 + A\beta = 0$ at some frequency. This is the **Barkhausen Instability Condition**.

## 2.4 The Barkhausen Criterion (Sustained Oscillation)

For a feedback system to oscillate, **two** conditions must be met simultaneously:

$$|A\beta| = 1 \quad \text{(Magnitude condition)}$$

$$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360° \quad \text{(Phase condition)}$$

If $|A\beta| > 1$ with the phase condition satisfied, the oscillation **grows** until device non-linearity clips it.

> [!TIP]
> **Stability Criterion (Nyquist):** A feedback amplifier is stable if the Nyquist plot of $A\beta$ does **NOT enclose** the critical point $(-1, 0)$ in the complex plane.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifiers** use **voltage-series NFB** to lower output impedance and reduce hum/distortion.
- **Op-amps** use **voltage-shunt NFB** to set a precise closed-loop gain (e.g., the inverting/non-inverting configurations).
- **Transconductance stages** (e.g., in OTAs) inherently use **current-series feedback**.
- **Current sources / active loads** rely on **current-shunt feedback** to deliver constant $I_o$ independent of load.
- **RF and instrumentation amplifiers** rely on carefully designed NFB to achieve wide bandwidth, high CMRR, and low offset drift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Gain (Black's Equation)

Consider the standard single-loop feedback block diagram. Let $x_s$ be the source signal, $x_d$ the difference (error) signal, $x_f$ the feedback signal, and $x_o$ the output signal.

**Step 1 — Define the open-loop gain:**

$$x_o = A \cdot x_d$$

**Step 2 — Define the feedback factor:**

$$x_f = \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 3 — Define the mixing (subtraction) at the input node:**

$$x_d = x_s - x_f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$x_d = x_s - \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 1:**

$$x_o = A \cdot (x_s - \beta \cdot x_o)$$

**Step 6 — Distribute $A$:**

$$x_o = A \cdot x_s - A\beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 7 — Collect the $x_o$ terms on the left side:**

$$x_o + A\beta \cdot x_o = A \cdot x_s$$

$$x_o \cdot (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot x_s$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_f = x_o / x_s$:**

$$A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

This is **Black's Feedback Equation** — the cornerstone of every KTU feedback problem. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Negative Feedback on Input Impedance (Series Mixing)

When the feedback signal is **subtracted in series** with the source, the input port of the basic amplifier sees a smaller effective voltage for a given source voltage.

**Step 1 — Original input current (no feedback):**

$$i_i = \frac{v_i}{Z_i}$$

**Step 2 — With series NFB, the voltage actually appearing across the input terminals of the A-block is reduced by the feedback voltage $v_f$:**

$$v_i' = v_s - v_f = v_s - \beta \cdot v_o = v_s - A\beta \cdot v_i'$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_i'$ in terms of $v_s$:**

$$v_i' + A\beta \cdot v_i' = v_s$$

$$v_i' (1 + A\beta) = v_s$$

$$v_i' = \frac{v_s}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 4 — The input current is still determined by $Z_i$ (since current flows into the same port):**

$$i_i = \frac{v_i'}{Z_i} = \frac{v_s}{(1 + A\beta) Z_i}$$

**Step 5 — Therefore the effective input impedance with feedback is:**

$$Z_{if} = \frac{v_s}{i_i} = (1 + A\beta) \cdot Z_i$$

Series mixing **increases** $Z_i$ by the desensitivity factor — an ideal characteristic for a **voltage amplifier** (high $Z_{if}$ loads the source minimally). $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Negative Feedback on Output Impedance (Voltage Sampling)

For voltage sampling, the feedback network senses $v_o$. The output stage can be modelled as a Thevenin equivalent with internal source $A v_d$ and series output impedance $Z_o$. The feedback samples $v_o$ and feeds it back.

**Step 1 — Output voltage with feedback, by definition:**

$$v_o = A \cdot v_d - i_o \cdot Z_o$$

where $i_o$ is the load current drawn from the output.

**Step 2 — The difference voltage $v_d$ is reduced by NFB:**

$$v_d = \frac{v_s}{1 + A\beta} \quad \text{(from Section 3.2 for series case)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$v_o = A \cdot \frac{v_s}{1+A\beta} - i_o \cdot Z_o$$

**Step 4 — Define the open-circuit voltage (set $i_o = 0$):**

$$v_{oc} = \frac{A \cdot v_s}{1+A\beta}$$

**Step 5 — To find $Z_{of}$, we apply a test current $i_t$ at the output (with $v_s = 0$):**

With $v_s = 0$, the feedback loop tries to maintain $v_o = 0$. Thevenin analysis yields:

$$Z_{of} = \frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

Voltage sampling **decreases** $Z_o$ — a desirable feature for a **voltage source** (low $Z_{of}$ maintains constant $v_o$ under varying load). $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Gain Sensitivity Reduction

**Step 1 — From Black's equation, $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$:**

Take the differential $dA_f$ with respect to a small change $dA$ in $A$, holding $\beta$ constant.

$$dA_f = \frac{(1+A\beta) \cdot dA - A \cdot \beta \cdot dA}{(1+A\beta)^2} = \frac{dA}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Form the relative sensitivity:**

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{dA}{(1+A\beta)^2} \cdot \frac{1+A\beta}{A} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the sensitivity function is:**

$$S_A^{A_f} = \frac{dA_f / A_f}{dA / A} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}$$

A **10% change** in $A$ produces only a **1% change** in $A_f$ if $1 + A\beta = 10$. This is why precision amplifier stages are heavily NFB-stabilized. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Bandwidth Extension (Single-Pole Amplifier)

Assume the open-loop amplifier has a single-pole transfer function:

$$A(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

where $A_0$ is the midband gain and $\omega_H$ is the upper 3-dB cutoff.

**Step 1 — Apply Black's equation:**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A(s)}{1 + A(s)\beta} = \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1+s/\omega_H}}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{1+s/\omega_H}}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + s/\omega_H)$:**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H + A_0 \beta} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H (1+A_0\beta)}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the new midband gain and the new upper cutoff:**

$$A_{f0} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0 \beta} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1 + A_0 \beta)$$

**Step 4 — Gain-Bandwidth Product is invariant:**

$$A_{f0} \cdot \omega_{Hf} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta} \cdot \omega_H (1+A_0\beta) = A_0 \cdot \omega_H = \text{Gain-Bandwidth Product}$$

This invariant is the **fundamental reason** KTU students should always consider a higher-gain active device (e.g., op-amp) when demanding wide bandwidth NFB. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Feedback Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Signal xs] --> mixerNode{Subtraction Node Sigma}
    mixerNode -- Error xd --> Ablock[Basic Amplifier Block A]
    Ablock -- Forward xo --> outputNode[Output Port xo]
    outputNode --> loadPort[Load ZL]
    outputNode -. Sample .-> betaBlock[Feedback Network Beta]
    betaBlock -. Feedback xf .-> mixerNode
    style src fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style mixerNode fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style Ablock fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34
    style betaBlock fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a71d2a
    style outputNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style loadPort fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
```

**Reading the diagram:** The source $x_s$ enters the subtraction node $\Sigma$. The difference $x_d = x_s - x_f$ is amplified by $A$ to produce $x_o$. A portion $\beta x_o$ is fed back and subtracted from $x_s$. This is the **single-loop feedback** that underlies every KTU feedback-amplifier problem.

## 4.2 The Four Feedback Topologies — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Start Output Sampling and Input Mixing] --> q1{How is output sampled?}
    q1 -- Voltage Parallel Shunt --> v_samp[Output is Voltage]
    q1 -- Current Series --> i_samp[Output is Current]
    v_samp --> q2v{How is feedback combined at input?}
    i_samp --> q2i{How is feedback combined at input?}
    q2v -- Series Voltage --> top1[Voltage Series: Voltage Amplifier]
    q2v -- Shunt Current --> top2[Voltage Shunt: Transresistance Amplifier]
    q2i -- Series Voltage --> top3[Current Series: Transconductance Amplifier]
    q2i -- Shunt Current --> top4[Current Shunt: Current Amplifier]
    top1 --> z1[Zif increases by 1 plus AB; Zof decreases]
    top2 --> z2[Zif decreases by 1 plus AB; Zof decreases]
    top3 --> z3[Zif increases by 1 plus AB; Zof increases]
    top4 --> z4[Zif decreases by 1 plus AB; Zof increases]
    style start fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style top1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style top2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34
    style top3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a71d2a
    style top4 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
```

## 4.3 Stability Analysis Flow — Nyquist Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plot Nyquist of AB] --> B{Does the plot encircle the point -1 plus j0?}
    B -- No --> C[System is STABLE for all gains]
    B -- Yes --> D{Does it encircle exactly once?}
    D -- Yes --> E[UNSTABLE: 1 closed loop pole in RHP]
    D -- Conditional --> F[Examine Phase Margin]
    F --> G{Phase margin greater than 45 degrees?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Marginally stable but acceptable]
    G -- No --> I[Compensate with dominant pole or lead network]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a71d2a
    style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style I fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a71d2a
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Real Feedback Op-Amp Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vs[Source Vs] --> rin[Input Resistor Rin]
    rin --> inNode[Inverting Node of Op Amp]
    inNode --> opamp[Open Loop Op Amp Aol]
    opamp --> out[Output Vo]
    out --> load[Load RL]
    out --> fbNet[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    fbNet --> inNode
    nonInv[Non Inverting Input] --> gnd[Ground Reference]
    style vs fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style opamp fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34
    style fbNet fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a71d2a
    style out fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

**Interpretation:** The op-amp's open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ is the A-block. The resistor divider formed by $R_f$ and $R_{in}$ is the $\beta$-network. By Black's equation, $A_f \approx -R_f / R_{in}$ when $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$. This is the canonical **voltage-shunt (shunt-shunt) feedback** topology — the most common KTU op-amp circuit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define negative feedback in an amplifier. Mention any two merits of applying negative feedback.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Negative feedback is the technique of returning a fraction of the output signal to the input in **opposition** to the applied input signal, such that the feedback signal reduces the effective input to the amplifier.

The closed-loop gain is given by Black's equation:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where $1 + A\beta > 1$ for negative feedback.

**Two Merits:**
1. **Gain stabilization** — Sensitivity to device parameter variations is reduced by a factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
2. **Reduction of nonlinear distortion** — Harmonic distortion components are reduced by the same factor $(1 + A\beta)$.

*(Other acceptable merits: bandwidth extension, modification of input/output impedances, noise reduction.)* **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between voltage-series and current-shunt feedback configurations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Voltage-Series Feedback | Current-Shunt Feedback |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Output sampling | Voltage (shunt) | Current (series) |
| Input mixing | Series (voltage subtraction) | Shunt (current subtraction) |
| Ideal amplifier | Voltage amplifier | Current amplifier |
| Effect on $Z_i$ | Increases by $(1 + A\beta)$ | Decreases by $(1 + A\beta)$ |
| Effect on $Z_o$ | Decreases by $(1 + A\beta)$ | Increases by $(1 + A\beta)$ |
| Transfer quantity | $A_v = v_o / v_s$ | $A_i = i_o / i_s$ |

**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier. Discuss the effect of negative feedback on **(i)** gain sensitivity, **(ii)** bandwidth, and **(iii)** input impedance when the feedback is connected in series with the input. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the four types of feedback topologies. For each topology, state the corresponding ideal amplifier type and the effect on input and output impedances. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Derivation of Closed-Loop Gain:**

From the standard feedback block diagram, with $x_s$ the source, $x_d$ the error, $x_o$ the output, and $x_f = \beta x_o$ the feedback:

$$x_o = A x_d, \quad x_d = x_s - x_f, \quad x_f = \beta x_o$$

Combining these:

$$x_o = A(x_s - \beta x_o) \implies x_o(1 + A\beta) = A x_s \implies A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**(i) Gain Sensitivity:**

Differentiating $A_f$ w.r.t. $A$ (with $\beta$ constant) and simplifying:

$$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_A^{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}$$

NFB reduces sensitivity by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$. **(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Bandwidth:**

For a single-pole amplifier, $A(s) = A_0 / (1 + s/\omega_H)$. Substituting into Black's equation and simplifying:

$$\omega_{Hf} = \omega_H (1 + A_0 \beta)$$

The new bandwidth exceeds the original by the desensitivity factor. **(1.5 Marks)**

**(iii) Input Impedance (Series Mixing):**

With series NFB, the voltage actually driving the A-block is reduced by $(1 + A\beta)$:

$$Z_{if} = Z_i (1 + A\beta)$$

This increase is essential for voltage amplifiers to avoid loading the source. **(1.5 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The four feedback topologies are obtained from the two choices of **output sampling** (voltage or current) and two choices of **input mixing** (series or shunt).

**Block Diagram of Each Topology:**

1. **Voltage-Series (Series-Shunt):** Output is sensed in parallel (voltage); feedback is subtracted in series at the input. **Ideal amplifier: Voltage amplifier.** $Z_{if} = Z_i(1+A\beta)$; $Z_{of} = Z_o/(1+A\beta)$.

2. **Voltage-Shunt (Shunt-Shunt):** Output is sensed in parallel; feedback is subtracted as a current at the input. **Ideal amplifier: Transresistance amplifier** ($A_r = v_o / i_s$). $Z_{if} = Z_i/(1+A\beta)$; $Z_{of} = Z_o/(1+A\beta)$.

3. **Current-Series (Series-Series):** Output is sensed in series (current); feedback is subtracted as a voltage at the input. **Ideal amplifier: Transconductance amplifier** ($A_g = i_o / v_s$). $Z_{if} = Z_i(1+A\beta)$; $Z_{of} = Z_o(1+A\beta)$.

4. **Current-Shunt (Shunt-Series):** Output is sensed in series; feedback is subtracted as a current. **Ideal amplifier: Current amplifier** ($A_i = i_o / i_s$). $Z_{if} = Z_i/(1+A\beta)$; $Z_{of} = Z_o(1+A\beta)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** An amplifier with open-loop gain $A = 1000 \pm 200$ (i.e., gain varies from 800 to 1200 due to temperature) and a feedback factor $\beta = 0.01$ is operated in a closed loop. Calculate: **(i)** the nominal closed-loop gain, **(ii)** the range of variation of the closed-loop gain, and **(iii)** the percentage reduction in sensitivity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained oscillation. Under what condition does a feedback amplifier become unstable? Mention two methods of ensuring stability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $A = 1000$ (nominal), $\Delta A = \pm 200$, $\beta = 0.01$.

**(i) Nominal closed-loop gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{1000}{1 + 1000 \times 0.01} = \frac{1000}{1 + 10} = \frac{1000}{11} \approx 90.91$$

**(3 Marks)**

**(ii) Range of variation of $A_f$:**

Lower bound: $A = 800$:

$$A_{f,\min} = \frac{800}{1 + 800 \times 0.01} = \frac{800}{9} \approx 88.89$$

Upper bound: $A = 1200$:

$$A_{f,\max} = \frac{1200}{1 + 1200 \times 0.01} = \frac{1200}{13} \approx 92.31$$

**Range of variation:** $88.89$ to $92.31$ — a swing of about $\pm 1.76$ around $90.91$, compared to the open-loop swing of $\pm 200$ around $1000$. **(2 Marks)**

**(iii) Percentage reduction in sensitivity:**

$$S_A^{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 9.09\%$$

So the sensitivity is reduced from 100% (open-loop) to 9.09% (closed-loop), a reduction of about **90.91%**. **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Barkhausen Criterion (Sustained Oscillation):**

For a feedback system to produce sustained oscillations, **both** of the following must be satisfied at the oscillation frequency $f_o$:

1. **Magnitude condition:** $|A\beta| = 1$
2. **Phase condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or an integer multiple of $360°$)

Under these conditions, the closed-loop gain $A_f = A/(1 + A\beta)$ becomes **infinite**, meaning the amplifier produces a finite output even with zero input — i.e., it oscillates. **(3 Marks)**

**Instability Condition:**

A feedback amplifier becomes unstable if the **return difference** $1 + A\beta = 0$ at any real positive frequency. This corresponds to the Nyquist plot of $A\beta$ enclosing the critical point $-1 + j0$ in the complex plane. Physically, extra phase shifts introduced by reactive components (capacitors, inductors, transistor transit times) cause the originally negative feedback to flip phase and become positive. **(2 Marks)**

**Two Methods of Ensuring Stability:**

1. **Dominant-Pole Compensation (Lag Compensation):** Add a low-frequency pole (e.g., a Miller capacitor across the gain stage) to ensure the open-loop gain rolls off to **unity** ($0$ dB) at a frequency well before the second pole contributes significant phase shift. Typical phase margin: $45°$ to $60°$.

2. **Lead Compensation:** Introduce a zero (e.g., a resistor in series with the Miller capacitor) that cancels one of the existing poles, advancing the phase and increasing the phase margin before the loop gain reaches $0$ dB. **(2 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Feedback Concepts:**
> 1. **Topology Confusion** — Mixing up *sampling* (output) with *mixing* (input). Always label them separately in the answer.
> 2. **Sign Error in Black's Equation** — Using $1 - A\beta$ for negative feedback. The correct sign is $+A\beta$ because the feedback signal is *subtracted*, giving $x_d = x_s - x_f$ and hence the denominator $1 + A\beta$.
> 3. **Forgetting Units** — The feedback factor $\beta$ is **not always dimensionless**. In transresistance and transconductance amplifiers, $\beta$ has units ($\Omega$ or $\mho$). A unit mismatch is a red flag to the examiner.
> 4. **Omitting $\beta$-loading corrections** — In a precise analysis, the loading effect of the feedback network on the input and output ports of the A-block must be included. The two-port analysis is **not optional** for full marks.
> 5. **Stability Confusion** — Confusing the **Barkhausen criterion** (sine-wave oscillator condition) with the **Nyquist stability criterion** (general closed-loop stability). They are related but distinct.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Black's feedback equation:** $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$ — the single most important equation in this module.
- **Loop gain:** $A\beta$ — the "engine" of all feedback effects.
- **Desensitivity factor:** $1 + A\beta$ — improves linearity, bandwidth, and impedances; reduces gain.
- **Series mixing** $\Rightarrow$ input impedance **increases** by $(1 + A\beta)$. **Shunt mixing** $\Rightarrow$ it **decreases**.
- **Voltage sampling** $\Rightarrow$ output impedance **decreases**. **Current sampling** $\Rightarrow$ it **increases**.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product is invariant** for a dominant-pole amplifier under NFB.
- **Four topologies:** Voltage-Series (Voltage amplifier), Voltage-Shunt (Transresistance), Current-Series (Transconductance), Current-Shunt (Current amplifier).
- **Barkhausen criterion** for oscillation: $|A\beta| = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or multiples of $360°$).
- **Stability check:** Use Nyquist plot; ensure $\angle A\beta$ does **not** reach $-180°$ before $|A\beta|$ drops below unity.
- **Compensation methods:** Dominant-pole (lag) and lead compensation are the two most common KTU-expected techniques.
- **Practical note:** All real op-amp configurations (inverting, non-inverting, integrator, differentiator) are concrete realizations of one of the four feedback topologies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Configurations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Configurations

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) amplifier analysis**, the term **"Basic Configurations"** refers to the three fundamental single-stage amplifier topologies obtained by selecting one of the three transistor terminals (Emitter, Base, or Collector) as the common reference point between the input and output ports. The three canonical configurations defined by the **KTU 2024 Analog Electronics syllabus** are:

1. **Common Emitter (CE) Configuration**
2. **Common Base (CB) Configuration**
3. **Common Collector (CC) Configuration** — also known as the *Emitter Follower*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBMT302 – Module 4):** Students are expected to derive the small-signal voltage gain ($A_v$), current gain ($A_i$), input resistance ($R_i$), and output resistance ($R_o$) for each of the three basic configurations using the **hybrid-$\pi$ / $r$-parameter model**, and to compare their relative merits in terms of phase reversal, impedance levels, and applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention:** The terminal labelled *common* is the one shared (typically AC-grounded) between the input loop and the output loop. It is *not* always physically connected to ground — it merely serves as the reference node for both input and output signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three-Way Water Tap

Imagine a **water pipeline system** where a control valve regulates the flow:

- **Common Emitter (CE):** Think of it as a **medium-pressure spray nozzle** — you apply a small twist at the input handle (Base) and get a large, strong, *inverted-direction* jet of water out (Collector). It is the *workhorse* of amplifier design.

- **Common Base (CB):** Imagine a **high-pressure fire hose with a foot pedal** — the input control (Emitter) is referenced to a fixed base, the output is very strong, but the input "leakage" is minimal. It is *fast* and used for high-frequency RF work.

- **Common Collector (CC):** Picture an **electrical buffer / hydraulic damper** — the output pressure closely *follows* your input pressure (no inversion), and it can drive a heavy load without "back-pressuring" the delicate control valve. This is the *Emitter Follower*, used for impedance matching.

## 1.3 Why These Three? — The Underlying Symmetry

A BJT has **three terminals**. For a two-port network representation, the *common* terminal acts as the shared reference. There are therefore exactly **three** such single-stage configurations, and the choice between them dictates the amplifier's behaviour in terms of:

| Design Property | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Input Impedance** | Determines loading on the source |
| **Output Impedance** | Determines driving capability |
| **Voltage / Current Gain** | Defines amplification type |
| **Phase Relationship** | Critical for feedback and oscillator design |
| **Bandwidth** | Limits high-frequency performance |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always redraw the small-signal equivalent circuit with the common terminal explicitly AC-grounded. Many students lose marks by failing to identify the correct input/output ports in the AC equivalent.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Small-Signal $r_e$ (T-Model) Framework

To analyse all three configurations uniformly, we use the **simplified T-model** of the BJT, valid for low-frequency, small-signal operation. The four fundamental small-signal resistances are:

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \frac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})}$$

$$r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e$$

$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C} \quad \text{(Early resistance, often neglected at low frequencies)}$$

Where:
- $V_T = kT/q \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at **300 K (room temperature)** — the **thermal voltage**.
- $V_A$ is the **Early voltage** (typically 50 V – 200 V for a standard BJT).
- $\beta$ is the **common-emitter AC current gain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $26\,\text{mV}$?** It comes from $V_T = kT/q$ at room temperature ($T = 300$ K, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C). It is a **physical constant of nature** for silicon semiconductors at standard conditions.

## 2.2 Configuration-Wise Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Common Emitter (CE) — The Workhorse

- **Input Port:** Base–Emitter | **Output Port:** Collector–Emitter
- **Input Signal** $v_i$ is applied at the Base, **Output Signal** $v_o$ is taken at the Collector.
- Emitter is AC-grounded (large bypass capacitor $C_E$).
- The output voltage is **180° out of phase** with the input — a phase reversal occurs because increasing $v_{BE}$ increases $i_C$, which in turn drops more voltage across $R_C$, *lowering* $v_C$.

### 2.2.2 Common Base (CB) — The High-Frequency Specialist

- **Input Port:** Emitter–Base | **Output Port:** Collector–Base
- **Input Signal** $v_i$ is applied at the Emitter, **Output Signal** $v_o$ is taken at the Collector.
- Base is AC-grounded.
- The output voltage is **in phase (0°)** with the input.
- Excellent for **RF and high-frequency** applications because the base is screened, minimising the **Miller effect**.

### 2.2.3 Common Collector (CC) — The Impedance Matcher

- **Input Port:** Base–Collector | **Output Port:** Emitter–Collector
- **Input Signal** $v_i$ is applied at the Base, **Output Signal** $v_o$ is taken at the Emitter.
- Collector is AC-grounded.
- The output voltage is **in phase (0°)** with the input and slightly less than 1 in magnitude — hence the name **"Emitter Follower"**.
- Provides **high input impedance** and **low output impedance** — ideal for **buffering**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — The Three Configurations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most high-yield reference** for the KTU Module 4 board exam. Memorise it. Substitute in the appropriate formula and method marks are guaranteed.

| Parameter | Common Emitter (CE) | Common Base (CB) | Common Collector (CC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Gain $A_i$** | $A_i \approx \beta$ (large, $\sim$ 100) | $A_i = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \approx 1$ (small, $<1$) | $A_i \approx \beta + 1$ (large) |
| **Voltage Gain $A_v$** | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ (large, with negative sign) | $A_v = \dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ (large, positive) | $A_v = \dfrac{(\beta+1)R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E} \approx 1$ |
| **Input Resistance $R_i$** | $R_i = r_\pi = \beta r_e$ (medium) | $R_i = r_e$ (very low) | $R_i = r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$ (very high) |
| **Output Resistance $R_o$** | $R_o = R_C$ (medium-high) | $R_o = R_C$ (medium-high) | $R_o = R_E \Vert \dfrac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$ (very low) |
| **Phase Shift ($v_o$ vs $v_i$)** | **180°** (inverted) | **0°** (in-phase) | **0°** (in-phase) |
| **Power Gain** | **High** | Medium | Medium |
| **Typical Use** | General-purpose voltage amplifier | RF / High-frequency amp, current buffer | Impedance buffer, driver stage |
| **Bandwidth** | Medium (Miller-limited) | **Widest** | Medium-Wide |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical pipe rule:** When writing inline expressions, use `\vert` or `\mid` for *absolute value / parallel* notation (e.g., $R_E \Vert R_s$). Never type a bare `|` inside markdown table cells, as it breaks the table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Configuration | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **CE** | Audio preamplifiers, op-amp gain stages, analog signal conditioning in biomedical sensors (ECG, EEG front-ends) |
| **CB** | RF amplifiers in radio receivers, microwave amplifiers, cascode stages in ICs (used together with CE) |
| **CC** | Output buffer in op-amps (e.g., the **LM386** audio driver), cable-driving stages, Zener-regulated voltage references, headphone amplifiers |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Note:** The internal output stage of nearly every commercial op-amp (e.g., **µA741, TL081, LM358**) uses a **CC (emitter follower) configuration** as the final buffer because it can drive low-impedance loads (like an 8 Ω speaker) without significant signal loss.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **full derivations** required for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly, with a textual explanation following each transition.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Common Emitter (CE) — Voltage Gain $A_v$, Input Resistance $R_i$, Current Gain $A_i$

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup

Consider a CE amplifier with:
- Collector resistor $R_C$
- Emitter resistor $R_E$ (with bypass capacitor $C_E$ across it for AC analysis → effectively shorted)
- Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ (short at signal frequency)
- Source $v_s$ with source resistance $R_s$

### 3.1.2 AC Equivalent (Small-Signal T-Model)

Replace the BJT with its T-model: a resistance $r_\pi$ between Base and Emitter, a dependent current source $\beta i_b$ from Collector to Emitter, and $r_e$ from Emitter to ground.

With $C_E$ shorted, the Emitter is directly at AC ground.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Current Gain $A_i$

Define $A_i = \dfrac{i_o}{i_i}$ where $i_i = i_b$ and $i_o = i_c = \beta i_b$.

Therefore:

$$A_i = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \beta$$

> **Explanation:** The current gain is simply the AC $\beta$ of the transistor. This is a *direct* consequence of the BJT's current-controlled current-source nature.

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_i$

Looking into the Base, the resistance is the resistance seen between Base and Emitter (since Emitter is AC ground), which is $r_\pi$.

$$R_i = r_\pi = \beta r_e$$

> **Explanation:** The factor $\beta$ multiplies the intrinsic emitter resistance $r_e$ when viewed from the Base, because a small base current produces a much larger emitter current.

---

### 3.1.5 Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$

The input voltage at the Base (assuming $R_s \ll R_i$ for simplicity) is:

$$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi$$

The output voltage at the Collector is:

$$v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C = -\beta i_b \cdot R_C$$

The negative sign arises because an increase in collector current increases the voltage drop across $R_C$, leaving less voltage at the collector node (with $V_{CC}$ fixed at the top).

Therefore:

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{-\beta i_b R_C}{i_b r_\pi} = -\frac{\beta R_C}{r_\pi}$$

Substituting $r_\pi = \beta r_e$:

$$A_v = -\frac{\beta R_C}{\beta r_e} = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

> **Final CE Voltage Gain:**
> $$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}}$$

> **Explanation:** The voltage gain is set by the *ratio* of the collector resistor to the intrinsic emitter resistance. The negative sign encodes the **180° phase inversion** — a hallmark of CE amplifiers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Common Base (CB) — Voltage Gain $A_v$, Input Resistance $R_i$

### 3.2.1 AC Equivalent

In the CB configuration, the **Base is AC-grounded**. The input signal $v_i$ is applied between the Emitter and Base, and the output $v_o$ is taken between the Collector and Base.

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_i$

Looking into the Emitter, the current entering the Emitter is $i_e = i_b + i_c = i_b + \beta i_b = (\beta+1) i_b$.

The voltage across the input port (Emitter to Base) is:

$$v_i = i_e \cdot r_e = (\beta+1) i_b \cdot r_e$$

So the resistance seen looking into the Emitter is:

$$R_i = \frac{v_i}{i_e} = \frac{(\beta+1) i_b r_e}{(\beta+1) i_b} = r_e$$

> **Final CB Input Resistance:**
> $$\boxed{R_i = r_e}$$

> **Explanation:** Looking into the Emitter, the resistance is just $r_e$ — typically a few ohms to tens of ohms. This *very low* input resistance is the defining characteristic of the CB stage and the reason it is used as a **current buffer**.

---

### 3.2.3 Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$

The input voltage is $v_i = i_e \cdot r_e$ (with Emitter current flowing *into* the emitter for the conventional sign convention).

The output voltage is $v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C$ (collector current flows from $V_{CC}$ through $R_C$ into the collector; an increase in $i_c$ drops more voltage across $R_C$, lowering $v_o$).

But $i_c \approx i_e$ (since $i_b$ is very small and $i_c = \alpha i_e$ with $\alpha \approx 1$).

Therefore:

$$v_o = -\alpha \, i_e \, R_C \approx -i_e R_C$$

And:

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{-i_e R_C}{i_e r_e} = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

> **Final CB Voltage Gain:**
> $$\boxed{A_v = +\frac{R_C}{r_e}} \quad \text{(non-inverting)}$$

> **Explanation:** Wait — the negative signs actually *cancel* in the sign convention, because the direction of $i_e$ entering the Emitter is opposite to the direction of $i_c$ leaving the Collector. The net result is a **positive (in-phase)** voltage gain. This is the signature of the CB configuration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Common Collector (CC) — Voltage Gain $A_v$, Input Resistance $R_i$, Output Resistance $R_o$

### 3.3.1 AC Equivalent

In the CC configuration, the **Collector is AC-grounded**. The input signal $v_i$ is applied at the Base, and the output $v_o$ is taken at the Emitter (across $R_E$).

### 3.3.2 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_i$

The input current is $i_b$. The voltage at the Base relative to the AC ground is $v_i$, and the voltage at the Emitter is $v_o$.

KVL around the Base–Emitter–$R_E$ loop:

$$v_i = v_{BE} + v_o$$

$$v_{BE} = i_b \cdot r_\pi$$

The Emitter current is $i_e = (\beta+1) i_b$, and the Emitter voltage is $v_o = i_e R_E = (\beta+1) i_b R_E$.

Therefore:

$$v_i = i_b r_\pi + (\beta+1) i_b R_E = i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \right]$$

$$R_i = \frac{v_i}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$$

> **Final CC Input Resistance:**
> $$\boxed{R_i = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \approx (\beta+1) R_E}$$

> **Explanation:** The input resistance is *boosted* by a factor of $(\beta+1)$ over the emitter resistor. This is the secret behind the CC stage's high input impedance. For example, with $R_E = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $\beta = 100$, $R_i \approx 470\,\text{k}\Omega$ — a very high value.

---

### 3.3.3 Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$

$$v_o = (\beta+1) i_b R_E$$

$$v_i = i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \right]$$

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{(\beta+1) i_b R_E}{i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \right]} = \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

> **Final CC Voltage Gain:**
> $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E} \approx 1}$$

> **Explanation:** The voltage gain is always **less than 1** and **positive**. The output *follows* the input, hence the name "Emitter Follower." It is a near-unity-gain buffer.

---

### 3.3.4 Derivation of Output Resistance $R_o$

To find $R_o$, set the source $v_s = 0$ (short-circuit the source), apply a test voltage $v_t$ at the output (Emitter), and measure the test current $i_t$.

With $v_s = 0$, looking back from the Emitter, we see:
- $R_E$ to AC ground (in parallel with whatever we find)
- Looking into the Emitter of the BJT: The Thevenin resistance seen looking into the Emitter (with Base connected to ground through $R_s$) is:

$$R_{o,\text{BJT}} = \frac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of $R_{o,\text{BJT}}$:** When a resistance $R_s$ is at the Base (and Base is grounded through it for AC), the resistance looking into the Emitter is the *impedance reflection rule*: $R_{in,\text{Emitter}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{base,to-ground}}}{\beta + 1}$. Here, the base-to-ground resistance is $R_s + r_\pi$, hence the formula.

The total output resistance is the parallel combination:

$$R_o = R_E \Vert \frac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}$$

> **Final CC Output Resistance:**
> $$\boxed{R_o = R_E \,\Vert\, \frac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta + 1} \approx \frac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}}$$

> **Explanation:** The output resistance is *very low* (often a few ohms) — making the CC stage an excellent **current booster** that can drive low-impedance loads (like 8 Ω speakers) from a high-impedance source.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Summary Table — All Three Configurations

| Property | CE | CB | CC |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_v$ magnitude | $\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ (large) | $\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ (large) | $\approx 1$ |
| $A_i$ | $\beta$ | $\approx 1$ | $\beta+1$ |
| $R_i$ | $\beta r_e$ (medium) | $r_e$ (low) | $(\beta+1) R_E$ (high) |
| $R_o$ | $R_C$ (medium-high) | $R_C$ (medium-high) | $\dfrac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$ (low) |
| Phase | 180° | 0° | 0° |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (KTU examiner's favourite line):** *"CE inverts, CB and CC don't; CE has medium everything, CB has low input, CC has high input and low output."*
