# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
*List the three basic configurations of a BJT amplifier. Which configuration provides a phase reversal between input and output, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three basic configurations of a single-stage BJT amplifier are:

1. **Common Emitter (CE)**
2. **Common Base (CB)**
3. **Common Collector (CC)**

**Phase reversal occurs in the Common Emitter (CE) configuration.** When the input base voltage $v_{BE}$ increases, the collector current $i_C$ increases. This larger current produces a greater voltage drop across the collector resistor $R_C$, causing the collector voltage $v_C$ (which is the output) to *decrease*. Hence $v_o$ moves in the opposite direction to $v_i$, producing a **180° phase shift**. The CB and CC configurations do not invert the signal.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
*Define $r_e$ and $r_\pi$ for a BJT. What is the relation between them?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **$r_e$** is the intrinsic *emitter resistance* of the BJT in the small-signal T-model. It is given by $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E\,(\text{mA})}$, where $V_T$ is the thermal voltage (~26 mV at 300 K).
- **$r_\pi$** is the input resistance looking into the Base of the BJT in the hybrid-$\pi$ model.

**Relation:** $r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e$, where $\beta$ is the AC current gain. This relation arises because the base current $i_b$ is $\beta$ times smaller than the emitter current $i_e$, and the same $v_{BE}$ corresponds to both.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student must answer EITHER OR.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Common Emitter Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a single-stage CE amplifier with voltage-divider bias and emitter bypass capacitor. Derive expressions for voltage gain $A_v$, input resistance $R_i$, and current gain $A_i$ using the small-signal $r_e$ model. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier with $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 1.0\,\text{mA}$, and $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$, calculate the small-signal parameters $r_e$, $r_\pi$, $A_v$, $A_i$, and $R_i$. Comment on the phase of the output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Circuit & Assumptions:** The CE amplifier has the input $v_i$ coupled via $C_{in}$ to the Base, the output $v_o$ taken from the Collector via $C_{out}$ across $R_C$, and the Emitter connected to ground through $R_E$ bypassed by $C_E$ (so that $R_E$ is shorted at signal frequency). *Assumptions:* midband frequency (coupling and bypass capacitors act as short circuits), $r_o \to \infty$ (Early effect neglected), $I_C \approx I_E$.

**Step 2 — Small-signal equivalent:** Replace the BJT with the T-model: $r_\pi$ between Base and Emitter, dependent current source $\beta i_b$ from Collector to Emitter, intrinsic emitter resistance $r_e$.

**Step 3 — Current Gain:** $i_i = i_b$, $i_o = i_c = \beta i_b$, so $A_i = \dfrac{i_o}{i_i} = \beta$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Input Resistance:** With Emitter at AC ground, the resistance looking into the Base is $R_i = r_\pi = \beta r_e$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Voltage Gain:** $v_i = i_b r_\pi$, $v_o = -i_c R_C = -\beta i_b R_C$, hence

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -\frac{\beta R_C}{r_\pi} = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

**(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute $r_e$:** 

$$r_e = \frac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E\,(\text{mA})} = \frac{26}{1.0} = 26\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r_\pi$:** 

$$r_\pi = \beta r_e = 120 \times 26 = 3120\,\Omega = 3.12\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_v$:** 

$$A_v = -\frac{R_C}{r_e} = -\frac{4700}{26} \approx -180.77$$

**Step 4 — Compute $A_i$:** 

$$A_i = \beta = 120$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_i$:** 

$$R_i = r_\pi = 3.12\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Comment on Phase:** The negative sign of $A_v = -180.77$ indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output, i.e., the output is *inverted* relative to the input. **(7 marks total: 1 mark each for $r_e$, $r_\pi$, $A_v$, $A_i$, $R_i$ + 2 marks for the phase comment)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Common Collector & Comparison Focus (Alternative to Question A)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the AC equivalent circuit of an emitter follower (CC configuration). Derive the expressions for input resistance $R_i$, voltage gain $A_v$, and output resistance $R_o$. Explain why this configuration is called an "emitter follower." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the three basic BJT amplifier configurations (CE, CB, CC) in terms of (i) phase relationship, (ii) input impedance, (iii) output impedance, and (iv) a typical application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Step 1 — AC Equivalent:** In the CC stage, the Collector is at AC ground, the input is at the Base, the output is at the Emitter (across $R_E$). The T-model is used: $r_\pi$ between Base and Emitter, dependent source $\beta i_b$ from Collector to Emitter.

**Step 2 — Input Resistance Derivation:** KVL around the input loop gives $v_i = i_b r_\pi + (\beta+1) i_b R_E$, hence

$$R_i = \frac{v_i}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \approx (\beta+1) R_E$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Voltage Gain Derivation:** $v_o = (\beta+1) i_b R_E$, so

$$A_v = \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E} \approx 1$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Output Resistance Derivation:** With $v_s = 0$, looking into the Emitter of the BJT, the reflected resistance is $\dfrac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$, in parallel with $R_E$:

$$R_o = R_E \,\Vert\, \frac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — "Emitter Follower" Explanation:** Because $A_v \approx 1$ and the output is taken at the Emitter, the output voltage *follows* the input voltage (with a fixed offset of $\approx V_{BE}$) — hence the name. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

| Property | CE | CB | CC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Phase relationship** | 180° (inverted) | 0° (in-phase) | 0° (in-phase) |
| **(ii) Input impedance** | Medium ($r_\pi = \beta r_e$) | Low ($r_e$, a few ohms) | High ($(\beta+1) R_E$) |
| **(iii) Output impedance** | Medium-high ($R_C$) | Medium-high ($R_C$) | Low ($\dfrac{R_s+r_\pi}{\beta+1}$) |
| **(iv) Typical application** | General-purpose voltage amplifier | RF / high-frequency amplifier | Impedance buffer / driver stage |

**(7 marks: 1.5 marks per row + 1 mark for synthesis)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Sign error in $A_v$ for CE:** Students often write $A_v = +R_C/r_e$ instead of $A_v = -R_C/r_e$. The negative sign is **mandatory** for CE; missing it costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting AC-grounding:** If you draw the small-signal equivalent but forget to show the bypass capacitor $C_E$ explicitly shorting the Emitter, your $R_i$ calculation will be wrong by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Confusing $A_i$ and $A_v$:** A current amplifier has $A_i \gg 1$ but $A_v \le 1$ (e.g., CC). A voltage amplifier has $A_v \gg 1$ but $A_i \le 1$ (e.g., CB). Mixing them up is a common error.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Every small-signal derivation *must* explicitly state: (a) midband frequency operation, (b) $r_o \to \infty$, (c) $I_C \approx I_E$. Skipping assumptions costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units:** $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ and $I_C$ in mA give $r_e$ in ohms. Writing $r_e = 26/I_C$ without units is *incomplete*.
> 6. **CC output resistance mistake:** Writing $R_o = R_E$ alone is **wrong** — you must include the parallel term $\dfrac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Final high-density revision checklist for KTU Module 4 — Basic Configurations:**

- 🔑 There are **exactly three** basic BJT configurations: **CE, CB, CC**.
- 🔑 The *common* terminal is AC-grounded; it is the reference node for both input and output.
- 🔑 **CE** inverts (180°), **CB** and **CC** are in-phase (0°).
- 🔑 **CE** voltage gain: $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ (large, negative).
- 🔑 **CB** voltage gain: $A_v = +R_C / r_e$ (large, positive).
- 🔑 **CC** voltage gain: $A_v \approx 1$ (unity, positive — "follower").
- 🔑 **CE** current gain: $A_i = \beta$ (large).
- 🔑 **CB** current gain: $A_i = \alpha \approx 1$ (less than unity).
- 🔑 **CC** current gain: $A_i = \beta + 1$ (large).
- 🔑 **CE** input resistance: $R_i = r_\pi = \beta r_e$ (medium).
- 🔑 **CB** input resistance: $R_i = r_e$ (very low, a few ohms).
- 🔑 **CC** input resistance: $R_i = r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$ (very high, hundreds of kΩ).
- 🔑 **CC** output resistance: $R_o = R_E \Vert \dfrac{R_s + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$ (very low, a few ohms).
- 🔑 The **emitter resistance** $r_e = V_T / I_E \approx 26\,\text{mV} / I_E$ (in mA), giving $r_e$ in **ohms**.
- 🔑 The **thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K — a *physical constant*.
- 🔑 Use the **T-model** (or hybrid-$\pi$ model) with the assumption $r_o \to \infty$ for low-frequency small-signal analysis.
- 🔑 The **Miller effect** limits CE bandwidth; CB avoids it → CB is the **high-frequency choice**.
- 🔑 The **CC (emitter follower)** is the universal **buffer** — used as the output stage in almost every op-amp.
- 🔑 Memorise the **formula cheat sheet (Section 2.3)** — it is the single most important exam asset.
- 🔑 Always write **assumptions** explicitly before starting any derivation.
- 🔑 Always include **units** in numerical answers.
- 🔑 Always mention the **phase** (inverting or non-inverting) when asked for $A_v$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Negative Feedback on Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Effect of Negative Feedback on Amplifiers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Negative Feedback** is a control mechanism in amplifiers where a portion of the output signal is *subtracted* from the input signal and fed back to the input, creating a closed loop. The feedback signal opposes the original input, reducing the overall gain but simultaneously stabilizing and improving the amplifier's performance characteristics. In formal KTU syllabus terminology: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Negative Feedback Amplifier:** A configuration in which a fraction of the output (voltage or current) is returned to the input *out of phase* (180° phase shift) with the original input signal. The loop gain $A\beta$ determines the trade-off between gain reduction and performance enhancement. The **feedback factor** $\beta$ is defined as the ratio of the feedback signal to the output signal: $$\beta = \frac{X_f}{X_o}$$ where $X_f$ is the feedback signal and $X_o$ is the output signal. The fundamental closed-loop transfer function is: $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ Here, $A$ is the open-loop gain, $\beta$ is the feedback factor, and the term $(1 + A\beta)$ is known as the **desensitivity factor** or **amount of feedback** (typically expressed in decibels). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control System Imagine driving a car on a highway using **cruise control**: - **Input (Reference):** You set 80 km/h - **System (Amplifier):** The car's throttle control - **Output (Car speed):** Your actual speed - **Feedback loop:** Speed sensor on the wheel - **Error signal:** Difference between 80 km/h and actual speed When you go **uphill** (disturbance), the car slows down. The sensor detects this, and the controller automatically increases the throttle. The "feedback" here is the actual speed signal compared to the desired speed. In a **negative feedback amplifier**, this same principle applies: - If temperature changes cause the gain to drift → the feedback automatically compensates - If load changes affect output → the feedback maintains the desired output - The "price" you pay is a **reduction in overall gain** > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Negative feedback sacrifices raw gain magnitude in exchange for *predictability, stability, and robustness* — qualities that are far more valuable in practical engineering applications than extreme gain values. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics In negative feedback analysis, the following standard metrics are universally used: - **Open-Loop Gain ($A$):** Gain without feedback, typically very large (e.g., $10^5$ for op-amps) - **Closed-Loop Gain ($A_f$):** Gain with feedback applied - **Feedback Factor ($\beta$):** Ratio $\frac{X_f}{X_o}$, dimensionless and $\leq 1$ - **Loop Gain ($A\beta$):** Product of open-loop gain and feedback factor - **Return Difference ($1 + A\beta$):** The amount of feedback in dB: $20\log_{10}(1 + A\beta)$ - **Feedback in dB:** $\text{NFB (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(1 + A\beta)$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Feedback Equation Consider a basic feedback system with open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$. The feedback signal $V_f$ is subtracted from the input $V_s$: $$V_{in} = V_s - V_f = V_s - \beta V_o$$ Since $V_o = A \cdot V_{in}$, substituting gives: $$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o) = A V_s - A\beta V_o$$ Solving for the closed-loop gain: $$\boxed{A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$ ### 2.2 The Four Topologies of Negative Feedback There are **four distinct configurations** based on what is sampled (voltage/current) and how it is mixed (series/shunt): | Topology | Samples | Mixes | Input Connection | Output Connection | |----------|---------|-------|------------------|-------------------| | **Voltage-Series** | Output Voltage | Series | Series | Shunt (across load) | | **Voltage-Shunt** | Output Voltage | Shunt | Shunt | Shunt (across load) | | **Current-Series** | Output Current | Series | Series | Series (with load) | | **Current-Shunt** | Output Current | Shunt | Shunt | Series (with load) | ### 2.3 Effect of Negative Feedback — Comprehensive Summary | Performance Parameter | Effect | Formula | |----------------------|--------|---------| | **Gain** | Decreased | $A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | | **Bandwidth** | Increased | $BW_f = BW(1 + A\beta)$ | | **Input Impedance (Series)** | Increased | $Z_{if} = Z_i(1 + A\beta)$ | | **Input Impedance (Shunt)** | Decreased | $Z_{if} = \frac{Z_i}{1 + A\beta}$ | | **Output Impedance (Voltage)** | Decreased | $Z_{of} = \frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | | **Output Impedance (Current)** | Increased | $Z_{of} = Z_o(1 + A\beta)$ | | **Noise** | Decreased | $N_f = \frac{N}{1 + A\beta}$ | | **Distortion (THD)** | Decreased | $D_f = \frac{D}{1 + A\beta}$ | | **Gain Sensitivity** | Decreased | $\frac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) Invariance:** In a single-pole amplifier, the product of gain and bandwidth remains constant: $A \cdot BW = A_f \cdot BW_f$. This is the fundamental trade-off — **bandwidth is gained exactly in proportion to the gain loss**. ### 2.4 The Four Feedback Topologies — Effect on Impedances | Topology | $Z_{in}$ Effect | $Z_{out}$ Effect | Ideal Source | Ideal Load | |----------|----------------|------------------|--------------|------------| | Voltage-Series | $Z_i(1 + A\beta)$ | $\frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | Voltage source | Any load | | Voltage-Shunt | $\frac{Z_i}{1 + A\beta}$ | $\frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | Voltage source | High $Z_L$ | | Current-Series | $Z_i(1 + A\beta)$ | $Z_o(1 + A\beta)$ | Current source | Any load | | Current-Shunt | $\frac{Z_i}{1 + A\beta}$ | $Z_o(1 + A\beta)$ | Current source | Low $Z_L$ | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility Negative feedback is the **backbone of modern electronics**: - **Operational Amplifiers:** All op-amp configurations (inverting, non-inverting, etc.) use negative feedback to set precise, stable gains - **Audio Amplifiers:** Reduces harmonic distortion to acceptable levels (e.g., from 10% to 0.01%) - **Control Systems:** Regulates motor speed, temperature, voltage regulators - **Communication Systems:** Maintains stable gain despite temperature variations and component aging ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain Equation **Given:** - Open-loop amplifier with gain $A$ - Feedback network with transfer ratio $\beta$ - The feedback signal is subtracted from the input **Step 1:** The error signal at the input of the basic amplifier is: $$V_e = V_s - V_f$$ **Step 2:** The feedback signal is proportional to the output: $$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$ **Step 3:** The output of the basic amplifier is: $$V_o = A \cdot V_e$$ **Step 4:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 3: $$V_o = A(V_s - V_f)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 4: $$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$$ **Step 6:** Expand the right-hand side: $$V_o = AV_s - A\beta V_o$$ **Step 7:** Collect $V_o$ terms on the left: $$V_o + A\beta V_o = AV_s$$ **Step 8:** Factor out $V_o$: $$V_o(1 + A\beta) = AV_s$$ **Step 9:** Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s}$: $$\boxed{A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$ This is the **fundamental negative feedback equation** that forms the basis for all subsequent analysis. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Gain Sensitivity (Stability) We want to find how much the closed-loop gain $A_f$ changes when the open-loop gain $A$ changes due to temperature, aging, or component tolerances. **Step 1:** Start with the closed-loop gain: $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ **Step 2:** Take the differential with respect to $A$: $$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{(1 + A\beta)(1) - A(\beta)}{(1 + A\beta)^2}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the numerator: $$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1 + A\beta - A\beta}{(1 + A\beta)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 + A\beta)^2}$$ **Step 4:** Form the sensitivity ratio: $$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1}{(1 + A\beta)^2}$$ **Step 5:** Divide both sides by $\frac{A_f}{A}$: $$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{(1 + A\beta)^2} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$ But $\frac{A_f}{A} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}$, so: $$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{(1 + A\beta)^2} \cdot (1 + A\beta) \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$ **Step 6:** Final sensitivity equation: $$\boxed{\frac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}}$$ **Interpretation:** A fractional change in $A$ produces only $\frac{1}{1+A\beta}$ times that change in $A_f$. For example, if $1 + A\beta = 100$, then a 10% change in $A$ produces only a 0.1% change in $A_f$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Bandwidth Extension For a single-pole amplifier, the open-loop gain as a function of frequency is: $$A(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\frac{\omega}{\omega_H}}$$ where $A_0$ is the midband gain and $\omega_H$ is the upper cutoff frequency. **Step 1:** Substitute into the feedback equation: $$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{\frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}}{1 + \frac{A_0 \beta}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}}$$ **Step 2:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega/\omega_H)$: $$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H + A_0 \beta}$$ **Step 3:** Factor $(1 + A_0\beta)$ from the denominator: $$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\frac{\omega}{\omega_H(1 + A_0\beta)}}$$ **Step 4:** Identify the new closed-loop midband gain and bandwidth: $$A_{f0} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta}, \quad \omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1 + A_0\beta)$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$\boxed{BW_f = BW(1 + A\beta)}$$ **Verification of GBW invariance:** $$A_{f0} \cdot \omega_{Hf} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot \omega_H(1 + A_0\beta) = A_0 \omega_H$$ The gain-bandwidth product remains constant. ✓ --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Input Impedance — Voltage-Series Feedback For series mixing at the input, the input current $I_i$ flows through both $Z_s$ and $Z_i$ without shunting: **Step 1:** The voltage across the input impedance: $$V_i = V_s - V_f = V_s - \beta V_o$$ **Step 2:** Since $V_o = A V_i$: $$V_i = V_s - \beta A V_i$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $V_i$: $$V_i(1 + A\beta) = V_s \implies V_i = \frac{V_s}{1 + A\beta}$$ **Step 4:** The input impedance with feedback: $$Z_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i} = \frac{V_i(1 + A\beta)}{I_i} = (1 + A\beta) \frac{V_i}{I_i}$$ **Step 5:** Since $Z_i = \frac{V_i}{I_i}$: $$\boxed{Z_{if} = Z_i(1 + A\beta)}$$ **Conclusion:** Series feedback **increases** input impedance, making the amplifier a better voltage amplifier (closer to ideal voltage amplifier with $Z_{in} = \infty$). --- ### 3.5 Derivation of Output Impedance — Voltage-Shunt Feedback For voltage sampling at the output, $V_o$ is directly sensed. To find $Z_{of}$, set $V_s = 0$ and apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the output: **Step 1:** With $V_s = 0$, the input signal becomes: $$V_i = -\beta V_t$$ **Step 2:** The dependent source produces: $$V_{dep} = A V_i = -A\beta V_t$$ **Step 3:** Using KVL at the output node: $$V_t - (-A\beta V_t) = I_t Z_o$$ $$V_t(1 + A\beta) = I_t Z_o$$ **Step 4:** Solving for $Z_{of}$: $$\boxed{Z_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$ **Conclusion:** Voltage sampling **decreases** output impedance, making the amplifier behave closer to an ideal voltage source (with $Z_{out} = 0$). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Fundamental Negative Feedback Block Diagram This is the master block diagram showing the architecture of any negative feedback amplifier system. The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal from the source signal to produce the error signal that drives the basic amplifier. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[V source V s] --> sumj[Summing Junction] sumj --> |Error V e| ampA[Basic Amplifier Gain A] ampA --> |V o| outp[V o Output Node] outp --> load[Load Z L] outp --> fbnet[Feedback Network Beta] fbnet --> |V f Beta V o| sumj sumj -.->|Subtraction| sumj style src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style ampA fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style fbnet fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style outp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` **Description of Flow:** 1. Source signal $V_s$ enters the **Summing Junction** 2. Feedback signal $V_f = \beta V_o$ is **subtracted** from $V_s$ to produce error $V_e = V_s - V_f$ 3. Error signal $V_e$ is amplified by the **Basic Amplifier** of gain $A$ to produce $V_o$ 4. Output is delivered to the load AND tapped by the **Feedback Network** 5. Feedback network produces $V_f$ which returns to the summing junction, completing the loop --- ### 4.2 The Four Feedback Topologies — Comparative Architecture The following diagram illustrates the four possible negative feedback configurations, classified by what is sampled at the output and how it is mixed at the input. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input_Mixing Series_Mix["SERIES MIXING
V in and V f in series
Z in INCREASES"] Shunt_Mix["SHUNT MIXING
V in and V f in parallel
Z in DECREASES"] end subgraph Output_Sampling Volt_Samp["VOLTAGE SAMPLING
V o sampled in parallel
Z out DECREASES"] Curr_Samp["CURRENT SAMPLING
I o sampled in series
Z out INCREASES"] end subgraph Four_Topologies VS["VOLTAGE SERIES
VS Amplifier
Z in high, Z out low"] VSH["VOLTAGE SHUNT
Transimpedance
Z in low, Z out low"] CS["CURRENT SERIES
Transconductance
Z in high, Z out high"] CSH["CURRENT SHUNT
CC Amplifier
Z in low, Z out high"] end Volt_Samp --> VS Volt_Samp --> VSH Curr_Samp --> CS Curr_Samp --> CSH Series_Mix --> VS Series_Mix --> CS Shunt_Mix --> VSH Shunt_Mix --> CSH style VS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style VSH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style CS fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style CSH fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.3 Detailed Voltage-Series Feedback Topology This is the most commonly used topology (e.g., non-inverting op-amp configuration). ```mermaid flowchart LR Vsrc[V source V s] --> Rin[R s Source Res] Rin --> NodeA[Node A Input] NodeA --> Zin[Z in Input Impedance] Zin --> GndA((Ground)) NodeA --> Amp[BASIC AMP Gain A] Amp --> NodeB[Node B Output] NodeB --> Ro[Z o Output Impedance] Ro --> NodeC[Node C Load] NodeC --> Load[Load Z L] Load --> GndB((Ground)) NodeB --> BetaNet[Feedback Net Beta] BetaNet --> NodeA style Amp fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style BetaNet fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style NodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style NodeB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style NodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` **Key Observation:** The feedback network $\beta$ samples the output voltage $V_o$ (parallel connection) and returns it in series with the source $V_s$. This is why voltage-series is sometimes called "series-shunt" feedback (series at input, shunt at output). --- ### 4.4 Signal Flow and Error Generation — Mathematical Model This diagram shows the mathematical signal processing within the feedback loop. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[V s] --> M1[Multiplier x 1] M1 --> S1[SUM] B1[V f] --> M2[Multiplier x minus 1] M2 --> S1 S1 --> |V e equals V s minus V f| M3[Multiplier x A] M3 --> |V o equals A V e| Split[Split Point] Split --> Out[V o to Load] Split --> M4[Multiplier x Beta] M4 --> |V f equals Beta V o| B1 style S1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style M3 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px style M4 fill:#9370DB,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px style Split fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define negative feedback. List any four effects of negative feedback on amplifier performance.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Negative feedback is a technique in which a portion of the output signal is fed back to the input *out of phase* with the input signal, such that the feedback signal opposes the original input. This creates a closed-loop system where the gain becomes $A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$. **Four Effects of Negative Feedback:** 1. **Gain Reduction:** The closed-loop gain decreases by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$: $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ 2. **Bandwidth Extension:** The upper cutoff frequency increases proportionally: $$BW_f = BW(1 + A\beta)$$ 3. **Input/Output Impedance Modification:** Series mixing increases $Z_{in}$, shunt mixing decreases $Z_{in}$; voltage sampling decreases $Z_{out}$, current sampling increases $Z_{out}$. 4. **Reduction of Nonlinear Distortion and Noise:** Both are reduced by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$, since the feedback loop "corrects" deviations from linearity. 5. *(Additional, if needed)* **Improved Gain Stability:** Sensitivity to temperature and component variations is reduced by the factor $\frac{1}{1+A\beta}$. --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the desensitivity factor in a negative feedback amplifier? An amplifier has open-loop gain $A = 1000$ and feedback factor $\beta = 0.01$. Calculate the closed-loop gain and amount of feedback in dB.** **Model Answer:** **Desensitivity Factor:** The desensitivity factor (also called the amount of feedback) is the quantity $(1 + A\beta)$. It represents: - The factor by which the open-loop gain is reduced - The factor by which sensitivity to variations is reduced - The factor by which distortion and noise are reduced - The factor by which bandwidth is increased **Given Data:** - Open-loop gain $A = 1000$ - Feedback factor $\beta = 0.01$ **Step 1:** Calculate the loop gain: $$A\beta = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the desensitivity factor: $$1 + A\beta = 1 + 10 = 11$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the closed-loop gain: $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{1000}{11} \approx 90.91$$ **Step 4:** Calculate the amount of feedback in dB: $$\text{NFB (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(1 + A\beta) = 20\log_{10}(11)$$ $$\text{NFB (dB)} = 20 \times 1.0414 = 20.83 \text{ dB}$$ **Final Answers:** $A_f \approx 90.91$, Amount of feedback $= 20.83$ dB. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expression for closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier. Explain how negative feedback affects the gain and bandwidth of an amplifier. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An amplifier has an open-loop gain of $A = 2000$, input impedance $Z_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, output impedance $Z_o = 50\text{ }\Omega$, and upper cutoff frequency $f_H = 20\text{ kHz}$. If voltage-series negative feedback with $\beta = 0.05$ is applied, find: (i) Closed-loop gain, (ii) New upper cutoff frequency, (iii) New input impedance, (iv) New output impedance. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution for (a):** **Derivation of Closed-Loop Gain:** Consider the block diagram of a negative feedback amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$. **Step 1:** The error signal at the input is the difference between source and feedback signals: $$V_e = V_s - V_f$$ **Step 2:** The feedback signal is proportional to the output: $$V_f = \beta V_o$$ **Step 3:** The output of the basic amplifier is: $$V_o = A V_e$$ **Step 4:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 3: $$V_o = A(V_s - V_f)$$ **Step 5:** Substituting Step 2: $$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$$ **Step 6:** Expanding: $$V_o = AV_s - A\beta V_o$$ **Step 7:** Rearranging: $$V_o(1 + A\beta) = AV_s$$ **Step 8:** Therefore, the closed-loop gain is: $$\boxed{A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$ **Effect on Gain:** The closed-loop gain is reduced by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$ compared to the open-loop gain. If $A\beta \gg 1$: $$A_f \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$$ This shows that when the loop gain is very large, the closed-loop gain depends *only on the feedback network* $\beta$ (which can be made using precision passive components), making the amplifier highly stable. **Effect on Bandwidth:** For a single-pole amplifier with upper cutoff $f_H$, applying feedback extends the bandwidth: $$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta)$$ **Derivation:** The open-loop response is $A(jf) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$. Substituting into $A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}$: $$A_f(jf) = \frac{A_0/(1 + jf/f_H)}{1 + A_0\beta/(1 + jf/f_H)} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta + jf/f_H}$$ Factoring $(1 + A_0\beta)$: $$A_f(jf) = \frac{A_0/(1 + A_0\beta)}{1 + j\frac{f}{f_H(1 + A_0\beta)}}$$ This yields new midband gain $A_{f0} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta}$ and new cutoff $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A_0\beta)$. **Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off:** $$A_{f0} \cdot f_{Hf} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta} \cdot f_H(1+A_0\beta) = A_0 f_H = \text{constant}$$ The GBW product remains invariant. --- **Solution for (b):** **Given Data:** - $A = 2000$ - $Z_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$ - $Z_o = 50\text{ }\Omega$ - $f_H = 20\text{ kHz}$ - Voltage-Series Feedback, $\beta = 0.05$ **Step 1:** Calculate loop gain: $$A\beta = 2000 \times 0.05 = 100$$ **Step 2:** Calculate desensitivity factor: $$1 + A\beta = 1 + 100 = 101$$ **(i) Closed-Loop Gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2000}{101} \approx 19.80$$ **(ii) New Upper Cutoff Frequency:** For voltage-series feedback, bandwidth increases: $$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta) = 20 \text{ kHz} \times 101 = 2.02 \text{ MHz}$$ **(iii) New Input Impedance:** Voltage-series feedback has series mixing at input, so $Z_{in}$ **increases**: $$Z_{if} = Z_i(1 + A\beta) = 2\text{ k}\Omega \times 101 = 202\text{ k}\Omega$$ **(iv) New Output Impedance:** Voltage-series has voltage sampling, so $Z_{out}$ **decreases**: $$Z_{of} = \frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{50}{101} \approx 0.495\text{ }\Omega$$ **Final Answers:** - $A_f \approx 19.80$ - $f_{Hf} = 2.02\text{ MHz}$ - $Z_{if} = 202\text{ k}\Omega$ - $Z_{of} \approx 0.495\text{ }\Omega$ --- #### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the concept of negative feedback. List the four types of feedback topologies and explain how each affects the input and output impedance of the amplifier. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An amplifier with negative feedback has the following specifications: open-loop gain $A = 10{,}000$, $Z_i = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $Z_o = 100\text{ }\Omega$, $f_H = 5\text{ kHz}$, and feedback factor $\beta = 0.02$. If a 10% change occurs in the open-loop gain due to temperature variation, calculate: (i) Closed-loop gain, (ii) Percentage change in closed-loop gain, (iii) New bandwidth, (iv) The new input and output impedances assuming voltage-series feedback. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution for (a):** **Block Diagram of Negative Feedback:** ``` V_s ──→(+)──→ V_e ──→[ A ]──→ V_o ──→ Load ↑(-) │ └──────[ β ]───────────────┘ ``` **Concept Explanation:** In a negative feedback amplifier, a portion of the output ($\beta V_o$) is sampled by the feedback network and returned to the input summing junction, where it is *subtracted* from the source signal $V_s$. The error signal $V_e = V_s - \beta V_o$ is then amplified by the basic amplifier of gain $A$ to produce the output. **Four Types of Feedback Topologies:** | # | Topology | Input Mixing | Output Sampling | $Z_{in}$ Effect | $Z_{out}$ Effect | |---|----------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------| | 1 | **Voltage-Series** | Series | Voltage (Shunt) | Increases $(1+A\beta)$ times | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | | 2 | **Voltage-Shunt** | Shunt | Voltage (Shunt) | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | | 3 | **Current-Series** | Series | Current (Series) | Increases $(1+A\beta)$ times | Increases $(1+A\beta)$ times | | 4 | **Current-Shunt** | Shunt | Current (Series) | Decreases by $(1+A\beta)$ | Increases $(1+A\beta)$ times | **Explanation of Each:** 1. **Voltage-Series:** Samples output voltage, returns in series. Best for voltage amplification — high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$. (Example: Non-inverting op-amp) 2. **Voltage-Shunt:** Samples output voltage, returns in shunt. Low $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$. (Example: Inverting op-amp) 3. **Current-Series:** Samples output current, returns in series. High $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$. Used as transconductance amplifier. 4. **Current-Shunt:** Samples output current, returns in shunt. Low $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$. Used as current amplifier. --- **Solution for (b):** **Given Data:** - $A = 10{,}000$ - $Z_i = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ - $Z_o = 100\text{ }\Omega$ - $f_H = 5\text{ kHz}$ - $\beta = 0.02$ - $\Delta A / A = 10\% = 0.10$ - Voltage-series feedback topology **Step 1:** Calculate loop gain: $$A\beta = 10{,}000 \times 0.02 = 200$$ **Step 2:** Desensitivity factor: $$1 + A\beta = 1 + 200 = 201$$ **(i) Closed-Loop Gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{10{,}000}{201} \approx 49.75$$ **(ii) Percentage Change in Closed-Loop Gain:** Using the sensitivity relation: $$\frac{\Delta A_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$ $$\frac{\Delta A_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{201} \times 0.10 = 4.975 \times 10^{-4}$$ Converting to percentage: $$\%\Delta A_f = 4.975 \times 10^{-4} \times 100\% \approx 0.04975\%$$ **Verification by Direct Calculation:** New $A = 10{,}000 + 0.10(10{,}000) = 11{,}000$ $$A_f^{new} = \frac{11{,}000}{1 + 11{,}000 \times 0.02} = \frac{11{,}000}{221} \approx 49.77$$ $$\Delta A_f = 49.77 - 49.75 = 0.02$$ $$\%\Delta A_f = \frac{0.02}{49.75} \times 100\% \approx 0.0402\%$$ (The slight discrepancy is due to using the differential approximation. The exact calculation confirms a very small change.) **(iii) New Bandwidth:** $$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta) = 5\text{ kHz} \times 201 = 1.005\text{ MHz}$$ **(iv) New Input and Output Impedances (Voltage-Series):** **Input Impedance** (series mixing → increases): $$Z_{if} = Z_i(1 + A\beta) = 1\text{ k}\Omega \times 201 = 201\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Output Impedance** (voltage sampling → decreases): $$Z_{of} = \frac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{100}{201} \approx 0.4975\text{ }\Omega$$ **Final Answers:** - $A_f \approx 49.75$ - $\%\Delta A_f \approx 0.05\%$ - $f_{Hf} = 1.005\text{ MHz}$ - $Z_{if} = 201\text{ k}\Omega$ - $Z_{of} \approx 0.4975\text{ }\Omega$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Confusion:** Students often forget that voltage-series feedback *increases* $Z_{in}$ and *decreases* $Z_{out}$. Memorize the rule: **"Series raises $Z_{in}$, Voltage sampling lowers $Z_{out}$"**. > 2. **GBW Misinterpretation:** Always verify that $A_f \times BW_f = A \times BW$. Many students incorrectly multiply gain by the desensitivity factor for bandwidth too. > 3. **dB Conversion Errors:** When asked for "feedback in dB," use $20\log_{10}(1+A\beta)$, not $10\log_{10}$. This is a voltage ratio, not power. > 4. **Topology Identification:** In the four topologies, remember the mnemonic **"V-S: V out, S in"** and so on. Mixing up the four types is the single most common error. > 5. **Sensitivity Calculation:** Use the differential formula $\frac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}$ for small changes only. For large changes, recalculate $A_f$ with the new value of $A$. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Master Equation:** $A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta}$ — the cornerstone of all negative feedback analysis. - **Desensitivity Factor:** $(1+A\beta)$ reduces gain, sensitivity, noise, and distortion by the same factor. - **Four Topologies:** Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt. - **Impedance Rules:** * Series mixing → $Z_{in}$ multiplies by $(1+A\beta)$ * Shunt mixing → $Z_{in}$ divides by $(1+A\beta)$ * Voltage sampling → $Z_{out}$ divides by $(1+A\beta)$ * Current sampling → $Z_{out}$ multiplies by $(1+A\beta)$ - **Bandwidth Extension:** $BW_f = BW \cdot (1+A\beta)$; GBW remains constant. - **Gain Sensitivity:** $\frac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}$ — fractional change reduced by desensitivity factor. - **Approximation:** When $A\beta \gg 1$, $A_f \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$ — gain depends only on feedback network. - **Loop Gain $A\beta$:** Must be positive and large for stability; excessive values can cause oscillation. - **Real-world Applications:** Op-amps, voltage regulators, audio amplifiers, automatic gain control (AGC) circuits. - **Always Show Your Work:** KTU examiners reward systematic derivations with full marks — don't skip algebraic steps.

topics.title: Oscillators: Classification, Criterion for Oscillation, RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillator (both using BJT), Design Equations and Working of the Circuits (analysis not required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oscillators: Classification, Criterion for Oscillation, RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillator (both using BJT), Design Equations and Working of the Circuits (analysis not required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — OSCILLATORS: FOUNDATIONS, CRITERION & RC/WIEN BRIDGE DESIGNS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Oscillator** is an electronic circuit that converts DC energy from the power supply into AC energy (sustained periodic waveforms — sinusoidal or non-sinusoidal) **without requiring any external input signal**. It produces a self-sustaining, periodic output by feeding back a portion of its own output through a frequency-selective feedback network to the input, satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion**.

A sinusoidal oscillator is therefore a **signal source** that internally generates a stable, continuous sine wave of a precise frequency, governed entirely by the components used in its feedback path.

### 🔑 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an oscillator like a **child on a swing**:

- The swing has natural motion (oscillation) when pushed.
- A small push at the **right moment** (in phase) keeps it going forever.
- If the push is mistimed (wrong phase) or too weak (insufficient amplitude), the swing dies down.
- The child is the **active element (BJT)**, the swing's pendulum is the **feedback network (RC/LC)**, and the timing of the push is the **phase condition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Every oscillator question tests whether you can identify the **type**, apply the **Barkhausen criterion** correctly, and write the **design frequency equation** with all conditions.

---

## 1.2 Role of Positive Feedback in Oscillators

An oscillator is essentially an **amplifier with positive feedback**. The general block diagram is:

- **Amplifier** (gain = $A$)
- **Feedback Network** (gain = $\beta$)
- **Loop Gain** = $A\beta$

When the loop gain is precisely tuned, the circuit begins to oscillate at a single frequency determined by the feedback network.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently give 2-3 marks just for stating the **Barkhausen criterion**. Memorize the two conditions verbatim.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Oscillators (KTU Board Favourite)

Oscillators are classified under multiple categories based on the **waveform, feedback network, frequency range**, and **active device**.

### 2.1.1 Based on Output Waveform

| Type | Examples | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Sinusoidal** | RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge, Colpitts, Hartley | Signal generators, communication |
| **Non-Sinusoidal (Relaxation)** | Astable Multivibrator, UJT, Blocking | Digital clocks, function generators |

### 2.1.2 Based on Frequency Range

| Range | Type | Examples |
|-------|------|----------|
| **1 Hz – 20 kHz** | Audio Frequency (AF) | RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge |
| **20 kHz – 300 MHz** | Radio Frequency (RF) | Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal |
| **> 300 MHz** | Microwave (VHF/UHF) | Cavity, Klystron, Magnetron |

### 2.1.3 Based on Components in Feedback Network

| Feedback Type | Oscillator | Frequency Range |
|---------------|------------|-----------------|
| **RC Network** | RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge | Audio (1 Hz – 1 MHz) |
| **LC Tank Circuit** | Colpitts, Hartley, Clapp | RF (1 MHz – 500 MHz) |
| **Crystal / Piezoelectric** | Pierce, Crystal Oscillator | Stable RF (Fixed) |

### 2.1.4 Based on Active Device Used

- **BJT-based** (this module)
- **FET / MOSFET-based**
- **Op-Amp based**
- **UJT-based relaxation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically focuses on:**
> ✅ RC Phase Shift Oscillator (using BJT)  
> ✅ Wien Bridge Oscillator (using BJT)  
> ✅ Barkhausen Criterion  
> ✅ Classification of Oscillators

---

## 2.2 The Barkhausen Criterion (Sustained Oscillation Condition)

For a feedback amplifier to behave as an **oscillator**, the closed-loop transfer function must have its **denominator equal to zero**, i.e., $1 - A\beta = 0$.

This yields **two simultaneous conditions**:

### 🔹 Condition 1: Magnitude Condition
$$|A\beta| = 1$$

The magnitude of the loop gain must equal **unity** (exactly 1). For practical startup, $|A\beta| > 1$ initially so that noise/perturbation grows, and then amplitude-limiting mechanism reduces it to 1.

### 🔹 Condition 2: Phase Condition
$$\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \quad \text{or} \quad 360^\circ \quad (n \cdot 360^\circ, n \in \mathbb{Z})$$

The total phase shift around the loop must be **zero (or an integer multiple of 360°)** so that the feedback signal is **in phase** with the input (positive feedback).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **sustained oscillation** with **amplification**. If $|A\beta| < 1$, the oscillations die out (damped). If $|A\beta| > 1$ without limiting, the output saturates (clipped sine wave).

### 2.2.1 Why the Two Conditions?

- The **phase condition** ensures **positive feedback**, which is the *only* way to regenerate the signal without an input.
- The **magnitude condition** ensures that the feedback signal is **exactly as large** as needed to sustain the original — no growth, no decay.
- A startup condition $|A\beta| \ge 1$ ensures the oscillation begins from thermal noise.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Barkhausen Magnitude | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Sustained oscillation |
| 2 | Barkhausen Phase | $\angle A\beta = n \cdot 360^\circ$ | Positive feedback |
| 3 | Startup Condition | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | For oscillation to build up |
| 4 | RC Phase Shift Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6 + \dfrac{4R_C}{R}}}$ | With BJT input resistance $R_C$ considered |
| 5 | RC Phase Shift Frequency (Ideal) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | When $R_C \gg R$ (no loading) |
| 6 | RC Phase Shift Required Gain | $A_{min} = 29$ | For the unloaded ideal case |
| 7 | RC Phase Shift Required Gain (Loaded) | $A = 4k + 23 + \dfrac{29}{k}$ | Where $k = R_C / R$ |
| 8 | Wien Bridge Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | For $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$ |
| 9 | Wien Bridge Required Gain | $A = 3$ | For non-inverting amplifier |
| 10 | Wien Bridge Lead-Lag Phase | $\phi = 0^\circ$ at $f_0$ | At resonance frequency |
| 11 | Lead Network Phase | $\phi_{lead} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{f/f_1}{1 - (f/f_1)^2}\right)$ | $f_1 = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$ |
| 12 | Lag Network Phase | $\phi_{lag} = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{f/f_2}{1 - (f/f_2)^2}\right)$ | $f_2 = 1/(2\pi R_2 C_2)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Board Rule:** When writing formulas, always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming equal resistors and capacitors"). Examiners deduct marks for missing assumptions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Oscillator | Application |
|------------|-------------|
| **RC Phase Shift** | Audio test equipment, function generators, low-cost tone generators |
| **Wien Bridge** | High-purity sine wave generators, audio frequency oscillators, lab instruments (HP 200-series) |
| **Colpitts** | RF oscillators, AM transmitters, local oscillators |
| **Hartley** | RF oscillators, FM transmitters |
| **Crystal** | Clocks, microcontrollers, radios — anywhere frequency stability is critical |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ for RC Phase Shift Oscillator

### Setup

The RC phase shift oscillator uses a **common-emitter BJT amplifier** (which gives $180^\circ$ phase inversion) followed by a **three-stage RC ladder network**. Each RC section contributes $60^\circ$ of phase shift, totaling $180^\circ$ from the network. Combined with the amplifier's $180^\circ$, the loop total is $360^\circ$, satisfying Barkhausen's phase condition.

### Step 1: Define the Three RC Sections

Let each section have resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$. The sections are identical.

The transfer function of a single RC high-pass section (lead network) is:

$$H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

### Step 2: Total Phase Shift from the Ladder

For three cascaded RC sections, the total phase shift $\phi$ must be $-180^\circ$ (because the amplifier adds the other $-180^\circ$ in CE configuration).

The total phase shift through three identical RC networks is:

$$\phi_{total} = -3 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) \quad \text{(high-frequency approximation)}$$

But this is not exact; the full derivation requires analysis of the ladder loaded by the next stage.

### Step 3: Apply the Barkhausen Magnitude Condition

The exact frequency condition emerges by setting the imaginary part of $1 - A\beta = 0$ to zero, and then computing the real part for the required gain.

For the **unloaded case** (BJT input resistance $R_i \to \infty$, output resistance $R_o \to 0$):

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

The required amplifier gain is:

$$A_{v,min} = 29$$

### Step 4: Loaded Case (More Accurate Design Equation)

When the BJT's input resistance $R_C$ (input resistance at collector-base node) and other loading are considered, the condition becomes more involved. Let $k = R_C / R$.

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6 + \dfrac{4R_C}{R}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6 + 4k}}$$

The required voltage gain is:

$$A_{v,min} = 4k + 23 + \frac{29}{k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The syllabus says "analysis not required" — meaning **exact nodal derivation is not expected**. However, you **must** be able to write the **design equations**, the **loop condition**, and **explain the working** with a clean circuit diagram. Always quote the *ideal* (unloaded) form first, then mention the *practical loaded* form.

### Step 3A: Verification by Substitution

Let's verify with $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1\text{ nF}$:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-9} \times \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5} \times 2.449}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.5 \text{ kHz}$$

This falls squarely in the **audio range**, confirming why the RC phase shift oscillator is the canonical **AF oscillator**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for Wien Bridge Oscillator

### Setup

The Wien Bridge uses a **lead-lag network** consisting of a **series RC** (lead) and a **parallel RC** (lag). At a specific frequency, the phase shift across the network is exactly **zero**.

### Step 1: Transfer Function of the Lead-Lag Network

Let $Z_1$ = series combination of $R_1$ and $C_1$:

$$Z_1 = R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1} = \frac{1 + sR_1C_1}{sC_1}$$

Let $Z_2$ = parallel combination of $R_2$ and $C_2$:

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2 \cdot \frac{1}{sC_2}}{R_2 + \frac{1}{sC_2}} = \frac{R_2}{1 + sR_2C_2}$$

The feedback ratio is:

$$\beta(s) = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

### Step 2: Substitute $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$

With matched components:

$$Z_1 = \frac{1 + sRC}{sC}, \quad Z_2 = \frac{R}{1 + sRC}$$

$$\beta(s) = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1+sRC}}{\dfrac{1+sRC}{sC} + \dfrac{R}{1+sRC}}$$

### Step 3: Simplify

Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC(1+sRC)$:

$$\beta(s) = \frac{sRC}{(1+sRC)^2 + sRC} = \frac{sRC}{1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $s = j\omega$

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - (\omega RC)^2 + 3j\omega RC}$$

### Step 5: For Zero Phase, Imaginary Part of Denominator Must Cancel

For the phase of $\beta$ to be zero, we need the **denominator to be real** (its imaginary part must equal zero). The denominator's imaginary part is $3\omega RC$, and the numerator's imaginary part is $\omega RC$.

Set the total phase to zero — equivalent to the imaginary part of $1/\beta$ being zero:

$$\text{Im}\left[\frac{1 - (\omega RC)^2 + 3j\omega RC}{j\omega RC}\right] = 0$$

$$\text{Im}\left[\frac{1 - (\omega RC)^2}{j\omega RC} + 3\right] = 0$$

The constant 3 is real. For the first term:

$$\frac{1 - (\omega RC)^2}{j\omega RC} = -j \cdot \frac{1 - (\omega RC)^2}{\omega RC}$$

The imaginary part of this expression is:

$$-\frac{1 - (\omega RC)^2}{\omega RC} = 0$$

This requires:

$$1 - (\omega RC)^2 = 0 \implies \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \implies \omega_0 RC = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \implies \boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

### Step 6: Magnitude of $\beta$ at $f_0$

Substituting $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$:

$$|\beta(j\omega_0)| = \frac{\omega_0 RC}{\sqrt{(1 - (\omega_0 RC)^2)^2 + (3\omega_0 RC)^2}}$$

$$|\beta(j\omega_0)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0 + 9}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

### Step 7: Apply Magnitude Condition

For $|A\beta| = 1$:

$$|A| \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \implies \boxed{A = 3}$$

This is the famous **Wien bridge condition**: the amplifier must provide a gain of exactly **3** (or slightly more for startup).

### Step 3B: Verification

For $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 10\text{ nF}$:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}$$

This is in the **audio range** (around the standard 1 kHz test tone), confirming Wien bridge's use in audio instrumentation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Frequency Range** | 1 Hz – 1 MHz (AF) | 1 Hz – 1 MHz (AF) |
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$ | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| **Phase Shift Network** | 3-stage RC ladder ($3 \times 60° = 180°$) | Lead-lag network ($0°$ at $f_0$) |
| **Amplifier Type** | Common-Emitter (inverting, $180°$) | Non-inverting (two CE stages or op-amp) |
| **Required Gain** | $A \ge 29$ (ideal) | $A \ge 3$ |
| **Feedback Factor $\beta$** | $1/29$ | $1/3$ |
| **Output Waveform Purity** | Moderate (more distortion) | High (low distortion, ~0.01%) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Moderate | High (with amplitude stabilization) |
| **Amplitude Stabilization** | Self-limiting via transistor non-linearity | Thermistor / Zener / lamp / FET |
| **Typical Use** | Inexpensive tone generators | Lab signal generators, audio test equipment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔧 STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Oscillators

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["OSCILLATORS"]:::root
    rootA --> nodeB["BASED ON WAVEFORM"]
    rootA --> nodeC["BASED ON FEEDBACK NETWORK"]
    rootA --> nodeD["BASED ON FREQUENCY RANGE"]
    rootA --> nodeE["BASED ON ACTIVE DEVICE"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["SINUSOIDAL"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["NON-SINUSOIDAL"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1a["RC PHASE SHIFT"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1b["WIEN BRIDGE"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1c["COLPITTS"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1d["HARTLEY"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1e["CRYSTAL"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2a["ASTABLE MULTIVIBRATOR"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2b["BLOCKING OSCILLATOR"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2c["UJT RELAXATION"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["RC NETWORK"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["LC NETWORK"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["CRYSTAL"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a["RC PHASE SHIFT"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b["WIEN BRIDGE"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a["COLPITTS"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b["HARTLEY"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c["CLAPP"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["AF 1HZ TO 20KHZ"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["RF 20KHZ TO 300MHZ"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["VHF UHF ABOVE 300MHZ"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1a["RC PHASE SHIFT, WIEN BRIDGE"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2a["COLPITTS, HARTLEY, CRYSTAL"]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD3a["CAVITY, KLYSTRON"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["BJT BASED"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["FET BASED"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["OP AMP BASED"]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef def fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class rootA root
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3,nodeD1,nodeD2,nodeD3,nodeE1,nodeE2,nodeE3,nodeB1a,nodeB1b,nodeB1c,nodeB1d,nodeB1e,nodeB2a,nodeB2b,nodeB2c,nodeC1a,nodeC1b,nodeC2a,nodeC2b,nodeC2c,nodeD1a,nodeD2a,nodeD3a def
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block Diagram of a Feedback Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA["DC SUPPLY"]:::supply
    ampA["AMPLIFIER\nGAIN A"]:::amp
    fbA["FEEDBACK NETWORK\nGAIN BETA"]:::fb
    outputA["OUTPUT VOUT\nSINE WAVE"]:::out
    loopA["SUMMING JUNCTION"]:::sum

    inputA -->|"VDD"| ampA
    ampA -->|"VS"| loopA
    loopA --> outputA
    outputA -->|"VFB"| fbA
    fbA -->|"BETA TIMES VOUT"| loopA

    classDef supply fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: RC Phase Shift Oscillator (BJT-based) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    vcc1["VCC +12V"]:::supply
    rb11["RB1 BASE BIAS"]:::bias
    rb21["RB2 BASE BIAS"]:::bias
    rc1["RC COLLECTOR RESISTOR"]:::bias
    re1["RE EMITTER RESISTOR"]:::bias
    ce1["CE EMITTER BYPASS"]:::bias
    cin1["CIN INPUT COUPLING"]:::bias
    cout1["COUT OUTPUT COUPLING"]:::bias
    bjt1["BJT Q1\nCE AMPLIFIER\nGAIN A 180 DEG PHASE"]:::amp
    rc1a["R C SECTION 1\n60 DEG PHASE"]:::fb
    rc2a["R C SECTION 2\n60 DEG PHASE"]:::fb
    rc3a["R C SECTION 3\n60 DEG PHASE"]:::fb
    load1["RL LOAD\nSINE WAVE OUTPUT"]:::out

    vcc1 --> rc1
    rb11 --> bjt1
    rb21 --> bjt1
    rc1 --> bjt1
    bjt1 --> re1
    re1 --> ce1
    cin1 --> bjt1
    bjt1 --> cout1
    cout1 --> rc1a
    rc1a --> rc2a
    rc2a --> rc3a
    rc3a --> cin1
    cout1 --> load1

    classDef supply fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bias fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of Working:**

1. **CE Amplifier (BJT Q1):** Provides voltage gain $A_v \ge 29$ and a $180°$ phase inversion between collector and base.
2. **Three-Stage RC Ladder:** Each stage contributes $60°$ of additional phase shift, totaling $180°$.
3. **Total Loop Phase:** $180°$ (amplifier) + $180°$ (ladder) = $360°$ — satisfies Barkhausen phase condition.
4. **Loop Gain:** At frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$, $|A\beta| = 1$ — satisfies magnitude condition.
5. **Oscillation Buildup:** Initial noise is amplified and recirculated; amplitude grows until transistor non-linearity (saturation/cutoff) limits it.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Wien Bridge Oscillator (BJT-based) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    vcc2["VCC +12V"]:::supply
    vcc2a["VEE -12V"]:::supply
    ampA2["BJT Q1\nCE STAGE 1\nGAIN A1 INVERTING"]:::amp
    ampB2["BJT Q2\nCE STAGE 2\nGAIN A2 INVERTING"]:::amp
    totalGain["A1 TIMES A2 = A\nTOTAL GAIN 3 NON INVERTING"]:::amp
    leadNet["LEAD NETWORK\nSERIES R1 C1"]:::fb
    lagNet["LAG NETWORK\nPARALLEL R2 C2"]:::fb
    wienNet["WIEN NETWORK\nLEAD LAG ZERO PHASE AT F0"]:::fb
    rfb1["RFB FEEDBACK RESISTOR"]:::fb
    rin1["RIN INPUT RESISTOR"]:::fb
    gainSet["GAIN SET\nA = 1 PLUS RFB DIVIDED BY RIN = 3"]:::amp
    out2["SINE WAVE OUTPUT\nF0 = 1 DIV 2 PI RC"]:::out
    ampstab["AMPLITUDE LIMITER\nTHERMISTOR OR ZENER OR FET"]:::stab

    vcc2 --> ampA2
    vcc2a --> ampA2
    ampA2 --> ampB2
    ampB2 --> totalGain
    totalGain --> out2
    out2 --> leadNet
    leadNet --> lagNet
    lagNet --> wienNet
    wienNet -->|"BETA = 1 DIV 3"| ampA2
    out2 --> rfb1
    rfb1 --> rin1
    rin1 --> gainSet
    gainSet --> ampA2
    out2 --> ampstab
    ampstab -->|"STABILIZES AMPLITUDE"| gainSet

    classDef supply fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef stab fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Description of Working:**

1. **Two-Stage CE Amplifier (Q1 + Q2):** Two inversions = no net phase inversion (non-inverting overall), $A = A_1 \times A_2 \ge 3$.
2. **Wien Network (Lead + Lag):** Series RC ($Z_1$) and parallel RC ($Z_2$) form the positive feedback loop. At $f_0$, the network phase shift is $0°$.
3. **Total Loop Phase:** $0°$ (amplifier) + $0°$ (network) = $0°$ — satisfies phase condition.
4. **Magnitude Condition:** $|\beta| = 1/3$ at $f_0$, so $|A| = 3$ is required.
5. **Negative Feedback Loop:** $R_{FB}$ and $R_{in}$ set the gain to 3 precisely.
6. **Amplitude Stabilization:** A thermistor, lamp, or zener-based limiter keeps the gain from drifting.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Barkhausen Criterion Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    barkRoot["BARKHAUSEN CRITERION"]:::root
    barkRoot --> magCond["MAGNITUDE CONDITION\nVERTICAL BAR A TIMES BETA VERTICAL BAR = 1"]:::mag
    barkRoot --> phaseCond["PHASE CONDITION\nANGLE A TIMES BETA = 0 DEG OR 360 DEG"]:::phase
    barkRoot --> startup["STARTUP CONDITION\nVERTICAL BAR A TIMES BETA VERTICAL BAR GREATER THAN 1"]:::start
    magCond --> mag1["ENSURES NO GROWTH NO DECAY"]:::note
    phaseCond --> phase1["ENSURES POSITIVE FEEDBACK IN PHASE"]:::note
    startup --> start1["ENSURES BUILDUP FROM NOISE"]:::note

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef mag fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef start fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Barkhausen criterion** specifies two simultaneous conditions for sustained oscillation in a feedback amplifier used as an oscillator:
>
> 1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity, i.e., $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.
> 2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be $0°$ or an integer multiple of $360°$, i.e., $\angle A\beta = n \cdot 360°$, where $n$ is an integer.
>
> The magnitude condition ensures that the feedback signal is just enough to maintain the oscillation without growth or decay. The phase condition ensures the feedback is **positive** (in phase with the input). At startup, $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ so that oscillations build up from thermal noise; amplitude-limiting circuitry then reduces it to exactly 1 for steady-state.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Classify oscillators based on the feedback network used.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Oscillators can be classified based on the **feedback network** as follows:
>
> 1. **RC Oscillators** — Use resistor-capacitor networks in the feedback path. Examples: RC Phase Shift Oscillator, Wien Bridge Oscillator. Used for audio frequencies (1 Hz – 1 MHz).
> 2. **LC Oscillators** — Use inductor-capacitor tank circuits in the feedback path. Examples: Colpitts, Hartley, Clapp oscillators. Used for radio frequencies (1 MHz – 500 MHz).
> 3. **Crystal Oscillators** — Use a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz) as a highly selective feedback element. Examples: Pierce oscillator. Used where very high frequency stability is needed.
> 4. **Negative Resistance Oscillators** — Use devices like tunnel diodes, Gunn diodes that exhibit negative resistance.

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of an **RC phase shift oscillator using BJT** and explain its working. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an RC phase shift oscillator to generate a sine wave of frequency **2 kHz** using a BJT. Choose appropriate component values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📗 Solution for Q.A (a)

**Step 1: Circuit Description**

The RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- A **Common-Emitter (CE) BJT amplifier** providing voltage gain $A$ and a $180°$ phase inversion.
- A **three-stage RC ladder network** (each stage having equal $R$ and $C$) connected between the collector (output) and the base (input) of the BJT.
- Each RC section acts as a **high-pass filter** providing $60°$ of phase shift, totaling $180°$ from the ladder.

**Step 2: Working**

- The CE amplifier introduces a $180°$ phase shift between its input (base) and output (collector).
- The three-stage RC ladder introduces an additional $180°$ phase shift.
- Total loop phase: $180° + 180° = 360°$, satisfying the **Barkhausen phase condition**.
- At a specific frequency $f_0$, the magnitude of the feedback $\beta$ becomes exactly $1/29$, so the amplifier must provide a minimum gain of $A = 29$.
- Initial thermal noise gets amplified and recirculated, building up oscillations until transistor non-linearity stabilizes the amplitude.

**Step 3: Frequency of Oscillation**

For the **ideal unloaded case** (BJT input impedance $R_i = \infty$, output impedance $R_o = 0$):

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

The required minimum gain:

$$A_{min} = 29$$

For the **practical loaded case** with BJT input resistance $R_C$ considered:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6 + \dfrac{4R_C}{R}}}$$

$$A_{min} = 4k + 23 + \frac{29}{k}, \quad \text{where } k = \frac{R_C}{R}$$

**Step 4: Condition for Sustained Oscillation**

$$\boxed{\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \text{ at } f = f_0, \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 360°}$$

---

#### 📗 Solution for Q.A (b) — Design Problem

**Given:** $f_0 = 2 \text{ kHz}$

**Step 1: Choose $C$ (a convenient value, typically 1 nF to 100 nF).**

Let $C = 10 \text{ nF}$.

**Step 2: Solve for $R$ using the ideal frequency formula.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} \implies R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C\sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.449} = \frac{1}{3.078 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R \approx 3.25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Choose the BJT biasing for $A_v = 29$.**

Set emitter resistor $R_E = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. The voltage gain of a CE stage (unbypassed) is approximately:

$$A_v \approx \frac{R_C}{R_E}$$

For $A_v = 29$:

$$R_C = 29 \times R_E = 29 \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 29 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Set bias divider for $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$ to maximize swing.**

For $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$, choose $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard values).

**Final Design Summary:**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $C$ (each) | $10 \text{ nF}$ |
| $R$ (each) | $3.25 \text{ k}\Omega$ (use 3.3 kΩ standard) |
| $R_C$ (collector) | $29 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_E$ (emitter) | $1 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_1, R_2$ (bias) | $10 \text{ k}\Omega, 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $V_{CC}$ | $12 \text{ V}$ |
| BJT | BC547 or 2N2222 |
| $f_0$ (designed) | $\approx 1.97 \text{ kHz}$ (using 3.3 kΩ) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **Wien bridge oscillator using BJT** and explain its working. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, distinguish between **RC phase shift oscillator** and **Wien bridge oscillator** based on frequency, gain required, phase network, output purity, and applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📗 Solution for Q.B (a)

**Step 1: Circuit Description**

The Wien bridge oscillator consists of:
- A **two-stage CE BJT amplifier** (Q1 and Q2) providing non-inverting overall gain.
- A **Wien bridge network** in the **positive feedback path** — a series $R_1$-$C_1$ (lead) arm and a parallel $R_2$-$C_2$ (lag) arm.
- A **negative feedback network** of $R_{FB}$ and $R_{in}$ to set the gain precisely.
- An **amplitude stabilization** element (thermistor / zener / FET) in the negative feedback path.

**Step 2: Working**

- The two CE stages together provide a non-inverting gain $A = A_1 \times A_2$ (each CE gives $180°$, so two stages give $360° = 0°$).
- The Wien network provides a **frequency-selective positive feedback** — at the resonant frequency $f_0$, the network has $0°$ phase shift and feedback factor $\beta = 1/3$.
- To satisfy the magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, the amplifier gain must be **$A = 3$**.
- This is set by the negative feedback: $A = 1 + R_{FB}/R_{in} = 3$, giving $R_{FB} = 2 R_{in}$.
- At startup, $A$ is slightly greater than 3 (e.g., 3.1) so oscillations build up. The amplitude-limiting element (e.g., thermistor whose resistance increases with temperature) reduces $A$ back to 3 for stable output.

**Step 3: Derivation of Frequency**

The feedback factor of the Wien network is:

$$\beta(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$ and setting the imaginary part of the denominator relation to zero for zero phase:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 4: Required Gain**

$$|\beta(j\omega_0)| = \frac{1}{3} \implies |A| = 3$$

---

#### 📗 Solution for Q.B (b) — Comparison

| Feature | RC Phase Shift | Wien Bridge |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$ | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| **Phase Network** | 3-stage RC ladder ($3 \times 60°$) | Lead-lag network ($0°$ at $f_0$) |
| **Amplifier** | Single CE (inverting) | Two CE stages (non-inverting) |
| **Gain Needed** | $\ge 29$ | $\ge 3$ |
| **Feedback Factor** | $\beta = 1/29$ | $\beta = 1/3$ |
| **Output Purity** | Moderate (more THD) | High (low THD, ~0.01%) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Moderate | Good (with amplitude stabilization) |
| **Stabilization Method** | Self (transistor saturation) | Thermistor/Zener/FET |
| **Applications** | Inexpensive tone generators | Lab oscillators, audio test gear |
| **Complexity** | Simple (1 BJT) | Higher (2 BJTs + bridge) |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Oscillator Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the phase condition** — Many students write only $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and forget the phase condition $\angle A\beta = 0°/360°$. The Barkhausen criterion has **BOTH** conditions. Missing one means losing 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Writing the wrong Wien bridge gain** — Some students write $A = 1/3$ (this is the feedback factor, not the gain). The correct amplifier gain is $A = 3$.
>
> 3. **Confusing "ideal" vs "loaded" formulas** — For the RC phase shift, the question may ask either form. If the question says "with BJT input resistance considered," use the loaded form. Always state the assumption.
>
> 4. **Skipping the amplitude stabilization discussion** — Wien bridge questions specifically test the role of the thermistor/limiter. Don't just write the frequency equation; explain **how the amplitude is stabilized**.
>
> 5. **Mermaid diagram reserved words** — If drawing on paper in the exam, label your diagrams clearly. Examiners deduct for unlabeled components and missing arrow directions.
>
> 6. **Missing "why three sections"** — In RC phase shift, justify why exactly **three** RC sections are used: two would give $120°$ (insufficient for CE amplifier's $180°$), and four is unnecessary overhead.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Must-Know Bullets:**
>
> - ✅ An oscillator needs **no external input** — it generates its own signal.
> - ✅ Oscillators are essentially **amplifiers with positive feedback**.
> - ✅ **Barkhausen Criterion** has two conditions: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ **AND** $\angle A\beta = 0°/360°$.
> - ✅ At startup, $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ to grow from noise; amplitude limiter reduces it to 1.
> - ✅ Classification is by: **waveform, frequency range, feedback type, and active device**.
> - ✅ **RC oscillators** are for **audio frequencies** (1 Hz – 1 MHz).
> - ✅ **LC oscillators** are for **radio frequencies** (1 MHz – 500 MHz).
> - ✅ **Crystal oscillators** give the **highest frequency stability**.
> - ✅ **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** uses **3 RC sections** ($3 \times 60° = 180°$) + CE amplifier ($180°$) = $360°$.
> - ✅ RC Phase Shift frequency: $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$ (ideal); $1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6 + 4R_C/R})$ (loaded).
> - ✅ RC Phase Shift required gain: $A = 29$ (ideal).
> - ✅ **Wien Bridge Oscillator** uses a **lead-lag network** with $0°$ phase shift at $f_0$.
> - ✅ Wien Bridge frequency: $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
> - ✅ Wien Bridge required gain: $A = 3$, set by $A = 1 + R_{FB}/R_{in}$.
> - ✅ Wien Bridge has **lower distortion** than RC Phase Shift.
> - ✅ Wien Bridge uses **two CE stages** (or op-amp) for non-inverting gain.
> - ✅ Amplitude stabilization in Wien Bridge: **thermistor, zener, FET, or incandescent lamp**.
> - ✅ "Analysis not required" in KTU 2024 syllabus means: **no detailed nodal analysis** — focus on **circuit, design equations, working, and block diagrams**.
> - ✅ Always state **assumptions** when writing formulas (e.g., $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$).
> - ✅ Be able to **numerically design** an oscillator (choose $C$, find $R$, set bias).

---
