topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT302 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regulated Power Supplies: Simple Zener Voltage Regulator, Series Voltage Regulator, IC 723 Regulators (Block Diagram, Typical Low/High Voltage Circuits), 3-Pin Regulators: 78XX and 79XX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Regulated Power Supply?

A **Regulated Power Supply (RPS)** is an electronic circuit that converts an unregulated AC input into a constant, stable DC output voltage (or current) that is largely independent of variations in **line voltage (input)**, **load current**, and **temperature**. It is the final, most critical stage in a linear DC power supply block chain: `Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator → DC Load`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Examiner's Wording):**
> A voltage regulator is an electronic circuit that provides a constant DC output voltage irrespective of variations in the supply voltage, the load current, or the temperature. The block that performs this function is the heart of any regulated DC power supply.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine filling a glass of water from a tap:
- If the water pressure (analogous to **input line voltage**) fluctuates wildly, or
- If the glass size (analogous to **load current demand**) keeps changing,

...you still want exactly the same amount of water flowing steadily. A **voltage regulator is the perfect electronic equivalent of a "pressure-compensating tap"** — no matter what happens upstream or downstream, the output pressure (voltage) stays rock-steady.

The term "regulation" quantifies exactly *how steady* the output is. Mathematically:

$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} \quad \text{(V/V or %/V)}$$

$$\text{Load Regulation} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

where $V_{NL}$ is the no-load output voltage and $V_{FL}$ is the full-load output voltage. **Lower percentage = better regulation.**

---

## 1.2 Classification of Voltage Regulators

Voltage regulators are broadly classified as:

| Category | Sub-Types | Key Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete Component Regulators** | Zener-based, Series Pass, Shunt Pass | Zener regulator, Emitter-follower regulator |
| **Linear IC Regulators** | Fixed 3-pin, Adjustable 3-pin, Multi-function | **78XX, 79XX, LM317, LM723, LM337** |
| **Switching Regulators** | Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost | LM2596, LT1083 (out of syllabus scope) |

This module focuses on the **discrete regulators** and the **monolithic linear IC regulators** (723, 78XX, 79XX).

---

## 1.3 The Simple Zener Voltage Regulator

### Formal Definition

The **Zener voltage regulator** is the simplest shunt-type voltage regulator. It exploits the **Zener breakdown region** of a reverse-biased PN-junction diode, where the diode maintains a nearly constant voltage ($V_Z$) across its terminals over a wide range of reverse currents, provided the current stays between the **minimum Zener current ($I_{Zmin}$ or $I_{ZK}$)** and the **maximum Zener current ($I_{Zmax}$ or $I_{ZM}$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Zener as a Reference Element:**
> The Zener diode in a regulator is almost always operated in **reverse bias**, in the region beyond its knee voltage. The dynamic (AC) resistance $r_Z$ is typically very small (a few ohms to tens of ohms), which is what gives it excellent voltage-stabilizing capability.

### Circuit Intuition

Think of the Zener diode as a "smart pressure-relief valve":
- When input voltage tries to rise → extra current is shunted through the Zener (diverted to ground), keeping output constant.
- When load tries to draw more current → Zener supplies less current (or input supplies more), keeping output constant.

### The Essential Series Resistor ($R_S$)

The **series resistor $R_S$** is mandatory — it is the element that *absorbs* the difference between input and output voltages and limits Zener current to a safe value. Without $R_S$, the Zener would burn out instantly.

$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_S} = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$$

---

## 1.4 The Series Voltage Regulator (Series Pass Regulator)

### Formal Definition

A **series voltage regulator** is a linear regulator in which a **controllable active element (transistor)** is placed **in series** between the unregulated DC input and the load. The transistor, called the **series pass element** (or **pass transistor**), is driven by an error amplifier such that its effective resistance dynamically adjusts to maintain a constant output voltage.

### Circuit Intuition

Visualize the series pass transistor as a **variable resistor controlled automatically**:
- If $V_{OUT}$ tries to rise → the transistor's resistance is increased, dropping more voltage across it.
- If $V_{OUT}$ tries to fall → the transistor's resistance is decreased, dropping less voltage.

The "controller" is the **error amplifier**, which continuously compares a sample of $V_{OUT}$ with a fixed **reference voltage** (typically provided by a Zener diode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Series" and not "Shunt"?**
> In a shunt regulator, current is *diverted* away from the load (Zener path). In a series regulator, current is *controlled* by varying the voltage drop across a series element. Series regulators are far more efficient and form the basis of every linear IC regulator (78XX, 79XX, LM723, LM317).

---

## 1.5 The IC 723 Voltage Regulator (LM723 / µA723)

### Formal Definition

The **IC 723** (LM723, µA723, NE555 of power — a classic workhorse) is a **monolithic, multi-function, linear voltage regulator IC** containing an internal **reference voltage source, error amplifier, series pass transistor, and current-limiting circuitry** — all on a single silicon chip. It can be configured for both **low-voltage (2 V – 7 V)** and **high-voltage (7 V – 37 V)** operation, with either **positive or negative** output polarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** The syllabus specifically asks for the **block diagram**, the **typical low-voltage circuit**, and the **typical high-voltage circuit** of the 723.

### Circuit Intuition

The 723 is essentially a "regulator on a chip." It gives you the building blocks (reference, error amp, pass transistor) and lets *you* decide how to wire them externally. It is to analog design what LEGO is to toys — versatile, modular, and a bit fiddly.

### Key Internal Blocks (as required by KTU)

1. **Zener Reference Source** — generates a stable internal reference $V_{REF} \approx 7.15$ V (typical).
2. **Error Amplifier** — a high-gain differential amplifier that compares a feedback sample to $V_{REF}$.
3. **Series Pass Transistor** — an NPN Darlington capable of $\approx$ 150 mA (internally) and up to **several amps with an external pass transistor**.
4. **Current-Limit Transistor** — protects the pass transistor from over-current.
5. **Three Output Voltage Access Pins** — $V_{OUT}$ (pin 10), $V_{REF}$ (pin 6), and the $V_{IN}$ to pass transistor (pin 11/12).

### Low-Voltage vs High-Voltage Mode (Critical Distinction)

| Mode | Output Voltage Range | Internal Pass Transistor Used? | $V_{IN}$ to Pass Transistor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Voltage Mode** | $V_{OUT} = 2$ V to $7$ V | **Yes** — internal transistor only | Connected to $V_{OUT}$ pin (i.e., output of regulator) |
| **High-Voltage Mode** | $V_{OUT} = 7$ V to $37$ V | **Internal transistor acts as a pre-driver; external NPN pass transistor required** | Connected to unregulated $V_{IN}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In high-voltage mode, the internal pass transistor's collector goes to the **unregulated input**, not the regulated output. This is the single most-asked KTU question on the 723.

---

## 1.6 The 3-Pin Fixed Voltage Regulators — 78XX and 79XX

### Formal Definition

The **78XX series** are **positive fixed-output, three-terminal linear voltage regulators** in the TO-220 (or TO-3) package, where "XX" denotes the output voltage (e.g., 7805 → +5 V, 7812 → +12 V, 7815 → +15 V). Similarly, the **79XX series** are the **negative fixed-output** counterparts (7905 → −5 V, 7912 → −12 V). Both families are internally complete — they contain the reference, error amplifier, pass transistor, and thermal/short-circuit protection, requiring only **two external capacitors** for stable operation in most applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Are Industry Workhorses:**
> 78XX and 79XX ICs dominate real-world electronics because of **3 killer features**: (1) only **3 pins** (Input, Ground, Output) — drop-in simplicity, (2) **built-in thermal shutdown and current limiting** — virtually indestructible, and (3) **inexpensive** (₹15–₹40 in Indian market). They are the "USB-C of linear regulators."

### Pinout Standard (TO-220, viewed from front with tab)

**78XX:**
```
      [Tab = Ground (GND)]
  ┌─────────────┐
  │ 1   2   3   │
  └────┬──┬──┬──┘
       IN  GND OUT
```

**79XX:**
```
      [Tab = Ground (GND)]
  ┌─────────────┐
  │ 1   2   3   │
  └────┬──┬──┬──┘
       GND IN  OUT
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pinout Trap (Most Common Board Mistake):** The **input/output pin assignments are reversed** between 78XX and 79XX. Wiring a 7812 like a 7912 (or vice versa) will instantly destroy the IC.

### Standard Application Circuit (78XX)

The classic circuit is the most-tested 78XX question in KTU. It requires only:
- **$C_{IN} = 0.33$ µF** (bypass at input) — placed close to IN pin.
- **$C_{OUT} = 0.1$ µF** (improves transient response at output).
- These are tantalum or ceramic; electrolytics also work for $C_{IN}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Zener Voltage Regulator — Deep Analysis

### Circuit Description

The simple Zener regulator consists of:
- An unregulated DC source $V_{IN}$ (the output of a rectifier-filter combination, which still has some ripple).
- A series resistor $R_S$ — the current-limiting/dropping resistor.
- A Zener diode $D_Z$ operated in reverse breakdown — connected in parallel with the load $R_L$.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

Let $V_Z$ = Zener voltage (specified at a test current $I_{ZT}$), $I_Z$ = Zener current, $I_L$ = load current, $I_S$ = current through $R_S$.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**
$$V_{IN} = I_S \cdot R_S + V_{OUT}$$

Since $V_{OUT} = V_Z$ (clamped by the Zener), we have:
$$V_{OUT} = V_Z \quad \text{(constant, by Zener action)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Zener node:**
$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**
$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$$

### Key Design Constraints

For the regulator to function properly, the following inequalities **must** be satisfied:

1. **Regulation against input line variations** — the input must be high enough to keep Zener in breakdown:
$$V_{IN(min)} \geq \frac{V_Z (R_S + R_L)}{R_L} \quad \text{equivalently,} \quad V_{IN} - V_Z \geq I_{Z(min)} \cdot R_S$$

2. **Regulation against load variations** — the Zener current must not fall below $I_{Zmin}$ (knee current):
$$I_Z = I_S - I_L \geq I_{Zmin}$$

3. **Zener safety limit** — Zener current must not exceed $I_{Zmax}$ (power dissipation limit):
$$I_Z = I_S - I_L \leq I_{Zmax} = \frac{P_Z}{V_Z}$$

### Regulator Performance Analysis

The output is not perfectly constant. The Zener has a small **dynamic resistance** $r_Z$ in the breakdown region. When the input voltage changes by $\Delta V_{IN}$, the output changes by:

$$\Delta V_{OUT} = \Delta V_{IN} \cdot \frac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z} \approx \Delta V_{IN} \cdot \frac{r_Z}{R_S} \quad (R_S \gg r_Z)$$

This gives the **line regulation**:
$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \frac{r_Z}{R_S} \quad \text{(dimensionless, in V/V)}$$

Similarly, when the load current changes by $\Delta I_L$ (load resistance changes by $\Delta R_L$), the output ripple/resistance is given by:
$$\text{Output Impedance} \quad Z_{OUT} \approx r_Z \parallel R_S \approx r_Z$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $r_Z$ is small (typically 5–50 Ω for power Zeners), the Zener regulator has a **low output impedance** — usually under 10 Ω, which is excellent for its simplicity.

### Limitations of Simple Zener Regulator

1. **Poor efficiency** — significant power is wasted in $R_S$ and the Zener.
2. **Fixed output voltage** — only one output per Zener.
3. **Limited current** — practical Zener regulators handle only tens of mA.
4. **Ripple passes through partially** — $\Delta V_{IN}$ ripple is attenuated by factor $r_Z / R_S$ but not eliminated.
5. **No current limiting** — the Zener can overheat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trend:** Questions on the Zener regulator now emphasize the **design of $R_S$** to satisfy both upper (max power) and lower (min current) bounds — a 2- or 3-mark KTU favorite.

---

## 2.2 Series Voltage Regulator — Deep Analysis

### Circuit Description

A basic series regulator has the following building blocks:
- **Series pass transistor** ($Q_1$, an NPN) — the variable resistance in series with the load.
- **Zener reference** ($D_Z$) — provides a stable reference voltage $V_Z$ at the base of $Q_1$.
- **Sampling resistors** ($R_1$, $R_2$) — form a voltage divider that samples the output.
- **Error amplifier transistor** ($Q_2$) — compares the sampled output with $V_Z$ and drives the pass transistor.
- **Load** ($R_L$) — connected at $V_{OUT}$.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Reference voltage** is established at the base of $Q_1$ via the Zener:
$$V_{B1} = V_Z + V_{BE(Q1)} \approx V_Z + 0.7$$

Wait — in a basic series follower, the Zener is at the base and the emitter is the output. So:
$$V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE} \approx V_Z - 0.7$$

**Step 2 — Sampling** the output:
$$V_{SENSE} = V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:** The error amplifier $Q_2$ has its base at $V_{SENSE}$ and its emitter at $V_Z$. The difference $(V_Z - V_{SENSE})$ drives the base of $Q_1$, modulating its conduction.

**Step 4 — Negative feedback regulation**:
- If $V_{OUT} \uparrow$ → $V_{SENSE} \uparrow$ → $V_{BE(Q2)} \downarrow$ → $Q_2$ conducts less → $Q_1$ receives less base drive → $Q_1$ conducts less → $V_{OUT} \downarrow$ ✓
- If $V_{OUT} \downarrow$ → opposite chain → $V_{OUT} \uparrow$ ✓

**Step 5 — Closed-loop output equation:**
At equilibrium, $V_{SENSE} = V_Z$, so:
$$V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

This is **adjustable** — by choosing the resistor ratio, we can set any output above $V_Z$.

### Adding Current Limiting (Practical Series Regulator)

A series resistor $R_{SC}$ in the emitter (or source) of the pass transistor, plus a current-sense transistor $Q_3$, form a **current-limit foldback** circuit. When load current exceeds a threshold:
$$I_{L(max)} \cdot R_{SC} \approx V_{BE(Q3)} \approx 0.7 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{L(max)} = \frac{0.7}{R_{SC}}$$

### Series vs Shunt — Quantitative Comparison

| Parameter | Series Regulator | Shunt (Zener) Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | High (transistor drop small) | Low (Zener + $R_S$ dissipate full current) |
| Current capability | High (set by pass transistor) | Low (limited by Zener) |
| Short-circuit protection | Easy to add | Difficult |
| Output impedance | Very low (loop gain × $r_{CE}$) | $\approx r_Z$ |
| Complexity | Higher | Trivial |

---

## 2.3 IC 723 Voltage Regulator — Deep Analysis

### Internal Block Diagram (as required by KTU)

The 723 contains:
1. **Zener reference** (≈ 7.15 V) at pin 6.
2. **Constant-current source** to bias the reference.
3. **Error amplifier** — a high-gain differential amplifier with inverting (pin 4) and non-inverting (pin 5) inputs, and an output at pin 7.
4. **Series pass transistor** — NPN with collector at pin 11, emitter at pin 10, base driven by the error amplifier output via an internal Darlington.
5. **Current-limit transistor** — senses voltage across an external $R_{SC}$ between pins 2 and 3.

### Two Configurations (Most-Asked Topic)

#### A. Low-Voltage Configuration ($V_{OUT} = 2$ V to 7 V)

In this mode:
- The **internal pass transistor** is used.
- The collector of the pass transistor (pin 11) is **connected to the output (pin 10)** — not to the unregulated input.
- The unregulated $V_{IN}$ is applied to pin 12 (separate $V_{CC}$ for the chip's internal circuitry).
- The reference voltage and the feedback divider are arranged so that:
$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 7.15 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

To get $V_{OUT}$ below 7.15 V, the divider is set to tap *below* $V_{REF}$. The minimum $V_{OUT} \approx 2$ V is limited by the headroom of the internal pass transistor.

#### B. High-Voltage Configuration ($V_{OUT} = 7$ V to 37 V)

In this mode:
- The **internal pass transistor is used as a pre-amplifier**; an **external NPN pass transistor** ($Q_{ext}$) handles the main current.
- The collector of the **internal pass transistor (pin 11)** is connected to the **unregulated $V_{IN}$**.
- The base of $Q_{ext}$ is driven from pin 10 (internal pass transistor's emitter).
- The error amplifier uses the divider to compare $V_{OUT}$ to $V_{REF}$:
$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = 7.15 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

For $V_{OUT} > 7.15$ V, the divider must multiply the reference.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why high-voltage mode is needed for $V_{OUT} > 7$ V:** If you tried to use the internal pass transistor in low-voltage mode for $V_{OUT} = 15$ V, its $V_{CE}$ would be $V_{IN} - 15$. With $V_{IN}$ just above 15 V, the transistor would saturate or be unable to regulate. The high-voltage mode lets the external transistor take the high $V_{CE}$ hit.

### Current Limit Equation (Same for Both Modes)

$$I_{L(max)} = \frac{V_{sense}}{R_{SC}} \approx \frac{0.7 \text{ V}}{R_{SC}}$$

where $R_{SC}$ is the resistor between pins 2 and 3.

---

## 2.4 3-Pin Regulators 78XX and 79XX — Deep Analysis

### Functional Block Diagram (Internal)

Internally, a 78XX/79XX contains:
- **Bandgap reference** (more accurate than Zener).
- **Error amplifier** with very high gain.
- **Series pass transistor** (a Darlington NPN for 78XX, a Darlington PNP for 79XX).
- **Thermal shutdown** (kicks in at $\approx$ 150–175 °C junction).
- **Short-circuit / current-limit** circuitry.
- **Safe-Area Protection (SOA)**.

### Key Specifications (from datasheet — memorise these for KTU)

| Parameter | Symbol | 7805 (typical) | 7812 (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output voltage | $V_{OUT}$ | +5 V | +12 V |
| Input voltage (min) | $V_{IN(min)}$ | 7 V | 14.5 V |
| Input voltage (max) | $V_{IN(max)}$ | 35 V | 35 V |
| Dropout voltage | $V_{DROP}$ | 2 V | 2 V |
| Quiescent current | $I_Q$ | 5 mA | 5 mA |
| Output current | $I_{OUT(max)}$ | 1 A (with heatsink) | 1 A |
| Line regulation | – | 0.01 %/V | 0.01 %/V |
| Load regulation | – | 10 mV | 12 mV |
| Ripple rejection | PSRR | 78 dB | 71 dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Dropout voltage** is the minimum $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT})$ required for regulation. For 7805, $V_{IN}$ must be at least **7 V** (5 V + 2 V dropout) — often rounded to 7.5 V or 8 V in practice.

### Power Dissipation Calculation (Critical for Board Problems)

The power dissipated by the regulator is:
$$P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{OUT} + V_{IN} \cdot I_Q$$

The thermal resistance junction-to-ambient $\theta_{JA}$ determines the maximum allowable $P_D$ without exceeding the junction temperature:
$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$$

For TO-220 without heatsink, $\theta_{JA} \approx 50$ °C/W; with a good heatsink, it can drop to 5–10 °C/W.

### Standard Application Circuit

```
  V_IN (DC) ──►|── C_IN ──┬───── Pin 1 (IN)
              Diode       │
              (reverse    │
              protection) │
                          │
                ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                │       78XX         │
                │   [Internal       │
                │    Regulator]      │
                │                   │
   GND ────────┴──── Pin 2 (GND)    │
                                       │
                  Pin 3 (OUT) ──┬── C_OUT ── GND
                                │
                              V_OUT
```

- **$C_{IN} = 0.33$ µF** (tantalum) — improves transient response and filters high-frequency noise at the input.
- **$C_{OUT} = 0.1$ µF** (ceramic) — improves output impedance and transient response.
- **Reverse-polarity protection diode** (Schottky or 1N4001) at the input — recommended for industrial designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite 14-Mark Question:** "Design a 5 V regulated power supply using 7805 for a load requiring 1 A. Given: $V_{IN}$ = 9–12 V (unregulated), $T_A$ = 50 °C, $\theta_{JA}$ of TO-220 = 50 °C/W. Determine whether a heatsink is required." — This combines the dropout, power dissipation, and thermal analysis.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zener regulator series resistor | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$ | $R_S$ in $\Omega$, all voltages in V, currents in A |
| 2 | Zener line regulation | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \dfrac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z} \approx \dfrac{r_Z}{R_S}$ | Dimensionless (V/V) |
| 3 | Zener output impedance | $Z_{OUT} \approx r_Z$ | In ohms |
| 4 | Maximum Zener current | $I_{Zmax} = \dfrac{P_Z}{V_Z}$ | A; limited by Zener power rating |
| 5 | Series regulator output | $V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | $V_{OUT} > V_Z$ |
| 6 | Series regulator current limit | $I_{L(max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}} \approx \dfrac{0.7}{R_{SC}}$ | A |
| 7 | 723 — Low-voltage mode output | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $2\text{ V} \le V_{OUT} \le 7\text{ V}$ |
| 8 | 723 — High-voltage mode output | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | $7\text{ V} \le V_{OUT} \le 37\text{ V}$ |
| 9 | 723 — Internal reference | $V_{REF} \approx 7.15$ V | Typical value |
| 10 | 723 — Current limit | $I_{L(max)} = \dfrac{0.7}{R_{SC}}$ | $R_{SC}$ between pins 2 and 3 |
| 11 | 78XX — Dropout requirement | $V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DROP} = V_{OUT} + 2$ | At full load |
| 12 | 78XX/79XX — Power dissipation | $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) I_{OUT}$ | In watts; add $V_{IN} I_Q$ for accuracy |
| 13 | Thermal limit | $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$ | $T_{J(max)} = 125\text{ °C}$ typical |
| 14 | Load regulation | $\text{Load Reg.} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | In % |
| 15 | Line regulation | $\text{Line Reg.} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}}$ | In V/V or %/V |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These regulator topologies are the **invisible backbone of every electronic device**:

- **Zener regulators** — used as low-cost **reference sources** in ADCs, DACs, comparators, and as protection clamps. Still found in **every op-amp's input protection**.
- **Series regulators** — the internal architecture of nearly all linear IC regulators (LM317, 7805, LM723, TPS7A45).
- **LM723** — the "Swiss Army knife" of analog design. Used in **lab bench power supplies**, **battery chargers**, **automotive ECUs**, and **avionics**, especially where the design must be qualified for high reliability.
- **78XX/79XX** — present in **every computer motherboard, every USB charger, every router, every TV, every Arduino board** (the Arduino Uno's barrel-jack input feeds a 7805 or its switching equivalent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Trend:** Even though switching regulators dominate modern high-efficiency designs, **linear regulators remain preferred for noise-sensitive analog/RF circuitry** (audio DACs, phono preamps, RF front-ends) because they produce virtually no EMI.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Zener Regulator — Design of $R_S$ (Comprehensive)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** Design a Zener voltage regulator to provide a regulated output of **+9.1 V** from an unregulated input that varies between **+12 V and +15 V**. The load requires a constant current of **20 mA**, and the Zener diode (1N4739A) has $I_{Zmin} = 1$ mA and $P_Z = 1$ W. Calculate:
> 1. The required series resistance $R_S$.
> 2. Verify that the Zener operates safely under all input conditions.

### Given Data

$$V_{IN(min)} = 12 \text{ V}, \quad V_{IN(max)} = 15 \text{ V}, \quad V_Z = 9.1 \text{ V}$$

$$I_L = 20 \text{ mA} = 0.020 \text{ A}, \quad I_{Zmin} = 1 \text{ mA} = 0.001 \text{ A}$$

$$P_Z = 1 \text{ W}, \quad V_{OUT} = V_Z = 9.1 \text{ V}$$

### Step 1 — Compute $I_{Zmax}$ from power rating

$$I_{Zmax} = \frac{P_Z}{V_Z} = \frac{1 \text{ W}}{9.1 \text{ V}}$$

$$I_{Zmax} = 0.1099 \text{ A} \approx 110 \text{ mA}$$

This is the **upper bound** on Zener current — beyond this, the diode overheats.

### Step 2 — Compute current through $R_S$ at minimum input (worst-case for low Zener current)

At $V_{IN} = V_{IN(min)} = 12$ V, the voltage across $R_S$ is smallest, so $I_S$ is smallest, and $I_Z$ is smallest. This is the **critical** case — the Zener must still be in breakdown (i.e., $I_Z \geq I_{Zmin}$).

$$I_{S(min)} = I_{Zmin} + I_L = 0.001 + 0.020 = 0.021 \text{ A}$$

The voltage across $R_S$ at this condition:
$$V_{R_S(min)} = V_{IN(min)} - V_Z = 12 - 9.1 = 2.9 \text{ V}$$

So the **maximum allowable** $R_S$ (to keep $I_Z \geq I_{Zmin}$):
$$R_{S(max)} = \frac{V_{R_S(min)}}{I_{S(min)}} = \frac{2.9}{0.021} = 138.1 \text{ }\Omega$$

### Step 3 — Compute current through $R_S$ at maximum input (worst-case for high Zener current)

At $V_{IN} = V_{IN(max)} = 15$ V, the voltage across $R_S$ is largest, so $I_S$ is largest, and $I_Z$ is largest. This is the critical case for **Zener power dissipation** (must not exceed $I_{Zmax}$).

$$I_{S(max)} = I_{Zmax} + I_L = 0.110 + 0.020 = 0.130 \text{ A}$$

The voltage across $R_S$ at this condition:
$$V_{R_S(max)} = V_{IN(max)} - V_Z = 15 - 9.1 = 5.9 \text{ V}$$

So the **minimum allowable** $R_S$ (to keep $I_Z \leq I_{Zmax}$):
$$R_{S(min)} = \frac{V_{R_S(max)}}{I_{S(max)}} = \frac{5.9}{0.130} = 45.4 \text{ }\Omega$$

### Step 4 — Choose $R_S$ in the allowable range

We need:
$$45.4 \text{ }\Omega \leq R_S \leq 138.1 \text{ }\Omega$$

Choose the **nearest standard E12/E24 value** in the safe middle of the range. A common choice:
$$R_S = 82 \text{ }\Omega \text{ (or 100 }\Omega\text{)}$$

Let's verify with $R_S = 82$ Ω.

### Step 5 — Verification

**At $V_{IN(min)} = 12$ V:**
$$I_{S(min)} = \frac{12 - 9.1}{82} = \frac{2.9}{82} = 0.0354 \text{ A} = 35.4 \text{ mA}$$
$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 35.4 - 20 = 15.4 \text{ mA} \geq I_{Zmin} = 1 \text{ mA} \quad ✓$$

**At $V_{IN(max)} = 15$ V:**
$$I_{S(max)} = \frac{15 - 9.1}{82} = \frac{5.9}{82} = 0.0719 \text{ A} = 71.9 \text{ mA}$$
$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 71.9 - 20 = 51.9 \text{ mA} \leq I_{Zmax} = 110 \text{ mA} \quad ✓$$

### Step 6 — Power rating of $R_S$

The maximum power dissipated in $R_S$ occurs at $V_{IN(max)}$:
$$P_{R_S(max)} = I_{S(max)}^2 \cdot R_S = (0.0719)^2 \times 82 = 0.424 \text{ W}$$

Choose a resistor with at least **2× safety margin** → use a **1 W, 82 Ω resistor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the derivation:** The design of $R_S$ is a **two-bound problem** — $R_S$ must be simultaneously small enough to not exceed the Zener's power rating and large enough to keep the Zener in breakdown. This duality is what makes Zener regulator design non-trivial and is the KTU examiner's favorite trap.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: IC 723 — High-Voltage Regulator Design

> **Problem (KTU Style):** Design a +15 V regulated power supply using the **LM723** in **high-voltage configuration**. The unregulated input is $V_{IN} = +25$ V, the load current is $I_L = 1.5$ A, and the current limit is to be set at $I_{L(max)} = 2$ A. Use the standard reference $V_{REF} = 7.15$ V. Calculate:
> 1. The values of feedback resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$.
> 2. The current-sense resistor $R_{SC}$.
> 3. The minimum $V_{CE}$ across the external pass transistor.

### Given Data

$$V_{IN} = 25 \text{ V}, \quad V_{OUT} = 15 \text{ V}, \quad V_{REF} = 7.15 \text{ V}, \quad I_L = 1.5 \text{ A}, \quad I_{L(max)} = 2 \text{ A}$$

### Step 1 — High-Voltage Mode Output Equation

In high-voltage mode, the divider is in a **non-inverting amplifier** configuration:
$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the resistor ratio

Rearranging for $R_1/R_2$:
$$\frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{REF}} = 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}$$

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{REF}} - 1 = \frac{15}{7.15} - 1 = 2.0979 - 1 = 1.0979$$

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} \approx 1.10$$

### Step 3 — Choose a standard value for $R_2$ and compute $R_1$

A common choice in LM723 designs: $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

Then:
$$R_1 = 1.10 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 11.0 \text{ k}\Omega$$

This is a standard 1% resistor value, or we can fine-tune with $R_1 = 11$ kΩ. Alternatively, use $R_2 = 4.7$ kΩ and $R_1 = 5.1$ kΩ for $\approx 1.085$ ratio (giving $V_{OUT} \approx 14.9$ V, close enough).

### Step 4 — Compute $R_{SC}$ for current limit

$$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{I_{L(max)}} = \frac{0.7 \text{ V}}{2 \text{ A}} = 0.35 \text{ }\Omega$$

Use $R_{SC} = 0.33$ Ω (standard value), 1 W or 2 W rated (since it carries the full load current during limiting):
$$P_{R_{SC}(max)} = I_{L(max)}^2 \times R_{SC} = 4 \times 0.33 = 1.32 \text{ W}$$

Use a **2 W** resistor for safety.

### Step 5 — Voltage across the external pass transistor

$$V_{CE(ext)} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT} = 25 - 15 = 10 \text{ V}$$

This is the voltage the external pass transistor must handle. The power dissipated in it:
$$P_{Q_{ext}} = V_{CE} \cdot I_L = 10 \times 1.5 = 15 \text{ W}$$

This is **significant** — the external transistor (e.g., 2N3055, TIP31, or a Darlington like TIP122) **must be mounted on a substantial heatsink**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** High drop = high waste heat. This is why linear regulators are inefficient for large $V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$ drops. A switching regulator (buck converter) would dissipate far less. However, the LM723 linear design has **no switching noise** — preferred for analog/RF applications.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: 78XX — Heatsink Requirement Analysis

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A 7805 regulator provides +5 V at 800 mA to a load. The unregulated input is $V_{IN} = 12$ V. The ambient temperature is $T_A = 45$ °C. The TO-220 package has $\theta_{JC} = 4$ °C/W, and the maximum junction temperature is $T_{J(max)} = 125$ °C. Determine:
> 1. The power dissipated in the 7805.
> 2. The maximum thermal resistance junction-to-ambient $\theta_{JA(max)}$.
> 3. Whether a heatsink is needed if free-air $\theta_{JA}$ is 50 °C/W.

### Given Data

$$V_{IN} = 12 \text{ V}, \quad V_{OUT} = 5 \text{ V}, \quad I_{OUT} = 0.8 \text{ A}, \quad T_A = 45 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{J(max)} = 125 \text{ °C}, \quad \theta_{JC} = 4 \text{ °C/W}, \quad \theta_{JA(free)} = 50 \text{ °C/W}$$

### Step 1 — Power Dissipation

$$P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{OUT} = (12 - 5) \times 0.8 = 5.6 \text{ W}$$

(We ignore the quiescent current term, since $I_Q \approx 5$ mA, contributing $V_{IN} \times I_Q = 0.06$ W, only 1% of the total.)

### Step 2 — Maximum allowable $\theta_{JA}$

Using the thermal equation:
$$T_{J(max)} = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA(max)}$$

Solving:
$$\theta_{JA(max)} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} = \frac{125 - 45}{5.6} = \frac{80}{5.6} = 14.29 \text{ °C/W}$$

### Step 3 — Compare to free-air $\theta_{JA}$

$$\theta_{JA(free)} = 50 \text{ °C/W} \gg \theta_{JA(max)} = 14.29 \text{ °C/W}$$

Since free-air thermal resistance is **3.5× too high**, the junction would overheat without a heatsink.

### Step 4 — Required heatsink thermal resistance $\theta_{SA}$

The thermal network is:
$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

where $\theta_{CS}$ is the case-to-sink thermal resistance (typically 0.5–1 °C/W for a TO-220 with thermal compound). Using $\theta_{CS} = 0.5$ °C/W:

$$\theta_{SA} = \theta_{JA(max)} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} = 14.29 - 4 - 0.5 = 9.79 \text{ °C/W}$$

So we need a heatsink with $\theta_{SA} \leq 9.79$ °C/W — readily available as a small extruded aluminum clip-on heatsink.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A 7805 dissipating 5+ W **always** needs a heatsink. The 7805 in TO-220 without heatsink can typically only handle $\approx 1$ W safely (i.e., $\approx 140$ mA at 12 V input). This is why most PCB designs route a copper pour under the tab as a poor-man's heatsink.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Four Regulators

| Parameter | Zener Regulator | Series Regulator | LM723 | 78XX/79XX |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topology | Shunt, discrete | Series, discrete | Series, IC (multi-function) | Series, fixed IC |
| Output $V_{OUT}$ | Fixed ($= V_Z$) | Adjustable | Adjustable | Fixed (±5/12/15 V) |
| Output current | 5–50 mA | 100 mA – 5 A | 150 mA (internal) to 10 A (external) | 1 A typical (1.5 A peak) |
| Line regulation | $\approx r_Z/R_S$ | Very high (loop gain) | Very high | $\approx 0.01$ %/V |
| Load regulation | $\approx r_Z$ | Very low $Z_{OUT}$ | Very low $Z_{OUT}$ | 10–15 mV |
| Current limiting | None | Optional (external $Q_3$) | Built-in (external $R_{SC}$) | Built-in + thermal |
| Thermal protection | None | None | None (relies on external) | Built-in |
| Complexity | Trivial (1 R, 1 D) | Moderate (2 Q, 1 D, 2 R) | High (full IC + ext. pass) | Trivial (1 IC, 2 caps) |
| Cost | ₹2 | ₹10 | ₹25–₹40 | ₹15–₹25 |
| KTU typical marks | 3 marks | 3–7 marks | 7–14 marks | 3–14 marks |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a voltage regulator. What is the difference between line regulation and load regulation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **voltage regulator** is an electronic circuit that maintains a constant DC output voltage despite variations in input voltage, load current, or temperature.

- **Line regulation** is the ability of the regulator to keep $V_{OUT}$ constant when $V_{IN}$ varies. Quantified as $\dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}}$ (in V/V or %/V). A *lower* value means *better* regulation.
- **Load regulation** is the ability to keep $V_{OUT}$ constant when the load current $I_L$ varies. Quantified as $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ where $V_{NL}$ is no-load and $V_{FL}$ is full-load output.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the key features of the 78XX series of three-terminal voltage regulators.**

**Model Answer (4–5 points):**

1. **Fixed output voltage** — designated by the "XX" suffix (7805 → +5 V, 7812 → +12 V, 7815 → +15 V, etc.).
2. **Three-terminal package** — Input, Ground, Output. Drop-in simplicity.
3. **Built-in current limiting** — protects against output short circuits.
4. **Built-in thermal shutdown** — disables output at junction temperature $\approx 150$–$175$ °C, protecting against overheating.
5. **Safe-Area (SOA) protection** — protects the pass transistor from secondary breakdown.
6. **Output current** up to **1 A** (with adequate heatsinking).
7. **High ripple rejection** ($\approx 70$ dB) and excellent line/load regulation.
8. **Wide input voltage range** — up to 35 V (derated by the dropout requirement).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full-Length with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q3(a) **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**
**With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a simple Zener voltage regulator. Derive the expression for the series resistance $R_S$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
        V_IN (unregulated)
            │
            │
           R_S
            │
            ├──── V_OUT (= V_Z)
            │
         ┌──┴──┐
         │  D_Z │  (Zener, reverse biased)
         └──┬──┘
            │
           GND
            │
           R_L
            │
           GND
```

**Operation:**

The unregulated DC input $V_{IN}$ (output of a rectifier-filter combination, still containing some ripple) is applied across the series combination of $R_S$ and the parallel combination of the Zener diode $D_Z$ and the load $R_L$. The Zener is reverse-biased and operated in its **breakdown region**, where the voltage across it remains essentially constant at $V_Z$ (the Zener voltage), provided the current through it lies between the minimum knee current $I_{Zmin}$ and the maximum rated current $I_{Zmax}$.

**When $V_{IN}$ increases:** The voltage across $R_S$ increases, so the current $I_S$ through $R_S$ increases. Since the load draws a constant current $I_L = V_Z/R_L$, the *excess* current is shunted through the Zener: $I_Z = I_S - I_L$ increases, but $V_Z$ remains unchanged → $V_{OUT}$ stays constant. ✓

**When $I_L$ increases** (load resistance decreases): For $V_{OUT}$ to stay constant, the Zener must reduce its share of current: $I_Z = I_S - I_L$ decreases, with the extra current going to the load. ✓

**Derivation of $R_S$:**

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** around the input loop:
$$V_{IN} = I_S \cdot R_S + V_{OUT} = I_S \cdot R_S + V_Z$$

Therefore:
$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_S}$$

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law** at the output node:
$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$

Substituting:
$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$$

**Design Bounds for $R_S$:**

For proper regulation under all conditions, $R_S$ must be chosen so that:
1. **$I_Z \geq I_{Zmin}$** (at the lowest $V_{IN}$):
$$R_S \leq \frac{V_{IN(min)} - V_Z}{I_{Zmin} + I_L} = R_{S(max)}$$
2. **$I_Z \leq I_{Zmax}$** (at the highest $V_{IN}$):
$$R_S \geq \frac{V_{IN(max)} - V_Z}{I_{Zmax} + I_L} = R_{S(min)}$$

The chosen $R_S$ must lie within the range $R_{S(min)} \leq R_S \leq R_{S(max)}$.

#### Q3(b) **[7 Marks]**
**A Zener diode has $V_Z = 5.6$ V, $P_Z = 500$ mW, and $I_{Zmin} = 5$ mA. It is used in a regulator with $V_{IN} = 12$ V (constant) supplying a load of $R_L = 560$ Ω. Calculate $R_S$ and verify safe operation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$V_Z = 5.6$ V, $P_Z = 500$ mW, $I_{Zmin} = 5$ mA, $V_{IN} = 12$ V, $R_L = 560$ Ω.

**Step 1 — Compute $I_L$:**
$$I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L} = \frac{5.6}{560} = 0.010 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{Zmax}$:**
$$I_{Zmax} = \frac{P_Z}{V_Z} = \frac{0.5}{5.6} = 0.0893 \text{ A} \approx 89.3 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_S$:**
$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_{Zmin} + I_L} = \frac{12 - 5.6}{0.005 + 0.010} = \frac{6.4}{0.015} = 426.7 \text{ }\Omega$$

Choose the nearest standard value: $R_S = 430$ Ω (1% tolerance).

**Step 4 — Verify with $R_S = 430$ Ω:**
$$I_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{6.4}{430} = 14.88 \text{ mA}$$
$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 14.88 - 10 = 4.88 \text{ mA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$I_Z = 4.88$ mA is *just below* $I_{Zmin} = 5$ mA — the Zener is on the edge of regulation!**

**Step 5 — Increase $R_S$ to a safer value or use 470 Ω:**
$$I_S = \frac{6.4}{470} = 13.62 \text{ mA}$$
$$I_Z = 13.62 - 10 = 3.62 \text{ mA}$$

This is *even worse*. So we should **decrease** $R_S$. Try $R_S = 390$ Ω:
$$I_S = \frac{6.4}{390} = 16.41 \text{ mA}$$
$$I_Z = 16.41 - 10 = 6.41 \text{ mA} \geq 5 \text{ mA} \quad ✓$$

**Step 6 — Power dissipation in $R_S$:**
$$P_{R_S} = (V_{IN} - V_Z) \cdot I_S = 6.4 \times 16.41 \text{ mA} = 0.105 \text{ W}$$

Use a ¼ W or ½ W resistor. (½ W recommended for 2× safety margin.)

**Conclusion:** Use $R_S = 390$ Ω, ½ W. The Zener operates safely with $I_Z = 6.41$ mA, well within $[5, 89.3]$ mA bounds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Q4(a) **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Draw and explain the block diagram of the IC 723 voltage regulator. Compare its low-voltage and high-voltage configurations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
                  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │             IC 723                 │
   V+(pin 12) ──► │                                    │
                  │  ┌─────────┐      ┌──────────┐     │
                  │  │ Constant│      │  Zener   │     │
                  │  │ Current │─────►│ Reference│     │
                  │  │ Source  │      │  7.15 V  │     │──► Pin 6 (V_REF)
                  │  └─────────┘      └──────────┘     │
                  │                       │            │
                  │                       ▼            │
                  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌─────────────┐     │
                  │  │ Current  │  │   Error     │     │
                  │  │  Limit   │  │  Amplifier  │     │──► Pin 7
                  │  │   Q_CL   │  │             │     │
                  │  └──────────┘  └──────┬──────┘     │
                  │                       │            │
                  │                       ▼            │
                  │              ┌────────────────┐    │
                  │              │  Series Pass   │    │──► Pin 10 (V_OUT)
                  │              │   Transistor   │    │
                  │              │   (Internal)   │    │
                  │              └────────┬───────┘    │
                  │                       │            │
                  │         Pin 11 (Collector)         │
                  │                                    │
                  └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Pin connections (14-pin DIP):**
1. NC | 2. Current Limit | 3. Current Sense | 4. INV Input | 5. NON-INV Input | 6. V_REF | 7. Error Amp Output | 8, 9. NC | 10. V_OUT (Pass Emitter) | 11. V_C (Pass Collector) | 12. V+ | 13, 14. NC

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Low-Voltage Mode | High-Voltage Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Output range | 2 V – 7 V | 7 V – 37 V |
| Internal pass transistor | Used directly | Used as a pre-driver |
| External pass transistor | Not needed | Required |
| Pin 11 (collector) | Connected to V_OUT (pin 10) | Connected to V_IN (unregulated) |
| Pin 12 (V+) | Connected to V_IN | Connected to V_IN |
| Feedback divider config | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ |
| Typical application | 5 V logic supply, op-amp rails | +15 V op-amp, ±15 V supplies |

**Operation:** The Zener reference generates a stable $V_{REF} = 7.15$ V. The error amplifier compares a fraction of the output (sensed via the external resistor divider) to $V_{REF}$, and drives the series pass transistor so that $V_{OUT}$ becomes the desired regulated value. The current-limit transistor senses the voltage across $R_{SC}$ and shuts down the pass transistor if $I_L$ exceeds the limit.

#### Q4(b) **[7 Marks]**
**Design a 12 V, 1 A regulated DC supply using the LM723 in the high-voltage configuration with an external pass transistor. Use $V_{REF} = 7.15$ V and set the current limit at 1.2 A. The unregulated input is 24 V. Determine the feedback resistors, current-sense resistor, and the power dissipated in the external pass transistor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{OUT} = 12$ V, $I_L = 1$ A, $V_{REF} = 7.15$ V, $I_{L(max)} = 1.2$ A, $V_{IN} = 24$ V.

**Step 1 — High-voltage mode output equation:**
$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{REF}} - 1 = \frac{12}{7.15} - 1 = 0.678$$

**Step 2 — Choose standard values:**
Let $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Then $R_1 = 6.78 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard 1% value).

**Step 3 — Verify:** $V_{OUT} = 7.15 \times (1 + 6.8/10) = 7.15 \times 1.68 = 12.01$ V ✓

**Step 4 — Current-sense resistor:**
$$R_{SC} = \frac{0.7 \text{ V}}{I_{L(max)}} = \frac{0.7}{1.2} = 0.583 \text{ }\Omega$$

Use $R_{SC} = 0.56$ Ω (closest standard), 2 W rated.

**Step 5 — Power in external pass transistor:**
$$P_{Q_{ext}} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L = (24 - 12) \times 1 = 12 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Heatsink check for Q_ext (e.g., TIP31C, $\theta_{JC} = 10$ °C/W, $T_{J(max)} = 150$ °C, $T_A = 50$ °C):**
$$\theta_{JA(max)} = \frac{150 - 50}{12} = 8.33 \text{ °C/W}$$
$$\theta_{SA(max)} = 8.33 - 10 - 0.5 = -2.17 \text{ °C/W} \text{ (impossible!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is impossible** — $\theta_{SA}$ is *negative*, meaning a single transistor in TO-220 with $\theta_{JC} = 10$ °C/W *cannot* dissipate 12 W. We need a **Darlington (e.g., TIP122, $\theta_{JC} = 1.92$ °C/W)** or a **parallel pair of transistors**.

For TIP122: $\theta_{JC} = 1.92$ °C/W.
$$\theta_{SA(max)} = 8.33 - 1.92 - 0.5 = 5.91 \text{ °C/W}$$

A small clip-on heatsink of $\theta_{SA} \leq 5$ °C/W is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This kind of "negative $\theta_{SA}$" revelation is a classic board-question trick. Always verify the **complete thermal chain** — single transistor is often insufficient at high power.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Power Supply Regulator Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing pinouts of 78XX and 79XX** — In 78XX, pin 1 is INPUT; in 79XX, pin 1 is GROUND. Reversing them destroys the IC. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting dropout voltage** — Students often write $V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT}$ for a 7805, but 2 V dropout means $V_{IN(min)} = 7$ V. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Mixing up low- and high-voltage modes of LM723** — The collector pin (pin 11) is connected to *different* nodes in each mode. **Cost: 2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Not checking the upper AND lower bound** of $R_S$ in Zener regulator design — only one bound is often verified. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Ignoring thermal resistance in 78XX design** — A 7805 delivering 1 A *without* a heatsink will overheat in seconds. Board questions specifically test this. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Using $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$** instead of the actual saturation voltage in pass-transistor calculations. Use $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V (typical) or 0.3 V (worst case).
> 7. **Drawing wrong feedback equation** for the 723 in high-voltage mode — it is a *non-inverting* amplifier, NOT inverting.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Zener Voltage Regulator
- [ ] Operates the Zener diode in **reverse breakdown** with $I_{Zmin} \leq I_Z \leq I_{Zmax}$.
- [ ] $R_S = \dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$ — design bounded by **both** $I_{Zmin}$ and $I_{Zmax}$.
- [ ] Output impedance $\approx r_Z$ (typically 5–50 Ω). Line regulation $\approx r_Z / R_S$.
- [ ] **Limitations:** Poor efficiency, fixed $V_{OUT}$, no current limiting, only tens of mA.

### 🔑 Series Voltage Regulator
- [ ] Pass transistor in **series** with load, driven by an **error amplifier**.
- [ ] **Negative feedback** forces $V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ — adjustable.
- [ ] Far more efficient than shunt regulator. Easily add current limiting via $R_{SC}$ and a sense transistor.
- [ ] Forms the **architectural basis** of all linear IC regulators.

### 🔑 IC 723 Voltage Regulator
- [ ] Internal blocks: **Zener ref (7.15 V) + Error amplifier + Pass transistor + Current-limit transistor**.
- [ ] **Low-voltage mode (2–7 V):** Internal pass transistor used directly. Pin 11 → Pin 10.
- [ ] **High-voltage mode (7–37 V):** Internal transistor drives external NPN pass. Pin 11 → $V_{IN}$.
- [ ] Output equation: $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (low) or $V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ (high).
- [ ] Current limit: $I_{L(max)} = \dfrac{0.7 \text{ V}}{R_{SC}}$, set by external resistor between pins 2 and 3.

### 🔑 3-Pin Regulators — 78XX and 79XX
- [ ] **78XX = +V fixed** (7805 → +5 V, 7812 → +12 V, 7815 → +15 V).
- [ ] **79XX = −V fixed** (7905 → −5 V, 7912 → −12 V).
- [ ] Pinout **reversed** between the two families — a known KTU trap.
- [ ] Need only **two external capacitors** ($C_{IN} = 0.33$ µF, $C_{OUT} = 0.1$ µF) for typical operation.
- [ ] Built-in **thermal shutdown**, **current limiting**, and **SOA protection**.
- [ ] **Dropout voltage** $\approx 2$ V → $V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + 2$ V.
- [ ] Always verify **heatsink requirement** using $\theta_{JA(max)} = (T_{J(max)} - T_A)/P_D$.

### 🔑 Universal Design Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] $R_S$ design bounds for Zener regulator.
- [ ] $V_{OUT}$ for 723 in both modes.
- [ ] $R_{SC} = 0.7 / I_{L(max)}$ for current limit (both 723 and series discrete).
- [ ] $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{OUT}$ for linear regulator power.
- [ ] $P_{D(max)} = (T_{J(max)} - T_A) / \theta_{JA}$ for thermal limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "design a regulated power supply," the examiner expects you to (1) choose the regulator topology, (2) compute the components, (3) verify thermal limits, and (4) state the final bill of materials with ratings. Skipping any of these four steps costs marks.
